<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define AADT. What is its significance in traffic engineering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT) is the total volume of traffic passing a point on a roadway during a full year, divided by 365 days. Mathematically:

$$
AADT = \frac{\text{Total Annual Traffic Volume}}{365}\ \text{(veh/day)}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** AADT is the **fundamental parameter** used for highway geometric design, pavement thickness design (ESAL calculation), determining number of lanes, and prioritizing road improvement projects. It smooths out daily, weekly, and seasonal fluctuations to give a single representative value for the year.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**What is the Peak Hour Factor (PHF)? State its range and significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Peak Hour Factor is the ratio of the total volume observed during the peak hour to four times the peak 15-minute volume within that hour:

$$
PHF = \frac{PHV}{4 \times V_{15,\ max}}
$$

**Range (0.5 marks):** $0.25 \leq PHF \leq 1.00$

**Significance (1 mark):** PHF indicates the **uniformity of traffic flow** within the peak hour. A low PHF (e.g., 0.70) means the actual peak 15-minute flow is much higher than the hourly average, requiring designers to use the higher 15-minute rate for **intersection signal design and capacity checks** to avoid congestion failure.

---

### PART B — Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]

**(a) Explain the various methods of conducting traffic volume studies. Discuss the advantages and limitations of manual and automatic methods. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Traffic volume studies are conducted using the following methods:

**1. Manual Method (3 marks):**
- **Description:** Human enumerators stationed at the survey location record vehicles using tally sheets, tally counters, or electronic tablets.
- **Types of data collected:** Classified counts by vehicle type, turning movements at intersections, directional splits, and lane usage.
- **Advantages:** Can capture vehicle classification, occupancy, and turning movements simultaneously. Low capital cost. Flexible for complex intersections.
- **Limitations:** Labor-intensive, expensive for long durations, susceptible to human error and fatigue, limited to short survey periods (typically 8–12 hours per enumerator shift).

**2. Automatic Method (3 marks):**
- **Pneumatic Tubes:** Rubber tubes stretched across the road; vehicle axles create air pulses counted by a recorder. Suitable for short-term counts on rural roads.
- **Inductive Loop Detectors:** Wire loops embedded in the pavement detect metal mass of passing vehicles. Provide continuous counts, often used for signal actuation and freeway monitoring.
- **Video Detection:** Cameras with image processing software. Useful for classified counts and turning movements.
- **Advantages:** Can record 24/7 for weeks, minimal human intervention, high data accuracy for volume.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost, difficult to install, may not classify vehicles accurately without calibration, sensitive to pavement conditions.

**3. Moving Observer Method (1 mark):**
- A test vehicle travels at the average stream speed; the number of vehicles overtaken and overtaking gives volume estimates. Useful for low-volume roads but less common in modern practice.

**Conclusion:** For KTU context, **manual classified counts** remain dominant for intersection studies in India, while **induction loops and video analytics** are used for long-term automatic counts on highways.

---

**(b) A rural highway has an AADT of 15,000 veh/day. The K-factor is 0.12 and the directional split (D-factor) is 0.60. The peak hour factor is 0.83. Calculate: (i) 30th Highest Hourly Volume, (ii) Directional Design Hourly Volume, and (iii) Peak 15-minute flow rate. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** AADT = 15,000 veh/day, K = 0.12, D = 0.60, PHF = 0.83

**(i) 30th Highest Hourly Volume (≈ DHV):**

$$
DHV = AADT \times K
$$

$$
DHV = 15000 \times 0.12 = 1800\ \text{veh/hr}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) Directional Design Hourly Volume (DDHV):**

$$
DDHV = DHV \times D
$$

$$
DDHV = 1800 \times 0.60 = 1080\ \text{veh/hr (in heavier direction)}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Peak 15-minute Flow Rate:**

Using PHF relation:

$$
PHF = \frac{PHV}{4 \times V_{15,\ max}}
$$

$$
V_{15,\ max} = \frac{PHV}{4 \times PHF}
$$

$$
V_{15,\ max} = \frac{1800}{4 \times 0.83}
$$

$$
V_{15,\ max} = \frac{1800}{3.32}
$$

$$
V_{15,\ max} = 542.17 \approx 542\ \text{veh/15-min}
$$

**(3.5 marks)**

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| 30th HV (DHV) | 1800 veh/hr |
| DDHV (one direction) | 1080 veh/hr |
| Peak 15-min flow | 542 veh/15-min |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]

**(a) Define traffic volume. Explain with neat sketches the different types of volume studies: mid-block, intersection, cordon, and screen line studies. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Traffic volume is the number of vehicles (or persons) passing a point or section of a roadway during a specified time period, usually expressed in vehicles per hour (vph) or vehicles per day (vpd).

**Types of Volume Studies (6 marks):**

**1. Mid-Block Volume Study:** A count is taken on a roadway section away from intersections. The line of count is perpendicular to the direction of flow. Data gives total volume on that segment. Useful for lane design, pavement design, and segment-level capacity analysis.

**2. Intersection Volume Study:** Counts are taken at all approaches of an intersection, including classified turning movements (left, through, right). Required for signal design, channelization, and roundabout design.

**3. Cordon Volume Study:** An imaginary boundary (cordon line) is drawn around a study area (e.g., a CBD or zone). All vehicles crossing this boundary are counted. Used for origin-destination studies and to determine external-internal trips.

**4. Screen Line Study:** An imaginary line is drawn along a natural or artificial barrier (e.g., a river, railway line, or major road) dividing the study area into zones. All vehicles crossing the line are counted. Used to verify O-D survey data and to measure inter-zonal trips.

```
MID-BLOCK STUDY              INTERSECTION STUDY
   --> --> -->                  |   ↑   |
   ============                 |   |   |
   ============                 --->---<---
   --> --> -->                  |   ↓   |
```

```
CORDON STUDY                SCREEN LINE STUDY
  +-------------+            Zone A    Zone B
  |   Study     |          ===|================|=====
  |   Area      |              |  Screen Line  |
  +-------------+           ===|================|=====
   (Cordon Line)
```

---

**(b) At a four-legged intersection approach, the following classified counts were observed during a 1-hour peak period:**

| Vehicle Type | Count in 1 hour |
|---|---|
| Car | 320 |
| Two-wheeler | 180 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 60 |
| Bus | 25 |
| Truck | 15 |

**Using PCU factors: Car = 1.0, Two-wheeler = 0.5, Auto = 1.0, Bus = 3.0, Truck = 3.0, calculate the equivalent hourly flow in PCU/hr. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Write the PCU conversion formula:**

$$
V_{PCU/hr} = \sum (n_i \times PCU_i)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute each value:**

$$
V_{PCU/hr} = (320 \times 1.0) + (180 \times 0.5) + (60 \times 1.0) + (25 \times 3.0) + (15 \times 3.0)
$$

**Step 3: Compute each term:**

$$
= 320 + 90 + 60 + 75 + 45
$$

**Step 4: Final answer:**

$$
V_{PCU/hr} = 590\ \text{PCU/hr}
$$

**(2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for intermediate calculations, 2 marks for final answer with units)**

**Interpretation:** The total classified volume is $320 + 180 + 60 + 25 + 15 = 600$ vehicles, but in PCU terms it is 590 PCU/hr. This is because the high count of two-wheelers (low PCU = 0.5) reduces the PCU total relative to the raw vehicle count.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AADT with ADT:** ADT is for a partial period (e.g., 7-day average); AADT is the full-year average. Examiners deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 2. **PHF Denominator Error:** Many students write $PHF = V_h / V_{15}$ instead of $V_h / (4 \times V_{15,\ max})$. The 4× factor is **mandatory** because the peak hour consists of four 15-minute intervals.
> 3. **Forgetting Direction in DDHV:** The DDHV is for **one direction only** (the heavier one). Make it explicit in the answer.
> 4. **PCU Factor Source:** Always **state the PCU factors** at the start of the solution, even if given in the question. Examiners allocate marks for clear setup.
> 5. **Unit Errors:** Mixing vph, vpd, PCU/hr, and PCU/day without clarity costs marks. Always box the final answer with units.
> 6. **Not Converting 15-min to hourly:** Some students leave the answer in veh/15-min. Always convert to veh/hr for comparison with AADT or DHV.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Reference Definitions

- **Traffic Volume:** Number of vehicles passing a point per unit time
- **AADT:** Total annual volume / 365 days
- **ADT:** Average daily volume for a partial period
- **30th HV:** Hourly volume exceeded only 29 times per year (design standard)
- **K-factor:** DHV / AADT (typically 0.08 to 0.12 for rural, 0.10 to 0.15 for urban)
- **D-factor:** Proportion of DHV in the heavier direction (typically 0.55 to 0.65)
- **PHF:** Peak hour volume / (4 × peak 15-min volume)
- **DDHV:** AADT × K × D
- **PCU:** Passenger Car Unit — normalizes heterogeneous traffic
- **HEF:** Converts 1-hour count to 24-hour volume
- **DEF:** Converts daily count to AADT

### Formula Recall Triggers (for KTU Exams)

| If the question gives... | Then apply this formula first... |
|---|---|
| AADT and K | $DHV = AADT \times K$ |
| AADT, K, and D | $DDHV = AADT \times K \times D$ |
| Peak hour volume and peak 15-min volume | $PHF = PHV / (4 \times V_{15,max})$ |
| Classified vehicle counts | $V_{PCU} = \sum (n_i \times PCU_i)$ |
| Short count with HEF and DEF | $AADT = V_{short} \times HEF \times DEF$ |
| Hourly volume and PHF | $V_{15,max} = PHV / (4 \times PHF)$ |

### Common Units to Memorize

- Volume: **veh/hr, veh/day, veh/yr**
- PCU Flow: **PCU/hr, PCU/day**
- Expansion factors: **dimensionless (unitless ratio)**
- PHF: **dimensionless (0.25 to 1.00)**
- K and D factors: **dimensionless (0 to 1)**

### KTU High-Yield Numerical Patterns

1. **AADT → DHV → DDHV chain** (3-step calculation)
2. **Vehicle mix → PCU conversion** (5–6 vehicle types typical)
3. **Expansion factor problem** (HEF × DEF)
4. **PHF computation from given peak volumes**
5. **Reverse engineering:** Given DHV and AADT, find K-factor

### Conceptual Pearls (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)

- **Why 30th hour?** A compromise between economic design and operational sufficiency.
- **Why PCU?** Indian traffic is highly heterogeneous; PCU provides a uniform metric.
- **Why PHF matters?** Hourly averages mask short-term surges that cause congestion.
- **Cordon vs Screen Line:** Cordon encloses an area; Screen Line divides an area.
- **HEF and DEF:** Used to convert **short-duration counts** to **long-term estimates**, saving cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:** Practice 5 numerical problems covering (AADT→DHV→DDHV), (PCU conversion), (Expansion factors), (PHF), and (Reverse K-factor). These 5 patterns cover **90% of all KTU numerical questions on Volume**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Travel Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Travel Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Travel Time** is defined as the total time consumed by a vehicle (or a representative vehicle) to traverse a given length of roadway, segment, or a complete route between two defined points, expressed typically in **seconds** or **minutes**.

In the formal KTU 2024 scheme terminology, travel time is a **primary measure of traffic performance** and a direct input to determine the **Level of Service (LOS)** of a highway facility. It encompasses the entire duration from the moment a vehicle crosses the upstream reference line until it crosses the downstream reference line, irrespective of whether the vehicle is moving, decelerating, idling, or stopped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematically:**
> $$T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i$$
> where $\Delta t_i$ are the elementary time intervals (running, decelerating, idling, accelerating) that together constitute the journey.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are driving from your college hostel to the railway station, a distance of **8 km**.

- If the road is **empty at 6 AM**, you might cover the 8 km in just **12 minutes** — this is largely **running time**.
- If you do the **same trip at 6 PM**, you might take **35 minutes** because of red lights, congestion, jaywalkers, and parking manoeuvres — this is the **total travel time**.

The **extra 23 minutes** is not because the road got longer; it is the *delay* the traffic system imposed on you. The study of travel time in Traffic Engineering is essentially the scientific measurement of this entire experience — and identifying **how much of it is productive motion versus wasted delay**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Travel time is a direct parameter used in:
> - **LOS Analysis** (Highway Capacity Manual, HCM methodology)
> - **Economic evaluation** of highway projects (value of travel time savings, VTTS)
> - **Route choice & navigation studies**
> - **Congestion management & signal coordination**

### 1.3 Key Terminology Used in Travel Time Studies

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Travel Time (T)** | Total time taken to traverse a road segment (motion + delay) |
| **Running Time (T_r)** | Time during which the vehicle is actually in motion |
| **Delay (D)** | Time lost due to causes other than normal running |
| **Free-Flow Travel Time (T_0)** | Travel time at posted speed limit under no-impedance conditions |
| **Space Mean Speed (V_s)** | Average speed based on a spatial length of road (km/h) |
| **Time Mean Speed (V_t)** | Arithmetic mean of individual spot speeds of vehicles (km/h) |
| **Travel Time Index (TTI)** | Ratio of actual travel time to free-flow travel time |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Travel Time

Travel time is not a single uniform quantity — it is a **composite** of several operational sub-phases. A rigorous understanding requires decomposing it:

**1. Running Time ($T_r$)**
The portion of journey time during which the vehicle is **physically in motion**. It is calculated as:
$$T_r = \frac{L}{V_r}$$
where $L$ is the section length, $V_r$ is the **running speed** (distance ÷ running time).

**2. Delay Time ($D$)**
Delay is the **difference** between travel time and running time. It is classified by **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)** as:

- **Fixed Delay:** Caused by traffic control devices — signals, stop signs, railway crossings. *Predictable and recurrent.*
- **Operational Delay:** Caused by interference from other traffic — lane changes, parking manoeuvres, pedestrian crossings, bus stops. *Semi-random.*
- **Stopped Delay:** The sub-component where the vehicle is **physically at rest** (zero velocity). Measured by observers using stopwatches.
- **Approach Delay:** The time lost due to **deceleration upon arrival** at a queue and **acceleration after departure**. This is hard to capture with a stopwatch.
- **Travel Time Delay:** The total additional time beyond the free-flow travel time, encompassing all the above.

The total relationship is:
$$T = T_r + D_{fixed} + D_{operational}$$

### 2.2 The Greenshields & Wardrop Foundation

Two pioneering works form the theoretical bedrock of modern travel-time studies:

> [!NOTE]
> **Greenshields (1947)** performed the first systematic photographic study of traffic and laid down the **linear speed–density model**. He also pioneered the **floating car technique** for measuring travel time, where the test driver tries to "float" by overtaking as many vehicles as overtake the test car.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wardrop (1953)** formalized the floating car method mathematically. If the test car records a journey time $t_w$ and encounters $x$ overtaking vehicles and $y$ overtaken vehicles in a stream of flow $q$, the **true average travel time** is given by a simple but elegant correction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Travel Time (Basic) | $T = L \div V$ | $L$ in km, $V$ in km/h, $T$ in hours |
| Running Time | $T_r = L \div V_r$ | $V_r$ = running speed (km/h) |
| Total Delay | $D = T - T_r$ | Both in same time units |
| **Wardrop's Floating Car (1-way)** | $T_{avg} = t_w - \frac{(x - y)}{q}$ | $t_w$ = test car time, $x$ = overtaken by, $y$ = overtakes, $q$ = flow (veh/h) |
| **Floating Car (Round Trip)** | $T_{avg} = \frac{(t_w + t_a)}{2} - \frac{(x - y)}{2q}$ | $t_a$ = return time |
| **Space Mean Speed** | $V_s = \frac{L}{\frac{1}{n}\sum T_i} = \frac{n}{\sum (1/V_i)}$ | $T_i$ = individual travel times |
| **Time Mean Speed** | $V_t = \frac{1}{n}\sum V_i$ | $V_i$ = individual spot speeds |
| **SMS–TMS Relationship** | $V_s = V_t - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ = variance of spot speeds |
| **Travel Time Index** | $\text{TTI} = \dfrac{T}{T_0}$ | $T_0$ = free-flow travel time |
| **Buffer Index** | $\text{BI} = \dfrac{T_{95} - T_{avg}}{T_{avg}}$ | $T_{95}$ = 95th percentile travel time |
| **Planning Time Index** | $\text{PTI} = \dfrac{T_{95}}{T_0}$ | |
| **Greenshields Speed–Density** | $u = u_f\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$ | $u_f$ = free-flow speed, $k_j$ = jam density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention for Wardrop's Formula:**
> - $x$ = **Number of vehicles that OVERTAKE the test car** (going faster)
> - $y$ = **Number of vehicles the test car OVERTAKES** (going slower)
> - If $x > y$: test car is **slower** than average → subtract a positive correction
> - If $y > x$: test car is **faster** than average → correction becomes additive

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Travel time data is the **currency of modern traffic engineering**:

1. **Congestion Monitoring:** Agencies like NHAI and MoRTH publish real-time TTI maps for Indian highways.
2. **Signal Coordination:** Green-wave design along urban corridors is validated by measuring travel time progression.
3. **Route Choice Modelling:** In a road network, travellers choose the path of **minimum generalized cost** (travel time + tolls + fuel).
4. **Economic Appraisal:** Every minute saved on a highway has a monetary value (₹/hr) used in cost-benefit analysis.
5. **Incident Detection:** A sudden spike in corridor travel time indicates an accident or breakdown.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Wardrop's Floating Car Formula

We derive the one-direction formula $T_{avg} = t_w - \dfrac{(x - y)}{q}$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Set up the experimental situation**

Consider a road section of length $L$ between two reference points A and B. A test car enters point A and exits at point B, recording the journey time as $t_w$. The traffic flow on the section in this direction is $q$ (vehicles/hour).

**Step 2: Count the vehicles that pass point B during the journey time**

During the time interval $t_w$ (in hours), the total number of vehicles crossing point B is:
$$N = q \cdot t_w$$

**Step 3: Decompose this stream of vehicles into two groups**

Out of the $N$ vehicles that passed point B during the test run:

- **Group 1:** Vehicles that were **behind the test car at point A** but **finished ahead** of it at B — they **overtook the test car**. Count = $x$.
- **Group 2:** Vehicles that were **ahead of the test car at point A** but the test car **finished ahead** of them at B — the **test car overtook them**. Count = $y$.

So the net count of vehicles **ahead** of the test car at B but **behind** it at A is $(x - y)$.

**Step 4: Calculate the time by which the test car is slower than the average**

If the test car is slower than the average, then the average vehicle took less time. The time difference per vehicle for the $(x - y)$ vehicles is the time correction.

The flow $q$ means in 1 hour, $q$ vehicles pass point B. Therefore, in the time taken for **one extra vehicle** to overtake the test car, an extra time of $\frac{1}{q}$ hours is consumed.

For $(x - y)$ extra vehicles overtaking:
$$\text{Time correction} = \frac{x - y}{q}$$

**Step 5: Apply the correction**

The test car's time $t_w$ is **larger** than the true average $T_{avg}$ by this correction amount. Hence:
$$t_w = T_{avg} + \frac{x - y}{q}$$

Rearranging:
$$T_{avg} = t_w - \frac{x - y}{q}$$

This is the **Wardrop's one-way floating car formula**, and it elegantly requires only a stopwatch and a counter — making it the most field-friendly method in traffic engineering. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of SMS–TMS Relationship

We derive the fundamental relationship $V_s = V_t - \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t}$.

**Step 1: Definition of Time Mean Speed**

For $n$ vehicles, each observed with spot speed $V_i$:
$$V_t = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$

**Step 2: Definition of Space Mean Speed**

Space mean speed is the harmonic mean of spot speeds (length weighted). If a length $L$ is observed, vehicle $i$ takes time $L/V_i$, so:
$$V_s = \frac{L}{\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{L}{V_i}} = \frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{V_i}}$$

**Step 3: Use a Taylor Series expansion for $1/V_i$ around $V_t$**

Let $V_i = V_t + \epsilon_i$ where $\epsilon_i$ is a small deviation. Using the first-order Taylor expansion:
$$\frac{1}{V_i} = \frac{1}{V_t} - \frac{\epsilon_i}{V_t^{2}} + \frac{\epsilon_i^{2}}{V_t^{3}} - \ldots$$

Retaining up to second-order terms and summing over $i$:
$$\sum \frac{1}{V_i} = \frac{n}{V_t} - \frac{1}{V_t^{2}}\sum \epsilon_i + \frac{1}{V_t^{3}}\sum \epsilon_i^{2}$$

**Step 4: Apply statistical identities**

Since $V_t$ is the mean, $\sum \epsilon_i = 0$. Also, $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \epsilon_i^{2} = \sigma^{2}$, so $\sum \epsilon_i^{2} = n\sigma^{2}$.

Substituting:
$$\sum \frac{1}{V_i} = \frac{n}{V_t} + \frac{n\sigma^{2}}{V_t^{3}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into $V_s$ formula**

$$V_s = \frac{n}{\frac{n}{V_t} + \frac{n\sigma^{2}}{V_t^{3}}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{V_t} + \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t^{3}}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $V_t^{3}$:
$$V_s = \frac{V_t^{3}}{V_t^{2} + \sigma^{2}} = V_t \cdot \frac{V_t^{2}}{V_t^{2} + \sigma^{2}}$$

Since $\sigma^{2} \ll V_t^{2}$ in most engineering situations, we apply the approximation $\dfrac{1}{1+\delta} \approx 1 - \delta$:

$$V_s \approx V_t \left(1 - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t^{2}}\right) = V_t - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t}$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** linking space mean speed (used in macroscopic flow theory) to time mean speed (measured in spot-speed studies). $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Floating Car Method

**Problem:** A test car traverses a 2 km urban road section. The following data is collected:
- Time in direction 1 (with traffic): $t_1 = 3.0$ min
- Vehicles overtaking the test car in direction 1: $x_1 = 12$
- Vehicles overtaken by the test car in direction 1: $y_1 = 8$
- Traffic flow in direction 1: $q = 1500$ veh/h

**Find:** Average travel time on the section in direction 1.

**Solution:**

Convert flow to a consistent time base:
$$q = 1500 \text{ veh/h} = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ veh/min}$$

Apply Wardrop's formula:
$$T_{avg} = t_1 - \frac{(x_1 - y_1)}{q}$$

Substituting:
$$T_{avg} = 3.0 - \frac{(12 - 8)}{25}$$

$$T_{avg} = 3.0 - \frac{4}{25}$$

$$T_{avg} = 3.0 - 0.16 = 2.84 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_{avg} = 2.84 \text{ min} = 170.4 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Interpretation:** The test car recorded 3.0 min, but the test car was **driving slower than the average driver** (since $x_1 > y_1$, more vehicles overtook it than it overtook). The true average travel time is 2.84 min — about 9.3% lower.

Corresponding average running speed:
$$V_{avg} = \frac{L}{T_{avg}} = \frac{2 \text{ km}}{2.84/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{2}{0.0473} = 42.25 \text{ km/h}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — SMS and TMS

**Problem:** Spot speeds (km/h) of 8 vehicles observed at a mid-block section:
$$V = \{42, 48, 55, 51, 60, 45, 53, 50\}$$

**Find:** (a) Time Mean Speed, (b) Space Mean Speed, (c) Standard Deviation.

**Solution (a): Time Mean Speed**

$$V_t = \frac{1}{8}(42+48+55+51+60+45+53+50) = \frac{404}{8} = 50.5 \text{ km/h}$$

**Solution (b): Space Mean Speed (Direct Method — Harmonic Mean)**

Compute $\sum \frac{1}{V_i}$:

$$\sum \frac{1}{V_i} = \frac{1}{42}+\frac{1}{48}+\frac{1}{55}+\frac{1}{51}+\frac{1}{60}+\frac{1}{45}+\frac{1}{53}+\frac{1}{50}$$

$$= 0.02381 + 0.02083 + 0.01818 + 0.01961 + 0.01667 + 0.02222 + 0.01887 + 0.02000$$

$$= 0.16019$$

$$V_s = \frac{n}{\sum \frac{1}{V_i}} = \frac{8}{0.16019} = 49.94 \text{ km/h}$$

**Solution (b) Alternative: Using SMS–TMS Relationship**

First compute variance:
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (V_i - V_t)^{2}$$

Computing each squared deviation:
$$(42-50.5)^{2} = 72.25$$
$$(48-50.5)^{2} = 6.25$$
$$(55-50.5)^{2} = 20.25$$
$$(51-50.5)^{2} = 0.25$$
$$(60-50.5)^{2} = 90.25$$
$$(45-50.5)^{2} = 30.25$$
$$(53-50.5)^{2} = 6.25$$
$$(50-50.5)^{2} = 0.25$$

Sum = $226.00$, so:
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{226.00}{8} = 28.25 (\text{km/h})^{2}$$

Apply the relationship:
$$V_s = V_t - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t} = 50.5 - \frac{28.25}{50.5}$$

$$V_s = 50.5 - 0.559 = 49.94 \text{ km/h}$$

Both methods agree. $\checkmark$

**Solution (c): Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{28.25} = 5.32 \text{ km/h}$$
