## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define travel time. How is it different from running time?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Travel Time** is the total time taken by a vehicle to traverse a specified length of road or highway between two defined reference points. It includes all the time the vehicle spends in motion, decelerating, idling, accelerating, and stopped conditions.

**Running Time**, in contrast, is the portion of travel time during which the vehicle is **actually in motion**. It is the time the vehicle is not delayed or stopped.

**Mathematically:**
$$T = T_r + D \quad \text{where } D = \text{Total Delay}$$

For example, if a vehicle takes **15 minutes** to travel 10 km, but is **stopped at signals for 3 minutes**, then:
- Travel Time = 15 min
- Running Time = 12 min
- Delay = 3 min

Running time is used to compute the **running speed** ($V_r = L/T_r$), while travel time is used to compute the **overall journey speed** or **space mean speed** ($V_s = L/T$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is the Floating Car Method? Why is it called "floating"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Floating Car Method** (also called the **Test Car Method**) is a direct field technique for measuring the average travel time on a road section. A test vehicle is driven along the study section, and the driver attempts to **"float"** with the traffic stream by overtaking as many vehicles as overtake the test car.

It is called "floating" because the test car is **neither leading nor trailing** the traffic — it moves at the **average pace of all vehicles**, with the **number of vehicles passing the test car ($x$) equal to the number of vehicles the test car passes ($y$)**. When perfect floating is achieved ($x = y$), the test car's recorded time **equals the true average travel time** without any mathematical correction.

The governing formula (Wardrop) when $x \neq y$ is:
$$T_{avg} = t_w - \frac{(x - y)}{q}$$

The method is widely used because it requires **only a stopwatch, a counter, and a driver** — making it the most economical travel time study technique for field deployment.

---

### Part B: Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the various methods of conducting travel time and delay studies on a road section. Compare their **relative merits and demerits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A test car is run on a 4 km urban arterial. The data collected is:
- Time from A to B: 5.2 min  
- Time from B to A: 6.0 min  
- Vehicles overtaking the test car (A→B): 15  
- Vehicles overtaken by the test car (A→B): 9  
- Vehicles overtaking the test car (B→A): 10  
- Vehicles overtaken by the test car (B→A): 12  
- Traffic flow (assumed equal in both directions): 1200 veh/h

Calculate the **average travel time** in each direction using Wardrop's method. Also determine the **average journey speed** in each direction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Methods of Travel Time and Delay Studies:**

**1. Floating Car / Test Car Method (Wardrop's Method)**
- *Procedure:* Test car driver tries to "float" with traffic. Records time, overtakes, overtaken vehicles.
- *Merits:* Low cost; requires only a stopwatch and counter; provides running time and delay simultaneously.
- *Demerits:* Driver cannot be perfectly objective; only one vehicle sample per run; not suitable for very high flows.

**2. License Plate Method**
- *Procedure:* Observers stationed at both ends note the last 3 or 4 digits of license plates. Matching plates yield individual travel times.
- *Merits:* Captures the **entire traffic stream**; objective; no observer inside vehicle.
- *Demerits:* Manual matching is tedious; large samples needed; difficult at night; does not easily capture mid-link delays.

**3. Interview Method**
- *Procedure:* Roadside interviews of drivers/commuters to estimate origin–destination and perceived travel time.
- *Merits:* Useful for **route choice and O-D studies**; large sample possible.
- *Demerits:* Subjective (perception-based); recall bias; not precise for LOS.

**4. Photographic / Video Method**
- *Procedure:* Time-lapse camera or video records vehicle entry and exit.
- *Merits:* Permanent record; can be re-analysed; high accuracy.
- *Demerits:* Equipment cost; weather dependence; needs proper illumination at night.

**5. ITS-Based Methods (GPS, Bluetooth, ANPR, Cellular)**
- *Procedure:* Probe vehicles with GPS or Bluetooth MAC readers detect re-identification at corridor ends.
- *Merits:* Continuous, automated, large sample; works 24×7; real-time.
- *Demerits:* High installation cost; privacy concerns; GPS penetration bias.

**6. Spot-Speed + Volume Indirect Method**
- *Procedure:* Measure spot speed at a mid-block and volume; use Greenshields' model to infer travel time.
- *Merits:* Uses already-collected data; cheap.
- *Demerits:* Approximate; assumes steady-state flow.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given Data:**
- $L = 4$ km  
- $t_{AB} = 5.2$ min  
- $t_{BA} = 6.0$ min  
- For A→B: $x_1 = 15$, $y_1 = 9$  
- For B→A: $x_2 = 10$, $y_2 = 12$  
- $q = 1200$ veh/h $= 20$ veh/min

**Step 1: Convert flow to per-minute basis**
$$q = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ veh/min}$$

**Step 2: Apply Wardrop's formula for direction A→B**
$$T_{AB} = t_{AB} - \frac{(x_1 - y_1)}{q}$$
$$T_{AB} = 5.2 - \frac{(15 - 9)}{20}$$
$$T_{AB} = 5.2 - \frac{6}{20}$$
$$T_{AB} = 5.2 - 0.30$$
$$\boxed{T_{AB} = 4.9 \text{ min} = 294 \text{ sec}}$$

**Step 3: Apply Wardrop's formula for direction B→A**
$$T_{BA} = t_{BA} - \frac{(x_2 - y_2)}{q}$$
$$T_{BA} = 6.0 - \frac{(10 - 12)}{20}$$
$$T_{BA} = 6.0 - \frac{(-2)}{20}$$
$$T_{BA} = 6.0 - (-0.10)$$
$$T_{BA} = 6.0 + 0.10$$
$$\boxed{T_{BA} = 6.1 \text{ min} = 366 \text{ sec}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In direction B→A, the test car overtook more vehicles than overtook it ($y_2 > x_2$), meaning the test car was **driving faster** than the average. The correction is therefore **additive** (subtracting a negative number).

**Step 4: Compute average journey speed**

For direction A→B:
$$V_{AB} = \frac{L}{T_{AB}} = \frac{4 \text{ km}}{4.9/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{4 \times 60}{4.9} = 48.98 \text{ km/h}$$

For direction B→A:
$$V_{BA} = \frac{L}{T_{BA}} = \frac{4 \text{ km}}{6.1/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{4 \times 60}{6.1} = 39.34 \text{ km/h}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Direction | Travel Time | Journey Speed |
|---|---|---|
| A → B | 4.9 min | 48.98 km/h |
| B → A | 6.1 min | 39.34 km/h |

The asymmetry suggests the peak flow direction (A→B) is faster, or that the test car experienced fewer obstacles in that direction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define **Space Mean Speed** and **Time Mean Speed**. State the fundamental relationship between the two and **derive it mathematically**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The spot speeds of vehicles (in km/h) recorded at a section of a highway are:
$$\{ 35, 42, 48, 52, 55, 60, 65, 48, 50, 45 \}$$

Calculate:
(i) Time Mean Speed  
(ii) Space Mean Speed (using both harmonic mean and the SMS–TMS relation)  
(iii) Standard Deviation  
(iv) Travel Time Index, assuming a free-flow speed of 70 km/h on this section. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition 1: Time Mean Speed (TMS)**
Time Mean Speed is the **arithmetic mean** of the speeds of all vehicles passing a **fixed point** (a spot) over a specified time interval.
$$V_t = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$
It is essentially the average **spot speed** at a location.

**Definition 2: Space Mean Speed (SMS)**
Space Mean Speed is the **harmonic mean** of the speeds of vehicles occupying a **specified length of road** at a given instant. It is calculated as the section length divided by the **average travel time** of vehicles traversing that section.
$$V_s = \frac{L}{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i} = \frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{V_i}}$$

**Physical Distinction:** TMS weights every vehicle **equally** (regardless of how long it occupies the road), while SMS weights vehicles **by the time they spend** on the road (slow vehicles get more weight). SMS is the more meaningful parameter in macroscopic flow theory (since density $k = q / V_s$ uses it).

**Derivation of the SMS–TMS Relationship:**

The full derivation has been shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The result is:
$$V_s = V_t - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t}$$

where $\sigma^{2}$ is the variance of spot speeds. The derivation uses a **Taylor series expansion** of $1/V_i$ around $V_t$, then applies the statistical identity $\sum \epsilon_i = 0$ (since $V_t$ is the mean) and $\sum \epsilon_i^{2} = n\sigma^{2}$.

For the detailed step-by-step derivation, refer to Section 3.2 of this study material.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given data:** $V = \{35, 42, 48, 52, 55, 60, 65, 48, 50, 45\}$, $n = 10$

**(i) Time Mean Speed:**
$$V_t = \frac{35+42+48+52+55+60+65+48+50+45}{10} = \frac{500}{10}$$
$$\boxed{V_t = 50.0 \text{ km/h}}$$

**(ii)(a) Space Mean Speed — Harmonic Mean Method:**

Compute $\sum 1/V_i$:

| $V_i$ | $1/V_i$ |
|---|---|
| 35 | 0.02857 |
| 42 | 0.02381 |
| 48 | 0.02083 |
| 52 | 0.01923 |
| 55 | 0.01818 |
| 60 | 0.01667 |
| 65 | 0.01538 |
| 48 | 0.02083 |
| 50 | 0.02000 |
| 45 | 0.02222 |
| **Sum** | **0.20573** |

$$V_s = \frac{n}{\sum 1/V_i} = \frac{10}{0.20573}$$
$$\boxed{V_s = 48.61 \text{ km/h}}$$

**(ii)(b) Space Mean Speed — SMS–TMS Relation:**

First compute variance:
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (V_i - V_t)^{2}$$

Squared deviations:
$$(35-50)^{2} = 225, \quad (42-50)^{2} = 64$$
$$(48-50)^{2} = 4, \quad (52-50)^{2} = 4$$
$$(55-50)^{2} = 25, \quad (60-50)^{2} = 100$$
$$(65-50)^{2} = 225, \quad (48-50)^{2} = 4$$
$$(50-50)^{2} = 0, \quad (45-50)^{2} = 25$$

Sum $= 225+64+4+4+25+100+225+4+0+25 = 676$

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{676}{10} = 67.6 (\text{km/h})^{2}$$

Apply relation:
$$V_s = V_t - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t} = 50 - \frac{67.6}{50} = 50 - 1.352$$
$$\boxed{V_s = 48.65 \text{ km/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two methods give **slightly different** results (48.61 vs 48.65 km/h). The relationship is an **approximation** valid when $\sigma^{2} \ll V_t^{2}$. The exact value is the harmonic mean (48.61 km/h).

**(iii) Standard Deviation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{67.6} = 8.22 \text{ km/h}$$

**(iv) Travel Time Index (TTI):**

Assume free-flow speed $V_f = 70$ km/h, so free-flow travel time per km = $1/70$ h. For a unit length section:
$$\text{TTI} = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{free}} = \frac{1/V_s}{1/V_f} = \frac{V_f}{V_s} = \frac{70}{48.61}$$
$$\boxed{\text{TTI} = 1.44}$$

A TTI of 1.44 indicates that during the study period, **journeys took 44% longer** than under free-flow conditions — a typical value for **moderately congested urban arterials** (TTI > 1.3 indicates noticeable congestion).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Wardrop's formula:** Many students write $T = t + \frac{x-y}{q}$ instead of minus. **Always remember:** if the test car is **overpassed** (i.e., $x > y$, more vehicles overtake it), the test car is **slower** than the average, so its time is **larger** than the average → **subtract** the correction.
> 2. **Confusing SMS with TMS:** Spot-speed studies yield **TMS**; travel-time studies on a known length yield **SMS**. Using the wrong quantity in flow–density–speed equations (e.g., Greenshields) gives wrong LOS.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion in $q$:** Flow $q$ must be in **vehicles per same time unit** as $t$. If $t$ is in **minutes**, convert $q$ to **veh/min** (divide by 60).
> 4. **Confusing variables in the floating car method:** Some textbooks (older editions) reverse the definitions of $x$ and $y$. In the KTU 2024 scheme convention, $x$ = vehicles **overtaking the test car** (i.e., faster than test car).
> 5. **Neglecting delay components:** A travel-time question asking "list causes of delay" requires all 4 HCM categories (Fixed, Operational, Stopped, Approach) — partial marking only if one is missed.
> 6. **Mixing up TTI with BI:** TTI uses **average** travel time; Buffer Index uses **95th percentile** travel time.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Travel Time**

- **Travel time (T)** is **total journey time** = running time + delay; measured in seconds or minutes.
- **Running time ($T_r$)** is the time the vehicle is **actually in motion**; $T = T_r + D$.
- **Free-flow travel time ($T_0$)** is the journey time at the **posted speed limit** with no impedance.
- **Travel Time Index (TTI)** = $T/T_0$; values > 1.3 indicate congestion; > 2.0 = severe congestion.
- **Wardrop's Floating Car Formula (1-way):** $T_{avg} = t_w - \frac{(x-y)}{q}$; requires only a stopwatch, counter, and known flow.
- **Round-trip variant:** $T_{avg} = \frac{(t_w + t_a)}{2} - \frac{(x-y)}{2q}$ when $q$ is the same in both directions.
- **Sign rule:** $x > y$ → test car **slower** → subtract; $y > x$ → test car **faster** → add correction.
- **Time Mean Speed (TMS):** Arithmetic mean of spot speeds; **Space Mean Speed (SMS):** Harmonic mean (or $L$/average travel time).
- **Key relationship:** $V_s = V_t - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{V_t}$ (where $\sigma^{2}$ is variance of spot speeds); always $V_s \leq V_t$.
- **Delay types (HCM):** Fixed (signals/controls), Operational (interference), Stopped (at rest), Approach (decel/accel), Travel time delay (excess over free-flow).
- **Study methods:** Floating Car (cheapest), License Plate (most accurate for streams), Interview (subjective), Photographic (permanent record), ITS/GPS/Bluetooth (modern, large samples).
- **Minimum sample size for travel-time studies** (95% confidence, $\pm 10\%$): typically **6 runs per direction** as a practical minimum for floating car.
- **Greenberg's or Greenshields' models** convert spot-speed + volume data into **travel time** indirectly.
- **Always convert units consistently** in numerical problems: $q$ in veh/h with $t$ in hours; or $q$ in veh/min with $t$ in minutes.
- **TTI = 1.0** = free flow; **TTI = 1.3** = light congestion; **TTI ≥ 1.5** = moderate-to-heavy congestion.
- **Buffer Index (BI)** quantifies travel time **reliability** — extra time needed to be on time 95% of the time.
- **Planning Time Index (PTI)** = $T_{95}/T_0$ — used by motorists to **plan departure** to ensure on-time arrival.
- In KTU answer sheets, **always draw a block diagram** for component-based questions; **always show unit conversion** explicitly in numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Headway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Headway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Headway** is a fundamental microscopic traffic stream parameter that quantifies the separation between successive vehicles in a traffic stream, measured either in time or in space. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for Traffic Engineering and Management, headway is rigorously classified into two principal dimensions:

1. **Time Headway (h):** The elapsed time, usually expressed in **seconds**, between the passage of two successive vehicles measured from a common reference point (e.g., a roadside detector or a cross-section) on the roadway. Formally, it is the time difference between the rear bumper of the leading vehicle and the front bumper of the following vehicle crossing the same point.

2. **Space Headway / Spacing (s):** The physical distance, expressed in **meters**, between two successive vehicles measured from the rear bumper of the leading vehicle to the front bumper of the following vehicle at a given instant of time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Headway is treated as a primary descriptor of traffic flow behaviour. It is intrinsically linked to macroscopic parameters (flow, speed, density) and is also used as the basic input variable for **Poisson's distribution** modelling of vehicle arrivals — a mandatory topic in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train of Bubbles"

Imagine standing on a footpath watching a line of **soap bubbles** floating in a stream of water. Each bubble represents a vehicle. The time between consecutive bubbles passing your fingertip is the **time headway** (you measure it with a stopwatch). The distance between two adjacent bubbles at the exact moment you press "stop" is the **space headway** (you measure it with a ruler). 

If the bubbles float faster but stay equally spaced, the **time headway decreases** (they arrive at your finger more frequently), but the **space headway remains constant**. Conversely, if bubbles bunch closer together at the same speed, **space headway decreases** but the bubbles do not necessarily arrive at your finger any faster.

This intuition is the key to understanding the entire traffic-flow theory built upon headway.

## 1.3 Why Headway Matters in Engineering Practice

- It directly determines the **capacity** of a roadway (in vehicles/hour).
- It governs the **signal timing design** at intersections (green time = critical headway × saturation flow).
- It serves as the **safety cushion** in car-following theory and intelligent transportation systems (ITS).
- It is a critical input to **level-of-service (LOS)** evaluation as per the **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reminder for KTU 2024:**
> - Time Headway → **seconds (s)**
> - Space Headway → **meters (m)**
> - Flow derived from headway → **vehicles/hour (vph)**
> - Density derived from spacing → **vehicles/km (vpkm)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Headway Concept

The complete theoretical structure of headway rests on three foundational pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Time Headway (h)

- Defined at a **fixed spatial cross-section** (a point on the road).
- Measured as the **time interval** between the instants two consecutive vehicles cross that cross-section.
- Typically measured between the **front bumpers** of successive vehicles (Bumper-to-Bumper Time) or sometimes front bumper of leader to front bumper of follower.
- Statistically, headways are **random variables** that follow specific probability distributions (Poisson, Negative Exponential, etc.) for low-to-moderate flows.

### Pillar 2 — Space Headway / Spacing (s)

- Defined at a **fixed instant of time** (a snapshot of the traffic stream).
- Measured as the **distance** between successive vehicles along the longitudinal axis of the road.
- It is a **spatial distribution parameter** of vehicles in the stream.
- The reciprocal of spacing gives **density (k)**.

### Pillar 3 — The Coupling Equation (Why they are linked)

Time headway and space headway are not independent. They are coupled through the **average running speed (v)** of the traffic stream using the **Fundamental Equation of Traffic Flow**:

The **"Why"** behind the coupling: In a continuous traffic stream, if vehicles move at a constant speed, the time interval between two vehicles at a point is exactly the time it takes the second vehicle to cover the distance separating them. That distance is the space headway, and the time is the time headway. Therefore: $s = v \times h$.

## 2.2 The Poisson / Negative Exponential Headway Model

For low-to-moderate flow conditions where vehicle arrivals are **random and independent** (e.g., on an urban arterial with light traffic), KTU examiners frequently test the **Poisson arrival model** for headways.

The key probability statements are:

- Probability of exactly $n$ vehicles arriving in time $t$:

$$P(n) = \frac{(\lambda t)^n \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$

- Probability of a headway being **greater than or equal to $t$** seconds:

$$P(h \geq t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

- Probability of a headway being **less than $t$** seconds:

$$P(h < t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **mean arrival rate** in vehicles/second (numerically equal to $q / 3600$ when $q$ is in vph).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Sl. No.** | **Parameter / Relation** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary / Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time headway (definition) | $h$ = time gap between successive vehicles at a point | $\text{seconds}$ | $h \geq 0$ |
| 2 | Space headway (definition) | $s$ = distance gap between successive vehicles at an instant | $\text{meters}$ | $s \geq 0$ |
| 3 | Flow from time headway | $q = \dfrac{3600}{h}$ | $\text{vph}$ | Applies for average headway in seconds |
| 4 | Density from space headway | $k = \dfrac{1000}{s}$ | $\text{vpkm}$ | $s$ in meters |
| 5 | Coupling relation | $s = v \cdot h$ | meters | $v$ in m/s; or $s = \dfrac{v_{kmph} \cdot h}{3.6}$ |
| 6 | Fundamental relation | $q = k \cdot v$ | — | Universal traffic flow identity |
| 7 | Mean headway from flow | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{3600}{q}$ | seconds | Inverse relationship |
| 8 | Negative exponential (headway $\geq t$) | $P(h \geq t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda$ in veh/s |
| 9 | Negative exponential (headway $< t$) | $P(h < t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Used for gap-acceptance problems |
| 10 | Mean of negative exponential | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | seconds | Confirms $q = \lambda \cdot 3600$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Unit Consistency:**
> Always remember to convert km/h → m/s by dividing by 3.6, and seconds → hours by dividing by 3600. A large percentage of KTU valuation marks are lost on **unit conversion errors** between speed, headway, and flow.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Signal Design:** Saturation flow is essentially $1/h_{\min}$ (the reciprocal of the minimum headway observed in a saturated queue).
- **Capacity Estimation:** Capacity = $3600 / h_{\min}$, where $h_{\min}$ is the minimum sustainable time headway.
- **Tunnel and Bridge Safety:** Maximum allowable flow is governed by minimum headway for emergency braking.
- **Autonomous / Connected Vehicle Platoons:** Modern adaptive cruise control systems target a constant time headway (e.g., **1.5 seconds**) rather than a constant distance, mimicking human driver behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flow as the Reciprocal of Time Headway

**Statement to prove:** $q = \dfrac{3600}{\bar{h}}$

**Starting Logic:** Consider a fixed observer at a roadside cross-section. Vehicles pass this cross-section in a continuous stream. If the **average time headway** is $\bar{h}$ seconds, then in one full **hour of observation** ($T = 3600$ seconds), the number of vehicles crossing is obtained by dividing total time by the average time gap.

**Step 1:** Number of intervals in 1 hour = $\dfrac{T}{\bar{h}} = \dfrac{3600}{\bar{h}}$

**Step 2:** Each interval contains exactly one vehicle crossing (by definition of successive headway).

**Step 3:** Therefore, the number of vehicles crossing in 1 hour = $\dfrac{3600}{\bar{h}}$.

**Step 4:** By definition, flow $q$ = number of vehicles per hour.

$$\boxed{q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}} \;\; \text{vph}}$$

**Conversion Check (utility):** If $\bar{h} = 2$ seconds, then $q = 1800$ vph. This is the saturated flow rate typical of a high-capacity urban lane.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coupling Between Space Headway and Time Headway

**Statement to prove:** $s = v \cdot h$

**Starting Logic:** In a steady-state traffic stream where all vehicles travel at the same speed $v$, the time taken by the following vehicle to traverse the distance equal to the space headway is exactly equal to the time headway observed at a fixed point.

**Step 1:** Imagine a photograph of the stream is taken at time $t_0$. The distance between the front of vehicle 1 and the front of vehicle 2 (or rear of vehicle 1 to front of vehicle 2, depending on convention) is $s$.

**Step 2:** Both vehicles move forward at speed $v$ (m/s). Therefore, the time taken by the following vehicle to reach the position previously occupied by the leader is $h = s / v$.

**Step 3:** Rearranging the equation to express space headway in terms of time headway:

$$\boxed{s = v \cdot h}$$

**Step 4 (Speed unit conversion):** If $v$ is given in km/h and $h$ in seconds, and we want $s$ in meters:

$$s = \frac{v_{kmph}}{3.6} \cdot h \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{v_{kmph} \cdot h}{3.6}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Density as the Reciprocal of Space Headway

**Statement to prove:** $k = \dfrac{1000}{s}$ (in vpkm when $s$ is in meters)

**Starting Logic:** Density is defined as the number of vehicles per unit length of roadway. Consider a road segment of length $L$ km. The number of vehicles $N$ on that segment is approximated as $L \cdot k$. Equivalently, the **length occupied per vehicle** is $s$ (the space headway), and the number of vehicles in 1 km = $1000 / s$.

**Step 1:** Length of 1 km = 1000 meters.

**Step 2:** Number of vehicles in 1 km = $\dfrac{\text{Total length}}{\text{Length per vehicle}} = \dfrac{1000}{s}$.

**Step 3:** By definition of density:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{1000}{s} \;\; \text{veh/km}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Complete Identity $q = v \cdot k$ from Headway

**Statement to prove:** $q = k \cdot v$ (Fundamental Equation of Traffic Flow)

**Starting Logic:** We start from the two reciprocal relations and the coupling equation derived above.

**Step 1:** Write flow from headway: $q = \dfrac{3600}{h}$ vehicles/hour.

**Step 2:** Convert this to vehicles/second: $q_{vps} = \dfrac{1}{h}$ (where $q_{vps}$ is flow in veh/s).

**Step 3:** Write density from space headway: $k = \dfrac{1}{s}$ vehicles/meter (if $s$ in meters).

**Step 4:** Substitute $s = v \cdot h$ (coupling) into $k$:

$$k = \frac{1}{v \cdot h}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply $k$ by $v$:

$$k \cdot v = \frac{1}{v \cdot h} \cdot v = \frac{1}{h} = q_{vps}$$

**Step 6:** After re-incorporating the 3600 conversion, the macroscopic identity is recovered:

$$\boxed{q = k \cdot v}$$

**This proves the link between microscopic headway parameters and the macroscopic Greenshields fundamental diagram.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mean and Variance of the Negative Exponential Headway

**Starting Logic:** When vehicle arrivals follow a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ (veh/s), the inter-arrival time (headway) follows a **negative exponential distribution** with probability density function:

$$f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{for} \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 1:** Compute the mean (expected headway):

$$\bar{h} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$:

$$\bar{h} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, $\bar{h} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$, confirming $q = 3600 \lambda = \dfrac{3600}{\bar{h}}$.

**Step 4:** The variance of the negative exponential is $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$, and the standard deviation equals the mean: $\sigma = \bar{h}$. This **mean = standard deviation** property is a signature of random (Poisson) arrivals and is frequently tested in KTU problems.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A traffic stream has a space mean speed of **54 km/h** and a density of **20 vehicles/km**. Calculate the (a) flow, (b) time headway, and (c) space headway.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Flow:**
Using $q = k \cdot v$:

$$q = 20 \times 54 = 1080 \;\text{vph}$$

**Step (b) — Time headway:**
Using $\bar{h} = \dfrac{3600}{q}$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{3600}{1080} = 3.333 \;\text{seconds}$$

**Step (c) — Space headway:**
First convert speed to m/s: $v = \dfrac{54}{3.6} = 15$ m/s. Then $s = v \cdot h$:

$$s = 15 \times 3.333 = 50 \;\text{meters}$$

**Cross-check with density:** $k = 1000/s \Rightarrow s = 1000/20 = 50$ m. ✓ **Consistent.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification & Coupling of Headway Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Traffic Stream] --> B[Time Headway h]
    A --> C[Space Headway s]
    B --> D[Reciprocal gives Flow q]
    C --> E[Reciprocal gives Density k]
    D --> F[Fundamental Equation q = k v]
    E --> F
    B --> G[Coupling Equation s = v h]
    C --> G
    F --> H[Capacity and LOS Design]
    G --> H
    B --> I[Poisson / Negative Exponential Model]
    I --> J[Gap Acceptance at Intersections]
    I --> K[Signal Timing Saturation Flow]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow begins with the **Traffic Stream** (A), which is characterized by **two coupled headway parameters** (B and C). Reciprocals of these headways yield the macroscopic flow (D) and density (E), which combine via the **Fundamental Equation** (F) to drive design decisions (H). The coupling equation (G) ties the microscopic headway types together. Finally, the **Poisson modelling branch** (I) extends the time headway into intersection design (J, K).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Microscopic vs. Macroscopic View of Headway

```mermaid
graph LR
    MicA[Vehicle 1 Position] -->|distance s| MicB[Vehicle 2 Position]
    MicB -->|distance s| MicC[Vehicle 3 Position]
    MicA -.->|time h at cross section| MacA[Flow q vph]
    MicB -.->|time h at cross section| MacA
    MicC -.->|time h at cross section| MacA
    MacA --> MacB[Capacity vph]
    MacA --> MacC[LOS Grade A to F]
    MacA --> MacD[Saturation Flow at Signals]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The top branch shows the **spatial arrangement** of vehicles (microscopic space headway $s$). The bottom branch shows how the **temporal spacing** ($h$) at a cross-section aggregates into the **macroscopic flow** $q$ (vph), which is the principal input to all KTU-level capacity and LOS computations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define headway. Differentiate clearly between time headway and space headway. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Headway** is the measure of separation between two successive vehicles in a traffic stream, expressed either in time or in distance.

| **Aspect** | **Time Headway (h)** | **Space Headway (s)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Time between two successive vehicles at a fixed point | Distance between two successive vehicles at a fixed instant |
| Measured at | A cross-section of the road | A snapshot of the road segment |
| Units | Seconds | Meters |
| Reciprocal gives | Flow $q = 3600/h$ | Density $k = 1000/s$ |

---

### Q2. State and explain the relationship between time headway and traffic flow. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The traffic flow $q$ (in vehicles per hour) and the average time headway $\bar{h}$ (in seconds) are inversely related by:

$$q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}}$$

**Explanation:** If vehicles pass a point on average every $\bar{h}$ seconds, then in 3600 seconds (one hour), the number of vehicles that pass is $3600 / \bar{h}$. By definition, this number is the hourly flow rate. Hence, **a smaller headway corresponds to a higher flow**, and vice versa.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between time headway ($h$), space headway ($s$), speed ($v$), flow ($q$), and density ($k$). Show clearly how the microscopic headway parameters lead to the macroscopic fundamental equation $q = k \cdot v$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Vehicles arrive at an unsignalized intersection according to a Poisson process with a mean arrival rate of $\lambda = 1$ vehicle per 3 seconds. Calculate the probability that the time headway between two successive vehicles is (i) less than 2 seconds, (ii) between 4 and 6 seconds, and (iii) greater than 9 seconds. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definitions:** Time headway $h$ is measured at a fixed point in time units; space headway $s$ is measured at a fixed instant in distance units. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Flow from time headway:** In one hour, the number of vehicles is $3600/h$. Hence, by definition, $q = 3600/h$ vph. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Density from space headway:** In one km (1000 m), the number of vehicles is $1000/s$. Hence, $k = 1000/s$ vpkm. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Coupling equation:** Since the following vehicle must traverse the spacing $s$ at speed $v$ to occupy the leader's position, $h = s / v$, which gives $s = v \cdot h$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Derive the macroscopic identity:** Substituting $k = 1/s$ and $s = v \cdot h$:

$$k = \frac{1}{v \cdot h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \cdot v = \frac{1}{h} = q \;\; (\text{veh/s basis})$$

Re-incorporating the 3600 scaling yields the well-known **fundamental equation of traffic flow**: $q = k \cdot v$. **(2 marks)**

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $\lambda = 1/3$ veh/s. The headway distribution is negative exponential: $P(h \geq t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. **(1 mark)**

**(i) $P(h < 2)$:**

$$P(h < 2) = 1 - e^{-(1/3)(2)} = 1 - e^{-0.6667} = 1 - 0.5134 = 0.4866 \;\; \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) $P(4 \leq h \leq 6) = P(h \geq 4) - P(h \geq 6)$:**

$$P(h \geq 4) = e^{-4/3} = e^{-1.3333} = 0.2636$$

$$P(h \geq 6) = e^{-6/3} = e^{-2} = 0.1353$$

$$P(4 \leq h \leq 6) = 0.2636 - 0.1353 = 0.1283 \;\; \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) $P(h > 9)$:**

$$P(h > 9) = e^{-9/3} = e^{-3} = 0.0498 \;\; \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of headway with the help of neat diagrams. Discuss the various factors that affect the time headway on a roadway. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traffic stream has a space mean speed of $60$ km/h and a density of $25$ vehicles/km. Calculate (i) the flow, (ii) the average time headway, and (iii) the average space headway. If the flow increases to $1800$ vph at the same density, find the new space mean speed and the new time headway. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Concept of Headway (3 marks):** Headway is the separation between consecutive vehicles in a traffic stream, measured either temporally (time headway, in seconds) at a fixed cross-section, or spatially (space headway, in meters) at a fixed instant. A diagram of two successive vehicles $V_1$ and $V_2$ with spacing $s$ between them and a roadside observer measuring time gap $h$ between their front bumpers crossing a marked line should be sketched.

**Factors affecting time headway (4 marks):**

1. **Driver characteristics** — perception-reaction time, age, attentiveness.
2. **Vehicle characteristics** — braking capability, acceleration, vehicle type (car vs. truck).
3. **Roadway geometry** — gradient, curvature, lane width, sight distance.
4. **Environmental conditions** — weather (rain, fog), lighting (day/night), road surface condition.
5. **Traffic composition** — proportion of heavy vehicles, mixed traffic vs. homogeneous.
6. **Speed of the stream** — at higher speeds, drivers tend to maintain larger time headways for safety.
7. **Control conditions** — presence of signals, signs, and pavement markings.

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $v = 60$ km/h, $k = 25$ vpkm. **(1 mark for setup)**

**(i) Flow:**
$q = k \cdot v = 25 \times 60 = 1500$ vph. **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Time headway:**
$\bar{h} = 3600 / q = 3600 / 1500 = 2.4$ s. **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Space headway:**
$s = 1000 / k = 1000 / 25 = 40$ m.
Cross-check: $s = v \cdot h$ with $v = 60/3.6 = 16.67$ m/s: $s = 16.67 \times 2.4 = 40$ m. ✓ **(1 mark)**

**New scenario:** $q' = 1800$ vph, $k = 25$ vpkm (same density).
New speed: $v' = q'/k = 1800/25 = 72$ km/h. **(1 mark)**
New time headway: $\bar{h}' = 3600 / 1800 = 2.0$ s. **(1 mark)**

**Inference:** Higher flow at the same density implies higher speed and a shorter time headway — physically consistent with a more efficient traffic stream. **(1 mark for concluding remark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Headway Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert $v$ from km/h to m/s when computing $s = v \cdot h$. This single error can cost up to **3 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Misapplication of Poisson:** Using Poisson's distribution to model headways at **saturated / congested** flow, where arrivals are NOT random. The negative exponential is valid only for **low-to-moderate, uninterrupted, random flow**.
> 3. **Sign Flips in Probability:** Writing $P(h \geq t)$ instead of $P(h < t)$ in gap-acceptance problems. Always draw the distribution curve and shade the correct region first.
> 4. **Confusing "Per Hour" with "Per Second":** Using $q$ directly in veh/s while plugging $\lambda$ in veh/s. Standardise units before substitution.
> 5. **Skipping the Coupling Step:** In derivation questions, jumping from microscopic to macroscopic without showing $s = v \cdot h$ as the bridge. KTU examiners allocate a full 2 marks specifically to this step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Headway** = separation between successive vehicles; classified as **Time Headway ($h$, seconds)** and **Space Headway / Spacing ($s$, meters)**.
- **Three golden formulas to memorize:**
$$q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}}, \quad k = \frac{1000}{s}, \quad s = v \cdot h$$
- **Fundamental equation** $q = k \cdot v$ emerges naturally from these headway relations — this is the most-tested identity in Module 1.
- **Poisson arrivals** imply **negative exponential headways**: $P(h \geq t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Mean = Standard deviation** in the negative exponential distribution — a unique property often asked in KTU 2-mark / 3-mark questions.
- **Capacity of a lane** = $3600 / h_{\min}$, where $h_{\min}$ is the minimum saturation headway (~2 seconds in real-world urban traffic).
- **Density in vpkm** requires **space headway in meters**; **flow in vph** requires **time headway in seconds** — never mix the two scales.
- **Gap acceptance** at unsignalized intersections uses the **cumulative probability** $P(h \geq t_c)$, where $t_c$ is the critical gap.
- **Higher speed** at the same density → **shorter time headway** → **higher flow**.
- **Higher density** at the same speed → **shorter space headway** → **higher flow** (until jam density is reached).
- The **coupling equation $s = v \cdot h$** is the **bridge** between microscopic (per-vehicle) and macroscopic (per-stream) descriptions — always derive it explicitly in KTU 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Traffic Engineering, **Spacing** (also called **Space Headway** or **Space Occupancy**) is defined as the **distance between successive vehicles in a traffic stream**, measured from a common reference point (typically front bumper of the leading vehicle to front bumper of the following vehicle) along the direction of traffic flow.

Mathematically, the **average spacing** $\bar{s}$ is inversely related to the traffic **density** $k$:

$$\bar{s} = \frac{1}{k}$$

where,
- $\bar{s}$ = average spacing, expressed in **metres per vehicle (m/veh)** or **kilometres per vehicle (km/veh)**
- $k$ = traffic density, expressed in **vehicles per kilometre (veh/km)** or **vehicles per metre (veh/m)**

The reciprocal is the **Jam Spacing** $s_j$, which is the minimum spacing when vehicles are bumper-to-bumper in a stopped queue:

$$s_j = \frac{1}{k_j}$$

where $k_j$ = **Jam Density** (maximum density, units: **veh/km**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Definition to Memorise**
> *"Spacing is the distance from the front of one vehicle to the front of the next vehicle in the same lane, measured along the direction of traffic flow. It is the spatial analogue of time headway."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cars on a Conveyor Belt" Model

Imagine a long conveyor belt carrying toy cars in a single file, each touching the next. If we stretch the belt so each car has a comfortable gap of 5 metres behind the previous one, the **spacing** is 5 m/veh. Now compress them tightly bumper-to-bumper with only 0.5 m gaps — the spacing drops to 0.5 m/veh, and we have reached the **jam spacing**.

- **More spacing** → fewer vehicles per kilometre → **low density** → free-flow conditions.
- **Less spacing** → more vehicles per kilometre → **high density** → congested / jammed conditions.

Think of spacing as the **"personal space bubble"** of each vehicle. In high-speed freeways, the bubble is large; in city traffic jams, it shrinks almost to zero.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Typical Range |
|--------|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Average Spacing | $s$ | **m/veh** | 5 – 100 m |
| Jam Spacing | $s_j$ | **m/veh** | 5 – 8 m (passenger cars) |
| Density | $k$ | **veh/km** | 0 – 150 veh/km |
| Time Headway | $h_t$ | **s/veh** | 1.5 – 10 s |
| Space Mean Speed | $u_s$ | **km/h** | 10 – 120 km/h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spacing Matters**
> Spacing is the *spatial fingerprint* of a traffic stream. A traffic engineer never sees density directly — they infer it from observed spacings using radar, video, or inductive loops. Hence spacing is the **fundamental observable** that links microscopic driver behaviour to macroscopic traffic flow theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

The relationship between spacing and the rest of the traffic stream parameters unfolds in five logical steps:

1. **Microscopic foundation** — A driver chooses a *time gap* $h_t$ (seconds) behind the leader based on speed, perception, and safety.
2. **Conversion to space** — At running speed $u_s$, this time gap translates into a *space gap* (spacing) $s = u_s \cdot h_t$.
3. **Macroscopic aggregation** — As $N$ vehicles occupy a stretch of road of length $L$, density $k = N/L$ and average spacing $\bar{s} = L/N = 1/k$.
4. **Flow linkage** — Flow $q$ (veh/h) is the number of vehicles passing a point per unit time, $q = k \cdot u_s$.
5. **Congestion asymptote** — As $k \to k_j$, $s \to s_j$, $u_s \to 0$, and the stream halts.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why spacing depends on density:** Spacing is simply the *inverse* of how tightly vehicles are packed. Double the vehicles per km ⇒ half the gap between them.
- **Why spacing depends on speed:** Faster cars need larger *following distances* (safe stopping sight distance rule). The classical safe-spacing rule is $s \approx u_s \cdot t_{reaction}$ where $t_{reaction} \approx 2$ s.
- **How spacing controls capacity:** Maximum flow $q_{max}$ occurs at an *optimum spacing* $s_o$, not minimum spacing, because below this optimum, speed collapses faster than density rises.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Spacing – Density inverse | $s = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | $s$ [m/veh], $k$ [veh/m] |
| 2 | Jam spacing | $s_j = \dfrac{1}{k_j}$ | $s_j$ [m/veh], $k_j$ [veh/m] |
| 3 | Time headway – Flow inverse | $h_t = \dfrac{1}{q}$ | $h_t$ [s/veh], $q$ [veh/s] |
| 4 | Space–Time coupling | $s = u_s \cdot h_t$ | $u_s$ [m/s] |
| 5 | Fundamental diagram | $q = k \cdot u_s$ | $q$ [veh/h], $k$ [veh/km], $u_s$ [km/h] |
| 6 | Greenshields speed-density | $u = u_f - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$ | $u_f$ [km/h], $k_j$ [veh/km] |
| 7 | Greenshields speed-spacing | $u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{s_j}{s}\right)$ | $u$ [km/h], $s_j, s$ [m] |
| 8 | Optimum spacing (Greenshields) | $s_o = \dfrac{2}{k_j}$ | $s_o$ [m/veh] |
| 9 | Maximum flow (Greenshields) | $q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | $q_{max}$ [veh/h] |
| 10 | Optimum speed | $u_o = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | $u_o$ [km/h] |
| 11 | Optimum density | $k_o = \dfrac{k_j}{2}$ | $k_o$ [veh/km] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Conversion Tip:** When $u_s$ is in km/h and $q$ is in veh/h, use $k$ in veh/km — do not mix m and km. The standard KTU convention is km–h units.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Capacity Design (IRC / HCM):** Spacing-based models define Level of Service (LOS) boundaries. LOS C corresponds to densities of 11–16 veh/km/lane.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Modern ACC systems maintain a constant *time headway* (1.5 s typical), automatically adjusting spacing with speed.
- **Traffic Signal Design:** Saturation flow (1800 veh/h/lane) corresponds to a minimum spacing of about **2 metres** at start-up.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Platoons:** Spacing shrinks to 1–2 m in platooned convoys, increasing lane capacity by 2–3×.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spacing from the Fundamental Diagram

**Given:** Flow $q$ and space mean speed $u_s$ are measured on a single-lane road.
**To derive:** Spacing $s$ in m/veh.

**Step 1.** Start from the macroscopic fundamental equation:

$$q = k \cdot u_s$$

**Step 2.** Solve for density:

$$k = \frac{q}{u_s}$$

**Step 3.** Use the inverse relation between density and spacing:

$$s = \frac{1}{k}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute the expression for $k$:

$$s = \frac{1}{q / u_s} = \frac{u_s}{q}$$

**Step 5.** Convert to consistent units. If $u_s$ is in **m/s** and $q$ is in **veh/s**, then $s$ is directly in **m/veh**. If $u_s$ is in **km/h** and $q$ in **veh/h**, the hour units cancel and $s$ is in **km/veh** — multiply by 1000 for metres.

$$\boxed{\,s \;[\text{m/veh}] = \frac{u_s \;[\text{m/s}]}{q \;[\text{veh/s}]} = 1000 \cdot \frac{u_s \;[\text{km/h}]}{q \;[\text{veh/h}]}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Greenshields Speed-Spacing Model

**Given:** Greenshields' linear speed–density model $u = u_f - \dfrac{u_f}{k_j} k$.
**To derive:** The relationship between speed $u$ and spacing $s$.

**Step 1.** Write the Greenshields model:

$$u = u_f - \frac{u_f}{k_j} k$$

**Step 2.** Replace $k$ using $k = 1/s$:

$$u = u_f - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 3.** Factor out $u_f$:

$$u = u_f \left( 1 - \frac{1}{k_j \cdot s} \right)$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $1/k_j = s_j$:

$$\boxed{\,u = u_f \left( 1 - \frac{s_j}{s} \right)\,}$$

**Interpretation:** As $s \to s_j$ (jam spacing), $u \to 0$ (vehicles stop). As $s \to \infty$, $u \to u_f$ (free flow speed).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimum Spacing and Maximum Flow

**Given:** The same Greenshields linear model.
**To derive:** The spacing $s_o$ at which flow is maximised.

**Step 1.** Express flow in terms of spacing:

$$q = u \cdot k = \frac{u}{s} = \frac{u_f}{s}\left(1 - \frac{s_j}{s}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Define $f(s) = \dfrac{q \cdot s}{u_f} = 1 - \dfrac{s_j}{s}$:

$$q = \frac{u_f}{s}\left(1 - \frac{s_j}{s}\right) = \frac{u_f}{s} - \frac{u_f \cdot s_j}{s^2}$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate $q$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dq}{ds} = -\frac{u_f}{s^2} + \frac{2 \, u_f \, s_j}{s^3} = 0$$

**Step 4.** Multiply through by $s^3 / u_f$ (assuming $u_f \neq 0$):

$$-s + 2 s_j = 0$$

**Step 5.** Solve:

$$\boxed{\,s_o = 2 s_j = \frac{2}{k_j}\,}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute $s_o$ back into the speed equation:

$$u_o = u_f \left( 1 - \frac{s_j}{2 s_j} \right) = u_f \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

**Step 7.** Compute $k_o$ and $q_{max}$:

$$k_o = \frac{1}{s_o} = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

$$q_{max} = u_o \cdot k_o = \frac{u_f}{2} \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{u_f \, k_j}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\,q_{max} = \frac{u_f \, k_j}{4}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 style]:**
A two-lane highway has a free flow speed of $u_f = 80$ km/h and jam density $k_j = 100$ veh/km/lane. Assuming Greenshields' model, determine:

(a) Spacing at optimum speed.
(b) Maximum flow per lane.
(c) Spacing when the stream is moving at 40 km/h.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Optimum spacing:
$$s_o = \frac{2}{k_j} = \frac{2}{100} \; \text{km/veh} = 0.02 \; \text{km/veh} = \mathbf{20 \; m/veh}$$

**(b)** Maximum flow per lane:
$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \, k_j}{4} = \frac{80 \times 100}{4} = \mathbf{2000 \; veh/h/lane}$$

**(c)** At $u = 40$ km/h, use $u = u_f (1 - s_j/s)$:
$$40 = 80 \left(1 - \frac{s_j}{s}\right)$$
$$0.5 = 1 - \frac{s_j}{s}$$
$$\frac{s_j}{s} = 0.5 \Rightarrow s = 2 s_j$$

Since $s_j = 1/k_j = 1/100$ km = 10 m:
$$s = 2 \times 10 = \mathbf{20 \; m/veh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the spacing at *half the free-flow speed* equals the *optimum spacing* under Greenshields — this is a unique property of the linear model and a favourite KTU twist-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spacing in the Traffic-Stream Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Behaviour] --> B[Time Headway h_t]
    B --> C[Space Coupling Equation]
    D[Stream Speed u_s] --> C
    C --> E[Spacing s]
    E --> F[Density k = 1 per s]
    F --> G[Fundamental Diagram]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Flow q = k times u_s]
    G --> I[Greenshields Model]
    I --> J[Optimum s_o = 2 per k_j]
    I --> K[Max Flow q_max = u_f k_j per 4]
    I --> L[Jam Spacing s_j = 1 per k_j]
    H --> M[Capacity and LOS Design]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Highway Capacity Manual]
    M --> O[Signal Timing Design]
    M --> P[ITS and ACC Systems]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — How Spacing is Computed in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Field Data Collection]) --> Step1[Measure Time Headway h_t via Loop Detector]
    Step1 --> Step2[Compute Stream Speed u_s via Radar or Video]
    Step2 --> Step3[Apply s = u_s times h_t to Get Spacing]
    Step3 --> Step4[Cross-check using Density k = 1 per s]
    Step4 --> Step5{Compare with Jam Spacing s_j}
    Step5 -->|s > s_j| Step6[Uncongested Regime]
    Step5 -->|s approx s_j| Step7[Critical Congestion]
    Step5 -->|s = s_j| Step8[Blocked / Jammed]
    Step6 --> Output[Report to Traffic Management Centre]
    Step7 --> Output
    Step8 --> Output
    Output --> End([Adaptive Signal Control Update])
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Spacing vs. Density Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FREE["FREE FLOW REGIME"]
        A1[Large Spacing: 50 to 100 m] --> A2[Low Density: 10 to 20 veh/km]
        A2 --> A3[High Speed: 80 to 100 km/h]
    end
    subgraph OPT["OPTIMUM / CAPACITY REGIME"]
        B1[Moderate Spacing: 20 m] --> B2[Mid Density: 50 veh/km]
        B2 --> B3[Half Free Speed: 40 km/h]
        B3 --> B4[Maximum Flow q_max]
    end
    subgraph JAM["JAMMED REGIME"]
        C1[Small Spacing: 5 to 8 m] --> C2[High Density: 120 to 200 veh/km]
        C2 --> C3[Near-Zero Speed: 0 to 5 km/h]
    end
    FREE --> OPT
    OPT --> JAM
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define spacing and jam spacing in a traffic stream. How are they related to density?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Spacing (s):** The distance between successive vehicles in a traffic stream, measured from the front bumper of one vehicle to the front bumper of the next, along the direction of flow. **Unit: m/veh.** *(1 mark)*
- **Relation to density:** Average spacing is the reciprocal of density, $s = 1/k$, where $k$ is in veh/m. Hence $s$ in m/veh. *(1 mark)*
- **Jam spacing ($s_j$):** The minimum achievable spacing when vehicles are bumper-to-bumper in a stopped queue. $s_j = 1/k_j$, where $k_j$ is the jam density (typically 120–150 veh/km for passenger cars). *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]

**Q: State the relationship between spacing, time headway, and space mean speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The space–time coupling relation is:

$$s = u_s \cdot h_t$$

where,
- $s$ = spacing [m/veh]
- $u_s$ = space mean speed [m/s]
- $h_t$ = time headway [s/veh]

This equation shows that for a given speed, a longer time gap translates directly into a longer spacing. *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variables, 1 mark for physical interpretation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain Greenshields' linear speed-density model. Derive the expressions for the optimum spacing, optimum density, optimum speed, and maximum flow. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** On a single-lane traffic stream, the average time headway observed is 4 seconds and the space mean speed is 50 km/h. Calculate (i) the spacing in metres, (ii) the density in veh/km, and (iii) the flow in veh/h. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Greenshields' Assumption:** Speed and density are linearly related. *(1 mark)*

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

2. **Express in terms of spacing** $k = 1/s$: *(1 mark)*

$$u = u_f \left(1 - \frac{s_j}{s}\right)$$

3. **Express flow:** $q = u \cdot k = u/s$:

$$q = \frac{u_f}{s}\left(1 - \frac{s_j}{s}\right)$$

4. **Differentiate with respect to $s$, set to zero:** *(2 marks)*

$$\frac{dq}{ds} = -\frac{u_f}{s^2} + \frac{2 u_f s_j}{s^3} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_o = 2 s_j = \frac{2}{k_j}$$

5. **Optimum density, speed, and max flow:** *(2 marks)*

$$k_o = \frac{k_j}{2}, \quad u_o = \frac{u_f}{2}, \quad q_{max} = \frac{u_f \, k_j}{4}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given: $h_t = 4$ s, $u_s = 50$ km/h.

**(i) Spacing:**
Convert speed: $u_s = 50 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 13.89$ m/s. Then: *(2 marks)*

$$s = u_s \cdot h_t = 13.89 \times 4 = \mathbf{55.56 \; m/veh}$$

**(ii) Density:**
$$k = \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{55.56 \text{ m/veh}} = 0.018 \; \text{veh/m} = \mathbf{18 \; veh/km}$$

**(iii) Flow:**
$$q = k \cdot u_s = 18 \times 50 = \mathbf{900 \; veh/h}$$

*Check via time-headway method:* $q = 3600/h_t = 3600/4 = 900$ veh/h. ✓ *(2 marks for computation + 1 mark for verification)*

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** List and briefly explain any four factors affecting spacing in a traffic stream. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** On a highway, the free flow speed is observed as 90 km/h and the jam density is 120 veh/km. Assuming Greenshields' model, calculate (i) the spacing at maximum flow, (ii) the maximum flow, and (iii) the spacing when the stream speed drops to 30 km/h. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Speed of the stream** — Higher speeds require longer safe stopping distances; thus spacing increases with speed. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Driver psychology and reaction time** — Aggressive drivers accept shorter spacings (~1 s); cautious drivers maintain longer ones (~3 s). *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Road and weather conditions** — Wet, icy, or foggy roads require larger spacing for safety. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Vehicle type and size** — Heavy vehicles (trucks, buses) need longer spacings due to longer braking distances. *(1.5 marks)*
5. *(Optional extra)* **Traffic control devices and lane discipline** — Signalised intersections enforce minimum spacing through platoon dispersion.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given: $u_f = 90$ km/h, $k_j = 120$ veh/km.

**(i) Spacing at maximum flow:**
$$s_o = \frac{2}{k_j} = \frac{2}{120} \; \text{km/veh} = 0.01667 \; \text{km/veh} = \mathbf{16.67 \; m/veh}$$
*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Maximum flow:**
$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \, k_j}{4} = \frac{90 \times 120}{4} = \mathbf{2700 \; veh/h}$$
*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Spacing at $u = 30$ km/h:**
Using $u = u_f (1 - s_j / s)$, where $s_j = 1/k_j = 1/120$ km = 8.333 m: *(2 marks)*

$$30 = 90 \left(1 - \frac{8.333}{s}\right)$$
$$\frac{1}{3} = 1 - \frac{8.333}{s}$$
$$\frac{8.333}{s} = \frac{2}{3}$$
$$s = 8.333 \times \frac{3}{2} = \mathbf{12.5 \; m}$$

*Verification: At this spacing, $k = 1/s = 0.08$ veh/m = 80 veh/km, and $q = 80 \times 30 = 2400$ veh/h, which is below $q_{max}$ — consistent with the congested side of the fundamental diagram.* ✓ *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mixing trap:** Students frequently write $s = u_s / q$ without converting km/h to m/s or vice versa. Always state units explicitly. *(–1 to –2 marks typical deduction)*
> 2. **Confusing $s$ with $s_j$:** Spacing is a *variable* depending on density; jam spacing is a *constant* depending only on vehicle length + buffer. Examiners check this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting that optimum ≠ maximum density:** Maximum flow occurs at $k_o = k_j/2$, NOT at $k_j$. Writing $q_{max} = u_f \cdot k_j$ is a *fatal error* costing full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the differentiation step:** KTU expects the full derivative $dq/ds = 0$ derivation, not a memorised formula dump.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spacing (Module 1)**

- ☐ Spacing $s$ is measured front-bumper to front-bumper; units are **m/veh**.
- ☐ Inverse relation: $s = 1/k$; remember to convert units consistently (m ↔ km).
- ☐ Jam spacing $s_j = 1/k_j$ — minimum spacing when traffic halts; typical value **5–8 m** for cars.
- ☐ Space–time coupling: $s = u_s \cdot h_t$ — links microscopic headway to macroscopic spacing.
- ☐ Greenshields linear model in spacing form: $u = u_f (1 - s_j / s)$.
- ☐ Optimum spacing (where flow is max): $s_o = 2/k_j = 2 s_j$.
- ☐ Optimum speed $u_o = u_f / 2$, optimum density $k_o = k_j / 2$.
- ☐ Maximum flow: $q_{max} = u_f \, k_j / 4$ — the **capacity** of the facility.
- ☐ Spacing shrinks as we move from free-flow → capacity → jam; speed follows the same monotonic decline.
- ☐ Always verify numerical answers: $q = k \cdot u_s$ must hold, and $s \ge s_j$ is a physical sanity check.
- ☐ Mention *driver behaviour, vehicle type, weather, road geometry* when asked about factors affecting spacing.
- ☐ Distinguish between **space headway (s)** and **time headway ($h_t$)** — examiners always test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time-Space Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time-Space Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Space Diagram:** A two-dimensional graphical representation used in traffic engineering that plots **time** on the horizontal axis (abscissa) and **space** (distance or position along a roadway) on the vertical axis (ordinate). It is used to depict the trajectories of individual vehicles, the motion of traffic platoons, and the propagation of disturbances (shock waves) along a roadway segment over a specific duration.

In formal KTU terminology, a Time-Space Diagram is the **fundamental analytical tool** used to study microscopic and macroscopic traffic stream parameters. Each vehicle in the traffic stream is represented as a **trajectory** (a curve or line) whose slope at any instant is the reciprocal of the instantaneous speed of that vehicle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the **footpath of a highway** with a **video camera** that records every passing vehicle, and you also have a **stopwatch**. Now, suppose you record:

- **Where** each vehicle was (in metres from a fixed reference point, like a milestone) at any given **when** (in seconds).

If you plot "where" on the **vertical axis** and "when" on the **horizontal axis**, you get a **Time-Space Diagram**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it as a "comet trail"** for every vehicle. A fast-moving car leaves a nearly horizontal trail (steep slope), while a slow-moving truck leaves a more vertical trail (gentle slope). The horizontal distance between two parallel trails at any vertical cross-section is the **time headway**; the vertical distance between two trails at any horizontal cross-section is the **space headway**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard units and metrics apply universally to time-space diagrams in KTU board examinations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Time | $t$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Distance / Space | $x$ or $s$ | **metres (m)** or **kilometres (km)** |
| Speed | $u$ or $v$ | **metres/second (m/s)** or **km/h** |
| Density | $k$ | **vehicles/km (veh/km)** |
| Flow | $q$ | **vehicles/hour (veh/h)** or **veh/s** |
| Time Headway | $h_t$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Space Headway | $h_s$ | **metres (m)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Time-Space diagrams form the foundational analytical framework for traffic flow theory. Mastering this concept enables analysis of vehicle trajectories, headway distributions, platoon behaviour, signalised intersection performance, and shock wave propagation — all of which are recurring 14-mark examination themes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Time-Space Diagram — Structured Logic

### Step 1: The Two Coordinate Axes
- **Horizontal axis (X-axis)** → Represents **Time** ($t$), increasing left-to-right.
- **Vertical axis (Y-axis)** → Represents **Space** ($x$), typically increasing **upward** (in some conventions it increases downward, representing the direction of travel).

### Step 2: Vehicle Trajectories
- Each vehicle's trajectory is plotted as a line/curve.
- The **slope** of the trajectory at any point equals the **inverse of the vehicle's instantaneous speed**:

$$ \text{Slope} = \frac{dx}{dt} = u(t) $$

- **Steeper slope → Faster vehicle** (more distance covered per unit time).
- **Gentler slope → Slower vehicle** (less distance covered per unit time).
- A vehicle **stopped** (queued) appears as a **perfectly horizontal** trajectory (no change in $x$ as $t$ progresses).

### Step 3: Time Headway ($h_t$)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Headway** is the time interval between two successive vehicles crossing the **same point** (same $x$ value) on the diagram. It is measured as a **horizontal distance** between two trajectories at any chosen vertical cross-section (i.e., fixed location).

$$ h_t = t_{n+1} - t_n \quad \text{(at fixed } x \text{)} $$

### Step 4: Space Headway ($h_s$)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Space Headway** is the spatial gap between two successive vehicles at the **same instant** of time. It is measured as a **vertical distance** between two trajectories at any chosen horizontal cross-section (i.e., fixed time).

$$ h_s = x_{n+1} - x_n \quad \text{(at fixed } t \text{)} $$

### Step 5: Density & Flow Extraction
- **Density** $k$ = number of vehicles per unit length at a given instant = inverse of average space headway.

$$ k = \frac{1}{\bar{h}_s} $$

- **Flow** $q$ = number of vehicles passing a point per unit time = inverse of average time headway.

$$ q = \frac{1}{\bar{h}_t} $$

### Step 6: The Fundamental Diagram of Traffic Flow
> [!NOTE]
> The most important macroscopic relationship in traffic flow theory — derivable directly from a time-space diagram:

$$ q = u_s \cdot k $$

where $u_s$ is the **space mean speed**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorised for KTU ESE examinations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | Speed (slope of trajectory) | m/s |
| 2 | $h_t = t_{n+1} - t_n$ | Time headway at a fixed point | s |
| 3 | $h_s = x_{n+1} - x_n$ | Space headway at a fixed instant | m |
| 4 | $q = \dfrac{N}{T}$ | Flow rate (vehicles per time) | veh/h |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{N}{L}$ | Density (vehicles per length) | veh/km |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{1}{\bar{h}_t}$ | Flow = inverse of avg. time headway | veh/s |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{1}{\bar{h}_s}$ | Density = inverse of avg. space headway | veh/m |
| 8 | $q = u_s \cdot k$ | Fundamental relation | — |
| 9 | $u_t = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum u_i$ | Time mean speed (arithmetic mean) | km/h |
| 10 | $u_s = \dfrac{N}{\sum (1/u_i)}$ | Space mean speed (harmonic mean) | km/h |
| 11 | $u_s = \dfrac{q}{k}$ | Space mean speed from fundamental relation | km/h |
| 12 | $w = \dfrac{\Delta u}{\Delta k}$ | Shock wave speed | km/h |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside KTU formula tables. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX form (e.g., $h_s = x_{n+1} - x_n$ written as $h_s = x_{n+1} - x_n$, but absolute value expressions like $\vert \Delta x \vert$ must be written as `\vert \Delta x \vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Time-Space diagrams are indispensable for:

1. **Signalised intersection design** — computing cycle length, green time, and queue clearance times.
2. **Platoon dispersion modelling** — analysing how a group of vehicles released from a signal disperses as they travel downstream.
3. **Shock wave analysis** — studying the propagation boundary between two traffic states (e.g., congested vs free-flow).
4. **Highway capacity studies** — determining LOS (Level of Service).
5. **Microsimulation validation** — benchmarking tools like VISSIM, SUMO, CORSIM.
6. **Ramp metering evaluation** — assessing how merging affects mainline flow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed as the Slope of a Trajectory

**Given:** A vehicle travels a distance $\Delta x$ in time $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Define the ratio:**
The average speed of the vehicle is the ratio of distance covered to time taken.

$$ \bar{u} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} $$

**Step 2 — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:**
Instantaneous speed is the time derivative of position:

$$ u(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \frac{dx}{dt} $$

**Step 3 — Geometric interpretation:**
On a time-space diagram with $t$ on the X-axis and $x$ on the Y-axis, the **slope of the trajectory** is exactly $dx/dt = u(t)$.

**Step 4 — Numerical check:**
If a vehicle covers 200 m in 10 s:

$$ u = \frac{200 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ s}} = 20 \text{ m/s} = 72 \text{ km/h} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Flow, Density, and Speed

**Given:** $N$ vehicles observed over length $L$ during time interval $T$, all moving at space mean speed $u_s$.

**Step 1 — Define flow:**
Flow is the number of vehicles passing a fixed point per unit time:

$$ q = \frac{N}{T} \quad \text{(veh/unit time)} $$

**Step 2 — Define density:**
Density is the number of vehicles occupying a fixed length of road at a given instant:

$$ k = \frac{N}{L} \quad \text{(veh/unit length)} $$

**Step 3 — Express $L$ in terms of $T$ and $u_s$:**
Since the traffic stream moves at space mean speed $u_s$, in time $T$ the entire platoon advances by a distance $u_s \cdot T$. Therefore:

$$ L = u_s \cdot T $$

**Step 4 — Substitute into density equation:**

$$ k = \frac{N}{u_s \cdot T} $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the fundamental relation:**

$$ k \cdot u_s = \frac{N}{T} = q $$

$$ \boxed{q = u_s \cdot k} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Headway to Flow Conversion

**Given:** Average time headway $\bar{h}_t$ between successive vehicles at a roadside detector.

**Step 1 — Conceptual basis:**
If the average gap between successive vehicles is $\bar{h}_t$ seconds, then in 1 second, $1/\bar{h}_t$ vehicles pass the detector.

**Step 2 — Convert to per-hour flow:**

$$ q = \frac{1}{\bar{h}_t} \quad \text{veh/s} $$

**Step 3 — Convert units to veh/h:**

$$ q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}_t \text{ (in seconds)}} \quad \text{veh/h} $$

**Step 4 — Worked example:**
If $\bar{h}_t = 2$ s:

$$ q = \frac{3600}{2} = 1800 \text{ veh/h} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Space Mean Speed from Individual Spot Speeds

**Given:** Spot speeds $u_1, u_2, u_3, \dots, u_N$ measured for $N$ vehicles occupying a road segment.

**Step 1 — Define space mean speed formally:**
Space mean speed is the harmonic mean of spot speeds because it weights each vehicle **equally by the time it spends on the segment**.

$$ u_s = \frac{\text{Total Distance Travelled by all vehicles}}{\text{Total Time Spent by all vehicles}} $$

**Step 2 — Express each vehicle's travel time:**
For vehicle $i$ travelling over segment of length $L$:

$$ t_i = \frac{L}{u_i} $$

**Step 3 — Sum total distance and total time:**

$$ \text{Total Distance} = N \cdot L $$

$$ \text{Total Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L}{u_i} = L \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{u_i} $$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**

$$ u_s = \frac{N \cdot L}{L \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{u_i}} = \frac{N}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{u_i}} $$

**Step 5 — Final harmonic mean form:**

$$ \boxed{u_s = \frac{N}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (1/u_i)}} $$

**Step 6 — Time mean speed (for contrast):**

$$ u_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i \quad \text{(arithmetic mean)} $$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix represents a **canonical 7-mark sub-question** pattern that examiners frequently use:

| Input Given | Required Output | Governing Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Spot speeds of $N$ vehicles | Time mean speed | $u_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum u_i$ |
| Spot speeds of $N$ vehicles | Space mean speed | $u_s = \frac{N}{\sum (1/u_i)}$ |
| Time headway data | Flow rate | $q = 3600 / \bar{h}_t$ |
| Space headway data | Density | $k = 1 / \bar{h}_s$ |
| $u_s$ and $k$ given | Flow rate | $q = u_s \cdot k$ |
| Vehicle trajectory points | Instantaneous speed | $u = \Delta x / \Delta t$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Time-Space Diagram Showing Vehicle Trajectories & Headways

```mermaid
graph TD
    axis1["X-AXIS: TIME t increasing rightward"]
    axis2["Y-AXIS: SPACE x increasing upward"]
    ptA["Vehicle A trajectory"]
    ptB["Vehicle B trajectory"]
    ptC["Vehicle C trajectory"]
    note1["Horizontal distance at fixed x = TIME HEADWAY h_t"]
    note2["Vertical distance at fixed t = SPACE HEADWAY h_s"]
    slopeA["Slope of trajectory = u_A = dx/dt"]
    slopeB["Slope of trajectory = u_B = dx/dt"]
    slopeC["Slope of trajectory = u_C = dx/dt"]
    regionF["Free-flow region"]
    regionQ["Queued horizontal segment"]
    arrow1["Vehicle entering queue"]
    arrow2["Queue discharge"]

    axis1 --> ptA
    axis2 --> ptA
    ptA --> ptB
    ptB --> ptC
    ptC --> note1
    ptC --> note2
    ptA --> slopeA
    ptB --> slopeB
    ptC --> slopeC
    ptA --> regionF
    ptA --> regionQ
    regionQ --> arrow1
    regionQ --> arrow2

    style ptA fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style ptB fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600
    style ptC fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900
```
