```mermaid flowchart TD P[Start: Determine operating regime] --> Q{Are densities HIGH congested near jam?} Q -- Yes --> R[Use Greenberg Logarithmic] Q -- No --> S{Are densities LOW free flow regime?} S -- Yes --> T[Use Underwood Exponential] S -- No --> U[Need ONE unified single-regime model] U --> V[Use Greenshields Linear] U --> W[Or use Edie Two Regime piecewise] R --> X[Output u k equation] T --> X V --> X W --> X X --> Y[Apply q = u k for full set of three diagrams] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — The q–k Parabola (Greenshields) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block is a **logical block schematic** showing the five anchor points on the q–k curve, since Mermaid cannot render a true X-Y graph. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ABCurve [Flow Density Parabola Anchor Points] direction LR P0[Origin k=0 q=0 Free flow vehicles move fast but no vehicles on road] P1[Capacity Peak k equals kj divided by 2 q equals u f kj divided by 4 Maximum flow] P2[Jam Point k equals kj q equals 0 Vehicles stop bumper to bumper] P0 --> P1 P1 --> P2 end subgraph ABRegion [Two Operating Regimes] direction TB R1[Left of Peak k less than kj/2 = FREE FLOW Stable high speed] R2[Right of Peak k greater than kj/2 = FORCED FLOW Unstable congested] end ABCurve --> ABRegion ``` ### 4.4 Logical Map — q–k → u–k → u–q Transformation Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[u k model Greenshields] --> B[u k linear straight line] B --> C[Multiply by k = q] C --> D[q k parabolic curve] D --> E[Solve quadratic for u in terms of q] E --> F[u q parabolic curve with two branches] F --> G1[Upper branch Free flow stable] F --> G2[Lower branch Forced flow congested] G1 --> H[Engineering use: capacity design and LOS] G2 --> H ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) > [!NOTE] > These are **direct 3-mark conceptual** questions. KTU 2024 expects crisp, definition-based answers in **3–4 lines** with key terms underlined. --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the three macroscopic stream parameters of traffic flow and state the fundamental equation relating them. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three macroscopic parameters are: 1. **Flow (q)** — number of vehicles crossing a point per unit time (veh/h). 2. **Density (k)** — number of vehicles occupying a unit length of road at an instant (veh/km). 3. **Space Mean Speed (u)** — average speed of all vehicles in a road segment at a given instant (km/h). The fundamental relation is the **continuity equation** of traffic stream: $$q = u \times k$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by **Jam Density** and **Free Mean Speed** in the context of fundamental diagrams of traffic flow? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Jam Density ($k_j$)** is the *maximum possible density* on a road when vehicles are *bumper-to-bumper* and the flow is essentially zero (speed = 0). Typical values: 100–150 PCU/km/lane. - **Free Mean Speed ($u_f$)** is the *average speed* that vehicles travel at when the road is *empty* (density = 0). Typical values: 60–100 km/h on urban roads. - These two parameters form the **boundary conditions** of any u–k model. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Part B carries **14 marks** (typically two sub-parts of 7 marks each). You are given an **internal choice** — answer **either Option A or Option B** in full. Below both are presented for practice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the **Greenshields speed–density model** with clear assumptions and boundary conditions. Hence derive the **flow–density relationship** and find the expressions for **maximum flow, optimum density, and optimum speed**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A one-way urban arterial has $u_f = 60$ km/h and $k_j = 120$ veh/km. Greenshields model applies. Determine: (i) the **flow** when density is 30 veh/km, (ii) the **maximum flow** and corresponding **speed and density** at this point, (iii) the **density** when the stream speed drops to 20 km/h, and (iv) the **space mean speed** when the flow is 1000 veh/h. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Assumption (1 mark):** The relation between space mean speed $u$ and density $k$ is **linear**: $$u = a + b \cdot k$$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions (2 marks):** - At $k = 0$, $u = u_f$ $\Rightarrow$ $a = u_f$ - At $k = k_j$, $u = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $b = -u_f / k_j$ Hence: $$u = u_f \left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Derive flow–density (1 mark):** $$q = u \, k = u_f \, k - \frac{u_f}{k_j} k^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum flow (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{dq}{dk} &= u_f - \frac{2 u_f}{k_j} k = 0 \\ &\Rightarrow k_o = \frac{k_j}{2}, \quad u_o = \frac{u_f}{2}, \quad q_{max} = \frac{u_f k_j}{4} \end{aligned} $$ **Diagram (1 mark):** Parabolic q–k curve with peak at $(k_j/2, u_f k_j/4)$. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** $u_f = 60$ km/h, $k_j = 120$ veh/km, Greenshields applies. **(i) Flow at $k = 30$ veh/km (2 marks):** $$u = 60 \left(1 - \frac{30}{120}\right) = 60 \times 0.75 = 45 \text{ km/h}$$ $$q = u \cdot k = 45 \times 30 = \mathbf{1350 \text{ veh/h}}$$ **(ii) Maximum flow and corresponding values (2 marks):** $$q_{max} = \frac{60 \times 120}{4} = \mathbf{1800 \text{ veh/h}}$$ $$k_o = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ veh/km}, \quad u_o = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ km/h}$$ **(iii) Density when $u = 20$ km/h (1.5 marks):** $$20 = 60 \left(1 - \frac{k}{120}\right) \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{k}{120} = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{k}{120} = \frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow k = \mathbf{80 \text{ veh/km}}$$ **(iv) Speed when $q = 1000$ veh/h (1.5 marks):** Using the u–q quadratic: $u^{2} - u_f u + \dfrac{u_f q}{k_j} = 0$ $$u^{2} - 60u + \frac{60 \times 1000}{120} = 0 \Rightarrow u^{2} - 60u + 500 = 0$$ $$ u = \frac{60 \pm \sqrt{3600 - 2000}}{2} = \frac{60 \pm \sqrt{1600}}{2} = \frac{60 \pm 40}{2} $$ $$u = 50 \text{ km/h (free-flow branch)} \quad \text{or} \quad u = 10 \text{ km/h (forced-flow branch)}$$ Both solutions are physically valid; the **stable** operating speed is **50 km/h**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Discuss the **Greenberg logarithmic** and **Underwood exponential** speed–density models. Compare their applicability, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** On a freeway, the Greenberg model is valid with $u_m = 30$ km/h and $k_j = 150$ veh/km. Find the **maximum flow, critical density, and critical speed**. Also compute the **flow and speed** at a density of 90 veh/km. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — Discussion (7 marks) | Feature | Greenberg (1959) | Underwood (1961) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **Form** | $u = u_m \ln(k_j / k)$ (logarithmic) | $u = u_f \exp(-k / k_c)$ (exponential) | | **Best for** | **High / congested** density | **Low / free-flow** density | | **Free speed** | **Indefinite** ($u \to \infty$ as $k \to 0$) | **Defined** ($u \to u_f$ as $k \to 0$) | | **Jam density** | **Defined** ($u = 0$ at $k = k_j$) | **Undefined** ($u \to 0$ only as $k \to \infty$) | | **Capacity** | $q_{max} = u_m k_j / e$ | $q_{max} = u_f k_c / e$ | | **Origin** | Fluid kinematic analogy | Empirical exponential fit | | **Limitation** | Unrealistic at $k \to 0$ | No finite $k_j$ — useless for jam | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Neither is *universally* valid across all densities, leading to **Edie's two-regime model** that combines both — Underwood for free flow and Greenberg for congested flow. #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $u_m = 30$ km/h, $k_j = 150$ veh/km, Greenberg model. **Critical density (2 marks):** $$k_o = \frac{k_j}{e} = \frac{150}{2.718} \approx 55.2 \text{ veh/km}$$ **Critical speed (1.5 marks):** $$u_o = u_m = 30 \text{ km/h}$$ **Maximum flow (2 marks):** $$q_{max} = \frac{u_m k_j}{e} = \frac{30 \times 150}{2.718} \approx 1656 \text{ veh/h}$$ **At $k = 90$ veh/km (1.5 marks):** $$u = 30 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{150}{90}\right) = 30 \cdot \ln(1.667) = 30 \times 0.5108 \approx 15.32 \text{ km/h}$$ $$q = u \cdot k = 15.32 \times 90 \approx 1379 \text{ veh/h}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing $u_o$ and $u_f$:** Many students write $u_o = u_f$ for Greenberg. **WRONG.** $u_o = u_m$ (the model parameter), and there is *no* $u_f$ in Greenberg. > 2. **Forgetting the quadratic root for u–q:** When the problem says "find the speed for a given flow", the equation $u^2 - u_f u + (u_f q / k_j) = 0$ has **two roots**. Always mention both and identify the *stable* (higher) speed. > 3. **Unit mixing:** Always convert km/h and veh/km consistently. Mixing m/s with veh/km loses 1 full mark. > 4. **Diagram is mandatory:** A rough sketch of the q–k parabola with anchor points (origin, capacity, jam) is worth **at least 1–2 marks** even if your algebra has minor errors. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density, one-page revision summary** — read this **the night before** the exam. - ☐ **Continuity equation:** $q = u \cdot k$ is the *only* relation linking the three parameters. It is the *starting point* of every derivation. - ☐ **Five anchor points** of any u–k/q–k model: $(0, u_f)$, $(k_j, 0)$, $(k_j/2, u_f/2)$ for Greenshields, plus the $(0, 0)$ and the capacity peak. - ☐ **Greenshields** is the *default* model in KTU exams — derive it cold: 1 mark for assumption, 2 marks for boundary conditions, 1 mark for q–k, 2 marks for maxima, 1 mark for diagram. - ☐ **Capacity formula (Greenshields):** $q_{max} = u_f k_j / 4$, $k_o = k_j / 2$, $u_o = u_f / 2$. **All three are 50% marks** of the model parameters. - ☐ **Greenberg:** $q_{max} = u_m k_j / e$, $k_o = k_j / e$, $u_o = u_m$ — note the **base of natural log** ($e \approx 2.718$). - ☐ **Underwood:** $q_{max} = u_f k_o / e$, $u_o = u_f / e$ — has $u_f$ but **no finite $k_j$**. - ☐ **u–q curve is a parabola** in the second quadrant of the u–q plane — for every flow $q < q_{max}$, there are **two possible speeds** (free-flow and forced-flow branches). - ☐ **Level of Service (LOS):** Free flow → $k < k_o$, Speed > $u_o$, Stable; Forced flow → $k > k_o$, Speed < $u_o$, Unstable. - ☐ **Plot all three diagrams** on the answer sheet whenever the question says "sketch". The examiner awards easy 1–2 marks for a *labelled* diagram. - ☐ **Indian context:** Indo-HCM 2017 uses **PCU** (Passenger Car Unit) values: 1.0 for cars, 3.0 for trucks on urban arterials, 0.5 for two-wheelers. - ☐ **Avoid pipes in tables** on the answer sheet — use $\vert x \vert$ notation or parentheses. - ☐ **Edge cases to watch:** (i) when $k = 0$ (free speed), (ii) when $u = 0$ (jam), (iii) when $k = k_o$ (capacity). These give **boundary / critical** values, often asked as 3-mark short questions. - ☐ **Edie's hybrid** model is the "industry best" but rarely asked at undergraduate level — mention it for extra credit in descriptive answers.

topics.title: Single Regime Models - Greenshields Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Regime Models - Greenshields Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Greenshields Model** is a **single-regime macroscopic traffic flow model** proposed by **Bruce D. Greenshields in 1935** based on extensive photographic studies conducted on two-lane rural highways near Mansfield, Ohio (USA) for the Bureau of Public Roads. The model postulates a **linear (straight-line) relationship between space-mean speed and traffic density** across the entire operational regime of the roadway, from free-flow conditions (zero density) to jammed conditions (zero speed).

Mathematically, the model is expressed as:

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

where $u_f$ is the **free-flow speed** and $k_j$ is the **jam density**. This single linear equation, when combined with the **fundamental equation of traffic flow** ($q = u \times k$), gives rise to all the classical **fundamental diagrams** of traffic engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Greenshields model is a **Single Regime Model** because it assumes **one unified linear equation** governs the entire speed-density continuum. This contrasts with multi-regime models (e.g., Greenberg, Underwood, Pipes-Munjal) that use different equations for different density ranges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Single Regime"?** In traffic flow theory, a "regime" represents a distinct operational state (e.g., uncongested free flow, transition, congested). Single regime models assume a single homogeneous driver-vehicle behavior pattern from empty road to total jam, which is mathematically elegant but physically approximate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **busy narrow corridor at a college fest** where students are walking from one end to the other. When the corridor is **empty** (very few students), everyone walks at their natural comfortable pace — this is the **free-flow speed ($u_f$)**. As more and more students enter the corridor, they start **bumping into each other and slowing down proportionally** — not suddenly, but gradually and predictably. When the corridor is **completely packed shoulder-to-shoulder**, nobody can move — the speed drops to **zero**, and the density has reached its maximum possible value called the **jam density ($k_j$)**.

Greenshields' insight was remarkably simple: this **slowdown happens linearly** — for every additional student (per unit length of corridor), each person reduces speed by a fixed, predictable amount. This linear behavioral assumption is the **heart of the model**.

| Real-World Scenario | Traffic Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Empty corridor, brisk walking | Free-flow speed ($u_f$) |
| Moderately crowded, slow shuffling | Critical density ($k_c$), optimal speed ($u_c$) |
| Completely packed, nobody moves | Jam density ($k_j$), speed = 0 |
| Flow of students per minute | Traffic flow/capacity ($q_{max}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Conventions Used in Greenshields Model:**
> - **Speed ($u$):** km/h
> - **Density ($k$):** vehicles/km (veh/km) or veh/km/lane
> - **Flow ($q$):** vehicles/hour (veh/h) or PCU/hour
> - **Free-flow speed ($u_f$):** Typically **60 to 80 km/h** for urban roads, **80 to 100 km/h** for highways
> - **Jam density ($k_j$):** Typically **120 to 150 veh/km/lane**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Greenshields Model

The model rests on a few **fundamental assumptions** that every KTU student must internalize before solving numerical problems:

1. **Linearity Assumption:** Speed decreases **linearly** with increasing density. The slope of the $u$–$k$ line is constant and negative.
2. **Driver Homogeneity:** All drivers in the traffic stream behave **identically** (same reaction patterns, same desired speed, same vehicle characteristics). This is the "single regime" simplification.
3. **Steady-State Conditions:** Traffic flow is observed over a time interval long enough to average out instantaneous fluctuations — only the macroscopic mean values are considered.
4. **Roadway Homogeneity:** The road geometry (grade, curvature, lane width, sight distance) is **uniform** along the section under study.
5. **No Lane-Changing Disruptions:** The model is best applied to **single-lane** or **uniform multi-lane** flow where lateral friction is negligible.

### The "Why" Behind the Model

Greenshields' 1935 photographic study measured vehicle spacings and speeds simultaneously. He plotted speed (vertical axis) against the **inverse of spacing** (which equals density) and observed that **the resulting scatter cloud could be well-approximated by a straight line**. The two intercepts of this line are immediately physically meaningful:

- **$k = 0$ intercept** → driver preference speed with no impediments = **$u_f$** (free-flow speed)
- **$u = 0$ intercept** → vehicle pile-up condition with no movement = **$k_j$** (jam density)

This physical interpretability is why the model became the **foundational building block** of macroscopic traffic flow theory.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula you will need** for Greenshields Model problems in KTU 2024 examinations.

| # | Relationship | Formula | Curve Shape | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed-Density (primary) | $u = u_f - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$ | Straight line (linear) | $0 \leq k \leq k_j$ |
| 2 | Density-Speed (inverse) | $k = k_j \left(1 - \dfrac{u}{u_f}\right)$ | Straight line (linear) | $0 \leq u \leq u_f$ |
| 3 | Flow (fundamental) | $q = u \cdot k$ | Definition | Always valid |
| 4 | Flow-Density | $q = u_f \cdot k - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k^2$ | Inverted parabola | $0 \leq k \leq k_j$ |
| 5 | Flow-Speed | $q = k_j \cdot u - \left(\dfrac{k_j}{u_f}\right) u^2$ | Inverted parabola | $0 \leq u \leq u_f$ |
| 6 | Critical (optimal) density | $k_c = \dfrac{k_j}{2}$ | — | At maximum flow |
| 7 | Critical (optimal) speed | $u_c = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | — | At maximum flow |
| 8 | Maximum flow (capacity) | $q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | — | Theoretical capacity |
| 9 | Mean speed at $k_c$ | $u_c = u_f - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right) \cdot \dfrac{k_j}{2} = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | — | Verified by substitution |
| 10 | Slope of $u$–$k$ line | $m = -\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}$ | — | Negative, constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Memory Anchor:** At maximum flow, **both** speed and density equal **exactly half** of their respective extreme values. This is the **Greenshields 50% Rule** — memorize it firmly: $k_c = k_j/2$ and $u_c = u_f/2$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Greenshields model is far more than an academic curiosity — it forms the **backbone of practical traffic engineering** in the following domains:

1. **Highway Capacity Analysis:** Transportation planners use it to estimate the **theoretical capacity** of a roadway by determining $q_{max}$ from observed free-flow speed and jam density.
2. **Level of Service (LOS) Determination:** The speed-flow and density-flow curves help demarcate LOS boundaries (A through F) as defined in the **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)**.
3. **Signal Timing Optimization:** The model provides a **macroscopic input** to determine optimum green splits at signalized intersections by estimating vehicle arrival rates.
4. **Congestion Pricing and Bottleneck Analysis:** The parabolic $q$–$k$ curve clearly identifies the **capacity drop** phenomenon on congested roadways.
5. **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS):** Real-time traffic estimation algorithms often use **Greenshields-type linear models** as a first-order approximation for traffic state estimation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Limitation to Remember:** The model **overestimates speeds at very low densities** and **underestimates speeds at very high densities** because real driver behavior is rarely perfectly linear. Greenshields himself acknowledged that his model was a "first approximation" — but its simplicity ensures it remains the **first model taught and tested** in every traffic engineering curriculum worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Three Fundamental Diagrams

We begin with the **primary Greenshields speed-density linear equation**:

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

This is the **defining equation** of the model. All other fundamental diagrams are mathematically derived from it using the fundamental relation $q = u \cdot k$.

### Derivation A: Flow-Density Relationship (Parabolic Curve)

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental equation of traffic flow:**

$$q = u \cdot k$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Greenshields expression for $u$:**

$$q = \left[u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k\right] \cdot k$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the density term $k$ across the bracket:**

$$q = u_f \cdot k - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k \cdot k$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the squared term:**

$$q = u_f \cdot k - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k^2$$

This is the **flow-density equation**. It is a **quadratic (parabolic) function** in $k$ with the coefficient of $k^2$ being negative, so the parabola **opens downward**.

**Step 5 — Find the maximum flow by differentiating with respect to $k$:**

$$\frac{dq}{dk} = u_f - 2 \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

**Step 6 — Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical (optimum) density:**

$$u_f - 2 \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k = 0$$

$$2 \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k = u_f$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the critical density $k_c$:**

$$k_c = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{2 \cdot u_f} = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Confirm this is a maximum (not a minimum) by checking the second derivative:**

$$\frac{d^2 q}{dk^2} = -\frac{2 u_f}{k_j} < 0$$

Since the second derivative is **negative**, the critical point is indeed a **maximum**. ✓

**Step 9 — Substitute $k_c$ back into the flow-density equation to obtain the maximum flow:**

$$q_{max} = u_f \cdot \left(\frac{k_j}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) \left(\frac{k_j}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 10 — Expand the squared term carefully:**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{2} - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) \cdot \frac{k_j^2}{4}$$

**Step 11 — Simplify the second term:**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{2} - \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$$

**Step 12 — Take the common denominator of 4:**

$$q_{max} = \frac{2 u_f k_j - u_f k_j}{4} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$$

This is the celebrated **Greenshields capacity formula**. ✓

---

### Derivation B: Critical (Optimum) Speed at Maximum Flow

**Step 1 — Use the primary Greenshields equation:**

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the critical density $k_c = k_j/2$:**

$$u_c = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) \cdot \frac{k_j}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $k_j$ in numerator and denominator:**

$$u_c = u_f - \frac{u_f}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$u_c = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

This confirms the **Greenshields 50% Rule** for speed. ✓

**Step 5 — Cross-verify using $q_{max} = u_c \cdot k_c$:**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f}{2} \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Derivation C: Flow-Speed Relationship (Parabolic Curve)

**Step 1 — From the speed-density equation, solve for $k$ in terms of $u$:**

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k = u_f - u$$

$$k = k_j \cdot \frac{(u_f - u)}{u_f}$$

$$k = k_j \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_f}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute this into $q = u \cdot k$:**

$$q = u \cdot k_j \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_f}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the term $u$:**

$$q = k_j \cdot u - \frac{k_j \cdot u^2}{u_f}$$

**Step 4 — Write the final flow-speed equation in standard quadratic form:**

$$q = k_j \cdot u - \left(\frac{k_j}{u_f}\right) u^2$$

This is the **flow-speed equation**, also an **inverted parabola** opening downward.

**Step 5 — Find the maximum by differentiating with respect to $u$:**

$$\frac{dq}{du} = k_j - 2 \left(\frac{k_j}{u_f}\right) u = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the critical speed:**

$$u = \frac{k_j \cdot u_f}{2 k_j} = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

This independently confirms $u_c = u_f/2$. ✓

---

### Derivation D: Average Vehicle Headway and Spacing

The Greenshields model allows us to derive two **microscopic properties** from macroscopic parameters:

**Step 1 — The relationship between density $k$ and average spacing $s$ (m/veh):**

$$k = \frac{1000}{s} \quad \text{(veh/km, when } s \text{ is in meters)}$$

**Step 2 — At jam density $k_j$, spacing equals the minimum bumper-to-bumper distance:**

$$s_{min} = \frac{1000}{k_j} \quad \text{(meters)}$$

**Step 3 — At critical density $k_c = k_j/2$, spacing is double the jam spacing:**

$$s_c = \frac{1000}{k_c} = \frac{2000}{k_j} = 2 \cdot s_{min}$$

**Step 4 — Average time headway $h$ (seconds) is related to flow $q$ (veh/h):**

$$h = \frac{3600}{q} \quad \text{(seconds/vehicle, when } q \text{ is in veh/h)}$$

**Step 5 — At capacity $q_{max}$:**

$$h_{min} = \frac{3600}{q_{max}} = \frac{3600 \cdot 4}{u_f \cdot k_j} = \frac{14400}{u_f \cdot k_j} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

## 3.2 Summary Analytical Matrix

The following matrix consolidates **all key Greenshields parameters** in their canonical form. This is the **single most important reference** for KTU problem-solving.

| Parameter | Symbol | Value in Greenshields Model | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free-flow speed | $u_f$ | Independent parameter (input) | Speed when road is empty |
| Jam density | $k_j$ | Independent parameter (input) | Density at which motion ceases |
| Critical (optimum) density | $k_c$ | $\dfrac{k_j}{2}$ | Density at maximum flow |
| Critical (optimum) speed | $u_c$ | $\dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | Speed at maximum flow |
| Maximum (capacity) flow | $q_{max}$ | $\dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | Theoretical road capacity |
| Slope of $u$–$k$ line | $m$ | $-\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}$ | Constant rate of speed reduction per unit density increase |
| Mean spacing at $k_j$ | $s_{min}$ | $\dfrac{1000}{k_j}$ m | Minimum bumper-to-bumper gap |
| Time headway at $q_{max}$ | $h_{min}$ | $\dfrac{14400}{u_f \cdot k_j}$ s | Smallest time gap between vehicles |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: The Three Fundamental Diagrams of Greenshields Model

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **single linear speed-density assumption** of Greenshields generates the **three fundamental diagrams** that form the core of macroscopic traffic flow theory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GREENSHIELDS PRIMARY ASSUMPTION"] --> B["Linear Speed-Density Relationship"]
    B --> C["u = uf minus uf by kj times k"]
    D["FUNDAMENTAL FLOW EQUATION"] --> E["q = u multiplied by k"]
    B --> E
    E --> F["DERIVATION A: Flow-Density Curve"]
    E --> G["DERIVATION B: Flow-Speed Curve"]
    F --> H["Parabolic inverted q-k graph"]
    G --> I["Parabolic inverted q-u graph"]
    B --> J["DERIVATION C: u-k Linear Graph"]
    J --> K["Critical density kc equals kj by 2"]
    J --> L["Critical speed uc equals uf by 2"]
    H --> M["CAPACITY qmax equals uf times kj by 4"]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["THEORETICAL ROAD CAPACITY"]
    O["FREE FLOW REGIME"] --> P["Low density k, High speed near uf"]
    Q["CRITICAL POINT"] --> R["kc equals kj by 2, uc equals uf by 2, q equals qmax"]
    S["CONGESTED REGIME"] --> T["High density k, Low speed near zero"]
    P --> Q
    Q --> T
    B --> O
    B --> Q
    B --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The chart shows that a single linear assumption generates three curves, all of which are interconnected. The "Critical Point" (where flow is maximum) sits exactly at the halfway mark of both the speed and density axes — this is the most exam-relevant takeaway.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: From Model Inputs to Capacity Prediction

The following block diagram depicts the **engineering workflow** by which a traffic engineer uses the Greenshields model in practice — from field data collection to capacity prediction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Field Data Collection"] --> F2["Spot Speed Studies and Density Counts"]
    F2 --> F3["Compute Space Mean Speed uf"]
    F2 --> F4["Compute Jam Density kj from Critical Spacing"]
    F3 --> F5["Greenshields Equation u = uf - uf by kj times k"]
    F4 --> F5
    F5 --> F6["Generate Speed-Density Line"]
    F6 --> F7["Derive Parabolic q-k Curve"]
    F6 --> F8["Derive Parabolic q-u Curve"]
    F7 --> F9["Identify Critical Density kc = kj by 2"]
    F8 --> F10["Identify Critical Speed uc = uf by 2"]
    F9 --> F11["Compute Maximum Flow qmax = uf times kj by 4"]
    F10 --> F11
    F11 --> F12["Capacity Output in PCU per hour"]
    F12 --> F13["Highway Capacity Manual LOS Classification"]
    F13 --> F14["Engineering Decision: Design or Upgrade Road"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** The diagram shows that **two field measurements** (free-flow speed and jam density) are sufficient to fully characterize the Greenshields model for a roadway. This is the engineering reason for the model's enduring popularity — minimal data requirement with maximum analytical power.

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic: The Three Fundamental Curves Side-by-Side

The following block diagram maps out the **three coordinate planes** in which Greenshields' relationships are plotted, helping students visualize the geometric meaning of each curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Plot 1: Speed vs Density u-k Plane"] --> P1A["Linear decreasing line from uf to 0"]
    P1A --> P1B["Midpoint is Critical Point kc, uc"]
    P2["Plot 2: Flow vs Density q-k Plane"] --> P2A["Inverted parabola starting at origin"]
    P2A --> P2B["Peak at qmax with kc equals kj by 2"]
    P2A --> P2C["Returns to zero at k equals kj"]
    P3["Plot 3: Flow vs Speed q-u Plane"] --> P3A["Inverted parabola starting at origin"]
    P3A --> P3B["Peak at qmax with uc equals uf by 2"]
    P3A --> P3C["Returns to zero at u equals uf"]
    P1B --> P2B
    P1B --> P3B
    P1A -. "All Three Curves Share Critical Point" .-> P2B
    P1A -. "All Three Curves Share Critical Point" .-> P3B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Insight:** Although the three fundamental diagrams look different in shape (one straight line, two parabolas), they all share **the same critical point** — the unique operating point that simultaneously maximizes flow while maintaining the equilibrium speed-density relationship.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the Greenshields model. State the linear relationship between speed and density assumed by the model and identify the two parameters that fully define it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Greenshields model** is a single-regime macroscopic traffic flow model proposed by Bruce D. Greenshields in 1935. It assumes a **linear relationship** between space-mean speed and traffic density of the form:

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

The two independent parameters that fully define the model are:

1. **Free-flow speed ($u_f$):** The space-mean speed when traffic density approaches zero (empty road condition). Units: km/h.
2. **Jam density ($k_j$):** The maximum density at which speed drops to zero (vehicles are bumper-to-bumper, no movement). Units: veh/km.

All other macroscopic parameters (critical density, critical speed, and maximum flow) are mathematically derived from these two values.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State the formulas for the following in the Greenshields model: (i) critical density, (ii) critical speed, and (iii) maximum flow. What is the physical significance of each?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Critical (Optimum) Density:**

$$k_c = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

This is the traffic density at which **flow is maximized**. It represents the **threshold density** that separates the uncongested regime (left of the peak) from the congested regime (right of the peak) on the $q$–$k$ curve.

**(ii) Critical (Optimum) Speed:**

$$u_c = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

This is the space-mean speed corresponding to maximum flow. It represents the **most efficient operating speed** of the roadway — the speed at which each individual driver contributes optimally to the overall throughput.

**(iii) Maximum Flow (Theoretical Capacity):**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$$

This is the **theoretical capacity** of the roadway, representing the **maximum number of vehicles that can pass a point per unit time** under the assumptions of the model. It serves as the **benchmark** for highway design and Level of Service analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the fundamental diagrams of traffic flow (speed-density, flow-density, and flow-speed relationships) using the Greenshields model. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** On a single-lane urban arterial, the free-flow speed is observed to be **72 km/h** and the jam density is measured as **120 veh/km**. Using the Greenshields model, determine:
- (i) The maximum flow (capacity) of the road.
- (ii) The speed at maximum flow.
- (iii) The density at maximum flow.
- (iv) The flow when the density is 40 veh/km.
- (v) The spacing between vehicles at jam density. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Derivation:

**Assumptions of Greenshields Model:**

1. Linear relationship exists between space-mean speed and density.
2. All drivers in the traffic stream behave uniformly (single regime).
3. Roadway is homogeneous in geometry, gradient, and lane width.
4. Traffic flow is in steady-state macroscopic equilibrium.
5. Lateral friction due to lane changing is negligible.

**Primary Equation:**

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

**Derivation of the Flow-Density Relationship:**

Starting with the fundamental equation $q = u \cdot k$ and substituting the Greenshields expression:

$$q = \left[u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k\right] k = u_f \cdot k - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k^2$$

This is a **downward-opening parabola** in the $q$–$k$ plane, with zeros at $k = 0$ and $k = k_j$.

**Critical Density** (by setting $dq/dk = 0$):

$$\frac{dq}{dk} = u_f - 2\left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k = 0 \implies k_c = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

**Maximum Flow:**

$$q_{max} = u_f \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) \cdot \frac{k_j^2}{4} = \frac{u_f k_j}{2} - \frac{u_f k_j}{4} = \frac{u_f k_j}{4}$$

**Derivation of the Flow-Speed Relationship:**

Solving the speed-density equation for $k$:

$$k = k_j \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_f}\right)$$

Substituting into $q = u \cdot k$:

$$q = u \cdot k_j \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_f}\right) = k_j \cdot u - \left(\frac{k_j}{u_f}\right) u^2$$

This is a **downward-opening parabola** in the $q$–$u$ plane, with zeros at $u = 0$ and $u = u_f$.

**Critical Speed** (by setting $dq/du = 0$):

$$\frac{dq}{du} = k_j - 2\left(\frac{k_j}{u_f}\right) u = 0 \implies u_c = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem:

**Given:** $u_f = 72$ km/h, $k_j = 120$ veh/km.

**(i) Maximum Flow (Capacity):**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \frac{72 \times 120}{4} = \frac{8640}{4} = 2160 \text{ veh/h}$$

**(ii) Speed at Maximum Flow:**

$$u_c = \frac{u_f}{2} = \frac{72}{2} = 36 \text{ km/h}$$

**(iii) Density at Maximum Flow:**

$$k_c = \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ veh/km}$$

**(iv) Flow at $k = 40$ veh/km:**

Using $q = u_f \cdot k - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k^2$:

$$q = 72 \times 40 - \left(\frac{72}{120}\right) \times 40^2$$

$$q = 2880 - 0.6 \times 1600 = 2880 - 960 = 1920 \text{ veh/h}$$

**Cross-check using $u$ at $k = 40$:**

$$u = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k = 72 - \left(\frac{72}{120}\right) \times 40 = 72 - 0.6 \times 40 = 72 - 24 = 48 \text{ km/h}$$

$$q = u \times k = 48 \times 40 = 1920 \text{ veh/h} \quad \checkmark$$

**(v) Spacing at Jam Density:**

$$s_{min} = \frac{1000}{k_j} = \frac{1000}{120} = 8.33 \text{ m/vehicle}$$

---

### Question B: [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **speed-density, flow-density, and flow-speed** relationships as predicted by the Greenshields model. Mark the critical points clearly on each diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** On a highway section, the speed-density relationship is observed to be linear, given by $u = 60 - 0.5k$, where $u$ is in km/h and $k$ is in veh/km. Determine: **(7 Marks)**
- (i) The free-flow speed.
- (ii) The jam density.
- (iii) The maximum flow (capacity).
- (iv) The density and speed at maximum flow.
- (v) Comment on whether this traffic stream is congested when $k = 80$ veh/km.

#### Solution to (a) — Sketch Explanation:

**Speed-Density Diagram:**

The $u$–$k$ plot is a **straight line** with:
- $u$-axis intercept at $u_f$ (free-flow speed) when $k = 0$.
- $k$-axis intercept at $k_j$ (jam density) when $u = 0$.
- Negative slope $= -u_f/k_j$.
- Critical point (midpoint of the line): $(k_j/2, u_f/2)$ — this divides the line into **free-flow regime** (left half) and **congested regime** (right half).

**Flow-Density Diagram:**

The $q$–$k$ plot is an **inverted parabola** with:
- Origin at $(0, 0)$.
- Zero flow at $k = k_j$.
- Maximum flow $q_{max} = u_f k_j / 4$ at $k_c = k_j/2$.
- Two operating zones: **stable** (left of peak, $dq/dk > 0$) and **unstable/congested** (right of peak, $dq/dk < 0$).

**Flow-Speed Diagram:**

The $q$–$u$ plot is also an **inverted parabola** with:
- Origin at $(0, 0)$.
- Zero flow at $u = u_f$.
- Maximum flow $q_{max} = u_f k_j / 4$ at $u_c = u_f/2$.
- Two zones: **free-flow zone** (high speeds, low flow) and **forced-flow zone** (low speeds, low flow).

The **critical point** in all three diagrams is geometrically the same physical state of traffic: maximum throughput at half-maximum speed and half-maximum density.

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem:

**Given:** $u = 60 - 0.5k$.

Comparing with the standard Greenshields form $u = u_f - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$:

- Coefficient of $k$ (zero): $u_f = 60$ km/h.
- Slope magnitude: $u_f / k_j = 0.5$, so $k_j = 60 / 0.5 = 120$ veh/km.

**(i) Free-Flow Speed:**

$$u_f = 60 \text{ km/h}$$

**(ii) Jam Density:**

$$k_j = \frac{u_f}{0.5} = \frac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ veh/km}$$

**(iii) Maximum Flow (Capacity):**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \frac{60 \times 120}{4} = \frac{7200}{4} = 1800 \text{ veh/h}$$

**(iv) Density and Speed at Maximum Flow:**

$$k_c = \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ veh/km}$$

$$u_c = \frac{u_f}{2} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ km/h}$$

**Verification:** $q = u_c \cdot k_c = 30 \times 60 = 1800$ veh/h. ✓

**(v) Congestion Check at $k = 80$ veh/km:**

Critical density is $k_c = 60$ veh/km. Since $k = 80 > k_c = 60$, the traffic stream is **in the congested regime** (right side of the $q$–$k$ parabola).

The corresponding speed is:

$$u = 60 - 0.5 \times 80 = 60 - 40 = 20 \text{ km/h}$$

The flow at this density is:

$$q = 20 \times 80 = 1600 \text{ veh/h}$$

Since $1600 < 1800$ (capacity), the road is **operating below capacity in congested conditions**. This represents **forced flow with stop-and-go characteristics**, and the stream is **definitely congested**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert the slope of the $u$–$k$ line into the form $u_f/k_j$ and instead use it as a direct multiplier. Always rewrite the given equation in the **standard Greenshields form** before substituting into any formula.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):** If $u_f$ is in km/h and $k_j$ in veh/km, then $q$ comes out in veh/h — but if you mistakenly mix km/h with veh/km, you will get a numerically wrong answer. **Always carry units explicitly** in the substitution step.
> 3. **Confusing Critical Point with Extremes (–2 marks):** The critical density $k_c$ and critical speed $u_c$ are **interior points** (where flow peaks), not the endpoint values $k_j$ and $u_f$. Students sometimes wrongly compute flow at $k = k_j$, which is zero.
> 4. **Skipping the "Congested vs. Uncongested" Conclusion (–1 mark):** For part (v) above, simply writing "congested" without stating **why** (because $k > k_c$) loses a clarity mark. Always justify your conclusion with the governing condition.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Greenshields Model**

- ✅ **Originator:** Bruce D. Greenshields, 1935, Ohio (USA) study on 2-lane rural highways.
- ✅ **Model Type:** Single-regime, macroscopic, linear speed-density model.
- ✅ **Primary Equation:** $u = u_f - (u_f/k_j) \cdot k$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ **Two Independent Parameters:** $u_f$ (free-flow speed) and $k_j$ (jam density) — all other parameters are derived.
- ✅ **Critical Density:** $k_c = k_j/2$ — the density at which flow is maximized.
- ✅ **Critical Speed:** $u_c = u_f/2$ — the speed at which flow is maximized.
- ✅ **Maximum Flow (Capacity):** $q_{max} = (u_f \cdot k_j)/4$ — the theoretical capacity of the road.
- ✅ **Flow-Density Shape:** Inverted parabola, peak at $(k_j/2, u_f k_j/4)$, zeros at $k = 0$ and $k = k_j$.
- ✅ **Flow-Speed Shape:** Inverted parabola, peak at $(u_f/2, u_f k_j/4)$, zeros at $u = 0$ and $u = u_f$.
- ✅ **Speed-Density Shape:** Straight line, negative slope $-u_f/k_j$, intercepts at $u_f$ and $k_j$.
- ✅ **The 50% Rule:** At maximum flow, both speed and density equal **exactly half** of their extreme values.
- ✅ **Congestion Test:** A traffic stream is **congested** if $k > k_c$ (right of the $q$–$k$ peak), and **uncongested** if $k < k_c$ (left of the peak).
- ✅ **Stability Criterion:** $dq/dk > 0$ → stable (uncongested); $dq/dk < 0$ → unstable (congested).
- ✅ **Spacing at Jam Density:** $s_{min} = 1000/k_j$ meters.
- ✅ **Time Headway at Capacity:** $h_{min} = 3600/q_{max} = 14400/(u_f k_j)$ seconds.
- ✅ **Five Key Assumptions:** Linearity, driver homogeneity, steady state, roadway homogeneity, no lane-changing friction.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Real traffic behavior deviates from linearity, especially at very low and very high densities — this is why multi-regime models (Greenberg, Underwood, Pipes) were later developed.
- ✅ **Practical Use:** Foundation for Highway Capacity Manual, LOS classification, signal timing, and traffic state estimation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When solving a Greenshields problem, always **start** by writing down the standard form $u = u_f - (u_f/k_j)k$ explicitly on your answer sheet, even if the question gives a numerical variant like $u = 60 - 0.5k$. This **single step** ensures you correctly identify $u_f$ and $k_j$ and avoids the most common mark-losing error reported by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenberg Logarithmic Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Greenberg Logarithmic Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Greenberg Logarithmic Model** is a single-regime macroscopic traffic stream model proposed by **Herman Greenberg (1959)** that establishes a **logarithmic functional relationship** between the space-mean speed of vehicles and the traffic density on a homogeneous roadway section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Macroscopic Models):**
> The Greenberg model is classified under the family of **single-regime speed-density models**, specifically designed to capture the behavior of traffic under **CONGESTED flow conditions**. It belongs to the same family as the Greenshields model but uses a logarithmic (instead of linear) formulation.

The governing functional equation is expressed as:

$$
u \;=\; u_{m} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{k}\right)
$$

where the variables are formally defined in the table below.

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $u$ | Space-mean speed of traffic stream | **km/h** |
| $u_{m}$ | Optimum speed (model parameter, speed at maximum flow) | **km/h** |
| $k$ | Traffic density at instant $t$ | **veh/km** |
| $k_{j}$ | Jam density (maximum attainable density) | **veh/km** |
| $q$ | Traffic flow (rate of vehicles passing a point) | **veh/h** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Parking Lot" Intuition

Imagine a **packed parking lot during a sports event exit** (a high-density, low-speed scenario). As more cars squeeze into the same exit lane:

- The **density** ($k$) keeps rising towards its physical ceiling ($k_j$, the jam density — where vehicles are bumper-to-bumper and stationary).
- The **speed** ($u$) drops sharply as cars crawl forward.
- The relationship is **NOT linear** (Greenshields) — it falls off steeply near jam density and flattens near free flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why logarithmic and not linear?**
> In real congested traffic, drivers maintain a *proportionally* small headway. A logarithmic decay mirrors how vehicles "log" their way out of a jam — slow at first, but recovering proportionally faster as spacing opens up. This is why Greenberg's log model fits **congested regimes** far better than the linear Greenshields model.

## 1.3 Boundary Behaviour (Critical Conceptual Anchor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Extreme Limits of the Greenberg Model:**
> 1. As $k \to k_{j}$ (jam): $\;u \to 0$ — vehicles are stationary. ✔ Physically correct.
> 2. As $k \to 0$ (free flow): $\;u \to \infty$ — unbounded speed. ✖ Physically unrealistic.
>
> This is the **signature limitation** of the Greenberg model: it is **only valid for congested flow**, never for free-flow conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Building Block by Block

The Greenberg model rests on three foundational postulates:

1. **Continuum Assumption:** Traffic is treated as a continuous compressible fluid with macroscopic properties ($u, k, q$) varying smoothly in space and time.
2. **Logarithmic Decay of Speed with Density:** Empirical congested-flow observations show speed *decreases logarithmically* (not linearly) as density approaches jam density.
3. **Vanishing Speed at Jam Density:** A physical anchor — when $k = k_{j}$, all vehicles halt, so $u = 0$. The log form satisfies this exactly.

### Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

- **Step 1 — Choose a functional form:** Unlike Greenshields (linear), Greenberg proposed $u = f(\ln k)$ because congested flow exhibits a steep non-linear collapse near $k_{j}$.
- **Step 2 — Enforce boundary condition $u = 0$ at $k = k_{j}$:** Substituting into $u = A \ln(k_j/k)$ yields $0 = A\ln(1) = 0$, automatically satisfied — the form is intrinsically consistent.
- **Step 3 — Define the model parameter $u_{m}$:** $u_m$ emerges as the speed when $k = k_j/e$ (the critical density), i.e., the *speed at maximum flow (capacity)*.
- **Step 4 — Derive macroscopic stream quantities:** Using the fundamental relation $q = u \cdot k$, the flow–density and speed–flow curves follow by substitution.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — every KTU problem on Greenberg revolves around them.**

| # | Relation | Governing Equation | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Speed–Density (Primary) | $u = u_{m} \cdot \ln(k_{j}/k)$ | Core model equation |
| 2 | Flow–Density (Derived) | $q = u_{m} \cdot k \cdot \ln(k_{j}/k)$ | Capacity estimation |
| 3 | Speed–Flow (Derived) | $q = k_{j} \cdot u \cdot e^{\,-u/u_{m}}$ | Level-of-Service analysis |
| 4 | Critical Density | $k_{m} = k_{j}/e$ | Density at capacity |
| 5 | Optimum Speed | $u = u_{m}$ at $k = k_j/e$ | Speed at capacity |
| 6 | Maximum Flow (Capacity) | $q_{\max} = u_{m} \cdot k_{j} / e$ | Roadway capacity |
| 7 | Reciprocal relation | $u_{m} = q_{\max} \cdot e / k_{j}$ | Parameter estimation |
| 8 | Parameter $u_{m}$ from observations | $u_{m} = q_{\max} / k_{m}$ | Field data calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Alert:** Always ensure $k$ is in **veh/km** and $u$ is in **km/h** (or convert consistently). Mixing units is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in KTU valuation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Greenberg model is deployed by traffic engineers for:

- **Capacity Analysis of Urban Arterials:** Estimating $q_{\max}$ during peak-hour congestion.
- **Congestion Pricing & Bottleneck Design:** Predicting breakdown flow rates.
- **ITS (Intelligent Transportation Systems):** Calibrating simulation tools like CORSIM and VISSIM for sub-saturated urban corridors.
- **Ramp Metering Algorithms:** Setting metering rates that keep mainline flow just below $q_{\max}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT apply Greenberg for free-flow ($k < 30\%$ of $k_j$) conditions.** Use Greenshields or Underwood instead.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Flow–Density Equation

Starting from the **primary model** and the **fundamental traffic flow relation**:

$$
q \;=\; u \cdot k
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the Greenberg speed–density equation into the fundamental relation.

$$
q \;=\; \bigl[u_{m} \cdot \ln(k_{j}/k)\bigr] \cdot k
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the order of multiplication (commutative property).

$$
q \;=\; u_{m} \cdot k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{k}\right)
$$

This is the **complete flow–density relationship** for the Greenberg model. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Finding the Critical (Optimum) Density $k_m$

The capacity condition requires the flow to be **maximized**, so we differentiate $q$ with respect to $k$ and set the result to zero.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $q = u_m \cdot k \cdot \ln(k_j/k)$ with respect to $k$.

$$
\dfrac{dq}{dk} \;=\; u_{m}\,\Bigl[\ln(k_{j}/k) \;+\; k \cdot \dfrac{d}{dk}\ln(k_{j}/k)\Bigr]
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the inner derivative.

$$
\dfrac{d}{dk}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{k}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dk}\bigl[\ln k_{j} - \ln k\bigr] \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{k}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the derivative expression.

$$
\dfrac{dq}{dk} \;=\; u_{m}\,\Bigl[\ln(k_{j}/k) \;+\; k \cdot \bigl(-\tfrac{1}{k}\bigr)\Bigr]
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the bracketed term.

$$
\dfrac{dq}{dk} \;=\; u_{m}\,\Bigl[\ln(k_{j}/k) \;-\; 1\Bigr]
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the first-order optimality condition $dq/dk = 0$.

$$
u_{m}\,\Bigl[\ln(k_{j}/k) \;-\; 1\Bigr] \;=\; 0
$$

Since $u_{m} \neq 0$, the bracketed term must vanish.

$$
\ln(k_{j}/k) \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate both sides.

$$
k_{j}/k \;=\; e^{1} \;=\; e
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $k$.

$$
k \;=\; k_{j}/e
$$

Therefore, the **critical (optimum) density** is:

$$
\boxed{\,k_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{j}}{e}\,}
$$

where $e \approx 2.71828$ is Euler's number. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deriving the Optimum Speed $u_m$

**Step 1:** Substitute $k = k_j/e$ back into the primary model equation.

$$
u \;=\; u_{m} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{k_{j}/e}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the argument of the logarithm.

$$
u \;=\; u_{m} \cdot \ln(e)
$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity $\ln(e) = 1$.

$$
u \;=\; u_{m} \cdot 1 \;=\; u_{m}
$$

This confirms the elegant self-consistency of the Greenberg model — the speed at the critical density **equals the model parameter** $u_m$:

$$
\boxed{\,u_{\text{at capacity}} \;=\; u_{m}\,}
$$

∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Flow (Capacity) $q_{\max}$

**Step 1:** Substitute $k = k_j/e$ and $u = u_m$ into $q = u \cdot k$.

$$
q_{\max} \;=\; u_{m} \cdot \left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{e}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Write the final compact form.

$$
\boxed{\,q_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{u_{m} \cdot k_{j}}{e}\,}
$$

∎

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Speed–Flow Relationship

We eliminate $k$ between $u = u_m\ln(k_j/k)$ and $q = u\,k$.

**Step 1:** Solve the primary equation for $k$.

$$
u/u_{m} \;=\; \ln(k_{j}/k) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k_{j}/k \;=\; e^{\,u/u_{m}}
$$

**Step 2:** Invert to express $k$.

$$
k \;=\; k_{j}\,e^{\,-u/u_{m}}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $u$ and substitute into $q = u\,k$.

$$
q \;=\; u \cdot k_{j}\,e^{\,-u/u_{m}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,q \;=\; k_{j}\,u\,e^{\,-u/u_{m}}\,}
$$

This is the **speed–flow curve** in the Greenberg regime — a curve that rises, peaks at $q_{\max}$ when $u = u_m$, and decays to zero as $u \to 0$. ∎

---

## 3.6 Analytical Master Table — Variable Resolution at a Glance

| Condition | $k$ (density) | $u$ (speed) | $q$ (flow) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| Free flow ($k \to 0$) | $0$ | $\infty$ | $0$ |
| At capacity ($k = k_j/e$) | $k_j/e$ | $u_m$ | $u_m k_j/e = q_{\max}$ |
| Jammed ($k = k_j$) | $k_j$ | $0$ | $0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Greenberg Model Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **logical flow** of how the Greenberg model transforms input traffic variables into engineering outputs. This is the preferred representation for KTU theory questions where physical plots are not feasible in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Traffic Stream Data"] --> B["Measure Density k and Speed u"]
    B --> C{"Is Regime Congested k greater than 30 percent kj?"}
    C -- YES --> D["Apply Greenberg Log Model"]
    C -- NO --> E["Use Greenshields or Underwood Model"]
    D --> F["Compute u_m via Regression u versus ln kj over k"]
    F --> G["Derive k_m equals kj divided by e"]
    G --> H["Derive u at capacity equals u_m"]
    H --> I["Compute q_max equals u_m kj divided by e"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT: Capacity q_max and LOS"]
```

## 4.2 Speed–Density Curve Schematic

The following conceptual schematic depicts the **logarithmic shape** of the Greenberg speed–density curve and its key landmarks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Origin k=0 u=infinity"] -.->|"Asymptotic rise"| P2["Curved log decay region"]
    P2 -->|"Critical Point"| P3["k = kj divided by e  AND  u = u_m"]
    P3 -->|"Steep falloff"| P4["k = kj  AND  u = 0 Jammed"]
```

## 4.3 Flow–Density Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Origin k=0 q=0"] -->|"Rising log-concave branch"| Q2["PEAK CAPACITY q_max at k = kj divided by e"]
    Q2 -->|"Falling branch"| Q3["k = kj q=0 Jammed"]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison — Greenberg vs Greenshields Regime

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FreeFlow["FREE FLOW REGIME"]
        FF1["Greenshields: Linear"]
        FF2["Underwood: Exponential"]
    end
    subgraph CongFlow["CONGESTED FLOW REGIME"]
        CF1["Greenberg: Logarithmic"]
        CF2["Greenshields: Also usable but less accurate"]
    end
    FF1 -.->|"Lower density transition"| CongFlow
    FF2 -.->|"Lower density transition"| CongFlow
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU exams:** Use the schematic to annotate the *three landmarks* — origin, capacity peak ($q_{\max}$), and jam point — on your speed–density, flow–density, and speed–flow plots. Examiners award partial marks specifically for correctly identifying $k_m = k_j/e$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Similar Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** State the Greenberg logarithmic speed–density model and mention the regime of traffic flow for which it is most suitable.

**Model Answer:**

The Greenberg logarithmic speed–density model is given by:

$$
u \;=\; u_{m} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{k}\right)
$$

where $u$ is the space-mean speed (km/h), $k$ is the density (veh/km), $k_j$ is the jam density, and $u_m$ is the optimum speed parameter (speed at maximum flow).

The model is most suitable for **congested flow conditions**, where density is high (typically $k > 30\%$ of $k_j$) and vehicles travel at low speeds approaching a jam. It is unsuitable for free-flow conditions because it predicts $u \to \infty$ as $k \to 0$. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** For the Greenberg model, derive the expression for the jam density in terms of the optimum speed and the maximum flow.

**Model Answer:**

The maximum flow (capacity) in the Greenberg model is:

$$
q_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{u_{m} \cdot k_{j}}{e}
$$

Solving for the jam density:

$$
k_{j} \;=\; \dfrac{q_{\max} \cdot e}{u_{m}}
$$

This expression is widely used in **field calibration** of the Greenberg parameters from measured speed and flow data. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Evaluation – Module 1 Choice-Based Question]**
> *Attempt any ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Greenberg Model Derivation & Numerical Application*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)
**Q:** Starting from the Greenberg speed–density model, derive the expressions for (i) the critical density at maximum flow, (ii) the optimum speed, and (iii) the maximum flow (capacity).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Critical density:** Starting from $q = u_m \cdot k \cdot \ln(k_j/k)$ and applying $dq/dk = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dq}{dk} \;=\; u_{m}\,\Bigl[\ln(k_{j}/k) \;-\; 1\Bigr] \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{j}}{e}
$$

**(ii) Optimum speed:** Substituting $k = k_j/e$ into the primary equation:

$$
u \;=\; u_{m}\cdot \ln(e) \;=\; u_{m}
$$

Hence the speed at capacity equals the model parameter $u_m$. **(iii) Maximum flow:**

$$
q_{\max} \;=\; u_{m} \cdot \left(\dfrac{k_{j}}{e}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{u_{m}\,k_{j}}{e}
$$

**(7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)
**Q:** On a single-lane urban arterial, the jam density is observed as **120 veh/km** and the optimum speed parameter for the Greenberg model is **40 km/h**. Determine:
(i) the critical density, (ii) the maximum flow (capacity), and (iii) the speed when the density is **90 veh/km**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $k_j = 120$ veh/km, $u_m = 40$ km/h.

**(i) Critical density:**

$$
k_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{j}}{e} \;=\; \dfrac{120}{2.71828} \;\approx\; 44.15 \;\text{veh/km}
$$

**(ii) Maximum flow (capacity):**

$$
q_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{u_{m}\,k_{j}}{e} \;=\; \dfrac{40 \times 120}{2.71828} \;\approx\; 1765.8 \;\text{veh/h}
$$

**(iii) Speed at $k = 90$ veh/km:**

$$
u \;=\; 40 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{120}{90}\right) \;=\; 40 \cdot \ln(1.3333) \;=\; 40 \times 0.28768 \;\approx\; 11.51 \;\text{km/h}
$$

**(7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice — Comparative & Field-Calibration Focus*

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)
**Q:** Compare the Greenberg logarithmic model with the Greenshields linear model for traffic stream behavior. Highlight the boundary conditions and specify the operational regime for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Greenshields (Linear) | Greenberg (Logarithmic) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Functional Form | $u = u_f(1 - k/k_j)$ | $u = u_m \ln(k_j/k)$ |
| At $k = 0$ | $u = u_f$ (finite free speed) | $u \to \infty$ (unrealistic) |
| At $k = k_j$ | $u = 0$ ✔ | $u = 0$ ✔ |
| Speed at capacity | $u_f/2$ | $u_m$ |
| Critical density | $k_j/2$ | $k_j/e$ |
| Best for regime | **Free flow & general use** | **Congested flow only** |
| Capacity | $u_f k_j / 4$ | $u_m k_j / e$ |

Greenshields assumes a *linear* speed-density decay and is symmetric about $k_j/2$, making it suitable for a wide range of conditions but less accurate near jam density. Greenberg uses a *logarithmic* decay that more faithfully captures the steep drop in speed as $k \to k_j$, but fails in free-flow because it allows unbounded speeds. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Field Calibration Problem (7 Marks)
**Q:** A congested urban roadway exhibits a capacity flow of **1800 veh/h** at an optimum speed of **36 km/h**. Using the Greenberg model, determine the jam density and the critical density. Also compute the flow when the density equals **80 veh/km**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $q_{\max} = 1800$ veh/h, $u_m = 36$ km/h.

**Step 1 — Jam density:** Using $q_{\max} = u_m k_j / e$:

$$
k_{j} \;=\; \dfrac{q_{\max}\,e}{u_{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{1800 \times 2.71828}{36} \;\approx\; 135.91 \;\text{veh/km}
$$

**Step 2 — Critical density:**

$$
k_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{j}}{e} \;=\; \dfrac{135.91}{2.71828} \;\approx\; 50.0 \;\text{veh/km}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed at $k = 80$ veh/km:**

$$
u \;=\; 36 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{135.91}{80}\right) \;=\; 36 \cdot \ln(1.6989) \;=\; 36 \times 0.5306 \;\approx\; 19.10 \;\text{km/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Flow at $k = 80$ veh/km:**

$$
q \;=\; u \cdot k \;=\; 19.10 \times 80 \;\approx\; 1528 \;\text{veh/h}
$$

**(7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant $e$:** Students often write $k_m = k_j/2$ (Greenshields) instead of $k_j/e$ — this is a direct 3-mark loss in derivation questions.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing km/h with veh/km without converting densities to veh/h is a recurrent error. Convert ALL densities to consistent units (veh/km with km/h, or veh/m with m/s) before substitution.
> 3. **Applying Greenberg to free-flow problems:** Examiners deliberately set questions with low densities to test whether students recognize the model's regime limitation.
> 4. **Skipping the $dq/dk$ derivation:** In "derivation" questions, simply stating $k_m = k_j/e$ without showing differentiation earns zero marks. Always show $dq/dk = u_m[\ln(k_j/k) - 1] = 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before every KTU exam attempt on this topic.**

- ✅ **Primary equation to memorize first:** $u = u_m \ln(k_j/k)$
- ✅ **Critical density:** $k_m = k_j / e \;\;(\approx 0.368 \, k_j)$
- ✅ **Optimum speed = model parameter:** $u_{\text{at cap}} = u_m$
- ✅ **Maximum flow (capacity):** $q_{\max} = u_m k_j / e$
- ✅ **Speed–flow equation:** $q = k_j \cdot u \cdot e^{\,-u/u_m}$
- ✅ **Boundary condition 1 (jam):** $k = k_j \Rightarrow u = 0$ ✔
- ✅ **Boundary condition 2 (free):** $k = 0 \Rightarrow u = \infty$ ✖ (limitation)
- ✅ **Regime validity:** Congested flow ONLY ($k > 30\%$ of $k_j$)
- ✅ **Comparison anchor:** Greenshields gives $k_m = k_j/2$; Greenberg gives $k_m = k_j/e$
- ✅ **Capacity ratio:** Greenberg gives $q_{\max}$ about $1.086 \times$ Greenshields for the same $u_m = u_f$ and $k_j$ — remember this!
- ✅ **Derivative identity used:** $d/dx[\ln(k_j/k)] = -1/k$
- ✅ **Exam pitfall:** Always carry $e \approx 2.71828$ explicitly to the final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Regime Models - Two and Three Regime Linear Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multi-Regime Models - Two and Three Regime Linear Models # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Single-Regime vs Multi-Regime Models — The Core Idea In traffic flow theory, the relationship between **speed (u)**, **density (k)**, and **flow (q)** is fundamental. The classical **Greenshields single-regime linear model** assumes that this relationship is a *single straight line* from free-flow to jam conditions. However, empirical observations repeatedly show that real traffic behaves **differently** in low-density (free-flow) and high-density (congested) conditions. **Multi-Regime Models** overcome this limitation by dividing the speed–density (or flow–density) curve into **two or more linear segments**, each governed by its own equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > *A multi-regime traffic flow model is a piecewise-linear representation of the speed–density (and consequently flow–density and speed–flow) relationship in which traffic behavior is divided into distinct regimes separated by critical (breakpoint) densities. Each regime is described by its own linear equation, allowing a more faithful representation of both uncongested and congested traffic states.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚗 Imagine you are driving a car on a highway: - **At very low density (early morning, empty road):** You can drive at your desired speed almost **regardless** of how many cars are there. Speed barely changes. - **As density increases (rush hour begins):** Your speed starts getting affected, but gradually. - **At very high density (bumper-to-bumper):** Even a small increase in the number of cars causes a **dramatic** drop in speed. A **single straight line** cannot capture this "flat-then-steeper-then-flat-again" behavior. Hence, we use **multiple straight lines (regimes)** stitched together — just like a tailor cuts fabric in pieces and sews them to fit the body shape! ## 1.3 Regimes — The Vocabulary of Multi-Regime Models | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Free-flow regime | k ≤ k₁ | Vehicles move at near free-flow speed | | Transitional regime | k₁ < k ≤ k₂ | Speed starts reducing noticeably | | Congested regime | k > k₂ | Speed drops sharply, stop-and-go | | Critical density (breakpoint) | k_c | Density where regime change occurs | | Jam density | k_j | Density at which speed = 0 | | Free-flow speed | u_f | Maximum desired speed | > [!NOTE] > KTU students must remember: **q = u × k** is the fundamental relation that links all three macroscopic variables in every regime. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Two-Regime Linear Model The **Two-Regime (Two-Phase) Linear Model** divides traffic into: 1. **Regime I — Uncongested / Free Flow (k ≤ k_c)** 2. **Regime II — Congested Flow (k > k_c)** ### Regime I — Free Flow In the uncongested regime, speed is assumed to remain **constant** at the free-flow speed u_f (some authors use a slight negative slope, but the simplest two-regime model assumes constant speed). $$u = u_f \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le k \le k_c$$ Corresponding flow: $$q = u_f \cdot k$$ At the **critical density** k_c, flow reaches maximum (capacity): $$q_{max} = u_f \cdot k_c$$ ### Regime II — Congested Flow Beyond the critical density, speed decreases linearly with density, reaching zero at the jam density k_j. $$u = u_f \cdot \frac{k_j - k}{k_j - k_c} \quad \text{for} \quad k_c \le k \le k_j$$ Or equivalently, using a slope m: $$u = u_f - m(k - k_c)$$ where $$m = \frac{u_f}{k_j - k_c}$$ Corresponding flow in Regime II: $$q = k \cdot u_f \cdot \frac{k_j - k}{k_j - k_c}$$ ## 2.2 Three-Regime Linear Model A more refined approach divides traffic into **three regimes**: 1. **Regime I — Free Flow (0 ≤ k ≤ k₁):** Speed = u_f (constant) 2. **Regime II — Transition (k₁ < k ≤ k₂):** Linear decrease in speed 3. **Regime III — Congested (k₂ < k ≤ k_j):** Steeper linear decrease to zero at jam density ### Regime I — Free Flow $$u = u_f \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le k \le k_1$$ ### Regime II — Transition $$u = u_f - m_1(k - k_1) \quad \text{for} \quad k_1 < k \le k_2$$ where $$m_1 = \frac{u_f - u_2}{k_2 - k_1}$$ ### Regime III — Congested $$u = u_2 - m_2(k - k_2) \quad \text{for} \quad k_2 < k \le k_j$$ where $$m_2 = \frac{u_2}{k_j - k_2}$$ At k = k_j, u = 0 (vehicle stream halts). > [!NOTE] > The **maximum flow (capacity)** typically occurs at the boundary between Regime I and Regime II (i.e., at k = k₁), because that is where flow q = u × k peaks in this piecewise model. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Relation | Formula | Applicable Regime | |---|----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Speed in Free Flow | $u = u_f$ | $0 \le k \le k_c$ (or k₁) | | 2 | Speed in Congested (2-Regime) | $u = u_f \cdot \dfrac{k_j - k}{k_j - k_c}$ | $k_c \le k \le k_j$ | | 3 | Speed in Transition (3-Regime) | $u = u_f - m_1(k - k_1)$ | $k_1 < k \le k_2$ | | 4 | Speed in Heavily Congested (3-Regime) | $u = u_2 - m_2(k - k_2)$ | $k_2 < k \le k_j$ | | 5 | Maximum Flow (2-Regime) | $q_{max} = u_f \cdot k_c$ | at k = k_c | | 6 | Jam Flow | q = 0 | at k = k_j | | 7 | Slope of congested curve | $m = \dfrac{u_f}{k_j - k_c}$ | Congested regime | | 8 | Continuity (always valid) | $q = u \cdot k$ | All regimes | > [!WARNING] > **Units Trap:** Always use **consistent units** — u in km/hr, k in veh/km, q in veh/hr. Mixing units (e.g., m/s with veh/km) will cost you marks in KTU exams. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Traffic Signal Design:** Capacity (q_max) determines green-time allocation. - **Highway Geometric Design:** Sets design speed (u_f) and LOS boundaries. - **Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS):** Real-time models for ramp metering use multi-regime logic. - **Microsimulation Calibration:** Multi-regime parameters feed VISSIM, CORSIM calibration. - **Incident Detection:** Sudden shift between regimes signals congestion onset. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Flow–Density Relationship (Two-Regime Model) We derive the **flow equation** for the congested regime starting from the speed–density linear relation. ### Given $$u = u_f \cdot \frac{k_j - k}{k_j - k_c} \quad ; \quad k_c \le k \le k_j$$ ### Step 1 — Multiply both sides by k (since q = u·k) $$q = k \cdot u_f \cdot \frac{k_j - k}{k_j - k_c}$$ ### Step 2 — Expand numerator $$q = \frac{u_f \cdot k \cdot (k_j - k)}{k_j - k_c}$$ ### Step 3 — Expand the product k(k_j − k) $$q = \frac{u_f \cdot (k_j \cdot k - k^2)}{k_j - k_c}$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange to standard quadratic form $$q = \frac{u_f}{k_j - k_c} \left( k_j k - k^2 \right)$$ This is a **downward-opening parabola** in the k–q plane, peaking at k = k_c. ### Step 5 — Maximum flow (Capacity) Verification Differentiate q with respect to k, set to zero: $$\frac{dq}{dk} = \frac{u_f}{k_j - k_c}(k_j - 2k) = 0$$ Solving: $$k = \frac{k_j}{2} = k_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_c = \frac{k_j}{2}$$ Substituting back: $$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_c (k_j - k_c)}{k_j - k_c} = u_f \cdot k_c$$ Hence **maximum flow** = free-flow speed × critical density. ✅ ## 3.2 Derivation — Continuity Check Across the Breakpoint At k = k_c, both regimes must give the **same flow** (otherwise the model is discontinuous). **From Regime I:** $$q(k_c) = u_f \cdot k_c$$ **From Regime II:** $$q(k_c) = u_f \cdot \frac{k_j - k_c}{k_j - k_c} \cdot k_c = u_f \cdot k_c \quad \checkmark$$ Hence the two-regime model is **continuous at the breakpoint**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Models | Property | Greenshields (1-Regime) | 2-Regime Linear | 3-Regime Linear | |----------|-------------------------|-----------------|-----------------| | Number of segments | 1 | 2 | 3 | | Free-flow behavior | Slight slope | Constant u_f | Constant u_f | | Congested behavior | Linear | Linear | Linear (steeper) | | Number of parameters | 2 (u_f, k_j) | 3 (u_f, k_c, k_j) | 5 (u_f, k₁, k₂, u₂, k_j) | | Realism | Low | Moderate | High | | Complexity of fit | Easiest | Easy | Moderate | | Capacity location | k_m = k_j/2 | at k = k_c | at k = k₁ | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Speed–Density Curve (Two-Regime Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Origin
k=0, u=0"] --> B["Free Flow Regime
u = u_f constant
0 to k_c"] B --> C{"Critical Point
k = k_c
u = u_f"} C --> D["Congested Regime
u decreases linearly
k_c to k_j"] D --> E["Jam Point
k = k_j
u = 0"] ``` ## 4.2 Speed–Density Curve (Three-Regime Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Origin
k=0, u=0"] --> B["Regime I
Free Flow
u = u_f constant"] B --> C{"Breakpoint 1
k = k1"} C --> D["Regime II
Transition
u decreases gently"] D --> E{"Breakpoint 2
k = k2, u = u2"} E --> F["Regime III
Congested
u decreases steeply"] F --> G["Jam Point
k = kj, u = 0"] ``` ## 4.3 Flow–Density Relationship (Two-Regime) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RegimeI["Free Flow Region"] direction LR O["k=0
q=0"] --> P1["Linear Rise
q = u_f × k"] P1 --> P2["Peak Capacity
q_max = u_f × k_c"] end subgraph RegimeII["Congested Region"] direction LR P2 --> P3["Parabolic Drop
q = u_f × k × (kj - k)/(kj - kc)"] P3 --> P4["k = kj
q = 0"] end RegimeI --> RegimeII ``` ## 4.4 Block Architecture — Real-Time Application of Multi-Regime Models ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Traffic Sensors
Loop Detectors / Cameras"] --> B["Data Acquisition Unit
Speed, Density, Flow"] B --> C["Density Classifier Module"] C --> D{"Determine
Current Regime"} D --> E1["Regime I
u = u_f"] D --> E2["Regime II
Linear Decline"] D --> E3["Regime III
Steeper Decline"] E1 --> F["Performance Calculator
q = u × k, LOS"] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G["Output to Traffic
Management Centre"] ``` > [!NOTE] > In the diagram, each block represents a **logical function**, not a physical component. In practice, these are implemented in software modules of an Advanced Traffic Management System (ATMS). # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Differentiate between single-regime and multi-regime traffic flow models. Why are multi-regime models preferred for congested traffic analysis?** **Model Answer (Key Points):** - Single-regime model (e.g., Greenshields): single linear speed–density relation for entire range. - Multi-regime model: divides traffic into 2 or 3 piecewise linear segments separated by breakpoint densities. - Real traffic shows non-uniform behavior — flat at low density, steeper at high density. - Multi-regime models capture this piecewise nature accurately, giving better capacity estimation. - Preferred in congested analysis because congested regime is governed by different physical rules (car-following instability) than free-flow. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is meant by a "critical density" in the context of multi-regime traffic flow models? What happens at this density?** **Model Answer:** - Critical density (k_c) is the density value at which the traffic regime changes from uncongested to congested flow in a two-regime model. - It corresponds to the **capacity point** (maximum flow). - Speed at k_c equals free-flow speed u_f. - Beyond k_c, speed decreases linearly with density, and flow reduces. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Many students forget to **convert units** (km/hr vs m/s) — instant 1-mark penalty. > - Skipping the **continuity check** at breakpoint loses 2 marks. > - Not drawing the **u–k, q–k, u–q** triangular plots loses a dedicated 2-mark allocation in Part B. > - Forgetting the condition **q = u·k** derivation is a common 1-mark slip. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** A two-regime linear speed–density model for an urban arterial has the following parameters: - Free-flow speed, **u_f = 60 km/hr** - Jam density, **k_j = 120 veh/km** - Critical density, **k_c = 30 veh/km** **(i)** Derive the speed equation for the **congested regime**. **(ii)** Compute the **speed and flow** when density is 80 veh/km. **(iii)** Determine the **maximum flow (capacity)**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Sketch the **speed–density**, **flow–density**, and **speed–flow** curves for the above two-regime model. Label all critical points (free-flow speed, critical density, jam density, capacity, optimum speed). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Given:** - u_f = 60 km/hr - k_j = 120 veh/km - k_c = 30 veh/km **(i) Speed equation for congested regime (k > k_c):** $$u = u_f \cdot \frac{k_j - k}{k_j - k_c}$$ Substituting values: $$u = 60 \cdot \frac{120 - k}{120 - 30}$$ $$\boxed{u = 60 \cdot \frac{120 - k}{90} = \frac{60}{90}(120 - k) = \frac{2}{3}(120 - k)}$$ So **u = (2/3)(120 − k) km/hr** for 30 ≤ k ≤ 120. **(ii) At k = 80 veh/km:** $$u = \frac{2}{3}(120 - 80) = \frac{2}{3}(40) = \frac{80}{3}$$ $$\boxed{u = 26.67 \text{ km/hr}}$$ Flow: $$q = u \cdot k = 26.67 \times 80$$ $$\boxed{q = 2133.33 \text{ veh/hr}}$$ **(iii) Maximum flow (Capacity):** At k = k_c, q_max = u_f × k_c: $$q_{max} = 60 \times 30$$ $$\boxed{q_{max} = 1800 \text{ veh/hr}}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Speed–Density Curve:** Horizontal line u = 60 from k = 0 to k = 30; then linear descent from (30, 60) to (120, 0). **Flow–Density Curve:** Linear rise from (0, 0) to (30, 1800); then parabolic drop from (30, 1800) to (120, 0). **Speed–Flow Curve:** Vertical line from (0, 60) to (1800, 60); then parabolic branch from (1800, 60) curving down to (0, 0). > All three plots should be drawn **on a single diagram** (triangular fundamental diagram) with proper axis labels and units — **2 marks reserved for clarity and labelling**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** A three-regime linear speed–density model has the following parameters: - u_f = 80 km/hr - k₁ = 20 veh/km, k₂ = 50 veh/km, k_j = 150 veh/km - u₂ = 50 km/hr (speed at k₂) **(i)** Derive the speed equations for **Regime II (transition)** and **Regime III (congested)**. **(ii)** Compute the **flow** at k = 35 veh/km and k = 100 veh/km. **(iii)** Find the **capacity** of the facility. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain, with neat sketches, why a three-regime model is more realistic than Greenshields' single-regime model. Discuss one **engineering application** where this added accuracy matters. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **(i) Regime II — Transition (k₁ = 20 < k ≤ k₂ = 50):** Slope: $$m_1 = \frac{u_f - u_2}{k_2 - k_1} = \frac{80 - 50}{50 - 20} = \frac{30}{30} = 1 \text{ km/hr per veh/km}$$ Speed equation: $$u = u_f - m_1(k - k_1) = 80 - 1(k - 20)$$ $$\boxed{u = 100 - k \quad \text{for} \quad 20 \le k \le 50}$$ **Regime III — Congested (k₂ = 50 < k ≤ k_j = 150):** Slope: $$m_2 = \frac{u_2}{k_j - k_2} = \frac{50}{150 - 50} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5$$ Speed equation: $$u = u_2 - m_2(k - k_2) = 50 - 0.5(k - 50)$$ $$\boxed{u = 75 - 0.5k \quad \text{for} \quad 50 \le k \le 150}$$ **(ii) At k = 35 veh/km (Regime II):** $$u = 100 - 35 = 65 \text{ km/hr}$$ $$q = u \cdot k = 65 \times 35$$ $$\boxed{q = 2275 \text{ veh/hr}}$$ **At k = 100 veh/km (Regime III):** $$u = 75 - 0.5(100) = 75 - 50 = 25 \text{ km/hr}$$ $$q = 25 \times 100$$ $$\boxed{q = 2500 \text{ veh/hr}}$$ **(iii) Capacity:** Capacity occurs at the **first breakpoint** k₁ (transition from constant u_f to declining u): $$q_{max} = u_f \cdot k_1 = 80 \times 20$$ $$\boxed{q_{max} = 1600 \text{ veh/hr}}$$ > Note: Although q at k = 100 is 2500, this is **not** the capacity because it occurs in the congested regime. Capacity = max flow in the **transition at boundary**, since q rises to 1600 at k = 20 (peak) and falls afterwards in Regime II itself: > At k₁ = 20: q = 80 × 20 = 1600 > At k = 50: q = 50 × 50 = 2500 (peak of Regime II) > > **Correction (KTU-style rigor):** In a 3-regime model with constant u in Regime I, the **maximum flow** is actually the larger of (a) q at k = k₁ and (b) peak of Regime II parabola. Checking peak of Regime II: > > q = (100 − k)·k = 100k − k² → dq/dk = 100 − 2k = 0 → k = 50 (boundary, u₂ = 50) > q = 100(50) − 50² = 5000 − 2500 = 2500 veh/hr. > > Therefore **true capacity = 2500 veh/hr at k = k₂ = 50 veh/km, u = 50 km/hr**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Why 3-Regime is more realistic than Greenshields:** 1. **Free-flow rigidity:** In real traffic, drivers maintain nearly constant speed until density crosses k₁. Greenshields incorrectly assumes linear speed reduction from k = 0 itself. 2. **Asymmetric congestion:** Speed drops more sharply in heavy congestion than predicted by Greenshields. Three-regime model captures this with steeper Regime III slope (m₂ > m₁ typically). 3. **Physical realism:** Car-following theory shows that at low density, interactions are weak; at high density, they are dominant — supporting distinct regimes. **Engineering Application — Signalized Intersection Design:** At a signalized intersection, the discharge flow from a queue initially accelerates to u_f and remains near it until the tail of queue dissipates.
