6. **Base conditions** — ideal geometry, weather, lighting, traffic mix

> [!NOTE] **Why so many adjustments?** A facility's *true* performance must be normalized to **base conditions** so that two different sites can be fairly compared. For example, a hill on the gradient will reduce the passenger-car equivalent flow, so we adjust using $f_{HV}$ to compare it to a flat, ideal road.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$ | Fundamental traffic stream relationship | q: veh/h, k: veh/km, $\bar{v}_s$: km/h |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{q}{\bar{v}_s}$ | Density derived from flow & speed | veh/km |
| 3 | $\bar{v}_s = \dfrac{n \cdot L}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}$ | Space-mean speed (Harmonic mean of spot speeds) | km/h |
| 4 | $\bar{v}_t = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ | Time-mean speed (Arithmetic mean) | km/h |
| 5 | $\bar{v}_s = \bar{v}_t - \dfrac{\sigma_t^2}{\bar{v}_t}$ | Relationship between space-mean and time-mean speed | km/h |
| 6 | $PHF = \dfrac{V_{1h}}{4 \cdot V_{15}}$ | Peak Hour Factor (ratio of hourly volume to 4× peak 15-min) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $v_p = \dfrac{V}{PHF \cdot N \cdot f_{HV} \cdot f_P}$ | Passenger-car equivalent flow rate per lane | pc/h/ln |
| 8 | $f_{HV} = \dfrac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_R(E_R - 1)}$ | Heavy vehicle adjustment factor (T: trucks, R: buses/rec vehicles) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k_j = \dfrac{1000}{L_{veh} + L_{jam}}$ | Jam density (vehicles bumper-to-bumper) | veh/km |
| 10 | $q_{max} = \dfrac{k_j \cdot v_f}{4}$ (for Greenshields) | Maximum flow (capacity) from Greenshields' linear model | veh/h |
| 11 | $LOS = \text{function}(k \text{ or delay or speed})$ | LOS is derived from the relevant service measure | category A–F |

> [!WARNING] **Do not confuse:** 
> - **Time-mean speed** $\bar{v}_t$ → arithmetic mean of spot speeds (used in spot speed studies) 
> - **Space-mean speed** $\bar{v}_s$ → harmonic mean, used in **macroscopic stream analysis** and in the $q$-$k$-$\bar{v}_s$ relationship 
> - They are related but **never equal**; $\bar{v}_t \geq \bar{v}_s$ always.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LOS grading is used in KTU-relevant Indian engineering practice for:

- **Capacity design** of new roads: designers target **LOS C or D** for urban arterials in peak hour.
- **Signal timing optimization** at intersections (target LOS C–D).
- **Justifying road widening** or flyover construction (a road operating at LOS F qualifies for upgrade).
- **Traffic impact assessment (TIA)** of new developments (mall, IT park, township).
- **Public transport prioritization** (BRT lanes justified when general LOS is poor).
- **NHAI / PWD design standards** that reference IRC documents derived from HCM concepts.

> [!NOTE] **In the KTU exam, always quote the relevant IRC or HCM clause.** For Indian highways, the practical LOS criteria are derived from **IRC:64-1990** and **Census of India road classifications**, often combined with HCM thresholds adapted by the Ministry of Road Transport & Highways (MoRTH).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Speed–Density Model to LOS Capacity

### 3.1.1 Starting Model — Greenshields' Linear Speed-Density Relationship

The classical Greenshields model assumes a *linear* relationship between space-mean speed and density:

$$\bar{v}_s = v_f - \left( \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \right) \cdot k$$

where:
- $v_f$ = free-flow speed (km/h) — the *y-intercept*
- $k_j$ = jam density (veh/km/ln) — the density at which $\bar{v}_s = 0$

> [!NOTE] **Why is this model important for LOS?** Because it gives us a *closed-form* equation for capacity. Most KTU numerical problems use this model.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Maximum Flow (Capacity) and the Density at Capacity

**Step 1** — Substitute $\bar{v}_s$ into the fundamental relationship $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$:

$$q = k \cdot \left[ v_f - \left( \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \right) k \right]$$

**Step 2** — Expand the bracket:

$$q = k \cdot v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \cdot k^2$$

**Step 3** — This is a quadratic in $k$ of the form $q = a k - b k^2$ where $a = v_f$ and $b = v_f / k_j$. To find the *maximum* flow, differentiate with respect to $k$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\dfrac{dq}{dk} = v_f - 2 \cdot \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \cdot k = 0$$

**Step 4** — Solve for the critical density $k_0$:

$$v_f = 2 \cdot \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \cdot k_0$$

$$k_0 = \dfrac{k_j}{2}$$

> [!NOTE] **Conclusion:** Under Greenshields' assumption, capacity occurs at **half the jam density**.

**Step 5** — The speed at capacity $v_0$ is found by substituting $k_0 = k_j/2$ into the speed-density equation:

$$v_0 = v_f - \left( \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \right) \cdot \dfrac{k_j}{2} = v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{2} = \dfrac{v_f}{2}$$

**Step 6** — Maximum flow (capacity) is:

$$q_{max} = k_0 \cdot v_0 = \dfrac{k_j}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_f}{2} = \dfrac{k_j \cdot v_f}{4}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} = \dfrac{k_j \cdot v_f}{4}}$$

This is the capacity that defines the **upper limit of LOS E**.

---

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example — KTU Style

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked Example (Module 3 — LOS Analysis)**
> **Given:** A 4-lane urban arterial has free-flow speed $v_f = 80$ km/h and jam density $k_j = 140$ veh/km/lane (one direction). The peak-hour flow is $q = 1500$ veh/h (total, in one direction). Find:
> 1. Density and speed at this flow
> 2. The LOS of the facility (using freeway-style density thresholds)
> 3. Capacity and density at capacity

**Step 1** — Apply Greenshields to find the operating density $k$:

$$q = k \cdot \left[ v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} k \right] = k \cdot v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} k^2$$

Substitute $q = 1500$, $v_f = 80$, $k_j = 140$:

$$1500 = 80k - \dfrac{80}{140}k^2$$

$$1500 = 80k - 0.5714 k^2$$

Rearrange into standard quadratic form $a k^2 - b k + c = 0$:

$$0.5714 k^2 - 80 k + 1500 = 0$$

Multiply through by 1/0.5714 to simplify (or use the quadratic formula):

$$k^2 - 140 k + 2625 = 0$$

**Step 2** — Apply the quadratic formula:

$$k = \dfrac{140 \pm \sqrt{140^2 - 4(1)(2625)}}{2}$$

$$k = \dfrac{140 \pm \sqrt{19600 - 10500}}{2} = \dfrac{140 \pm \sqrt{9100}}{2}$$

$$k = \dfrac{140 \pm 95.39}{2}$$

This yields two roots: $k_1 = 22.30$ veh/km/ln (the *stable* or pre-capacity solution) and $k_2 = 117.7$ veh/km/ln (the *forced* post-capacity solution). 

The correct physical answer is the **smaller** density:

$$\boxed{k \approx 22.30 \text{ veh/km/ln}}$$

**Step 3** — Find the corresponding speed:

$$\bar{v}_s = v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} k = 80 - \dfrac{80}{140}(22.30) = 80 - 12.74 = 67.26 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4** — Determine LOS using the density thresholds:

Since $16 < k = 22.30 \leq 22$ ... wait, $22.30 > 22$, so the condition is:

$$k = 22.30 \text{ veh/km/ln} \quad \Rightarrow \quad LOS = D \text{ (if we use the value precisely)}$$

Specifically, comparing to the threshold table: $22 < 22.30 \leq 28$ ⇒ **LOS E** (since 22.30 is just above the 22 boundary).

> [!IMPORTANT] The density at 22.30 is in the **D/E transition zone**. With PHF, heavy vehicles, and $f_P$ adjustments, the practical answer in a KTU exam would round to **LOS D** if $k \le 22$, or **LOS E** if $k > 22$.

**Step 5** — Compute capacity:

$$q_{max} = \dfrac{k_j \cdot v_f}{4} = \dfrac{140 \times 80}{4} = 2800 \text{ veh/h/ln}$$

Since there are 2 lanes per direction, total directional capacity = $2 \times 2800 = 5600$ veh/h.

**Conclusion:** The facility is operating at $\frac{1500}{5600} \approx 26.8\%$ of capacity, but because the *density* per lane is high, the perceived LOS is poor (D–E).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LOS Computation Flow

| Step | Action | Inputs | Output | Formula Used |
|------|--------|--------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Identify facility type | Road geometry | Freeway / Urban street / Intersection | — |
| 2 | Select service measure | Facility type | Density / Delay / Speed | Per HCM |
| 3 | Compute $v_p$ (pc/h/ln) | V, PHF, $f_{HV}$, $f_P$, N | Adjusted flow | $v_p = \dfrac{V}{PHF \cdot N \cdot f_{HV} \cdot f_P}$ |
| 4 | Compute density or delay | $\bar{v}_s$, $q$, geometry | Service measure value | $k = q / \bar{v}_s$ |
| 5 | Map to LOS letter | Service measure value | LOS = A to F | Threshold table |
| 6 | Recommend | LOS letter | Action (widen, signal retiming, etc.) | Engineering judgment |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — LOS Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Traffic Data] --> B[Identify Facility Type]
    B --> C{Freeway or Highway?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Density as Service Measure]
    C -- No --> E{Signalized or Unsignalized Intersection?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use Control Delay as Service Measure]
    E -- No --> G[Use Average Travel Speed]
    D --> H[Compute Passenger-Car Equivalent Flow v_p]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply Speed-Density Model or Delay Equation]
    I --> J[Compute Service Measure Value]
    J --> K{Compare to Threshold Table}
    K --> L[Output LOS Letter A to F]
    L --> M[Provide Engineering Recommendation]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:** Follow arrows top-to-bottom. Each diamond is a *decision* point, and each rectangle is a *processing* step. This is the same flowchart a KTU examiner expects you to draw in 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LOS Grading Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        A1[Volume V veh per h]
        A2[Spot Speeds vi]
        A3[Geometry N Lanes]
        A4[Vehicle Mix HV percent]
        A5[PHF Peak Hour Factor]
    end

    subgraph Processing[Processing Layer]
        B1[PHF Calculator]
        B2[Heavy Vehicle Factor f_HV]
        B3[Driver Population Factor f_P]
        B4[pc Conversion Engine]
        B5[Speed Density Model]
        B6[LOS Mapping Module]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        C1[Density k pc per km per ln]
        C2[Service Flow Rate v_p]
        C3[LOS Grade A to F]
        C4[Capacity q_max]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A5 --> B1
    A1 --> B4
    B1 --> B4
    A4 --> B2
    B2 --> B4
    A3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    A2 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C4
    C1 --> B6
    C4 --> B6
    B6 --> C3
    B4 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** Raw field data enters the *Input Layer*. The *Processing Layer* normalizes everything to base conditions and applies the speed-density model. The *Output Layer* produces the final LOS letter grade plus the underlying numerical service measure (density, delay, etc.) — both are required in a KTU answer.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationship of LOS to Speed, Density, Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Flow q veh per h] --> K[Density k veh per km]
    V[Space-Mean Speed vs km per h] --> K
    K --> LOS[LOS Letter A to F]
    Q --> LOS
    V --> LOS
    K -- High Density --> EF[LOS E to F Zone]
    K -- Mid Density --> CD[LOS C to D Zone]
    K -- Low Density --> AB[LOS A to B Zone]
    V -- High Speed --> AB
    V -- Low Speed --> EF
    Q -- Near Capacity --> EF
    Q -- Below Capacity --> CD
```

> [!IMPORTANT] The diagram emphasizes that **density is the common thread**: it grows monotonically as flow rises to capacity, and beyond capacity it rises further into LOS F territory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]**
**Define Level of Service (LOS) as applied in traffic engineering. List the six LOS categories.**

**Model Answer:**

> Level of Service (LOS) is a qualitative grading system, defined in the Highway Capacity Manual (HCM), that describes the operational conditions of a traffic stream and how these conditions are perceived by drivers and passengers. It is determined using a measurable *service measure* such as density, delay, or speed.

The six LOS categories, in order of best to worst, are:

$$LOS = A, B, C, D, E, F$$

- **A** — Free flow
- **B** — Reasonably free flow
- **C** — Stable flow
- **D** — Approaching unstable flow
- **E** — Unstable flow (at capacity)
- **F** — Forced / breakdown flow

> [!NOTE] Each letter corresponds to a defined numerical range of the service measure (e.g., for freeways, density ranges). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is *density* preferred over *flow* as the primary service measure for freeways? Justify in two points.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Density is monotonically related to service quality.** As density increases from 0 to jam density, the perceived quality steadily worsens. Flow, on the other hand, *rises* to capacity and then *falls* during breakdown — so the same flow value can correspond to two different operating conditions (pre- and post-capacity).

2. **Density is directly measurable in the field** using aerial photography, video, or occupancy from loop detectors, allowing *real-time* LOS estimation. Flow is a *rate* and does not directly describe the "crowdedness" experienced by a driver at an instant.

> [!NOTE] For freeways, HCM uses **density in pc/km/ln** as the LOS criterion. **(3 marks)**

---

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **write the definition of LOS but skip the service measure table** — this costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> - For numericals, students often confuse **time-mean speed $\bar{v}_t$** with **space-mean speed $\bar{v}_s$** and apply $q = k \bar{v}_t$ (wrong). Always use $\bar{v}_s$ in the $q$–$k$–$\bar{v}_s$ relationship.
> - When asked to determine LOS, you must **state the threshold**, **show the comparison**, and **conclude with the letter grade** — skipping any of these steps results in a partial mark deduction.
> - Do **not** write "LOS is good" or "LOS is bad" — always use the *letter grade*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]*

> **(a)** Explain the concept of Level of Service (LOS) with reference to the Highway Capacity Manual. Discuss the service measures used for (i) freeways, (ii) signalized intersections, and (iii) two-lane highways. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** On a 6-lane divided urban arterial (3 lanes per direction), the free-flow speed is $v_f = 70$ km/h and the jam density is $k_j = 120$ veh/km/lane. During the morning peak, the directional flow is $q = 1800$ veh/h. The PHF = 0.90 and the percentage of heavy vehicles is 10% (passenger-car equivalent of trucks $E_T = 2.5$). Assuming Greenshields' model, find:
> - (i) the operating density per lane,
> - (ii) the corresponding space-mean speed, and
> - (iii) the LOS of the facility using the freeway density thresholds. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
LOS is a qualitative measure describing operational conditions in a traffic stream and the perception of these conditions by drivers/passengers, expressed as letters A through F (HCM, 1950; updated 2010, 2016, 2022).

**Service Measures:**

| Facility | Primary Service Measure | LOS Determination |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Freeways** | **Density** (pc/km/ln) | Threshold table: A ≤ 7, B ≤ 11, C ≤ 16, D ≤ 22, E ≤ 28, F > 28 |
| **(ii) Signalized Intersections** | **Control Delay** (s/veh) | A ≤ 10, B ≤ 20, C ≤ 35, D ≤ 55, E ≤ 80, F > 80 |
| **(iii) Two-Lane Highways** | **Percent Time-Spent-Following (PTSF %)** + **Average Travel Speed** | Combined criteria (HCM Chapter 15) |

> [!NOTE] For each facility, the choice of service measure reflects the *driver's primary concern*: density for uninterrupted flow (crowding), delay for intersections (waiting), and PTSF + speed for two-lane roads (passing inability).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Convert to pc/h/lane flow using PHF and $f_{HV}$:**

$$v_p = \dfrac{V}{PHF \cdot N \cdot f_{HV} \cdot f_P}$$

For simplicity, take $f_P = 1$ (commuter traffic). Compute $f_{HV}$ with $P_T = 0.10$, $E_T = 2.5$:

$$f_{HV} = \dfrac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.10(2.5 - 1)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.15} = \dfrac{1}{1.15} = 0.8696$$

Directional flow $V = 1800$ veh/h, 3 lanes per direction ($N = 3$):

$$v_p = \dfrac{1800}{0.90 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.8696} = \dfrac{1800}{2.348} = 766.6 \text{ pc/h/ln}$$

> [!NOTE] This is the *passenger-car equivalent flow per lane* — what we plug into the speed-density model.

**Step 2 — Apply Greenshields' model:**

$$\bar{v}_s = v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \cdot k \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = k \cdot v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} k^2$$

Substitute $q = 766.6$, $v_f = 70$, $k_j = 120$:

$$766.6 = 70k - \dfrac{70}{120} k^2 = 70k - 0.5833 k^2$$

Rearrange:

$$0.5833 k^2 - 70 k + 766.6 = 0$$

Multiply by 1/0.5833:

$$k^2 - 120 k + 1314.3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic:**

$$k = \dfrac{120 \pm \sqrt{120^2 - 4(1314.3)}}{2} = \dfrac{120 \pm \sqrt{14400 - 5257.2}}{2} = \dfrac{120 \pm \sqrt{9142.8}}{2} = \dfrac{120 \pm 95.62}{2}$$

Two roots:
- $k_1 = (120 - 95.62)/2 = 12.19$ veh/km/ln ← **stable, pre-capacity**
- $k_2 = (120 + 95.62)/2 = 107.81$ veh/km/ln ← post-capacity, unphysical

**Operating density:** $\boxed{k \approx 12.19 \text{ veh/km/ln}}$

**Step 4 — Speed:**

$$\bar{v}_s = 70 - \dfrac{70}{120}(12.19) = 70 - 7.11 = 62.89 \text{ km/h}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{v}_s \approx 62.9 \text{ km/h}}$$

**Step 5 — Determine LOS:**

Compare $k = 12.19$ pc/km/ln with freeway thresholds:

$$11 < 12.19 \leq 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{LOS = C}$$

> [!NOTE] Although 1800 veh/h sounds high, the per-lane density is only 12.19, indicating stable flow with acceptable platoon formations. LOS C is the typical design target for urban arterials in peak hour.

**Capacity check:**

$$q_{max} = \dfrac{k_j \cdot v_f}{4} = \dfrac{120 \times 70}{4} = 2100 \text{ veh/h/ln}$$

Our per-lane flow $766.6$ is well below capacity — confirming the pre-capacity regime.

**End of Question A solution.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** What is the difference between *time-mean speed* and *space-mean speed*? Derive the relationship between them. Why is space-mean speed used in the $q$–$k$–$\bar{v}_s$ fundamental equation? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a rural highway, the free-flow speed is $v_f = 90$ km/h and the jam density is $k_j = 100$ veh/km/lane. If the facility is operating at LOS C (density range 11 to 16 pc/km/ln), determine the *maximum* and *minimum* space-mean speeds and the corresponding flow rates that the facility can sustain while still remaining in LOS C. Use Greenshields' model. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Time-Mean Speed (TMS):** Arithmetic mean of spot speeds of vehicles passing a *fixed point* over a time interval.

$$\bar{v}_t = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$$

**Space-Mean Speed (SMS):** Harmonic mean — average speed of vehicles occupying a *length* of road at an instant.

$$\bar{v}_s = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/v_i)} = \dfrac{n \cdot L}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}$$

**Derivation of the relationship:**

Let the speed distribution be approximated as continuous with mean $\bar{v}_t$ and variance $\sigma_t^2$. The harmonic mean of a positive-valued distribution can be expanded using a Taylor series:

$$\bar{v}_s = \dfrac{1}{E[1/v]} \approx \dfrac{1}{(1/\bar{v}_t)(1 + \sigma_t^2 / \bar{v}_t^2)} = \bar{v}_t - \dfrac{\sigma_t^2}{\bar{v}_t}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{v}_s = \bar{v}_t - \dfrac{\sigma_t^2}{\bar{v}_t}}$$

> [!NOTE] Since $\sigma_t^2 > 0$, this proves $\bar{v}_s \leq \bar{v}_t$ always.

**Why $\bar{v}_s$ is used in the $q$–$k$ equation:**

The relationship $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$ is **conservation-based**: in a time interval $\Delta t$, vehicles in a length $L$ of road = $k \cdot L$, and they travel at average speed $\bar{v}_s$, so distance covered = $k \cdot L \cdot \bar{v}_s$. The vehicles crossing a downstream point = $k \cdot L \cdot \bar{v}_s / L = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$. This is a *spatial* (space-mean) argument, hence $\bar{v}_s$ is required, not $\bar{v}_t$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Invert Greenshields' to find $\bar{v}_s$ at the boundaries of LOS C:**

$$\bar{v}_s = v_f - \dfrac{v_f}{k_j} \cdot k = 90 - \dfrac{90}{100} k = 90 - 0.9k$$

**Step 2 — At lower density boundary $k = 11$ pc/km/ln (start of LOS C):**

$$\bar{v}_{s, \min} = 90 - 0.9(11) = 90 - 9.9 = 80.1 \text{ km/h}$$

$$q_{\min} = k \cdot \bar{v}_s = 11 \times 80.1 = 881.1 \text{ pc/h/ln}$$

**Step 3 — At upper density boundary $k = 16$ pc/km/ln (end of LOS C):**

$$\bar{v}_{s, \max} = 90 - 0.9(16) = 90 - 14.4 = 75.6 \text{ km/h}$$

$$q_{\max} = 16 \times 75.6 = 1209.6 \text{ pc/h/ln}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Note: $\bar{v}_{s, \min}$ here is the *highest* speed in the LOS C range (lower density ⇒ higher speed), and $\bar{v}_{s, \max}$ is the *lowest* speed. The flow, however, increases with density in this stable-flow region.

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

For the rural highway to operate in **LOS C**, it must maintain:

- Density: $11 \leq k \leq 16$ pc/km/ln
- Space-mean speed: $75.6 \leq \bar{v}_s \leq 80.1$ km/h
- Flow rate: $881 \leq q \leq 1210$ pc/h/ln

> [!NOTE] The corresponding *maximum service flow rate* (MSF) at LOS C is approximately **1210 pc/h/ln** — the design value an engineer would use for a rural highway targeting LOS C.

**End of Question B solution.**

---

---

# ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LOS** is a *qualitative* grade (A to F) that describes traffic operational quality; **A = best (free flow)**, **F = worst (breakdown)**.
- LOS was introduced by the **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)**, first published by the **Transportation Research Board, USA in 1950**, and is adapted in India via **IRC:64-1990** and **IRC:SP:41**.
- The **fundamental relationship** $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$ governs all LOS calculations.
- **Service measures** differ by facility: **density** for freeways, **delay** for intersections, **PTSF + speed** for two-lane highways.
- **Capacity** (maximum flow) occurs at the *upper limit of LOS E*, *not* LOS A.
- Under **Greenshields' linear model**, capacity occurs at $k_0 = k_j/2$ and $v_0 = v_f/2$, giving $q_{max} = k_j v_f / 4$.
- **Time-mean speed** ($\bar{v}_t$) is arithmetic; **space-mean speed** ($\bar{v}_s$) is harmonic. They are related by $\bar{v}_s = \bar{v}_t - \sigma_t^2/\bar{v}_t$.
- Always **convert raw volume to pc/h/lane** using $v_p = V / (PHF \cdot N \cdot f_{HV} \cdot f_P)$ before applying speed-density models.
- For **KTU numericals**: identify the facility type, choose the correct service measure, apply the appropriate model (Greenhields is most common), and **always quote the threshold** when assigning the LOS letter.
- **Target LOS for design**: typically **LOS C** for urban arterials and **LOS B** for freeways in non-peak; **LOS D** may be acceptable in dense urban cores.
- **The single most important rule:** LOS is a *category* (A–F), not a number. Always end with the letter, not a qualitative phrase.

> [!TIP] **Quick Memory Trick — "ABCDE-Feeling":**
> - **A** — Alone, no one else on the road.
> - **B** — Barely any other cars.
> - **C** — Comfortable but a few cars visible.
> - **D** — Dense, but moving.
> - **E** — Extremely tight, no margin.
> - **F** — Failure: traffic has broken down.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Capacity and LOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Capacity and LOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Capacity (As per Highway Capacity Manual - HCM)

**Capacity** is defined as the maximum sustainable hourly flow rate at which persons or vehicles reasonably can be expected to traverse a point or a uniform section of a lane or roadway during a given time period under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Capacity**:
> - Units: **vehicles per hour (vph)** or **passenger cars per hour (pcph)**.
> - Always evaluated for the **prevailing conditions** (not ideal).
> - For multilane facilities, capacity is expressed per lane.

**Mathematical Representation**:

$$C = M_SF \times f_w \times f_{HV} \times f_p \times f_E$$

Where:
- $M_{SF}$ = Base service flow rate (pc/h/ln) at ideal conditions
- $f_w$ = Lane width adjustment factor
- $f_{HV}$ = Heavy vehicle adjustment factor
- $f_p$ = Driver population adjustment factor
- $f_E$ = Environmental (weather) adjustment factor

---

## 1.2 Definition of Level of Service (LOS)

**Level of Service (LOS)** is a qualitative measure describing operational conditions within a traffic stream, generally described in terms of speed, travel time, freedom to maneuver, traffic interruptions, comfort, and convenience.

Six LOS categories are defined: **A (best) to F (worst)**, with **LOS E** representing **at-capacity (breakdown)** operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: LOS is a **qualitative concept** but is measured using **quantitative performance measures** that differ by facility type.

| Facility Type | LOS Performance Measure (Primary) |
|---|---|
| Freeways & Multilane Highways | **Density (pc/km/ln)** |
| Two-Lane Highways | **Percent Time Spent Following (PTSF) %** & **Average Travel Speed (ATS) km/h** |
| Signalized Intersections | **Control Delay (s/veh)** |
| Unsignalized Intersections | **Control Delay (s/veh)** |
| Urban Streets | **Average Travel Speed (km/h)** |
| Pedestrian Walkways | **Space (m²/ped) & Flow rate (ped/min/m)** |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Highway as a Water Pipe"

Imagine a **water pipe** carrying traffic instead of water:

- **Pipe Diameter** = Number of lanes and lane width → bigger pipe = more capacity.
- **Pipe Roughness** = Pavement condition → smoother = more flow.
- **Bends in the Pipe** = Horizontal/vertical curves → restrict flow.
- **Valves** = Traffic signals & stop signs → control flow.
- **Debris in the Pipe** = Heavy vehicles (trucks) → reduce effective flow.
- **Water Pressure** = Driver aggressiveness and familiarity.

**Capacity** is the **maximum flow rate the pipe can sustain without bursting**, and **LOS** is the **comfort level** at which water flows (smooth jet vs. turbulent gush).

---

## 1.4 Capacity vs. LOS — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity** = a **quantitative maximum** (a single threshold value).
> **LOS** = a **qualitative grade** of operation across the full range from free-flow to breakdown.
> A facility may operate at LOS D but be far from capacity, OR at LOS E (which is at-capacity).

$$v/c \text{ ratio} = \frac{\text{Demand Volume}}{\text{Capacity}} = \frac{v}{C}$$

| $v/c$ Range | Typical LOS |
|---|---|
| 0.00 – 0.30 | A |
| 0.30 – 0.55 | B |
| 0.55 – 0.75 | C |
| 0.75 – 0.90 | D |
| 0.90 – 1.00 | E |
| > 1.00 | F |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars Influencing Capacity and LOS

The **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)** classifies all influencing factors under three broad groups:

### A. Roadway Factors (Geometric Characteristics)
These are **physical infrastructure** features:

- **Lane width** (ideal: **3.6 m**)
- **Lateral clearance / Shoulder width** (ideal: **1.8 m**)
- **Number of lanes**
- **Grade / Terrain classification** (Level, Rolling, Mountainous)
- **Design speed**
- **Horizontal alignment** (curvature)
- **Vertical alignment** (sight distance, grades)
- **Median type** (raised, depressed, flush)
- **Surface condition** (pavement type — flexible/rigid, roughness, skid resistance)
- **Auxiliary lanes** (climbing lanes, weaving sections, acceleration/deceleration lanes)
- **Length of analysis section**

### B. Traffic Factors (Operational Characteristics)
These are **demand-side** conditions:

- **Volume / Flow rate** ($v$ in veh/h)
- **Vehicle composition** (PCU distribution — cars, trucks, buses, 2-wheelers)
- **Directional split** (D, ideally 50/50)
- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** — measures flow uniformity within the peak hour
- **Speed** (mean and distribution)
- **Density** ($k$ in veh/km)
- **Driver population** (commuter familiar vs. recreational unfamiliar)
- **Spacing and headway distribution**

### C. Control & Environmental Factors
These are **regulatory and ambient** conditions:

- **Type of facility** (freeway, multilane, two-lane, urban street, intersection, roundabout)
- **Traffic control devices** (signals, signs, markings)
- **Speed limits**
- **Access control / Access point density** (driveways, intersections per km)
- **On-street parking**
- **Pedestrian activity**
- **Weather** (rain, snow, fog, ice)
- **Lighting conditions** (day vs. night)
- **Work zones and incidents**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fundamental Stream Relation** | $q = k \times \bar{u}_s$ | veh/h, veh/km, km/h | $q$ = flow, $k$ = density, $\bar{u}_s$ = space-mean speed |
| 2 | **Greenshields Model** | $\bar{u}_s = u_f - \left(\dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\right)k$ | km/h | Linear speed-density |
| 3 | **Maximum Flow (Capacity)** | $q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | veh/h | When $k = k_j / 2$ |
| 4 | **PCU Conversion** | $V_{pc/h} = \dfrac{V}{PCU_{mix}}$ | pc/h | $PCU_{mix} = \sum (p_i \cdot E_i)$ |
| 5 | **Heavy Vehicle Factor** | $f_{HV} = \dfrac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_B(E_B - 1)}$ | dimensionless | $P_T$ = truck %, $E_T$ = truck PCU |
| 6 | **Driver Population Factor** | $f_p$ | dimensionless | Recreat.: 0.85 – 1.00; Commuter: 1.00 |
| 7 | **Lane Width Factor ($f_w$)** | From HCM Table | dimensionless | Range: 0.85 – 1.00 (ideal at 3.6 m) |
| 8 | **Lateral Clearance Factor ($f_{LC}$)** | From HCM Table | dimensionless | Range: 0.95 – 1.00 |
| 9 | **Grade Factor ($f_g$)** | From HCM charts | dimensionless | Depends on % grade & length |
| 10 | **Peak Hour Factor** | $PHF = \dfrac{V_h}{4 \times V_{15}}$ | dimensionless | Ideal PHF = 1.0 |
| 11 | **Density** | $k = \dfrac{V}{\bar{u}_s}$ | veh/km | Used as LOS measure on freeways |
| 12 | **Control Delay (Intersections)** | $d = d_1 + d_2$ | s/veh | $d_1$ = uniform delay, $d_2$ = incremental delay |
| 13 | **Average Travel Speed (ATS)** | $ATS = \dfrac{\text{Length}}{\text{Travel Time}}$ | km/h | Two-lane highway LOS measure |
| 14 | **PTSF (Two-Lane)** | From HCM equations | % | Bidirectional following measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute Value Warning**: To represent absolute value of $x$ in equations, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the raw pipe character, as it breaks markdown tables.

---

## 2.3 Adjustment Factor Philosophy

The general capacity formula is built **multiplicatively** because each factor independently degrades (or improves) the base service flow:

$$\boxed{C = M_{SF} \times f_w \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_g \times f_p \times f_E \times \ldots}$$

**Why multiplicative?** Because if **lane width drops to 90%** AND **truck share is 20%**, both effects compound, they do not add. Hence a multiplicative form.

**Real-world utility**:
- Used by **NHAI, MoRTH, PWD (Kerala)** for road design and upgradation decisions.
- Used for **Bottleneck analysis** to identify which factor is the weakest link.
- Used in **Bypass feasibility studies** and **Expressway planning**.

---

## 2.4 LOS Criteria Summary Table (HCM-Style)

| LOS | Density (pc/km/ln) — Freeway | Control Delay (s/veh) — Signalized | v/c (typical) | Driver Experience |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | ≤ 7 | ≤ 10 | < 0.30 | Free flow, total freedom |
| **B** | 7 – 11 | 10 – 20 | 0.30 – 0.55 | Stable, slight restriction |
| **C** | 11 – 16 | 20 – 35 | 0.55 – 0.75 | Stable, restricted maneuverability |
| **D** | 16 – 22 | 35 – 55 | 0.75 – 0.90 | Approaching unstable flow |
| **E** | 22 – 28 | 55 – 80 | 0.90 – 1.00 | Unstable, at capacity |
| **F** | > 28 | > 80 | > 1.00 | Forced flow, breakdown, jam |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacity from Greenshields Speed-Density Model

The classical Greenshields linear model relates space-mean speed to density:

$$\bar{u}_s = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

**Step 1**: Write the fundamental flow equation:

$$q = k \cdot \bar{u}_s$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the Greenshields speed-density relation:

$$q = k \cdot \left[ u_f - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k \right]$$

**Step 3**: Expand the bracket:

$$q = u_f \cdot k - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k^2$$

**Step 4**: Differentiate $q$ with respect to $k$ to find the maximum:

$$\frac{dq}{dk} = u_f - \frac{2 u_f}{k_j} \cdot k = 0$$

**Step 5**: Solve for the critical density $k^*$:

$$u_f = \frac{2 u_f \cdot k^*}{k_j} \implies k^* = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

**Step 6**: Substitute $k^*$ back into the speed equation to find the speed at capacity:

$$\bar{u}_s^* = u_f - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

**Step 7**: Compute the maximum flow (capacity):

$$q_{max} = k^* \cdot \bar{u}_s^* = \frac{k_j}{2} \cdot \frac{u_f}{2} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore C = q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heavy Vehicle Adjustment Factor ($f_{HV}$)

Heavy vehicles consume more space and operate over longer headways. Let:
- $P_T$ = proportion of trucks
- $E_T$ = truck PCU (e.g., 1.5 for level terrain)
- $P_B$ = proportion of buses
- $E_B$ = bus PCU (e.g., 2.0 for level terrain)

**Step 1**: Express the equivalent flow in PCU:

$$V_{eq} = V_c + V_T \cdot E_T + V_B \cdot E_B$$

**Step 2**: Substitute volume proportions:

$$V_{eq} = V \cdot (1 - P_T - P_B) + V \cdot P_T \cdot E_T + V \cdot P_B \cdot E_B$$

**Step 3**: Group terms:

$$V_{eq} = V \cdot \left[ 1 - P_T - P_B + P_T E_T + P_B E_B \right]$$

**Step 4**: Rearrange:

$$V_{eq} = V \cdot \left[ 1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_B(E_B - 1) \right]$$

**Step 5**: Define the heavy vehicle adjustment factor as the ratio of flow to equivalent flow:

$$f_{HV} = \frac{V}{V_{eq}} = \frac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_B(E_B - 1)}$$

$$\boxed{f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_B(E_B - 1)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem** [KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style]:
> A 4-lane divided urban arterial (free-flow speed 80 km/h, jam density 140 veh/km/ln) has the following characteristics:
> - Lane width = 3.3 m → $f_w$ = 0.96
> - Lateral clearance = 1.0 m → $f_{LC}$ = 0.98
> - Heavy vehicle share = 15% trucks (E = 1.5), 5% buses (E = 2.0)
> - Driver population factor $f_p$ = 0.95
> - Volume observed = 1800 veh/h (both directions)

**Step 1**: Compute $f_{HV}$:

$$f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.15(1.5 - 1) + 0.05(2.0 - 1)}$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 + 0.075 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{1.125} = 0.8889$$

**Step 2**: Assume base service flow $M_{SF}$ for LOS E = 2200 pc/h/ln (HCM).

**Step 3**: Compute capacity per lane:

$$C = 2200 \times 0.96 \times 0.98 \times 0.8889 \times 0.95$$

$$= 2200 \times 0.7947 = 1748.4 \text{ pc/h/ln}$$

**Step 4**: Total capacity for 4 lanes (2 each direction):

$$C_{total} = 2 \times 1748.4 = 3496.8 \text{ pc/h}$$

**Step 5**: Convert observed volume to PCU:

$$V_{pc} = 1800 \times \frac{1}{0.8889} = 2025 \text{ pc/h}$$

**Step 6**: Compute $v/c$ ratio (one direction):

$$\frac{v}{c} = \frac{2025 / 2}{1748.4} = \frac{1012.5}{1748.4} = 0.579$$

**Step 7**: LOS determination — $v/c \approx 0.58$ → **LOS C** (stable flow, near free-flow speed).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Factor Dominates?"

| Scenario | Dominant Constraint | Recommended Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy truck % > 30% | $f_{HV}$ | Climbing lane; truck restriction hours |
| Lane width < 3.0 m | $f_w$ | Lane re-striping; edge hardening |
| Frequent intersections | Access density | Raised median; signal coordination |
| Rolling terrain (3–5%) | $f_g$ | Climbing lane; cut/fill optimization |
| Tourist route (unfamiliar drivers) | $f_p$ | Signage; road markings; ITS |
| Monsoon/rainfall (Kerala context) | $f_E$ | Drainage design; high-friction surface |
| Pedestrian-heavy urban road | Pedestrian LOS | Foot overbridge; signal phasing |
| Two-lane no-passing zones | PTSF | Passing lanes every 5–8 km |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define capacity of a road facility. List any four factors affecting the capacity of a multilane highway.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Definition (1.5 Marks)**:
Capacity is the maximum sustainable hourly flow rate at which vehicles (or persons) can reasonably be expected to traverse a uniform section of a lane or roadway under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions. It is generally expressed in **vehicles per hour (vph)** or **passenger cars per hour (pcph)**.

**Four Factors (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each)**:
1. **Lane width** — narrower lanes reduce capacity (e.g., 2.7 m vs 3.6 m).
2. **Lateral clearance / Shoulder width** — obstructions near the carriageway reduce effective width.
3. **Vehicle composition** — heavy vehicles (trucks, buses) consume more space, expressed through $f_{HV}$.
4. **Driver population** — recreational/unfamiliar drivers reduce capacity via $f_p < 1.0$.

*(Other acceptable factors: grade, number of lanes, access density, weather, traffic control devices.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**Distinguish between Capacity and Level of Service. Which is a quantitative measure?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Capacity | Level of Service (LOS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Quantitative | Qualitative |
| **Value** | Single maximum (e.g., 2200 pc/h/ln) | Letter grade (A to F) |
| **Basis** | Prevailing geometry, traffic, control | Speed, density, delay, PTSF |
| **Use** | Sizing the facility | Assessing operational quality |

**Capacity is the quantitative measure (1 Mark extra)**. LOS is a **qualitative** description expressed through quantitative thresholds (e.g., density for freeways, delay for intersections).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the various **roadway, traffic, and control factors** that affect the capacity and Level of Service of a multilane highway. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A multilane highway has the following characteristics: ideal free-flow speed = 90 km/h; lane width = 3.3 m ($f_w$ = 0.96); lateral clearance = 1.2 m ($f_{LC}$ = 0.99); trucks = 20% with $E_T$ = 1.5, buses = 10% with $E_B$ = 2.0; driver population factor $f_p$ = 0.95; access points = 15/km ($f_a$ = 0.96). Determine the **capacity per lane** and **LOS** when the demand is 1500 veh/h per lane. Take $M_{SF}$ for LOS E = 2200 pc/h/ln. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Roadway Factors (2.5 Marks)**:
- **Lane width** — ideal 3.6 m; reduces capacity if < 3.6 m.
- **Lateral clearance** — distance from edge of lane to nearest obstruction; ideal 1.8 m.
- **Number of lanes** — more lanes increase absolute capacity but each lane has its own LOS.
- **Grade / terrain** — level, rolling, or mountainous; affects $f_g$.
- **Design speed** — determines free-flow speed and curve radii.
- **Surface condition** — pavement type, roughness, drainage, skid resistance.
- **Auxiliary lanes** — climbing lanes, weaving sections.

**(ii) Traffic Factors (2.5 Marks)**:
- **Volume / flow rate** — directly used in $v/c$ ratio.
- **Vehicle composition** — heavy vehicles raise PCU and reduce effective capacity ($f_{HV}$).
- **Directional split** — uneven splits (e.g., 70/30) reduce capacity for the peak direction.
- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** — low PHF indicates sub-hourly peaks exceeding capacity.
- **Driver population** — unfamiliar drivers are more cautious, $f_p < 1.0$.

**(iii) Control & Environmental Factors (2 Marks)**:
- **Type of facility** — freeway vs. urban arterial.
- **Traffic signals, signs, markings** — control delay at intersections.
- **Speed limits** — restrict FFS.
- **Access point density** — driveways introduce friction.
- **Weather** — rain, fog, snow reduce capacity ($f_E < 1.0$); **highly relevant to Kerala monsoon**.
- **Lighting** — night conditions reduce effective width and FFS.

**Sketch** (Description, no actual drawing possible): A labelled cross-section of a 4-lane divided highway showing lane width, lateral clearance, median, shoulder, and an arrow indicating heavy vehicle, with a separate callout for control (signals) and environment (rain/fog).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given**:
- $M_{SF} = 2200$ pc/h/ln
- $f_w = 0.96$, $f_{LC} = 0.99$, $f_p = 0.95$, $f_a = 0.96$
- $P_T = 0.20$, $E_T = 1.5$
- $P_B = 0.10$, $E_B = 2.0$

**Step 1 — Compute $f_{HV}$** (1 Mark):

$$f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.20(1.5 - 1) + 0.10(2.0 - 1)} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.10 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{1.20} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2 — Compute Capacity per lane** (2 Marks):

$$C = 2200 \times 0.96 \times 0.99 \times 0.8333 \times 0.95 \times 0.96$$

$$= 2200 \times 0.7143 = 1571.4 \text{ pc/h/ln}$$

**Step 3 — Convert demand to PCU** (1.5 Marks):

$$V_{pc} = \frac{1500}{0.8333} = 1800 \text{ pc/h/ln}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $v/c$ ratio** (1 Mark):

$$\frac{v}{c} = \frac{1800}{1571.4} = 1.146$$

**Step 5 — Determine LOS** (1.5 Marks):
- $v/c > 1.0$ → flow exceeds capacity → **LOS F** (forced flow, breakdown, congestion).
- This is **not acceptable design**; remedial measures like lane addition, vehicle restriction, or signal coordination are required.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**

> **(a)** Derive the relationship between **space-mean speed and density** using Greenshields' model and obtain the **expression for maximum flow (capacity)**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **six levels of service (A–F)** for a basic freeway segment with reference to density, speed, and the $v/c$ ratio. A 6-lane freeway (3 lanes per direction) has a free-flow speed of 100 km/h and jam density of 120 veh/km/ln. Compute the capacity using Greenshields' model. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Greenshields' Linear Speed-Density Model** (5 Marks):

Assumptions:
- Speed varies **linearly** with density.
- At $k = 0$ (no vehicles), speed = **free-flow speed** $u_f$.
- At $k = k_j$ (jam density), speed = 0.

**Step 1**: Linear relationship:

$$\bar{u}_s = u_f - \left(\frac{u_f}{k_j}\right) k$$

**Step 2**: Fundamental flow:

$$q = k \cdot \bar{u}_s$$

**Step 3**: Substitute:

$$q = u_f k - \frac{u_f}{k_j} k^2$$

**Step 4**: Differentiate w.r.t. $k$:

$$\frac{dq}{dk} = u_f - \frac{2 u_f}{k_j} k = 0 \implies k^* = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

**Step 5**: Substitute back to get $u^*$:

$$u^* = u_f - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

**Step 6**: Maximum flow (capacity) (2 Marks):

$$\boxed{q_{max} = k^* \cdot u^* = \frac{k_j}{2} \cdot \frac{u_f}{2} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Six LOS for a Basic Freeway Segment** (3 Marks):

| LOS | Density (pc/km/ln) | Speed (FFS ≈ 100 km/h) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | ≤ 7 | Near 100 | Free flow, total freedom to maneuver |
| B | 7 – 11 | Slight drop | Stable, slight restriction |
| C | 11 – 16 | ~90 | Stable, restricted maneuverability |
| D | 16 – 22 | ~85 | Approaching unstable flow |
| E | 22 – 28 | ~70 | Unstable, **at capacity** |
| F | > 28 | Highly variable | Forced flow, breakdown |

**Numerical Computation** (4 Marks):

**Given**: $u_f = 100$ km/h, $k_j = 120$ veh/km/ln

**Step 1**: Apply Greenshields capacity formula:

$$C = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \frac{100 \times 120}{4} = 3000 \text{ veh/h/ln}$$

**Step 2**: Total capacity for 3 lanes (one direction):

$$C_{total} = 3 \times 3000 = 9000 \text{ veh/h/direction}$$

**Step 3**: Both directions:

$$C_{both} = 2 \times 9000 = 18000 \text{ veh/h}$$

**Conclusion**: The freeway's theoretical maximum capacity is **3000 veh/h/ln** (or 9000 veh/h/direction). LOS E boundary is reached at this flow.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert veh/h to pc/h** when vehicle mix is non-homogeneous (cost: 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using additive adjustment factors** instead of multiplicative (cost: 2–3 marks).
> 3. **Confusing LOS F with "E"**: Many students think $v/c = 1.0$ is the worst — but $v/c > 1.0$ is **LOS F (forced flow)**.
> 4. **Wrong LOS measure**: Using density for intersections, or delay for freeways (cost: 1–2 marks).
> 5. **Mixing up free-flow speed (FFS) with average travel speed (ATS)** in two-lane highway problems.
> 6. **Not specifying the type of facility** before calculating LOS — examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in final answer — capacity must carry units (veh/h or pc/h/ln).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Factors Affecting Capacity & LOS**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Capacity**: Maximum sustainable flow rate under prevailing conditions (vph or pcph).
- **LOS**: Qualitative grade A to F based on quantitative performance measures.
- **$v/c$ ratio**: Demand-to-capacity ratio; ≤ 0.90 typically acceptable design.

### 🔢 Key Formulas (Memorize)
- Greenshields: $\bar{u}_s = u_f - (u_f/k_j) k$
- Capacity: $C = u_f k_j / 4$
- $f_{HV} = 1 / [1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_B(E_B - 1)]$
- $C = M_{SF} \times f_w \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_p \times f_g \times f_E$

### 📋 Three Categories of Influencing Factors
1. **Roadway** (lane width, lateral clearance, grade, median, surface, design speed, auxiliary lanes)
2. **Traffic** (volume, vehicle mix, directional split, PHF, driver population, speed/density)
3. **Control/Environmental** (signals, signs, access density, parking, weather, lighting)

### 🎯 LOS Performance Measures by Facility
- **Freeways/Multilane** → Density
- **Two-lane** → PTSF & ATS
- **Signalized/Unsignalized Intersections** → Control delay
- **Urban Streets** → Average travel speed

### ⚠️ Critical Pitfalls
- $v/c > 1.0$ ⇒ **LOS F**, not LOS E.
- LOS E is **at capacity** (v/c between 0.90 and 1.00).
- All factors are **multiplicative**, not additive.
- PCU conversion is **mandatory** when vehicle mix varies.

### 🇮🇳 Kerala Context
- **Monsoon rains** significantly reduce capacity ($f_E$).
- **Two-lane NH-66 corridors** in hilly terrain are PTSF-limited.
- **Urban arterials in Kochi/Trivandrum** suffer from access-point density issues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Traffic Conditions

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Homogeneous Traffic:** A traffic stream in which all vehicles have **nearly identical** physical dimensions, performance characteristics, and operating speeds, and travel **without significant interaction** with vehicles of other categories. Such conditions exist primarily in **developed nations** and on **controlled-access highways** (expressways, freeways).

> [!NOTE]
> **Heterogeneous Traffic:** A traffic stream composed of vehicles of **widely varying types and sizes** — motorised and non-motorised, with no strict lane discipline, where vehicles share the same road space with **frequent interaction, lateral movement, and minimal lane discipline**. This is the **characteristic traffic condition of Indian roads** and most developing countries.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway in Germany** at 2 AM where only cars are zooming along — every vehicle looks the same, behaves the same, and follows perfect lane discipline. That is **homogeneous traffic**.

Now imagine **MG Road, Kochi at 5 PM** — you see cars, buses, two-wheelers, auto-rickshaws, cycles, hand carts, and the occasional cow, all squeezing into whatever gap they can find, often straddling lane markings. That is **heterogeneous traffic** — the real Indian scenario.

| Feature | Homogeneous Traffic | Heterogeneous Traffic |
|---|---|---|
| **Vehicle Types** | Almost one type (cars) | Wide variety (5–10+ categories) |
| **Lane Discipline** | Strict | Virtually none |
| **Geographical Prevalence** | USA, Europe, Japan | India, South-East Asia, Africa |
| **Speed Variation** | Low variance | Very high variance |
| **Analytical Modelling** | Simple (single mode) | Complex (multi-mode) |
| **Design Approach** | Capacity-based | PCU-based |

---

## 1.2 Vehicle Types in Indian Traffic Stream

The Indian traffic stream typically consists of the following categories, each with distinctly different **dynamic and static** characteristics:

### A. Motorised Vehicles
- **Two-wheelers (2W):** Motorcycles, scooters — small footprint, high manoeuvrability, frequent lane-splitting behaviour.
- **Three-wheelers (3W):** Auto-rickshaws — intermediate size, often used as shared/public transport.
- **Cars (PC):** Passenger cars, jeeps, vans — the **reference vehicle** for PCU.
- **Buses:** Mini-buses, standard buses, double-decker buses — large footprint, frequent stops.
- **Trucks (HCV):** Light Commercial Vehicles (LCV), Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCV), Multi-Axle Trucks (MAV).
- **Other Motorised:** Tractors, construction equipment, emergency vehicles.

### B. Non-Motorised Vehicles
- **Bicycles:** Major mode in Tier-2/3 cities and rural areas.
- **Animal-drawn vehicles:** Bullock carts, horse carts — slow, wide, unpredictable.
- **Hand carts / Pull carts:** Used for goods transport.
- **Pedestrians:** Critical, especially near intersections, schools, markets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For analytical design, vehicles are broadly classified as **Fast Moving Vehicles (FMV)** and **Slow Moving Vehicles (SMV)**. FMVs include cars, buses, trucks, two-wheelers, and auto-rickshaws. SMVs include bicycles, animal-drawn vehicles, and handcarts.

---

## 1.3 Concept of PCU (Passenger Car Unit)

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Passenger Car Unit (PCU), also called Passenger Car Equivalent (PCE):** The **number of passenger cars** that would occupy the **same space and produce the same operational effect** (delay, speed reduction) as a single subject vehicle under the prevailing roadway and traffic conditions.

By convention, the **passenger car is the reference vehicle** with **PCU = 1.0**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a single **bus** in traffic. It takes up roughly the space of **3 cars** and slows down traffic about as much as **3 cars** would. So, when we count vehicles for design purposes, we don't say "1 bus + 2 cars = 3 vehicles" — instead, we say "1 bus + 2 cars = 3 PCU + 2 PCU = 5 PCU." The bus is **counted as 3 PCUs** because that's how much **roadway real estate and traffic impact** it actually consumes.

This allows engineers to design roads for a **standardised "passenger-car-equivalent" flow** regardless of the actual vehicle mix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PCU is Critical in Indian Context:**
> - Indian roads are **heterogeneous** — direct vehicle counting is meaningless.
> - Capacity of an Indian road is expressed as **PCU/hour**, not vehicles/hour.
> - All IRC design standards (IRC:64, IRC:106, etc.) use PCU-based design.
> - PCU is the **fundamental currency of traffic engineering** in India.

### Standard Reference Metrics

- **Reference Vehicle:** Passenger Car
- **Reference PCU:** **1.0** (by definition, dimensionless)
- **Reference Speed:** **60 km/h** on urban arterials, **80–100 km/h** on highways (for homogeneous comparisons)
- **Reference Area:** Projected rectangular area of a standard car (typically **3.0 m × 1.7 m ≈ 5.1 m²** in Walker method)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static vs Dynamic PCU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:**
> - **Static PCU:** Based purely on **vehicle dimensions** (area occupancy). Fixed values published in IRC codes. Simple, but **inaccurate at varying traffic compositions**.
> - **Dynamic PCU:** Based on **speed and area ratios** in actual traffic stream. Computed for **specific volume and composition** conditions. More accurate for Indian heterogeneous traffic.

---

## 2.2 Methods of Determining PCU

### Method 1: Static PCU (IRC Recommended Values — IRC:64)

The Indian Roads Congress recommends **fixed PCU factors** based on vehicle type, used in routine design practice. The values are tabulated in IRC:64-1990 and updated in subsequent guidelines.

| Vehicle Category | Static PCU Factor |
|---|---|
| **Passenger Car** | **1.0** |
| **Two-wheeler (Motorcycle/Scooter)** | **0.5** |
| **Auto-rickshaw** | **1.0** |
| **Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV)** | **1.5** |
| **Truck / Bus** | **3.0** |
| **Heavy Commercial Vehicle (HCV/MAV)** | **4.5 – 4.7** |
| **Bicycle** | **0.5** |
| **Bullock Cart (with animal)** | **6.0 – 8.0** |
| **Hand Cart (without animal)** | **3.0** |
| **Pedestrian** | **0.5** |

---

### Method 2: Walker's Method (Speed-Area Ratio Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Walker's Method** is the most commonly examined method in KTU. It is based on the principle that PCU depends on the **ratio of speeds** and the **ratio of areas** of the subject vehicle and the reference passenger car.

**Core Formula:**

$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{\dfrac{V_c}{V_i}}{\dfrac{A_c}{A_i}}
$$

Where:
- $\text{PCU}_i$ = Passenger Car Unit of vehicle type *i*
- $V_c$ = Speed of passenger car in mixed traffic stream (m/s or km/h)
- $V_i$ = Speed of vehicle type *i* in mixed traffic stream (m/s or km/h)
- $A_c$ = Projected rectangular area of passenger car (m²)
- $A_i$ = Projected rectangular area of vehicle type *i* (m²)

**Why this formula works:**

The PCU of a vehicle is **directly proportional to the area it occupies** (more area = more PCU) and **inversely proportional to its speed** (slower vehicles cause more delay = more PCU).

- A **slow, large** vehicle (e.g., bullock cart) has a **low V_i and high A_i**, giving a **very high PCU** (numerator small, denominator small).
- A **fast, small** vehicle (e.g., two-wheeler) has a **high V_i and low A_i**, giving a **low PCU**.

---

### Method 3: Chandra's Stream Equivalency Method (Most Rigorous for India)

Chandra, Kumar, and Rastogi (1995) developed this method for **Indian heterogeneous traffic** considering actual stream speeds.

**Key Concept:** The PCU of vehicle *i* at a given traffic volume *q*:

$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{V_c}{V_i} \times \frac{A_c}{A_i} \times \frac{1}{\text{(correction for composition)}}
$$

A more refined form considers **density ratios**:

$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{k_i}{k_c}
$$

Where:
- $k_i$ = Density of vehicle *i* in mixed stream (veh/km/lane)
- $k_c$ = Density of passenger car in mixed stream

Equivalently:

$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{V_c}{V_i} \times \frac{A_c}{A_i}
$$

when stream densities are computed using rectangular projection areas.

---

### Method 4: Highway Capacity Manual (HCM) Method

Used in homogeneous traffic conditions. PCU = 1.0 for cars on freeways; PCU = 2.0 for trucks on level terrain (this is the **equivalent factor**, slightly different from PCU). Mainly applicable in **developed nations**.

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting PCU

| Factor | Effect on PCU |
|---|---|
| **Vehicle Size** | Larger vehicle → Higher PCU |
| **Vehicle Speed** | Slower vehicle → Higher PCU |
| **Road Width** | Narrower road → Higher PCU (more interaction) |
| **Gradient** | Uphill → Higher PCU for trucks; Downhill → Lower PCU |
| **Traffic Composition** | Higher % of slow vehicles → Inflated PCU for all |
| **Driver Behaviour** | Aggressive → Lower PCU (faster speeds) |
| **Type of Facility** | Urban → Higher PCU; Freeway → Lower PCU |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $\text{PCU}_i = \dfrac{A_i}{A_c} \times \dfrac{V_c}{V_i}$ | Walker's Method (Speed-Area) |
| **F2** | $\text{PCU}_i = \dfrac{k_i}{k_c}$ | Density Ratio Method |
| **F3** | $q_{\text{PCU}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (q_i \times \text{PCU}_i)$ | Total equivalent flow in PCU/hr |
| **F4** | $\text{PCU}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum (q_i \times \text{PCU}_i)}{\sum q_i}$ | Average weighted PCU of stream |
| **F5** | $A = L \times W$ | Projected area of vehicle (L = length, W = width) |
| **F6** | $\rho = \dfrac{q}{v}$ | Density = Flow / Speed (veh/km) |
| **F7** | $\text{V/C Ratio} = \dfrac{q_{\text{PCU}}}{C_{\text{PCU}}}$ | Degree of saturation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE ON F3:** The total PCU flow is **not** the sum of vehicles; it is the **weighted sum** using PCU values as weights. This is the single most important equation used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

- **Road Design Capacity:** Expressways in India are designed for **2,000 PCU/hour/lane** (single lane) capacity.
- **Signal Design (Webster's Method):** Saturation flow is expressed in **PCU/hour** (typically 1,800–1,950 PCU/hr/lane for urban Indian roads).
- **LOS (Level of Service) Thresholds:** IRC uses PCU/hr for all LOS thresholds.
- **Pavement Design:** Although empirical, PCU-based design is the standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Walker's PCU Formula

### Starting Concept
Walker assumed that the **PCU of a vehicle is proportional to the space it occupies** and **inversely proportional to the relative speed** it forces on the traffic stream.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider two vehicle types: car (c) and subject vehicle (i). The car is the reference.

**Step 2:** PCU is proportional to area occupied (more area = more impact):
$$
\text{PCU}_i \propto A_i
$$

**Step 3:** PCU is inversely proportional to speed (slower vehicles cause more delay):
$$
\text{PCU}_i \propto \frac{1}{V_i}
$$

**Step 4:** Combining these proportionalities and using the car as reference:
$$
\frac{\text{PCU}_i}{\text{PCU}_c} = \frac{A_i}{A_c} \times \frac{V_c}{V_i}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $\text{PCU}_c = 1.0$ (car is reference):
$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{A_i}{A_c} \times \frac{V_c}{V_i}
$$

**Step 6:** Rewriting in the standard form (commonly seen in textbooks):
$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{V_c \cdot A_i}{V_i \cdot A_c}
$$

Or equivalently:
$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{\dfrac{V_c}{V_i}}{\dfrac{A_c}{A_i}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Intuition Check:** A bullock cart is **slow** ($V_i$ small) and **large** ($A_i$ large). Therefore, the numerator $V_c / V_i$ becomes **very large**, and the denominator $A_c / A_i$ becomes **very small**, making the PCU very high (6–8). This matches reality.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: PCU of a Two-Wheeler using Walker's Method

**Problem Statement:**
In a mixed traffic stream on an urban road, the following observations were made:
- Passenger car: speed = 40 km/h, projected area = 2 m × 1.6 m = 3.2 m²
- Two-wheeler: speed = 35 km/h, projected area = 1.8 m × 0.7 m = 1.26 m²

Determine the PCU of the two-wheeler.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- $V_c = 40 \text{ km/h}$, $A_c = 2 \times 1.6 = 3.2 \text{ m}^2$
- $V_i = 35 \text{ km/h}$ (two-wheeler), $A_i = 1.8 \times 0.7 = 1.26 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 2:** Apply Walker's formula.
$$
\text{PCU}_{2W} = \frac{V_c}{V_i} \times \frac{A_i}{A_c}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.
$$
\text{PCU}_{2W} = \frac{40}{35} \times \frac{1.26}{3.2}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate speed ratio.
$$
\frac{V_c}{V_i} = \frac{40}{35} = 1.143
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate area ratio.
$$
\frac{A_i}{A_c} = \frac{1.26}{3.2} = 0.394
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply ratios.
$$
\text{PCU}_{2W} = 1.143 \times 0.394 = 0.450
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{PCU}_{\text{Two-Wheeler}} \approx 0.45 \text{ to } 0.50}
$$

**Comparison with IRC Static Value:** 0.50. Excellent agreement.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: PCU of a Bus using Walker's Method

**Problem Statement:**
On a two-lane highway, the following field data is collected under mixed traffic conditions:
- Car: $V_c = 50 \text{ km/h}$, $A_c = 4.0 \text{ m}^2$
- Bus: $V_b = 30 \text{ km/h}$, $A_b = 10.0 \text{ m}^2$

Find the PCU of the bus.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply Walker's formula.
$$
\text{PCU}_{\text{Bus}} = \frac{V_c}{V_b} \times \frac{A_b}{A_c}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.
$$
\text{PCU}_{\text{Bus}} = \frac{50}{30} \times \frac{10.0}{4.0}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate each ratio.
$$
\frac{V_c}{V_b} = \frac{50}{30} = 1.667
$$

$$
\frac{A_b}{A_c} = \frac{10.0}{4.0} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.
$$
\text{PCU}_{\text{Bus}} = 1.667 \times 2.5 = 4.167
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{PCU}_{\text{Bus}} \approx 4.17}
$$

**Interpretation:** The bus occupies space equivalent to **4.17 passenger cars** in mixed traffic. This is higher than the static IRC value of 3.0 because the bus is significantly slower and larger than the car.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: PCU of a Bullock Cart using Walker's Method

**Problem Statement:**
Field data on a rural road:
- Car: $V_c = 45 \text{ km/h}$, $A_c = 3.0 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m} = 4.5 \text{ m}^2$
- Bullock Cart: $V_{bc} = 8 \text{ km/h}$, $A_{bc} = 4.0 \text{ m} \times 1.8 \text{ m} = 7.2 \text{ m}^2$

Compute the PCU of the bullock cart.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute ratios.
$$
\frac{V_c}{V_{bc}} = \frac{45}{8} = 5.625
$$

$$
\frac{A_{bc}}{A_c} = \frac{7.2}{4.5} = 1.6
$$

**Step 2:** Apply formula.
$$
\text{PCU}_{bc} = 5.625 \times 1.6 = 9.0
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{PCU}_{\text{Bullock Cart}} = 9.0}
$$

**Comparison:** IRC static value = 6.0 to 8.0. Walker's dynamic value (9.0) is realistic for a slow, large vehicle in mixed traffic.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Total PCU Flow on a Mixed Traffic Stream

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-lane divided urban arterial (2 lanes per direction) carries the following traffic in 1 hour (one direction):

| Vehicle | Count (veh/h) | PCU (Static) |
|---|---|---|
| Two-wheeler | 1500 | 0.5 |
| Car | 800 | 1.0 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 300 | 1.0 |
| Bus | 80 | 3.0 |
| Truck | 50 | 3.0 |
| Bicycle | 200 | 0.5 |
| Bullock Cart | 10 | 6.0 |

Determine:
1. Total PCU flow
2. Total vehicle flow
3. Average weighted PCU
4. V/C ratio if capacity = 3000 PCU/hr/lane and 2 lanes per direction

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute PCU contribution from each vehicle type.
$$
q_{PCU,i} = q_i \times \text{PCU}_i
$$

| Vehicle | $q_i$ (veh/h) | $\text{PCU}_i$ | $q_{PCU,i}$ (PCU/h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-wheeler | 1500 | 0.5 | 750 |
| Car | 800 | 1.0 | 800 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 300 | 1.0 | 300 |
| Bus | 80 | 3.0 | 240 |
| Truck | 50 | 3.0 | 150 |
| Bicycle | 200 | 0.5 | 100 |
| Bullock Cart | 10 | 6.0 | 60 |
| **Total** | **2940** | — | **2400** |

**Step 2:** Total vehicle flow.
$$
\boxed{q_{\text{veh}} = 2940 \text{ veh/h}}
$$

**Step 3:** Total PCU flow.
$$
\boxed{q_{\text{PCU}} = 2400 \text{ PCU/h}}
$$

**Step 4:** Average weighted PCU.
$$
\text{PCU}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum (q_i \cdot \text{PCU}_i)}{\sum q_i} = \frac{2400}{2940} = 0.816
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{PCU}_{\text{avg}} = 0.82}
$$

This means each vehicle, on average, is equivalent to **0.82 passenger cars** — a high proportion of two-wheelers reduces the average.

**Step 5:** Capacity and V/C ratio.
- Capacity = 3000 PCU/h/lane × 2 lanes = 6000 PCU/h
- V/C ratio = 2400 / 6000 = 0.40

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{V/C} = 0.40 \text{ (Level of Service A)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** Despite **2,940 vehicles/hour** (a huge number), the **PCU flow is only 2,400 PCU/h** because two-wheelers and bicycles dominate (each counts as 0.5). If we replaced all 1,500 two-wheelers with cars, the PCU flow would **triple** and the road would fail.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Vehicle Speed on PCU

The following table demonstrates how **PCU varies with speed and area** for a hypothetical subject vehicle (fixed area $A_i = 6 \text{ m}^2$, car $V_c = 50 \text{ km/h}$, $A_c = 4 \text{ m}^2$):

| Case | $V_i$ (km/h) | $V_c / V_i$ | $A_i / A_c$ | PCU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 1.00 | 1.50 | 1.50 |
| 2 | 40 | 1.25 | 1.50 | 1.88 |
| 3 | 30 | 1.67 | 1.50 | 2.50 |
| 4 | 20 | 2.50 | 1.50 | 3.75 |
| 5 | 10 | 5.00 | 1.50 | 7.50 |
| 6 | 5 | 10.00 | 1.50 | 15.00 |

**Inference:** As $V_i$ decreases (slower vehicle), PCU **increases sharply** — for the same area, a stationary vehicle has **infinite PCU** mathematically. This is why peak-hour traffic with slow-moving bullock carts is so inefficient.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Direct Conceptual)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Passenger Car Unit (PCU). Why is it necessary in Indian traffic engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **PCU (Passenger Car Unit)** is the number of equivalent passenger cars displaced by a single subject vehicle of a different type, considering its space occupancy and operational impact on the traffic stream under prevailing roadway and traffic conditions.

**Why necessary in India:**
- Indian traffic is **heterogeneous** — vehicles of widely varying sizes, speeds, and dynamic characteristics share road space.
- Simple vehicle counts are **meaningless** for design because a bullock cart and a two-wheeler cannot be treated identically.
- PCU **normalises** all vehicle types into a **single reference unit (the passenger car)**, enabling uniform capacity, LOS, and signal design standards.
- All **IRC design codes (IRC:64, IRC:106)** use PCU-based parameters, making it the standard currency of Indian traffic engineering.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** Heterogeneous traffic, reference vehicle, normalisation, IRC standards, capacity.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Homogeneous and Heterogeneous traffic conditions. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Homogeneous Traffic | Heterogeneous Traffic |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Single vehicle type | Multiple vehicle types |
| **Lane Discipline** | Strict | Absent |
| **Examples** | German Autobahn at night, US Interstate, Expressways with access control | Indian urban roads, rural arterials, MG Road, market streets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-line crisp answer:** Homogeneous traffic = single-mode, lane-disciplined (e.g., cars only on expressways). Heterogeneous traffic = mixed-mode, no lane discipline (e.g., Indian city roads with cars, buses, two-wheelers, and bullock carts).

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern — Choose ONE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain Walker's method of determining PCU. Derive the expression and discuss its limitations.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** On a four-lane divided urban arterial road, the following traffic counts were recorded during the peak hour in one direction:

| Vehicle Type | Volume (veh/h) | Speed (km/h) | Length × Width (m × m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-wheeler | 1800 | 40 | 1.8 × 0.7 |
| Car | 1200 | 50 | 4.0 × 1.6 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 400 | 35 | 3.0 × 1.4 |
| Bus | 100 | 30 | 10.0 × 2.5 |
| Truck | 60 | 25 | 7.5 × 2.4 |
| Bicycle | 250 | 15 | 1.8 × 0.6 |
| Bullock Cart | 15 | 8 | 4.0 × 1.8 |

Using Walker's method, determine:
1. PCU of each vehicle type.
2. Total PCU flow per hour.
3. Total vehicle flow per hour.
4. Average weighted PCU of the stream.
5. V/C ratio, if the capacity of the road is **6000 PCU/h** in one direction.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a): Walker's Method — Derivation and Limitations [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
Walker's method determines the PCU of a vehicle as a function of its **projected area** and **operating speed** in mixed traffic.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Walker postulated:
- PCU is **directly proportional** to the area occupied: $\text{PCU}_i \propto A_i$
- PCU is **inversely proportional** to the operating speed: $\text{PCU}_i \propto 1/V_i$

Comparing subject vehicle *i* with reference car *c*:

$$
\frac{\text{PCU}_i}{\text{PCU}_c} = \frac{A_i}{A_c} \cdot \frac{V_c}{V_i}
$$

Since $\text{PCU}_c = 1.0$:

$$
\text{PCU}_i = \frac{A_i}{A_c} \cdot \frac{V_c}{V_i}
$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Assumes **rectangular projection** of vehicles, ignoring shape effects (e.g., motorcycles with diagonal parking).
2. Speed measurement is **point-based**; doesn't account for **acceleration/deceleration** behaviour in interrupted flow.
3. Does not capture **lateral clearance** or **side friction** effects.
4. Assumes **fixed speed ratio** for given conditions; in reality, speeds vary with **volume and composition**.
5. Projected area depends on **parking angle** of two-wheelers, which is highly variable in Indian traffic.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute areas and PCU for each vehicle type using Walker's formula.**

Given: $V_c = 50 \text{ km/h}$, $A_c = 4.0 \times 1.6 = 6.4 \text{ m}^2$.

**Two-wheeler:**
- $A_i = 1.8 \times 0.7 = 1.26 \text{ m}^2$, $V_i = 40 \text{ km/h}$
- $\text{PCU}_{2W} = \dfrac{50}{40} \times \dfrac{1.26}{6.4} = 1.25 \times 0.197 = 0.246$

**Car:** $\text{PCU} = 1.0$ (reference)

**Auto-rickshaw:**
- $A_i = 3.0 \times 1.4 = 4.2 \text{ m}^2$, $V_i = 35 \text{ km/h}$
- $\text{PCU}_{3W} = \dfrac{50}{35} \times \dfrac{4.2}{6.4} = 1.429 \times 0.656 = 0.937$

**Bus:**
- $A_i = 10.0 \times 2.5 = 25.0 \text{ m}^2$, $V_i = 30 \text{ km/h}$
- $\text{PCU}_{Bus} = \dfrac{50}{30} \times \dfrac{25.0}{6.4} = 1.667 \times 3.906 = 6.51$

**Truck:**
- $A_i = 7.5 \times 2.4 = 18.0 \text{ m}^2$, $V_i = 25 \text{ km/h}$
- $\text{PCU}_{Truck} = \dfrac{50}{25} \times \dfrac{18.0}{6.4} = 2.0 \times 2.813 = 5.63$

**Bicycle:**
- $A_i = 1.8 \times 0.6 = 1.08 \text{ m}^2$, $V_i = 15 \text{ km/h}$
- $\text{PCU}_{Bicycle} = \dfrac{50}{15} \times \dfrac{1.08}{6.4} = 3.333 \times 0.169 = 0.563$

**Bullock Cart:**
- $A_i = 4.0 \times 1.8 = 7.2 \text{ m}^2$, $V_i = 8 \text{ km/h}$
- $\text{PCU}_{BC} = \dfrac{50}{8} \times \dfrac{7.2}{6.4} = 6.25 \times 1.125 = 7.03$

**Step 2: Compute PCU flow = $q_i \times \text{PCU}_i$**

| Vehicle | $q_i$ (veh/h) | $\text{PCU}_i$ | $q_i \times \text{PCU}_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-wheeler | 1800 | 0.246 | 442.8 |
| Car | 1200 | 1.000 | 1200.0 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 400 | 0.937 | 374.8 |
| Bus | 100 | 6.51 | 651.0 |
| Truck | 60 | 5.63 | 337.8 |
| Bicycle | 250 | 0.563 | 140.8 |
| Bullock Cart | 15 | 7.03 | 105.5 |
| **Total** | **3825** | — | **3252.7** |

**Step 3: Compute requested quantities.**

1. **PCU of each vehicle** — listed above.
2. **Total PCU flow:** $\boxed{3253 \text{ PCU/h}}$
3. **Total vehicle flow:** $\boxed{3825 \text{ veh/h}}$
4. **Average weighted PCU:** $\dfrac{3252.7}{3825} = 0.85$
5. **V/C ratio:** $\dfrac{3252.7}{6000} = 0.542$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{q_{PCU} = 3253 \text{ PCU/h}, \quad q_{veh} = 3825 \text{ veh/h}, \quad \text{PCU}_{avg} = 0.85, \quad \text{V/C} = 0.54 \text{ (LOS A/B)}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for collecting traffic volume data in heterogeneous traffic conditions. What are the difficulties encountered in such surveys?

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A two-lane rural highway in Kerala has the following peak hour traffic composition. The carriageway width is 7.0 m. Compute the total PCU flow using static IRC factors and compare with Walker's method, given that field speeds observed for the car and bus are 45 km/h and 30 km/h respectively, and projected area of the car is 5.0 m² and the bus is 14.0 m².

| Vehicle | Volume (veh/h) | Length (m) | Width (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Car | 600 | 4.0 | 1.6 |
| Two-wheeler | 900 | 1.8 | 0.7 |
| Bus | 50 | 7.5 | 2.5 |
| Truck | 40 | 6.0 | 2.2 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 150 | 3.0 | 1.4 |

**Hint for Part (a):** Discuss video-graphic method, manual count challenges (classification errors, side-by-side movement, parked vehicles, observers' fatigue), and modern methods (loop detectors, video image processing).

**Hint for Part (b):**
- **Static PCU method:** Use fixed values (Car = 1.0, 2W = 0.5, Bus = 3.0, Truck = 3.0, Auto = 1.0).
- **Walker's method for Bus:** $\text{PCU} = \dfrac{45}{30} \times \dfrac{14.0}{5.0} = 1.5 \times 2.8 = 4.2$.
- Compute weighted PCU flow and compare.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units of area:** Students often write "$A_c = 1.6 \text{ m}$" instead of "$A_c = 4.0 \times 1.6 = 6.4 \text{ m}^2$". **Always** compute $A = L \times W$ and write the unit.
>
> 2. **Mixing up speed ratios:** Walker's formula is $V_c / V_i$, **not** $V_i / V_c$. A common error gives inverted PCU values (e.g., PCU of bus becomes 0.7 instead of 4+).
>
> 3. **Not including bullock carts and bicycles:** Many students ignore non-motorised vehicles in their PCU calculations, which is unrealistic for Indian conditions and loses full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing PCU with PCE (Passenger Car Equivalent):** Some texts use these interchangeably; in KTU exams, treat them as synonyms but mention both terms.
>
> 5. **Static PCU values must be quoted with reference:** Always mention **"as per IRC:64"** when using static values to demonstrate familiarity with standards.
>
> 6. **No significant figures in final answer:** Always round to **2 decimal places** (e.g., PCU = 4.17, not 4.166666...).
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert units:** $V_c$ and $V_i$ **must be in the same unit** (km/h or m/s). Mixing them is a common mistake.
>
> 8. **Ignoring limits of Walker's method:** The formula assumes **steady-state stream conditions**. Markers expect a brief comment on its limitations in your solution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Homogeneous/Heterogeneous Traffic & PCU):**
>
> - **Homogeneous traffic** = single-mode, lane-disciplined, common in developed countries on freeways.
> - **Heterogeneous traffic** = multi-mode, no lane discipline, characteristic of Indian roads. **India is a textbook example of heterogeneous traffic.**
> - **PCU (Passenger Car Unit)** = a dimensionless equivalence factor that normalises all vehicle types to a passenger car reference.
> - **Reference vehicle:** Passenger car with $\text{PCU} = 1.0$ (always, by definition).
> - **Static PCU** (IRC:64): Car = 1.0, 2W = 0.5, Auto = 1.0, LCV = 1.5, Bus = 3.0, HCV = 4.5, Bicycle = 0.5, Bullock Cart = 6–8.
> - **Walker's formula:** $\text{PCU}_i = \dfrac{V_c}{V_i} \times \dfrac{A_i}{A_c}$.
> - **Projected area** is always $A = L \times W$ for the **rectangular bounding box** of the vehicle.
> - **Slower + Larger vehicle → Higher PCU** (Bullock cart has PCU ≈ 6–9; two-wheeler has PCU ≈ 0.25–0.5).
> - **Total PCU flow:** $q_{PCU} = \sum (q_i \times \text{PCU}_i)$, **NOT** $\sum q_i$.
> - **Average weighted PCU** of a stream = total PCU flow / total vehicle flow (always $< 1$ if two-wheelers and bicycles dominate).
> - **V/C ratio** uses PCU-based flow: $\text{V/C} = q_{PCU} / C_{PCU}$.
> - **Limitations of Walker's method:** assumes steady-state, rectangular projection, ignores acceleration/deceleration, lane discipline, and lateral clearance.
> - **Dynamic PCU (Chandra et al.)** is more accurate for Indian heterogeneous traffic but computationally intensive.
> - **In KTU exams, ALWAYS:** quote the formula, define variables, show intermediate steps, maintain consistent units, and provide a concluding engineering interpretation (e.g., "LOS A", "V/C = 0.5").
> - **PCU is the fundamental unit of** capacity design, LOS analysis, signal timing (Webster's), and pavement design in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and LOS Analysis - Single Lane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity and LOS Analysis - Single Lane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capacity** of a single-lane facility is defined (per the *Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)*, the de-facto reference for KTU's PECET637 syllabus) as the **maximum sustained hourly flow rate** at which vehicles can reasonably be expected to traverse a uniform roadway segment under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions, expressed in **passenger cars per hour per lane (pc/h/ln)**.

**Level of Service (LOS)** is a **qualitative measure** describing operational conditions within a traffic stream, characterized by parameters such as **speed, travel time, freedom to maneuver, traffic interruptions, comfort, and convenience**. For uninterrupted-flow single-lane facilities (basic freeway segments, multilane highways analyzed lane-by-lane, two-lane highways in one direction), LOS is **primarily a function of density (pc/km/ln)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 mandates study of Capacity and LOS for uninterrupted flow facilities. For a **single lane**, density is the primary LOS determinant because it uniquely reflects both speed and flow (since $k = q/u$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Single-File Water Tap**
> Imagine a single pipe carrying water. When the tap is barely open (low flow), water molecules move fast and freely — like LOS A. As you open the tap more, molecules bunch up (density rises) and their individual speed drops — like LOS C or D. The moment the pipe is completely full and molecules can no longer accelerate (jam density), the flow chokes — this is **capacity**. Any further request for flow creates a **shockwave (breakdown)** — this is **LOS F (forced flow)**.
>
> **Translation to traffic:** A single lane of traffic behaves identically. You cannot push more vehicles than the lane physically accommodates without forcing stop-and-go conditions. The point at which flow peaks is **capacity**, and operating conditions below that are graded A through E based on how "crowded" the lane feels, which is precisely what **density** quantifies.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Base Capacity (single lane, freeway) | **2400** | pc/h/ln |
| Jam Density ($k_j$) | **~133** | pc/km/ln (theoretical) |
| Critical Density ($k_o$) | **~33.75** | pc/km/ln (under Greenshields) |
| Optimum Speed ($u_o$) | **~48** | km/h (under Greenshields) |
| Free-Flow Speed (FFS), default | **120** | km/h |
| Time Headway at capacity | **~1.5** | s |
| Space Headway at jam density | **~7.5** | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Stream Variables (Greenshields Framework)

For a single-lane uninterrupted flow, three macroscopic variables govern the entire analysis:

- **Speed ($u$)** in km/h — time-mean or space-mean speed
- **Flow ($q$)** in pc/h — vehicles passing a point per hour
- **Density ($k$)** in pc/km — vehicles occupying a kilometre of lane

The **fundamental relation** is:

$$q = u \cdot k$$
