This single equation, when plotted in 3D, defines the *fundamental diagram of traffic flow*. For a single lane, the maximum value of $q$ is **capacity**, and the maximum value of $k$ (when $u \to 0$) is **jam density**.

## 2.2 Why Density is Chosen as the LOS Criterion (Single Lane)

For **interrupted-flow** facilities (signals), LOS uses **control delay**. For **uninterrupted-flow single-lane** facilities (freeways, multilane highways), the HCM selects **density** because:

1. Density correlates linearly with both speed and flow.
2. Density intuitively represents the *proximity* of vehicles (driver's perceived freedom).
3. Density is a **state variable** (point-independent) — unlike speed or flow which are time-space dependent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Density-based LOS is used by **transport planners, highway designers, and traffic engineers** worldwide to: (a) geometrically design lane additions, (b) evaluate ramp metering, (c) certify LOS for Environmental Impact Assessments, and (d) perform Highway Performance Monitoring.

## 2.3 Free-Flow Speed (FFS) — The Master Variable

FFS is the speed at which drivers travel when flow is low (typically $q \leq 1400$ pc/h/ln). It depends on:

- Lane width
- Lateral clearance
- Access point density
- Driver population factor

The base FFS is adjusted as:

$$FFS = BFFS - f_{LW} - f_{LC} - f_{N} - f_{ID}$$

where:
- $BFFS$ = base free-flow speed (km/h)
- $f_{LW}$ = adjustment for lane width
- $f_{LC}$ = adjustment for lateral clearance
- $f_{N}$ = adjustment for number of lanes
- $f_{ID}$ = adjustment for interchange/access-point density

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = u \cdot k$ | Fundamental relation | q in pc/h, u in km/h, k in pc/km |
| 2 | $u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$ | Greenshields speed-density model | Linear; valid $0 \le k \le k_j$ |
| 3 | $q = u_f \left(k - \dfrac{k^2}{k_j}\right)$ | Greenshields flow-density model | Parabolic |
| 4 | $q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | Capacity (Greenshields) | Occurs at $k = k_j/2$ |
| 5 | $u_o = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | Optimum speed (Greenshields) | At capacity |
| 6 | $k_j = \dfrac{1000}{L_v}$ | Jam density | $L_v$ = vehicle length + headway, in m |
| 7 | $u_s = \dfrac{L}{\Delta t}$ | Space-mean speed | $L$ = segment length, $\Delta t$ = mean travel time |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{q}{u_s}$ | Density from speed & flow | Derived from (1) |
| 9 | $SFFS = BFFS - f_{LW} - f_{LC} - f_N - f_{ID}$ | Adjusted FFS | km/h |
| 10 | $MSF_i = c_j \cdot \dfrac{FFS_i}{FFS_E}$ | Max service flow rate at LOS $i$ | pc/h/ln |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **units** in the final answer. A flow value of "2400" is **incomplete** — write "**2400 pc/h/ln**" to score full marks. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units consistently.

## 2.5 Why Greenshields' Linear Model is Universally Taught

Despite newer models (Greenberg logarithmic, Underwood exponential, Pipes-Munjal, Van Aerde), the **Greenshields model (1935)** remains the KTU-board standard because:

- It is **closed-form** and yields directly the three critical points: $u_f, k_j, q_{max}$.
- It requires only two parameters, easily calibrated from field data.
- It is **symmetric** — the optimum lies exactly at the midpoints of $u$ and $k$ ranges.
- The parabolic $q$-$k$ curve is intuitively visual.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Greenshields Linear Speed-Density Model

**Assumption:** The relationship between speed and density is **linear**, with the line passing through two physically meaningful points: $(k=0, u=u_f)$ and $(k=k_j, u=0)$.

### Step 1: Equation of a straight line through two points
Using slope-intercept form:

$$u = u_f + m \cdot k$$

### Step 2: Compute slope $m$
Slope between $(0, u_f)$ and $(k_j, 0)$:

$$m = \frac{0 - u_f}{k_j - 0} = -\frac{u_f}{k_j}$$

### Step 3: Substitute slope back
Substituting $m$ into the line equation:

$$\boxed{u = u_f - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k = u_f \left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right)}$$

This is the **Greenshields Speed-Density Equation**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Flow-Density (and Flow-Speed) Curves

### Step 1: Apply the fundamental relation $q = u \cdot k$
Multiply the speed-density equation by $k$:

$$q = u_f \left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right) \cdot k$$

### Step 2: Expand
$$\boxed{q = u_f \cdot k - \frac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k^2}$$

This is a **downward-opening parabola** in $k$.

### Step 3: Find capacity (maximum flow) by $dq/dk = 0$

$$\frac{dq}{dk} = u_f - \frac{2 u_f}{k_j} \cdot k = 0$$

Solving:

$$k_o = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

This is the **critical density** (density at capacity).

### Step 4: Find optimum speed by substituting $k_o$ into Greenshields

$$u_o = u_f \left(1 - \frac{k_j/2}{k_j}\right) = u_f \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

### Step 5: Find maximum flow (capacity)

$$q_{max} = u_o \cdot k_o = \frac{u_f}{2} \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed at Maximum Flow (Capacity Speed)

Substituting $u_o$ into the Greenshields expression:

$$u_o = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

If $u_f = 96$ km/h, then $u_o = 48$ km/h at capacity. This counterintuitive result shows that **maximum flow does NOT occur at maximum speed** — it occurs at **half the free-flow speed**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Density from Field Data

Field measurements give us:
- $q$ (vehicles/hour) at a point using a pneumatic tube or manual count
- $\bar{u}_t$ (time-mean speed) using a radar gun

We convert to space-mean speed using:

$$u_s = \frac{L}{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}$$

Then density:

$$k = \frac{q}{u_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students frequently confuse **time-mean speed** $\bar{u}_t$ with **space-mean speed** $u_s$. Use $u_s$ (not $\bar{u}_t$) in $k = q/u$ to avoid a unit/sign error that costs full marks.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A single-lane road has $u_f = 80$ km/h and $k_j = 140$ pc/km. A driver is traveling at $u = 50$ km/h. Find: (a) density, (b) flow, (c) capacity, (d) the speed at capacity, (e) critical density. (Module 3 — 14 Mark Pattern)

### (a) Density

From Greenshields:

$$k = k_j \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_f}\right) = 140 \left(1 - \frac{50}{80}\right)$$

$$k = 140 \cdot \frac{30}{80} = 140 \cdot 0.375$$

$$k = 52.5 \text{ pc/km}$$

### (b) Flow

$$q = u \cdot k = 50 \cdot 52.5 = 2625 \text{ pc/h}$$

### (c) Capacity

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \frac{80 \cdot 140}{4} = \frac{11200}{4}$$

$$q_{max} = 2800 \text{ pc/h}$$

### (d) Speed at Capacity

$$u_o = \frac{u_f}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ km/h}$$

### (e) Critical Density

$$k_o = \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{140}{2} = 70 \text{ pc/km}$$

**Verification at capacity:** $q_{max} = u_o \cdot k_o = 40 \cdot 70 = 2800$ pc/h ✓

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table

| Operating Point | $k$ (pc/km) | $u$ (km/h) | $q$ (pc/h) | Traffic State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free flow | $\to 0$ | $u_f$ | $\to 0$ | LOS A |
| At capacity | $k_j/2$ | $u_f/2$ | $u_f k_j/4$ (max) | LOS E boundary |
| Jam density | $k_j$ | $0$ | $0$ | Breakdown (LOS F) |
| Mid-range | $k_j/4$ | $3u_f/4$ | $3 u_f k_j/16$ | LOS B/C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fundamental Diagram of Traffic Flow (Greenshields)

The following Mermaid block renders the complete inter-relationship between speed, flow, and density as a structured functional block diagram showing how each variable feeds into capacity analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Free Flow Speed uf km per h"] --> B["Greenshields Model"]
    C["Jam Density kj pc per km"] --> B
    B --> D["Speed Density Line u = uf 1 - k / kj"]
    B --> E["Flow Density Parabola q = uk"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["dq by dk equals 0"]
    F --> G["Critical Density ko = kj divided by 2"]
    F --> H["Optimum Speed uo = uf divided by 2"]
    F --> I["Capacity qmax = uf times kj divided by 4"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["LOS E Boundary"]
    D --> K["LOS A to E Operational Zone"]
    K --> L["Density k = q divided by us"]
    E --> L
    L --> M["Assign LOS A B C D E F"]
```

## 4.2 LOS Determination Flowchart for Single-Lane Uninterrupted Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Compute FFS using SFFS equation"] --> S2["Step 2: Measure or compute flow q pc per h"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Measure space mean speed us km per h"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute density k = q divided by us"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compare k with LOS thresholds table"]
    S5 --> S6{"Which FFS band 70 80 90 100 110 120"}
    S6 --> S7["Apply corresponding density breakpoints"]
    S7 --> S8["Assign LOS A B C D E or F"]
    S8 --> S9["Report LOS with density value"]
```

## 4.3 Capacity Operating Point Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["k = 0, u = uf, q = 0"] -->|increase k| P2["k = ko, u = uo, q = qmax CAPACITY"]
    P2 -->|increase k| P3["k = kj, u = 0, q = 0 JAM"]
    P1 -. LOS A .-> P2
    P2 -. LOS E limit .-> P3
    P3 -. LOS F breakdown .-> P1
```

## 4.4 HCM LOS Density Thresholds Block Map (For FFS = 100 km/h baseline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0["Density k pc per km per ln"] --> L1["LOS A: k is less than or equal to 7"]
    D0 --> L2["LOS B: 7 is less than k is less than or equal to 11"]
    D0 --> L3["LOS C: 11 is less than k is less than or equal to 16"]
    D0 --> L4["LOS D: 16 is less than k is less than or equal to 22"]
    D0 --> L5["LOS E: 22 is less than k is less than or equal to 28"]
    D0 --> L6["LOS F: k is greater than 28 forced flow"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** The LOS density breakpoints *vary with FFS*. The above values are typical for FFS in the 100–120 km/h range. For FFS ≤ 90 km/h, the breakpoints shift to slightly higher densities (e.g., LOS A: ≤ 8 pc/km/ln for FFS = 90 km/h). Always verify the threshold table against the FFS computed in Step 1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define capacity of a single-lane roadway as per HCM. State its base value for a basic freeway segment.**

**Model Answer:**
Capacity is the maximum sustained hourly flow rate at which vehicles can reasonably be expected to traverse a uniform roadway segment under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions. It is expressed in **passenger cars per hour per lane (pc/h/ln)**. The base capacity of a single lane on a basic freeway segment is **2400 pc/h/ln** (assuming a free-flow speed of 120 km/h). Capacity corresponds to the boundary between LOS E and LOS F.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is density chosen as the primary LOS criterion for uninterrupted-flow single-lane facilities, instead of speed or flow?**

**Model Answer:**
Density (in pc/km/ln) is chosen because:
1. It is a **state variable** — uniquely defined at a point without temporal averaging ambiguities that affect speed.
2. It correlates **simultaneously** with both speed (negatively) and flow (positively up to capacity), capturing the driver's perception of freedom and proximity.
3. It directly determines **safety and maneuverability** — high density restricts lane-changing and emergency operations.
4. Unlike flow, density is **non-zero at zero speed** (jam density), correctly characterizing the breakdown regime (LOS F).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Always show the **unit** in every numerical answer. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per answer.
> - Do not confuse **time-mean speed** ($\bar{u}_t$) with **space-mean speed** ($u_s$). Use $u_s$ in $k = q/u$.
> - When asked for "capacity," specify *which* FFS band — the answer changes (2200 to 2400 pc/h/ln).
> - Always **state the formula before substituting** values — this is the KTU board's "method mark."
> - For LOS assignment, **cite the density threshold explicitly** ("Since $k = 17 > 16$ and $\leq 22$, LOS is D"), not just the final letter.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A single-lane approach road has free-flow speed $u_f = 90$ km/h and jam density $k_j = 150$ pc/km. Using the **Greenshields model**, determine:
1. The capacity of the lane.
2. The speed and density at capacity.
3. The flow when the density is 50 pc/km. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A basic freeway segment has a free-flow speed of 100 km/h. The observed flow is 1800 pc/h/ln and the space-mean speed is 70 km/h. Determine the **Level of Service** with justification using the HCM density criteria. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1: State the Greenshields relations**

$$u = u_f\left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right), \quad q = u_f \cdot k \cdot \left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute capacity**

$$q_{max} = \frac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \frac{90 \cdot 150}{4} = \frac{13500}{4}$$

$$q_{max} = 3375 \text{ pc/h}$$

**Step 3: Speed at capacity**

$$u_o = \frac{u_f}{2} = \frac{90}{2} = 45 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4: Critical density at capacity**

$$k_o = \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ pc/km}$$

**Step 5: Flow at $k = 50$ pc/km**

First compute speed:

$$u = u_f\left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right) = 90\left(1 - \frac{50}{150}\right) = 90 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 60 \text{ km/h}$$

Then flow:

$$q = u \cdot k = 60 \cdot 50 = 3000 \text{ pc/h}$$

**Final Answer (a):** Capacity = **3375 pc/h**, $u_o$ = **45 km/h**, $k_o$ = **75 pc/km**, $q$ at 50 pc/km = **3000 pc/h**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1: Compute density**

$$k = \frac{q}{u_s} = \frac{1800}{70} = 25.71 \text{ pc/km/ln}$$

**Step 2: Apply LOS criteria for FFS = 100 km/h (HCM)**

| LOS | Density Range (pc/km/ln) |
|---|---|
| A | ≤ 7 |
| B | > 7 – 11 |
| C | > 11 – 16 |
| D | > 16 – 22 |
| E | > 22 – 28 |
| F | > 28 |

**Step 3: Compare**
Since $k = 25.71$ pc/km/ln satisfies $22 < k \leq 28$:

**Final Answer (b):** The level of service is **LOS E** (operating near capacity; flow is unstable, any minor incident will trigger breakdown).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Derive the **Greenshields speed-density model** starting from the assumption of a linear relationship, and hence obtain the expressions for **capacity** and **optimum speed** of a single-lane traffic stream. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-lane road segment has the following Greenshields parameters: $u_f = 100$ km/h, $k_j = 120$ pc/km. A traffic engineer measures a flow of 2500 pc/h on the lane. Determine: (i) the density, (ii) the space-mean speed, and (iii) classify the operating state (uncongested / capacity / forced flow). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1: Linear assumption**

It is assumed that speed decreases linearly with increasing density. The line passes through $(k=0, u=u_f)$ representing the free-flow state, and $(k=k_j, u=0)$ representing the jam state.

**Step 2: Equation of straight line**

$$u = u_f + m \cdot k$$

Slope:

$$m = \frac{0 - u_f}{k_j - 0} = -\frac{u_f}{k_j}$$

**Step 3: Greenshields equation**

$$u = u_f - \frac{u_f \cdot k}{k_j} = u_f\left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right)$$

**Step 4: Apply fundamental relation $q = u \cdot k$**

$$q = u_f\left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right) \cdot k = u_f k - \frac{u_f k^2}{k_j}$$

**Step 5: Find capacity by $dq/dk = 0$**

$$\frac{dq}{dk} = u_f - \frac{2 u_f k}{k_j} = 0 \Rightarrow k_o = \frac{k_j}{2}$$

Substitute back:

$$u_o = u_f\left(1 - \frac{k_j/2}{k_j}\right) = \frac{u_f}{2}$$

$$q_{max} = u_o \cdot k_o = \frac{u_f}{2} \cdot \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{u_f k_j}{4}$$

**Final Result (a):**

$$\boxed{u = u_f\left(1 - \frac{k}{k_j}\right), \quad q_{max} = \frac{u_f k_j}{4}, \quad u_o = \frac{u_f}{2}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1: Use the flow-density Greenshields equation**

$$q = u_f k - \frac{u_f k^2}{k_j}$$

Substituting $q = 2500$, $u_f = 100$, $k_j = 120$:

$$2500 = 100 k - \frac{100 k^2}{120}$$

$$2500 = 100 k - 0.8333 k^2$$

Rearranging:

$$0.8333 k^2 - 100 k + 2500 = 0$$

Multiply by 1.2 to clear decimals:

$$k^2 - 120 k + 3000 = 0$$

**Step 2: Solve the quadratic**

$$k = \frac{120 \pm \sqrt{14400 - 12000}}{2} = \frac{120 \pm \sqrt{2400}}{2}$$

$$k = \frac{120 \pm 48.99}{2}$$

Two roots: $k_1 = 35.5$ pc/km and $k_2 = 84.5$ pc/km.

**Step 3: Identify the correct root**

- $k_1 = 35.5$ pc/km → speed is high (free-flow side of capacity), **uncongested**.
- $k_2 = 84.5$ pc/km → speed is low (congested side), **forced flow**.

Since the problem does not specify congested/uncongested, we consider the uncongested operating regime: **(i) Density $k = 35.5$ pc/km**.

**Step 4: Space-mean speed**

$$u_s = \frac{q}{k} = \frac{2500}{35.5} = 70.42 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 5: Classify operating state**

Check critical density:

$$k_o = \frac{k_j}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ pc/km}$$

Since $k = 35.5 < 60$, we are on the **uncongested (left) branch** of the flow-density parabola.

**Verification using capacity:**

$$q_{max} = \frac{100 \cdot 120}{4} = 3000 \text{ pc/h}$$

Since $q = 2500 < q_{max} = 3000$ pc/h, the flow is sub-capacity. ✓

**Final Answer (b):** Density = **35.5 pc/km**, Speed = **70.42 km/h**, State = **Uncongested (sub-capacity, free-flow side of fundamental diagram)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Capacity and LOS — Single Lane**

- ✅ **Capacity** of a single lane (base, freeway) = **2400 pc/h/ln** at FFS = 120 km/h.
- ✅ **Density** is the **primary LOS criterion** for uninterrupted-flow single-lane facilities.
- ✅ **Fundamental relation:** $q = u \cdot k$ — the **only** relationship tying all three variables.
- ✅ **Greenshields model:** $u = u_f(1 - k/k_j)$ — linear, symmetric, two-parameter.
- ✅ **Capacity (Greenshields):** $q_{max} = u_f \cdot k_j / 4$, occurring at $k_o = k_j/2$ and $u_o = u_f/2$.
- ✅ **Capacity is NOT at maximum speed** — it occurs at *half* the free-flow speed.
- ✅ **Use space-mean speed** $u_s$ in $k = q/u$, not time-mean speed.
- ✅ **LOS A to F breakpoints** (FFS = 100–120 km/h): 7, 11, 16, 22, 28 pc/km/ln.
- ✅ **LOS A** = free-flow, near-zero density. **LOS F** = forced flow, breakdown.
- ✅ **FFS adjustment formula:** $SFFS = BFFS - f_{LW} - f_{LC} - f_N - f_{ID}$.
- ✅ **Always state units** in numerical answers — KTU board deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
- ✅ **For multilane highways**, analyze **per lane** (single-lane logic applies to each lane individually).
- ✅ **Time headway at capacity** ≈ **1.5 s**; **space headway at jam density** ≈ **7.5 m**.
- ✅ **Higher FFS → higher capacity** (capacity table: 2200 at FFS 70, up to 2400 at FFS 120).
- ✅ **Jam density** is a function of vehicle length: $k_j \approx 1000 / L_v$ in pc/km.
- ✅ The **flow-density curve is a parabola** with two $k$ values for the same $q$ (uncongested & congested branches) — always check both roots in exam problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermediate Lane and Two Lane Interurban Roads - Base Capacity and Adjustment Factors - Indo HCM (2017) Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Lane and Two Lane Interurban Roads - Base Capacity and Adjustment Factors - Indo HCM (2017) Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Two-Lane Interurban Roads (Definition)

A **Two-Lane Interurban Highway** is an undivided, bi-directional rural roadway serving intercity and regional traffic, having **one lane in each direction** with a combined carriageway width of typically 7.0 m (2 × 3.5 m lanes). Passing is achieved only by entering the opposing lane when sight distance permits. They are classified by the **Indo-HCM (2017)** (Indian Highway Capacity Manual, published by CSIR–CRRI, 2017) into:

- **Class I Two-Lane Highways** – Major interurban routes where mobility is critical; LOS is based on **both PTSF and ATS**.
- **Class II Two-Lane Highways** – Scenic/recreational routes where mobility is secondary; LOS is based on **PTSF only**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Indo-HCM (2017)** is the indigenous Indian adaptation of the US-HCM 2010, calibrated to Indian driver behaviour, vehicle composition, and roadway geometry. It supersedes IRC:64-1990 and IRC:SP:13 guidelines for capacity analysis.

## 1.2 Intermediate Lane Roads (Definition)

An **Intermediate Lane (Passing Lane)** is a short additional lane (typically 1.0–3.0 km long, 3.5 m wide) provided in one or both directions of a two-lane highway to **break up traffic platoons** and allow faster vehicles to overtake without depending on gaps in the opposing stream. They are NOT a third continuous lane — they are "tactical" geometric interventions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Two-Lane Village Road"**
>
> Imagine a narrow state highway passing through Kerala's rubber plantations. A KSRTC fast passenger bus and a Maruti Swift are stuck behind a slow-moving goods auto-rickshaw. The bus driver honks; the Swift driver looks for a gap in the **opposite lane** to overtake. If the opposite lane is also busy, the bus is **forced to follow** the auto. The "time the bus spends stuck behind" is exactly what **PTSF (Percent Time Spent Following)** measures. **Base capacity** is how many such vehicles the road can serve when the bus has a clear, empty road. **Adjustment factors** penalize the base value when shoulders are narrow, trucks are heavy, or trucks climb steep Western Ghats grades — all of which reduce the road's effective throughput.

## 1.4 Base Capacity — The Magic Number

Under **ideal base conditions**, Indo-HCM (2017) specifies:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{base}} = 3{,}200 \text{ pc/h} \quad (\text{total both directions})}
$$

Equivalently, **1,600 pc/h per direction** under a 50/50 directional split.

**Ideal Base Conditions (must all be satisfied):**

| # | Parameter | Base Value |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Lane width | **3.5 m** |
| 2 | Usable paved shoulder width | **2.5 m** |
| 3 | Directional split | **50 / 50** |
| 4 | Access point density | **0 / km** |
| 5 | Horizontal alignment | **No sharp curves (no impeding)}** |
| 6 | Vertical alignment | **Level terrain** |
| 7 | Heavy vehicles (% trucks + buses) | **0 %** |
| 8 | Driver population | **Commuter (familiar)** |
| 9 | No passing zones | **0 %** |

## 1.5 Performance Measures (MOEs)

Indo-HCM (2017) uses **two primary MOEs** for two-lane roads:

1. **PTSF — Percent Time Spent Following (%)** : The average percentage of travel time that vehicles travel in platoons behind slower leaders they cannot pass. This is the **primary MOE** for Indian two-lane roads.
2. **ATS — Average Travel Speed (km/h)** : Space-mean speed of all vehicles in the traffic stream.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike HCM 2010, Indo-HCM 2017 gives **equal analytical weight to PTSF** and uses speed only as a secondary criterion for Class I roads.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — Why Two-Lane Roads Behave Differently

Two-lane roads are **interrupted continuously** in both space (by no-passing zones) and time (by opposing traffic). Capacity is therefore governed by the **worst-direction flow** plus the opposing flow, since overtaking requires an *empty* gap in the opposite stream. The governing mechanism is:

$$
\text{Capacity} \;\propto\; f(\text{opposing flow},\; \text{passing sight distance},\; \text{platoon formation})
$$

This is fundamentally different from multi-lane highways where lanes are **operationally separated**.

## 2.2 The Master Service Flow Rate Equation

The maximum number of vehicles that can be served at a given LOS *i* on a two-lane road is:

$$
\boxed{
S F_i \;=\; MSF_i \;\times\; f_LW \;\times\; f_SW \;\times\; f_D \;\times\; f_A \;\times\; f_{HV} \;\times\; f_G \;\times\; f_P
}
$$

Where:
- $SF_i$ = Service flow rate at LOS *i* (pc/h, both directions combined)
- $MSF_i$ = Maximum Service Flow rate at LOS *i* under base conditions (pc/h)
- $f_{LW}$ = Lane-width adjustment factor
- $f_{SW}$ = Shoulder-width adjustment factor
- $f_D$ = Directional-distribution adjustment factor
- $f_A$ = Access-point density adjustment factor
- $f_{HV}$ = Heavy-vehicle adjustment factor (PCE based)
- $f_G$ = Grade adjustment factor (combined horizontal+vertical)
- $f_P$ = Driver-population adjustment factor

## 2.3 Maximum Service Flow Rates (MSFi)

| LOS | PTSF Range (%) | MSF (pc/h, both directions) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | ≤ 35 | 660 |
| B | 35 – 50 | 1,080 |
| C | 50 – 65 | 1,600 |
| D | 65 – 80 | 1,920 |
| **E** | **> 80** | **3,200 (Base Capacity)** |

## 2.4 The Seven Adjustment Factors — Theory & Tables

### Factor 1 — Lane-Width Factor $f_{LW}$

Compensates for the inability of narrow lanes to carry large trucks side-by-side and for shy-line offset.

| Lane Width (m) | $f_{LW}$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 3.50 | 1.00 |
| 3.25 | 0.94 |
| 3.00 | 0.88 |
| 2.75 | 0.81 |

### Factor 2 — Shoulder-Width Factor $f_{SW}$

Paved, usable shoulders allow broken-down vehicles to clear the lane and provide shy-distance. A shoulder is considered **usable** only if it has a stabilized surface of ≥ 1.2 m width and a lateral clearance of ≥ 1.8 m.

| Usable Shoulder Width (m) | $f_{SW}$ (for 3.5 m lane) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 2.50 | 1.00 |
| 2.00 | 0.97 |
| 1.50 | 0.95 |
| 1.00 | 0.90 |
| 0.50 | 0.85 |
| 0.00 | 0.75 |

### Factor 3 — Directional-Distribution Factor $f_D$

When the directional split deviates from 50/50, the **dominant direction** has fewer passing opportunities, degrading both directions.

| Directional Split (D/O) | $f_D$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 50 / 50 | 1.00 |
| 60 / 40 | 0.94 |
| 70 / 30 | 0.88 |
| 80 / 20 | 0.82 |
| 90 / 10 | 0.75 |

### Factor 4 — Access-Point Density Factor $f_A$

Frequent intersections, driveways, and roadside friction (e.g., NH-66 with shops) generate turbulence and side friction.

| Access Points per km (both sides) | $f_A$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 0.97 |
| 4 | 0.95 |
| 6 | 0.92 |
| 8 | 0.90 |
| 10 | 0.88 |
| 12 | 0.86 |
| 14 | 0.84 |
| 16 | 0.82 |
| 18 | 0.80 |
| 20 | 0.78 |

### Factor 5 — Heavy-Vehicle Adjustment Factor $f_{HV}$

Heavy vehicles (trucks, buses) occupy more space and accelerate more slowly on grades. Converted via **Passenger Car Equivalents (PCE)**:

$$
\boxed{
f_{HV} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 \;+\; P_T \bigl(E_T - 1\bigr) \;+\; P_R \bigl(E_R - 1\bigr)}
}
$$

Where $P_T$ = proportion of trucks & buses, $P_R$ = proportion of RVs, $E_T, E_R$ = PCEs.

**Recommended PCEs (Indo-HCM 2017, for two-lane roads):**

| Terrain | $E_T$ (Trucks/Buses) | $E_R$ (Recreational Vehicles) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Level | 1.1 | 1.0 |
| Rolling | 1.9 | 1.0 |
| Specific grade — see Indo-HCM Grade Tables | 3.0 – 8.0 | 1.0 – 3.0 |

### Factor 6 — Grade Adjustment Factor $f_G$

Combines effects of vertical and horizontal alignment. Calculated differently for **specific grades** vs. **general terrain**:

- For **level / rolling / mountainous** general terrain → $f_G = 1.00$ (already absorbed by $E_T$).
- For **specific grade segments**, an iterative procedure using $E_T$ from grade tables is applied.

### Factor 7 — Driver-Population Factor $f_P$

| Driver Type | $f_P$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| Commuter / Familiar | 1.00 |
| Recreational / Tourist | 0.85 |
| Mixed (50/50) | 0.925 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight — Order of Multiplication:** In Indo-HCM (2017), the product of all adjustment factors is a **dimensionless multiplier** between 0 and 1. The actual capacity in **vehicles per hour** (mixed traffic) for LOS E is:
>
> $$
> C_{\text{mixed}} \;=\; 3{,}200 \times f_{LW} \times f_{SW} \times f_D \times f_A \times f_{HV} \times f_P
> $$
> This is the value KTU examiners will ask you to compute.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pavement design** for two-lane rural roads (e.g., NH-544, NH-66 in Kerala).
- **Deciding whether to add passing lanes** — if $v/c > 0.7$ for extended periods, an intermediate lane pays for itself.
- **LOS certification** for roads built under Bharatmala and PMGSY schemes.
- **Toll-revenue forecasting** for PPP concessions.
- **Road safety audits** — high PTSF correlates strongly with rear-end collisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Service Flow Rate Equation

The Indo-HCM 2017 equation is empirical, not derived from first principles; however, the *multiplicative* structure can be justified as follows:

**Step 1 — Define base state.** Under base conditions, the maximum sustainable flow is $C_{\text{base}} = 3{,}200$ pc/h.

**Step 2 — Convert to vehicle units.** Real traffic contains heavy vehicles that consume more "headway-budget." We introduce the **PCE-based conversion**:

$$
v_{\text{pc/h}} \;=\; \frac{V}{f_{HV}} \;=\; V \bigl[1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_R(E_R - 1)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply geometric and operational penalties.** Each non-ideal condition reduces the *effective* capacity. Empirically, these penalties are approximately **multiplicative** (not additive), because a narrow lane on a steep grade is *worse* than the sum of independent effects:

$$
C_{\text{actual}} \;=\; C_{\text{base}} \times \prod_{k} f_k
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to any LOS.** The base capacity corresponds to LOS E. For lower LOS, we replace $C_{\text{base}}$ with the **Maximum Service Flow rate $MSF_i$** for that LOS:

$$
\boxed{S F_i \;=\; MSF_i \times f_{LW} \times f_{SW} \times f_D \times f_A \times f_{HV} \times f_G \times f_P}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
> A two-lane interurban highway in Kerala has the following characteristics:
> - Lane width = 3.0 m
> - Usable shoulder width = 1.0 m
> - Directional split = 60 / 40
> - Access points = 6 / km
> - Trucks & buses = 15 % ($P_T = 0.15$)
> - Recreational vehicles = 5 % ($P_R = 0.05$)
> - Terrain: Level → $E_T = 1.1$, $E_R = 1.0$
> - Driver population: Commuter ($f_P = 1.00$)
>
> **Compute:** (a) Capacity under ideal base conditions. (b) Adjusted capacity for LOS E. (c) LOS for an actual volume $V = 1{,}300$ veh/h. (d) Service flow rate at LOS C.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step (a) — Base Capacity:**

$$
C_{\text{base}} = 3{,}200 \text{ pc/h} \quad (\text{by definition, both directions})
$$

**Step (b) — Compute each adjustment factor:**

| Factor | Source | Value |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $f_{LW}$ | Lane width 3.0 m | **0.88** |
| $f_{SW}$ | Shoulder 1.0 m | **0.90** |
| $f_D$ | 60/40 split | **0.94** |
| $f_A$ | 6 access pts/km | **0.92** |
| $f_P$ | Commuter | **1.00** |

**Heavy-Vehicle Factor $f_{HV}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{HV} &= \frac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_R(E_R - 1)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + 0.15(1.1 - 1) + 0.05(1.0 - 1)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + 0.15(0.1) + 0.05(0)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + 0.015 + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.015} \\
&= 0.9852
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combined adjustment factor (product):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{prod}} &= 0.88 \times 0.90 \times 0.94 \times 0.92 \times 0.9852 \times 1.00 \\
&= 0.88 \times 0.90 = 0.792 \\
&= 0.792 \times 0.94 = 0.7445 \\
&= 0.7445 \times 0.92 = 0.6849 \\
&= 0.6849 \times 0.9852 = 0.6748
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adjusted Capacity (LOS E) in veh/h:**

$$
C_{\text{actual}} = 3{,}200 \times 0.6748 = 2{,}159.4 \text{ veh/h}
$$

**Step (c) — LOS Determination:**

Convert actual volume to pc/h:

$$
v_{pc} = V \times \frac{1}{f_{HV}} = 1{,}300 \times 1.015 = 1{,}319.5 \text{ pc/h}
$$

Effective service flow with all geometric/operational factors:

$$
SF_{\text{actual}} = v_{pc} \times \frac{1}{f_{LW} f_{SW} f_D f_A f_P} = \frac{1{,}319.5}{0.6748} = 1{,}955.5 \text{ pc/h}
$$

Compare with MSFi table:

| LOS | MSF (pc/h) | $1{,}955.5$ lies in? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| D | 1,920 | Just above |
| **E** | **3,200** | Operating below E |
| → **LOS D** |  |  |

Equivalently, $v/c = 1{,}319.5 / 3{,}200 = 0.412 < 0.80$ → **LOS C/D boundary**, technically **LOS D** by PTSF.

**Step (d) — Service flow rate at LOS C:**

$$
SF_C = MSF_C \times f_{\text{prod}} = 1{,}600 \times 0.6748 = 1{,}079.7 \text{ veh/h}
$$

## 3.3 Passing Lane (Intermediate Lane) Equivalency

When a passing lane of length $L$ is added, the **upstream platoon structure** is broken. Indo-HCM 2017 provides an equivalency table:

$$
\text{Passing Lane Benefit} \;\approx\; \text{Increase of } 8\text{–}15\% \text{ in effective capacity per km of passing lane length}
$$

A passing lane typically upgrades the **operational LOS by one full level** (e.g., D → C) for 3–5 km downstream of the lane.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Indo-HCM (2017) Capacity Calculation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Traffic Volume V veh/h] --> B[Input Vehicle Mix P_T and P_R]
    B --> C[Select PCE values E_T and E_R]
    C --> D[Compute f_HV using PCE formula]
    
    A --> E[Input Roadway Geometry]
    E --> F[Compute f_LW from lane width]
    E --> G[Compute f_SW from shoulder width]
    E --> H[Compute f_D from directional split]
    E --> I[Compute f_A from access density]
    E --> J[Compute f_P from driver type]
    
    D --> K[Compute Product of all f factors]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    
    K --> L[Compute C_actual for LOS E]
    L --> M[Compute SF_i for all LOS A through E]
    M --> N{Compare v_pc with MSFi table}
    N --> O[Determine Operating LOS]
    O --> P[Output LOS A B C D or E]
    
    style A fill:#ffe4b5
    style B fill:#ffe4b5
    style E fill:#ffe4b5
    style D fill:#b0e0e6
    style F fill:#b0e0e6
    style G fill:#b0e0e6
    style H fill:#b0e0e6
    style I fill:#b0e0e6
    style J fill:#b0e0e6
    style K fill:#98fb98
    style L fill:#98fb98
    style M fill:#dda0dd
    style N fill:#ffa07a
    style O fill:#ffa07a
    style P fill:#ff6347
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — LOS Determination for Two-Lane Roads

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Compute v in pc/h] --> S2[Compute f_LW]
    S2 --> S3[Compute f_SW]
    S3 --> S4[Compute f_D]
    S4 --> S5[Compute f_A]
    S5 --> S6[Compute f_P]
    S6 --> S7[Compute f_HV]
    S7 --> S8[Compute product f_prod]
    S8 --> S9[Compute adjusted MSF_i for each LOS]
    S9 --> S10[Compare v_pc with adjusted MSF_i]
    S10 --> S11[Identify highest LOS where v_pc less than or equal to MSF_i]
    S11 --> S12[Output LOS]
    
    style S1 fill:#e6f3ff
    style S12 fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Intermediate (Passing) Lane Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Platoon Formation upstream] --> B[Slow Vehicle acts as leader]
    B --> C{Fast vehicle wants to overtake}
    C -->|No gap| D[Stuck behind - High PTSF]
    C -->|Gap available| E[Overtake via opposite lane]
    D --> F[Passing Lane Section added]
    F --> G[Fast vehicle enters auxiliary lane]
    G --> H[Platoon disintegrates]
    H --> I[PTSF drops by 8 to 15 percent]
    I --> J[LOS upgraded by 1 level downstream]
    
    style A fill:#ffd700
    style D fill:#ff7f7f
    style F fill:#90ee90
    style I fill:#87ceeb
    style J fill:#dda0dd
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Adjustment Factor Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Road Capacity C_actual veh/h] --> P1[Geometric Factors]
    R --> P2[Operational Factors]
    R --> P3[Traffic Composition Factors]
    
    P1 --> F1[f_LW Lane Width]
    P1 --> F2[f_SW Shoulder Width]
    P1 --> F3[f_A Access Points]
    P1 --> F4[f_G Grade and Alignment]
    
    P2 --> F5[f_D Directional Split]
    P2 --> F6[f_P Driver Population]
    
    P3 --> F7[f_HV Heavy Vehicle Factor using PCE]
    
    style R fill:#ff6347
    style P1 fill:#ffd700
    style P2 fill:#98fb98
    style P3 fill:#87ceeb
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** Define **base capacity** of a two-lane interurban road as per Indo-HCM (2017). List any four ideal base conditions.

**Model Answer:**
The base capacity of a two-lane interurban road is the maximum sustainable flow rate under specified ideal roadway, traffic, and control conditions. As per Indo-HCM (2017), the base capacity = **3,200 pc/h (total, both directions combined)**, equivalent to 1,600 pc/h per direction under a 50/50 split.

Four ideal base conditions:
1. Lane width = 3.5 m
2. Usable paved shoulder width = 2.5 m
3. Directional split = 50/50
4. Access point density = 0/km
5. Level terrain with no impeding horizontal/vertical curves
6. No heavy vehicles (0 % trucks and buses)
7. Commuter (familiar) driver population
8. 0 % no-passing zones

### Question 2
**Q:** What is **PTSF**? State the LOS criteria for PTSF on a Class I two-lane highway in Indo-HCM (2017).

**Model Answer:**
**PTSF (Percent Time Spent Following)** is the average percentage of travel time that vehicles in the traffic stream travel in platoons behind slower vehicles they cannot pass. It is the primary measure of effectiveness (MOE) for two-lane interurban roads in Indo-HCM (2017).

**LOS Criteria (PTSF %):**

| LOS | PTSF (%) |
|:---:|:---:|
| A | ≤ 35 |
| B | 35 – 50 |
| C | 50 – 65 |
| D | 65 – 80 |
| E | > 80 |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Indo-HCM (2017) approach** for determining capacity and Level of Service (LOS) for two-lane interurban roads. List all seven adjustment factors with their typical values and physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Indo-HCM (2017) provides a methodology for analysing two-lane interurban roads using the **Service Flow Rate** concept. The governing equation is:

$$
SF_i = MSF_i \times f_{LW} \times f_{SW} \times f_D \times f_A \times f_{HV} \times f_G \times f_P
$$

**Physical significance of each factor:**

1. **$f_{LW}$ (Lane-Width Factor):** Lane width 3.5 m is ideal; below this, shy-line offset and inability to accommodate side-by-side trucks reduces capacity. Values: 3.5 m → 1.00, 3.0 m → 0.88, 2.75 m → 0.81.

2. **$f_{SW}$ (Shoulder-Width Factor):** Wide paved shoulders allow disabled vehicles to clear the traffic lane and provide recovery space. Values: 2.5 m → 1.00, 0 m → 0.75.

3. **$f_D$ (Directional-Distribution Factor):** An imbalanced split (e.g., 70/30) reduces passing opportunities for the dominant direction. Values: 50/50 → 1.00, 80/20 → 0.82.

4. **$f_A$ (Access-Point Density Factor):** Each intersection, driveway, or side friction point creates a conflict zone. Values: 0/km → 1.00, 10/km → 0.88, 20/km → 0.78.

5. **$f_{HV}$ (Heavy-Vehicle Factor):** Heavy vehicles consume more "car-equivalents" of road space. Computed using PCEs:

$$
f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_R(E_R - 1)}
$$

6. **$f_G$ (Grade Factor):** Combines vertical and horizontal alignment penalties. For level/rolling terrain, $f_G = 1.00$; for specific grades, iterative PCE-based procedure is used.

7. **$f_P$ (Driver-Population Factor):** Commuter drivers familiar with the road drive more efficiently than tourists/recreational drivers. Values: Commuter → 1.00, Recreational → 0.85.

**LOS Determination Procedure:**
- Step 1: Convert volume $V$ (veh/h) to pc/h using $f_{HV}$.
- Step 2: Apply all geometric/operational adjustment factors.
- Step 3: Compare with $MSF_i$ table.
- Step 4: The highest LOS where $v_{pc} \leq MSF_i \times \prod f$ is the operating LOS.

**(b)** A two-lane interurban road has the following data: Lane width = 3.25 m, Shoulder = 1.5 m, Directional split = 70/30, Access points = 8/km, $P_T$ = 20 %, $P_R$ = 0 %, $E_T$ = 1.1, Driver population = Mixed (50/50), Volume = 1,000 veh/h. Determine the **operating LOS**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Adjustment Factors:**

| Factor | Value | Source |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $f_{LW}$ | 0.94 | Lane 3.25 m |
| $f_{SW}$ | 0.95 | Shoulder 1.5 m |
| $f_D$ | 0.88 | 70/30 split |
| $f_A$ | 0.90 | 8 pts/km |
| $f_P$ | 0.925 | Mixed drivers |

**Step 2 — Heavy-Vehicle Factor:**

$$
f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.20(1.1 - 1) + 0} = \frac{1}{1.02} = 0.9804
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Volume to pc/h:**

$$
v_{pc} = \frac{V}{f_{HV}} = \frac{1{,}000}{0.9804} = 1{,}020 \text{ pc/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Adjustment Factors to vpc:**

$$
v_{\text{effective}} = v_{pc} \times \frac{1}{f_{LW} f_{SW} f_D f_A f_P}
$$

$$
= \frac{1{,}020}{0.94 \times 0.95 \times 0.88 \times 0.90 \times 0.925}
$$

$$
= \frac{1{,}020}{0.6543} = 1{,}559 \text{ pc/h}
$$

**Step 5 — LOS Comparison:**

| LOS | MSF (pc/h) | $v_{\text{eff}}$ vs MSF |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| C | 1,600 | 1,559 < 1,600 ✓ |
| D | 1,920 | 1,559 < 1,920 ✓ |
| E | 3,200 | 1,559 < 3,200 ✓ |

**Operating LOS = C** (since it is the highest LOS where the effective volume does not exceed the MSF).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Discuss the **concept of intermediate (passing) lanes** on two-lane interurban roads. Explain their geometric design, length criteria, and operational benefits as per Indo-HCM (2017). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:**
An intermediate (passing) lane is a short additional lane (typically 1.0–3.0 km long) provided on one or both directions of a two-lane highway to break up vehicle platoons and allow overtaking without depending on gaps in the opposing stream. It is **not** a continuous third lane.

**Geometric Design:**
- Lane width: 3.5 m (same as through lanes).
- Taper at entry: 1:20 (desirable) or 1:15 (minimum).
- Taper at exit: 1:40 to 1:50 to encourage smooth merging.
- Should be provided in **rolling/mountainous terrain** with restricted passing sight distance.

**Length Criteria (Indo-HCM 2017):**

| Speed (km/h) | Minimum Length (m) | Desirable Length (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 50 | 500 | 1,500 |
| 60 | 700 | 2,000 |
| 80 | 1,000 | 3,000 |

**Operational Benefits:**
1. Reduces PTSF by 8–15 % in the affected section.
2. Upgrades LOS by **one full level** for 3–5 km downstream.
3. Reduces rear-end and head-on collision risk.
4. Cost-effective compared to widening the entire road to four lanes.
5. Increases effective capacity by 200–400 veh/h per km of passing lane.

**Spacing:** Typically 3–5 km apart in rolling terrain; 5–8 km in level terrain.

**(b)** The following data is collected for a two-lane road: $P_T$ = 25 %, $E_T$ = 1.9 (rolling terrain), $P_R$ = 5 %, $E_R$ = 1.0, $f_{LW}$ = 1.00, $f_{SW}$ = 1.00, $f_D$ = 1.00, $f_A$ = 0.95, $f_P$ = 1.00. **Calculate the capacity in veh/h** and the **service flow rate at LOS C** in veh/h. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Heavy Vehicle Factor:**

$$
f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_R(E_R - 1)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{1 + 0.25(1.9 - 1) + 0.05(1.0 - 1)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{1 + 0.25(0.9) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.225} = \frac{1}{1.225} = 0.8163
$$

**Step 2 — Capacity (LOS E) in veh/h:**

$$
C_{\text{actual}} = 3{,}200 \times f_{LW} \times f_{SW} \times f_D \times f_A \times f_{HV} \times f_P
$$

$$
= 3{,}200 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 0.95 \times 0.8163 \times 1.00
$$

$$
= 3{,}200 \times 0.7755 = 2{,}481.6 \text{ veh/h}
$$

**Capacity ≈ 2,482 veh/h**

**Step 3 — Service Flow Rate at LOS C:**

From the MSFi table: $MSF_C = 1{,}600$ pc/h

$$
SF_C = MSF_C \times f_{LW} \times f_{SW} \times f_D \times f_A \times f_{HV} \times f_P
$$

$$
= 1{,}600 \times 0.95 \times 0.8163 = 1{,}241.7 \text{ veh/h}
$$

**Service Flow Rate at LOS C ≈ 1,242 veh/h**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 — Forgetting to convert veh/h to pc/h:** Many students compare raw $V$ (veh/h) with the MSFi table directly. This is WRONG. The MSFi values are in **pc/h**. You must first divide $V$ by $f_{HV}$ to get pc/h, then apply geometric factors.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 — Using $\prod f$ in the wrong direction:** Some students multiply MSFi by the inverse of the adjustment factors. Always remember: **non-ideal conditions reduce capacity**, so factors are always $\leq 1$ and multiply directly to reduce the effective value.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 — Confusing PCE values:** For two-lane roads in level terrain, $E_T = 1.1$, not 1.5 (which is the multi-lane highway value). Using 1.5 in a two-lane problem is a **favourite trap** in KTU papers.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 — Forgetting $f_G$ for specific grades:** When the question specifies a "specific grade" (e.g., 4 % upgrade, 500 m long), you must use the PCE-from-grade-table values, not the general $E_T = 1.1$.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 — Service flow rate ≠ Capacity:** Service flow rate is for a specific LOS. Capacity is the maximum (LOS E). State this clearly in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Base capacity of two-lane road = 3,200 pc/h** (1,600 pc/h per direction) under ideal base conditions.
- **Primary MOE for two-lane roads = PTSF (%)**; secondary = ATS (km/h) for Class I.
- **LOS boundaries (PTSF %):** A ≤ 35 | B 35–50 | C 50–65 | D 65–80 | E > 80.
- **Service flow equation:** $SF_i = MSF_i \times f_{LW} \times f_{SW} \times f_D \times f_A \times f_{HV} \times f_G \times f_P$
- **MSFi (both directions, pc/h):** A=660 | B=1080 | C=1600 | D=1920 | E=3200.
- **PCE for two-lane level terrain:** $E_T = 1.1$ (trucks), $E_R = 1.0$ (RVs).
- **PCE for two-lane rolling terrain:** $E_T = 1.9$, $E_R = 1.0$.
- **Heavy-Vehicle Factor formula:** $f_{HV} = 1 / [1 + P_T(E_T - 1) + P_R(E_R - 1)]$.
- **Base geometric conditions:** Lane 3.5 m, Shoulder 2.5 m, Split 50/50, 0 access/km.
- **Driver factor:** Commuter = 1.00, Recreational = 0.85.
- **Directional split factor:** 50/50 → 1.00, 60/40 → 0.94, 70/30 → 0.88, 80/20 → 0.82, 90/10 → 0.75.
- **Access point factor:** 0/km → 1.00, 6/km → 0.92, 10/km → 0.88, 20/km → 0.78.
- **Lane width factor:** 3.5 m → 1.00, 3.0 m → 0.88, 2.75 m → 0.81.
- **Shoulder width factor (3.5 m lane):** 2.5 m → 1.00, 1.0 m → 0.90, 0 m → 0.75.
- **Passing lane (intermediate lane) benefit:** 8–15 % PTSF reduction; upgrades LOS by one level for 3–5 km downstream.
- **Passing lane length:** 500–3,000 m depending on design speed.
- **Indo-HCM (2017) replaces IRC:64-1990** for two-lane capacity analysis.
- **Class I** highways use both PTSF and ATS; **Class II** uses PTSF only.
- **Units check:** MSFi in pc/h; final answer in veh/h requires division by $f_{HV}$.
- **Common error:** Comparing veh/h with MSFi directly — must convert to pc/h first.
- **Order of operations:** Convert to pc/h → divide by $\prod f$ → compare with MSFi.
- **Capacity is at LOS E**; SF at other LOS is obtained by replacing $MSF_E$ with $MSF_i$.
- **Multiplicative (not additive) combination** of adjustment factors — a narrow lane on a steep grade is worse than the sum of penalties.
- **Indo-HCM was developed by CSIR–CRRI, New Delhi, 2017** for Indian traffic conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and LOS Analysis of Urban Roads - Base Conditions - Adjustment Factors - Indo HCM (2017) Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Capacity and LOS Analysis of Urban Roads - Base Conditions - Adjustment Factors - Indo HCM (2017) Approach # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is Capacity of an Urban Road? **Capacity** is defined as the **maximum sustainable hourly flow rate** at which vehicles can reasonably be expected to traverse a point or a uniform section of a road during a given time period (usually the **15-minute peak period**) under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions. As per **Indo-HCM (2017)**, the capacity of an urban road is expressed in **Passenger Car Units (PCU) per hour**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Indo-HCM 2017 Definition (Formal):** > *"Capacity of a road is the maximum hourly rate at which vehicles, persons, or vehicles comprising a uniform mix of the vehicle types in the traffic stream, can traverse a point or uniform section of a lane or roadway during a given time period, usually a 15-minute period, under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions."* ### Conceptual Analogy 🚗 Imagine a **water pipeline**: - A pipe has a maximum carrying capacity (litres/second) - If you force more water, pressure builds up and eventually bursts - Similarly, a road has a **maximum vehicle carrying capacity** - Push more vehicles → congestion → breakdown of flow (LOS F) > **Just like a 1-inch pipe cannot carry the flow of a 2-inch pipe, a 2-lane road has lower capacity than a 4-lane road.** --- ## 1.2 What is Level of Service (LOS)? **Level of Service (LOS)** is a **qualitative measure** that describes the operational conditions within a traffic stream, generally in terms of service measures such as **speed, travel time, freedom to manoeuvre, traffic interruptions, comfort, and convenience**. Indo-HCM 2017 classifies LOS into **six categories (A to F)**, analogous to the **US Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)**: | LOS | Description | Operating Condition | |-----|-------------|---------------------| | **A** | Free Flow | Highest quality; low volumes, high speeds | | **B** | Reasonably Free Flow | Stable flow, slight speed reduction | | **C** | Stable Flow | Acceptable for urban design, more restrictions | | **D** | Approaching Unstable Flow | Tolerable but unstable, minor incidents cause breakdown | | **E** | Unstable Flow | At or near capacity, very unstable | | **F** | Forced / Breakdown Flow | Demand exceeds capacity, stop-and-go | > [!NOTE] > **For KTU Exam Tip:** LOS A–E represent *operating* conditions, while **LOS F = Failure** (breakdown). The boundary V/C ratio (Volume-to-Capacity) is the **key metric** in Indo-HCM for urban road LOS. --- ## 1.3 Why "Urban Roads" Need a Separate Methodology Unlike **highways (rural/intercity)** where uninterrupted flow prevails, **urban roads** are subject to: - **Interruptions** from signals, intersections, bus stops - **Side friction** from pedestrians, parked vehicles, encroachments - **Lower design speeds** (typically 30–80 km/h) - **Mixed traffic** with high two-wheeler share - **Narrow lanes** and **frequent access points** Hence, the **Indo-HCM (2017)** was developed by **CSIR-CRRI** to address **Indian heterogeneous, non-lane-based, mixed traffic conditions**, replacing generic US-HCM guidelines for Indian conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Indo-HCM 2017** is the **first Indian Highway Capacity Manual** officially published in **2017** by CSIR-Central Road Research Institute (CRRI), New Delhi, sponsored by **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)**. --- ## 1.4 What are "Base Conditions"? **Base conditions** are a set of **ideal/standardized roadway, traffic, and environmental conditions** against which the actual capacity is adjusted using **multiplicative correction factors**. > [!NOTE] > Think of base conditions as a **"calibration benchmark"** — like saying *"if the road was perfect, this is its capacity."* Real conditions are never perfect, so we apply correction factors. ### Standard Base Conditions for Urban Roads (Indo-HCM 2017) | Parameter | Base Condition Value | |-----------|---------------------| | **Lane width** | **3.5 m** | | **Lateral (shoulder/kerb) clearance** | **≥ 1.8 m** on right, **≥ 0.6 m** on left (median side) | | **Grade** | **Flat (0% longitudinal gradient)** | | **Heavy vehicles in stream** | **0%** (no HV) | | **Directional split** | **50:50** | | **Driver population** | **Regular, familiar drivers** | | **Roadside friction** | **None** (no parking, no pedestrians, no bus stops) | | **Design speed (Arterial)** | **80 km/h** | | **Design speed (Sub-arterial)** | **60 km/h** | | **Design speed (Collector)** | **40 km/h** | --- ## 1.5 What are Adjustment Factors? **Adjustment factors** are **multiplicative correction coefficients** applied to the base capacity to account for deviations from base conditions in **real-world field scenarios**. The general form of the capacity equation: $$C_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{base}} \times f_{LW} \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_{G} \times f_{P} \times f_{RP} \times f_{B}$$ > [!NOTE] > Each factor **f ≤ 1** (reduces capacity from base), unless a condition *improves* over base. The product of all factors represents the **overall reduction factor**. ### List of Major Adjustment Factors | Factor | Symbol | Accounts For | |--------|--------|--------------| | Lane width factor | $f_{LW}$ | Lane width deviation from 3.5 m | | Lateral clearance factor | $f_{LC}$ | Obstructions on sides (curb, parked vehicles) | | Heavy vehicle factor | $f_{HV}$ | Trucks, buses, MAVs occupying space | | Grade factor | $f_{G}$ | Uphill / downhill slopes | | Driver population factor | $f_{P}$ | Regular vs. weekend/stranger drivers | | Roadside friction factor | $f_{RP}$ | Pedestrians, parking, bus stops | | Bus stop factor | $f_{B}$ | Dwell time at bus stops | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Indo-HCM 2017 Methodology – Stepwise Logic The capacity analysis in **Indo-HCM (2017)** follows a **systematic 6-step procedure**: ### Step 1: Define the Road Type Classify the urban road as: - **Arterial** (design speed 80 km/h) - **Sub-arterial** (design speed 60 km/h) - **Collector** (design speed 40 km/h) - **Local street** (design speed 30 km/h) ### Step 2: Identify Base Conditions Apply the standard base condition matrix (as tabulated earlier). ### Step 3: Collect Field Data Gather: - Lane width, lateral clearance - Volume (veh/h) by vehicle category - Heavy vehicle %, gradient - Peak hour factor, directional split - Roadside friction index - Pedestrian flow, bus stop frequency ### Step 4: Determine Peak Hour Factor (PHF) $$\text{PHF} = \frac{\text{Hourly Volume}}{4 \times \text{Peak 15-min Volume}}$$ ### Step 5: Apply Adjustment Factors Compute each factor from Indo-HCM 2017 lookup tables or equations. ### Step 6: Compute Capacity and Service Flow Rate $$C = C_{\text{base}} \times \prod f_i$$ $$\text{SFL}_i = \text{MSF}_i \times \prod f_i$$ Where **MSF_i = Maximum Service Flow Rate at LOS i** (A to E). --- ## 2.2 Capacity Equation – Full Form The **ultimate capacity** of a multi-lane urban road is given by: $$C = 3600 \times \frac{1}{h_{s,\text{base}}} \times N \times f_{LW} \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_{G} \times f_{P} \times f_{RP} \times f_{B}$$ Where: - $h_{s,\text{base}}$ = base saturation headway (seconds/PCU) = **1.8 s** for urban roads - $N$ = number of lanes in one direction - $3600$ converts seconds to hours > [!IMPORTANT] > The base capacity per lane for an urban arterial = **3600 / 1.8 = 2000 PCU/h/lane**. > This is the **single most important constant** for KTU problems. --- ## 2.3 LOS Service Flow Rate Equation The **Maximum Service Flow Rate (MSF)** for each LOS is a fixed value from Indo-HCM 2017: $$\text{MSF}_A = 1200 \text{ PCU/h/lane}$$ $$\text{MSF}_B = 1800 \text{ PCU/h/lane}$$ $$\text{MSF}_C = 2400 \text{ PCU/h/lane}$$ $$\text{MSF}_D = 2850 \text{ PCU/h/lane}$$ $$\text{MSF}_E = 3200 \text{ PCU/h/lane}$$ > *(Approximate values for mid-block sections of urban arterials, subject to minor refinements in latest edition)* After adjustment, the **Service Flow Rate at LOS i** becomes: $$\text{SFL}_i = \text{MSF}_i \times f_{LW} \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_{G} \times f_{P} \times f_{RP} \times f_{B}$$ --- ## 2.4 PCU Conversion in Indo-HCM 2017 The **PCU values** in Indo-HCM 2017 are calibrated for Indian mixed traffic: | Vehicle Type | PCU Value | |--------------|-----------| | Two-wheeler (motorcycle/scooter) | **0.5** | | Car / Jeep / Van | **1.0** | | LCV (Mini-truck, Tata Ace) | **1.5** | | Bus / Truck | **3.0** | | MAV (Multi-Axle Vehicle) | **4.5** | | Cycle | **0.4** | | Auto-rickshaw | **0.8** | | Tractor-trailer | **4.0** | > [!NOTE] > These differ from the older **IRC:64-1990** PCU values, since Indo-HCM 2017 considers **dynamic and static PCU** concepts. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $C_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{3600}{h_{s,\text{base}}} \times N$ | Base capacity | PCU/h | | 2 | $C = C_{\text{base}} \times \prod f_i$ | Adjusted capacity | PCU/h | | 3 | $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{hour}}}{4 \times V_{15,\text{peak}}}$ | Peak Hour Factor | dimensionless | | 4 | $f_{HV} = \dfrac{1}{1 + P_{HV}(E_{HV} - 1)}$ | Heavy vehicle factor | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{SFL}_i = \text{MSF}_i \times \prod f_i$ | Service flow at LOS i | PCU/h/lane | | 6 | $V/C = \dfrac{V_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{adjusted}}}$ | V/C ratio → LOS | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{PCU}_i = \sum (N_i \times \text{PCU}_i)$ | Total volume in PCU | PCU/h | | 8 | $f_{G} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.01 \cdot \text{grade}\%)}$ (approx.) | Grade factor | dimensionless | ### EHV – Passenger Car Equivalents (by grade) | Grade (%) | HV PCU (Truck/Bus) | HV PCU (LCV) | |-----------|---------------------|----------------| | 0 (flat) | 1.5 | 1.2 | | 2 | 2.0 | 1.5 | | 4 | 2.5 | 1.8 | | 6 | 3.5 | 2.5 | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility of This System | Application | Engineering Use | |-------------|----------------| | **Urban road design** | Determine number of lanes needed for a target LOS | | **Traffic impact assessment** | Evaluate new developments on existing roads | | **Signal design** | Input saturation flow for signal timing | | **Congestion mitigation** | Identify bottlenecks (LOS E/F) | | **Public transport planning** | Bus lane, BRT corridor design | | **Smart city projects** | Benchmark LOS for performance evaluation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU 2024:** The ability to **identify the right formula, lookup the right factor table, and compute adjusted capacity/PCU** is a **direct 7- to 14-mark application** in end-semester exams. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Base Capacity per Lane from Saturation Headway ### Definition of Saturation Headway (hs) **Saturation headway** is the time interval between two successive vehicles crossing a reference line at the **stop line** under **saturated (maximum) flow conditions**, i.e., when the queue is discharging continuously. Let: - $h_s$ = saturation headway (seconds/vehicle) - $C$ = capacity per lane (vehicles/hour) By definition, in one hour (3600 seconds), the number of vehicles that can pass = $3600 / h_s$. $$\therefore C = \frac{3600}{h_s} \quad \text{(veh/h/lane)}$$ ### Indo-HCM 2017 Base Value For Indian urban arterial, $h_{s,\text{base}} = 1.8 \text{ s/PCU}$ $$C_{\text{base, lane}} = \frac{3600}{1.8} = 2000 \text{ PCU/h/lane}$$ ### General Multi-Lane Expression For $N$ lanes in one direction (same direction), capacity is additive if lanes are independent: $$C_{\text{base,total}} = \frac{3600}{h_{s,\text{base}}} \times N$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Heavy Vehicle Adjustment Factor (fHV) ### Concept A **heavy vehicle** (truck, bus) occupies more space and moves slower than a passenger car, especially on grades. Hence, it has a **Passenger Car Equivalent (EHV) > 1**. ### Mathematical Form Let $P_{HV}$ = proportion of heavy vehicles in traffic stream. Each HV consumes space equivalent to $E_{HV}$ passenger cars. For a stream of **1 PCU unit of total flow**, the equivalent flow considering HV = $1 + P_{HV}(E_{HV} - 1)$. Since capacity is reduced proportionally: $$f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + P_{HV}(E_{HV} - 1)}$$ > **Example:** If $P_{HV} = 0.10$ and $E_{HV} = 3.0$: > $$f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.10 \times (3.0 - 1)} = \frac{1}{1.20} = 0.833$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Grade Factor (fG) ### Concept Uphill grades reduce speed (especially of HV), increasing space consumption; downhill grades allow higher speeds but are limited by safety. For a given grade $G$ (%), the empirical Indo-HCM 2017 expression (approximate, level terrain baseline) is: $$f_{G} = \frac{1}{1 + k_G \cdot G}$$ Where $k_G$ is a terrain coefficient: - $k_G = 0.025$ for level terrain - $k_G = 0.05$ for rolling terrain (steep grades) > **Example:** $G = 2\%$, level terrain: > $$f_{G} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.025 \times 2} = \frac{1}{1.05} = 0.952$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: LOS Determination ### Problem Statement > A **4-lane divided urban arterial** (2 lanes per direction) has the following characteristics: > - Lane width = **3.2 m** > - Lateral clearance: **0.8 m (right)**, **0.5 m (left)** > - Traffic composition: **70% cars, 15% 2-wheelers, 10% trucks, 5% buses** > - Gradient: **+2% (uphill)** > - Total volume: **2400 veh/h** (50:50 directional split) > - Driver population: regular commuters > - Roadside friction: minor (pedestrians ~50/h, no parking) > - Bus stops: 1 per km **Find:** Capacity, V/C ratio, and LOS as per Indo-HCM 2017. --- ### Solution – Step by Step #### Step 1: Compute Total Volume in PCU Using Indo-HCM 2017 PCU values: - Car = 1.0 - 2W = 0.5 - Truck = 3.0 - Bus = 3.0 For a volume of 2400 veh/h (both directions combined), per direction = 1200 veh/h. In the **analysis direction (1200 veh/h)**: | Type | Fraction | Vehicles | PCU | Total PCU | |------|----------|----------|------|------------| | Car | 0.70 | 840 | 1.0 | 840 | | 2W | 0.15 | 180 | 0.5 | 90 | | Truck | 0.10 | 120 | 3.0 | 360 | | Bus | 0.05 | 60 | 3.0 | 180 | | **Total** | **1.00** | **1200** | — | **1470 PCU/h** | #### Step 2: Heavy Vehicle Proportion (P_HV) $$P_{HV} = \frac{120 + 60}{1200} = \frac{180}{1200} = 0.15 \text{ (15%)}$$ For 2% upgrade, $E_{HV} = 2.0$ (truck) approx. #### Step 3: Compute Adjustment Factors **Lane width factor (f_LW):** For lane width 3.2 m (base 3.5 m), from Indo-HCM 2017 table: $$f_{LW} = 0.96$$ **Lateral clearance factor (f_LC):** With obstructions 0.8 m right, 0.5 m left: $$f_{LC} = 0.95$$ **Heavy vehicle factor (f_HV):** $$f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.15 \times (2.0 - 1)} = \frac{1}{1.15} = 0.870$$ **Grade factor (f_G):** For +2% level terrain: $$f_{G} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.025 \times 2} = 0.952$$ **Driver population factor (f_P):** Regular drivers: $$f_{P} = 1.00$$ **Roadside friction factor (f_RP):** Low friction: $$f_{RP} = 0.98$$ **Bus stop factor (f_B):** 1 bus stop per km: $$f_{B} = 0.97$$ #### Step 4: Compute Adjusted Capacity $$C_{\text{base}} = 2000 \times 2 = 4000 \text{ PCU/h (per direction)}$$ $$C_{\text{adj}} = 4000 \times 0.96 \times 0.95 \times 0.870 \times 0.952 \times 1.00 \times 0.98 \times 0.97$$ Compute step by step: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{adj}} &= 4000 \times 0.96 = 3840 \\ &= 3840 \times 0.95 = 3648 \\ &= 3648 \times 0.870 = 3173.76 \\ &= 3173.76 \times 0.952 = 3021.42 \\ &= 3021.42 \times 1.00 = 3021.42 \\ &= 3021.42 \times 0.98 = 2960.99 \\ &= 2960.99 \times 0.97 = 2872.16 \text{ PCU/h} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 5: Compute V/C Ratio $$V/C = \frac{1470}{2872.16} = 0.512$$ #### Step 6: Determine LOS From Indo-HCM 2017 LOS threshold table: | V/C Range | LOS | |-----------|-----| | 0.00 – 0.30 | A | | 0.30 – 0.50 | B | | 0.50 – 0.70 | C | | 0.70 – 0.85 | D | | 0.85 – 1.00 | E | | > 1.00 | F | Since **0.30 < 0.512 < 0.70** → the road operates at **LOS C** (Stable Flow). --- ### Summary of Result | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Base capacity (2 lanes) | 4000 PCU/h | | Adjusted capacity | 2872 PCU/h | | Actual flow | 1470 PCU/h | | V/C ratio | 0.512 | | **LOS** | **C (Stable Flow)** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Insight:** In the derivation, **do not skip the step-wise multiplication** — examiners give **partial marks for each step** (1.5–2 marks per factor × 7 factors = ~10 marks out of 14). # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Indo-HCM 2017 Capacity Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Urban Road LOS Analysis] --> B[Step 1: Classify Road Type
Arterial Sub-arterial Collector] B --> C[Step 2: Define Base Conditions
Lane 3.5m Grade 0% No HV] C --> D[Step 3: Field Data Collection
Volume Composition Geometry] D --> E[Step 4: Convert Volume to PCU
Apply PCU factors per vehicle type] E --> F[Step 5: Compute Base Capacity
Cbase 3600 hs x N] F --> G[Step 6: Apply Adjustment Factors
fLW fLC fHV fG fP fRP fB] G --> H[Step 7: Compute Adjusted Capacity
Cadj Cbase x product fi] H --> I[Step 8: Calculate V/C Ratio
V/C Vact Cadj] I --> J{Step 9: LOS Lookup Table
Indo-HCM 2017 Thresholds} J -->|V/C less than 030| K[LOS A Free Flow] J -->|V/C 030 to 050| L[LOS B Reasonably Free] J -->|V/C 050 to 070| M[LOS C Stable Flow] J -->|V/C 070 to 085| N[LOS D Approaching Unstable] J -->|V/C 085 to 100| O[LOS E Unstable] J -->|V/C greater than 100| P[LOS F Breakdown] K --> Q[END: LOS Report Generated] L --> Q M --> Q N --> Q O --> Q P --> Q ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Adjustment Factor Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Base Capacity 2000 PCU per lane per hour] --> B[Lane Width Factor fLW] A --> C[Lateral Clearance Factor fLC] A --> D[Heavy Vehicle Factor fHV] A --> E[Grade Factor fG] A --> F[Driver Population Factor fP] A --> G[Roadside Friction Factor fRP] A --> H[Bus Stop Factor fB] B --> I[Adjusted Capacity] C --> I D --> I E --> I F --> I G --> I H --> I I --> J[V/C Ratio Computation] J --> K[LOS Determination A to F] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: LOS Hierarchy and Speed-Flow Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Urban Road Segment] --> B[Operational Condition] B --> C[LOS A: Free Flow
Speed 65 to 80 km/h
V/C less than 030] B --> D[LOS B: Reasonably Free
Speed 60 to 65 km/h
V/C 030 to 050] B --> E[LOS C: Stable
Speed 50 to 60 km/h
V/C 050 to 070] B --> F[LOS D: Approaching Unstable
Speed 40 to 50 km/h
V/C 070 to 085] B --> G[LOS E: Unstable
Speed 30 to 40 km/h
V/C 085 to 100] B --> H[LOS F: Forced Breakdown
Speed less than 30 km/h
V/C greater than 100] C --> I[High Quality Acceptable] D --> I E --> I F --> J[Acceptable but Borderline] G --> K[Critical Level Engineering Action Needed] H --> L[Failure Congestion] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: PCU Conversion Logic (Indian Mixed Traffic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Vehicle Count veh per hour] --> B[Vehicle Classification] B --> C1[2W Motorcycle 0.5 PCU] B --> C2[Car Jeep 1.0 PCU] B --> C3[Auto 0.8 PCU] B --> C4[LCV 1.5 PCU] B --> C5[Bus 3.0 PCU] B --> C6[Truck 3.0 PCU] B --> C7[MAV 4.5 PCU] C1 --> D[Weighted PCU Sum] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D C6 --> D C7 --> D D --> E[Total Volume in PCU per hour] E --> F[Capacity Analysis Input] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define "Base Conditions" and "Capacity" as per Indo-HCM (2017). List any four base conditions for urban roads.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Capacity (1 mark):** As per Indo-HCM 2017, capacity is the *maximum sustainable hourly flow rate (in PCU/h) at which vehicles can reasonably be expected to traverse a uniform section of a road under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions*, generally measured over the peak 15-minute period. **Base Conditions (1 mark):** These are the standardized ideal roadway, traffic, and environmental conditions against which the actual capacity is adjusted. **Four Base Conditions (1 mark – 0.25 each):** 1. **Lane width = 3.5 m** 2. **Lateral clearance ≥ 1.8 m (right) and ≥ 0.6 m (left)** 3. **Grade = 0% (flat terrain)** 4. **No heavy vehicles in traffic stream (0% HV)** *(Any 4 of the table in Section 1.4 is acceptable.)* --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the concept of Level of Service (LOS) for urban roads. List the six LOS categories.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** LOS is a *qualitative measure describing the operational conditions of a traffic stream* in terms of **speed, travel time, freedom to manoeuvre, comfort, and convenience**. In Indo-HCM 2017, it is determined by the **Volume-to-Capacity (V/C) ratio** for urban roads. **Six LOS Categories (1.5 marks – 0.25 each):** 1. **LOS A** – Free Flow (V/C ≤ 0.30) 2. **LOS B** – Reasonably Free Flow (V/C 0.30–0.50) 3. **LOS C** – Stable Flow (V/C 0.50–0.70) 4. **LOS D** – Approaching Unstable Flow (V/C 0.70–0.85) 5. **LOS E** – Unstable Flow (V/C 0.85–1.00) 6. **LOS F** – Forced/Breakdown Flow (V/C > 1.00) --- ## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme] **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the various **adjustment factors** used in capacity analysis of urban roads as per Indo-HCM 2017. Provide the formula for each. **(b) [7 marks]** A **4-lane divided urban road** (2 lanes per direction) has the following field data: | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Lane width | 3.0 m | | Lateral clearance | 0.5 m (both sides) | | Traffic | 800 cars/h, 200 2W/h, 100 LCV/h, 50 buses/h, 50 trucks/h | | Grade | +3% | | Driver population | Regular | | Roadside friction | Moderate (50 ped/h, parked vehicles present) | | Bus stops | 2 per km | Determine the **capacity, V/C ratio, and LOS** using Indo-HCM 2017. --- ### Model Answer to Question A #### Part (a) – Adjustment Factors [7 marks] | # | Factor | Formula | Purpose | |---|--------|---------|---------| | 1 | **Lane Width $f_{LW}$** | Lookup from Indo-HCM table (max 1.0 at 3.5 m) | Accounts for narrow lanes | | 2 | **Lateral Clearance $f_{LC}$** | Lookup from Indo-HCM table (max 1.0 at ≥1.8 m) | Accounts for side obstructions | | 3 | **Heavy Vehicle $f_{HV}$** | $f_{HV} = \dfrac{1}{1 + P_{HV}(E_{HV} - 1)}$ | Accounts for trucks/buses | | 4 | **Grade $f_{G}$** | $f_{G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k_G \cdot G}$ | Accounts for slopes | | 5 | **Driver Population $f_{P}$** | 1.0 (regular) to 0.85 (strangers) | Tourist/leisure drivers | | 6 | **Roadside Friction $f_{RP}$** | Lookup table (0.70 to 1.0) | Pedestrians, parking | | 7 | **Bus Stop $f_{B}$** | Lookup table (0.90 to 1.0) | Bus stop frequency | The **general capacity equation** is: $$C = C_{\text{base}} \times f_{LW} \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_{G} \times f_{P} \times f_{RP} \times f_{B}$$ (1 mark for each factor with formula = 7 marks; **or 4 factors × 1.5 + general equation × 1** = 7 marks) #### Part (b) – Numerical Solution [7 marks] **Step 1: Compute PCU (per direction, 1200 veh/h)** | Type | Count | PCU | Total | |------|-------|-----|-------| | Car | 800 | 1.0 | 800 | | 2W | 200 | 0.5 | 100 | | LCV | 100 | 1.5 | 150 | | Bus | 50 | 3.0 | 150 | | Truck | 50 | 3.0 | 150 | | **Total** | **1200** | — | **1350 PCU/h** | **Step 2: Compute factors** $$P_{HV} = \frac{100 + 50 + 50}{1200} = 0.167 \text{ (16.7%)}$$ For +3% grade, $E_{HV} \approx 2.5$ (truck) $$f_{HV} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.167 \times (2.5 - 1)} = \frac{1}{1.2505} = 0.800$$ (Continued in the style shown in Section 3.4 — multiply all 7 factors stepwise and present final result.) **Final Result (expected):** $C_{adj} \approx 2400$ PCU/h, $V/C \approx 1350/2400 = 0.56$ → **LOS C** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Part (a):** > Students often **forget the general capacity equation** in part (a). They list the factors but don't show how they combine. **You will lose 1 mark** for this. > > **Pitfall for Part (b):** > 1. **Wrong PCU values** — Use Indo-HCM 2017, not IRC:64-1990 values. > 2. **Forgetting to convert to per-direction** flow when the road is divided. > 3. **Mixing up LCV PCU (1.5) with truck PCU (3.0)**. > 4. **Not showing stepwise multiplication** — examiners allot partial marks per step. --- ### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme – Alternative] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Indo-HCM (2017) methodology** for capacity and LOS analysis of urban roads. State the **base conditions, capacity formula, and LOS thresholds**. **(b) [7 marks]** An urban **6-lane divided arterial** (3 lanes per direction) carries a total volume of **5400 veh/h** with the composition: 60% cars, 20% 2-wheelers, 10% buses, 10% trucks. The road has **lane width 3.3 m, lateral clearance 1.0 m, level grade, regular drivers, no friction, no bus stops**. Compute the **LOS** under these conditions. --- ### Model Answer to Question B #### Part (a) – Indo-HCM 2017 Methodology [7 marks] **Introduction (2 marks):** Indo-HCM 2017, developed by **CSIR-CRRI**, is the **first Indian Highway Capacity Manual** specifically calibrated for **Indian heterogeneous, mixed traffic** conditions. It provides **PCU-based, lane-based, and area-wide** analysis methods. **Base Conditions (2 marks):** Lane width 3.5 m, lateral clearance ≥1.8 m, no HV, level grade, 50:50 split, regular drivers, no friction. **Capacity Formula (2 marks):** $$C = \frac{3600}{h_{s,\text{base}}} \times N \times \prod f_i = 2000 \times N \times \prod f_i \text{ PCU/h}$$ **LOS Thresholds (1 mark):** V/C = 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 0.85, 1.00 for LOS A → E. #### Part (b) – Numerical [7 marks] **Step 1:** Per direction volume = 5400/2 = 2700 veh/h. **Step 2: PCU** | Type | Count | PCU | Total | |------|-------|-----|-------| | Car | 1620 | 1.0 | 1620 | | 2W | 540 | 0.5 | 270 | | Bus | 270 | 3.0 | 810 | | Truck | 270 | 3.0 | 810 | | **Total** | **2700** | — | **3510 PCU/h** | **Step 3: Base capacity** = $2000 \times 3 = 6000$ PCU/h **Step 4: Adjustment factors (estimated from Indo-HCM tables):** - $f_{LW} = 0.97$ (3.3 m) - $f_{LC} = 0.99$ (1.0 m) - $f_{HV} = 0.80$ (20% HV) - $f_{G} = 1.00$ (level) - $f_{P} = 1.00$, $f_{RP} = 1.00$, $f_{B} = 1.00$ **Step 5: Adjusted capacity** $$C_{adj} = 6000 \times 0.97 \times 0.99 \times 0.80 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 = 4610 \text{ PCU/h}$$ **Step 6: V/C ratio** $$V/C = \frac{3510}{4610} = 0.761$$ **Step 7: LOS** → $0.70 < 0.761 < 0.85$ → **LOS D (Approaching Unstable Flow)**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall – Question B:** > 1. **Don't forget to halve the volume** for divided roads. Common 0.5-mark error. > 2. **Mixing up 6-lane divided (3+3) vs 6-lane undivided** — capacity differs. > 3. **Not writing the units** (PCU/h) — 0.5 mark penalty. > 4. **Wrong V/C threshold** — read from table; do NOT memorize vaguely. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📌 ### Must-Know Definitions - ✅ **Capacity** = Max sustained flow in PCU/h under prevailing conditions (15-min peak). - ✅ **LOS** = Qualitative grade (A–F) based on V/C ratio for urban roads in Indo-HCM 2017. - ✅ **Base conditions** = Idealized benchmark (3.5 m lane, no HV, level grade, 50:50 split, no friction). ### Must-Know Numbers - ✅ **Base saturation headway** $h_s = 1.8$ s/PCU - ✅ **Base capacity per lane** = $3600/1.8 = 2000$ PCU/h - ✅ **V/C thresholds**: 0.30 / 0.50 / 0.70 / 0.85 / 1.00 - ✅ **PCU (Indo-HCM 2017)**: 2W = 0.5, Car = 1.0, LCV = 1.5, Bus/Truck = 3.0, MAV = 4.5 ### Must-Know Formulas - ✅ $C = C_{\text{base}} \times f_{LW} \times f_{LC} \times f_{HV} \times f_{G} \times f_{P} \times f_{RP} \times f_{B}$ - ✅ $f_{HV} = \dfrac{1}{1 + P_{HV}(E_{HV} - 1)}$ - ✅ $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{hour}}}{4 \times V_{15,\text{peak}}}$ ### Common Exam Pitfalls - ❌ Forgetting to convert volume to **PCU** before V/C calculation. - ❌ Using **IRC:64-1990** PCU values (outdated for KTU 2024 — use Indo-HCM 2017). - ❌ Not halving volume for **divided roads** per direction. - ❌ Mixing up **base capacity** (per lane) and **total capacity** (per direction). - ❌ Forgetting to write **units** (PCU/h) — minor but penalized. - ❌ Not referencing **Indo-HCM 2017** by name in theory answers. ### Key Takeaway > 🎯 **Indo-HCM 2017 = Indian-specific HCM for mixed traffic. Always: (1) classify road, (2) apply base, (3) adjust with factors, (4) compute capacity, (5) V/C → LOS.**

topics.title: Roundabouts - Geometric Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Roundabouts - Geometric Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ROUNDABOUTS: GEOMETRIC LAYOUT

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **roundabout** is a form of circular intersection at which vehicles travel counter-clockwise (in left-hand-drive countries like India) around a central raised island, with entering traffic required to **yield to circulating traffic** before merging. Unlike signalized intersections, roundabouts rely on **geometric deflection and yield-at-entry control** to regulate the right-of-way, eliminating perpendicular conflict points and converting them into merging and diverging (weaving) maneuvers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with **IRC:65-2017 — "Guidelines for Planning and Design of Roundabouts"** and **IRC:SP:45-2017 — Capacity of Roundabouts**), a roundabout is defined as a one-way circulatory intersection with the following mandatory geometric elements:
- **Inscribed Circle Diameter (ICD)**
- **Central Island**
- **Circulatory Carriageway / Roadway**
- **Splitter Island** (on each approach)
- **Entry Curb, Exit Curb, and Truck Apron**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** A roundabout is an unsignalized circular intersection where the *deflection of the vehicle path* (not the stop/yield signs alone) is the **primary speed-control mechanism**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Imagine a **whirlpool at a drain** — water flowing in from several streams swirls in a continuous circular path before leaving. The drain in the middle is the **central island**, the swirling water is the **circulating traffic**, and each incoming stream is an **approach leg**. Just as you would not pour water directly *across* the whirlpool, an entering driver must **bend their path and merge into the flow** — this bending is the *deflection* the geometry enforces.

If the drain were too small (mini roundabout), the swirl is tight and slow; if it were huge (mega roundabout), the swirl would be too fast and unsafe. Thus, **size dictates the natural speed** of the swirl — exactly how **ICD controls circulating speed**.

## 1.3 Classification of Roundabouts (KTU/IRC:65-2017)

| Category | ICD (m) | Design Speed (km/h) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mini | 13 – 25 | 25 – 30 | Low-volume local streets |
| Urban Compact | 25 – 30 | 25 – 30 | Built-up urban areas |
| Urban Single-Lane | 30 – 40 | 30 – 40 | Urban arterials |
| Urban Double-Lane | 45 – 55 | 35 – 40 | High-volume urban arterials |
| Rural Single-Lane | 35 – 40 | 40 – 50 | Rural highway junctions |
| Rural Double-Lane | 55 – 60 | 50 – 60 | National/State Highways |

> [!NOTE]
> All dimensions above are **IRC-recommended ranges**, not fixed values. Final dimensions depend on design vehicle (typically a **truck/bus with wheelbase 6 m**), available ROW, and entry traffic volume.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Geometric Components — Functional Logic

### 2.1.1 Central Island
- **Shape:** Circular, elliptical, or non-circular (for irregular junctions).
- **Function:** Forces vehicles to physically deviate from a straight path, which in turn *reduces speed* and *eliminates straight-through conflict*.
- **Diameter formula (for circular case):**
$$D_c = D_{ICD} - W_c$$
where $D_c$ = central island diameter, $D_{ICD}$ = inscribed circle diameter, $W_c$ = circulatory roadway width.

### 2.1.2 Inscribed Circle Diameter (ICD)
The **diameter of the largest circle that fits inside the outer curb lines** of the roundabout. It is the single most important design parameter because it directly governs:
- Circulating speed (larger ICD → higher speed)
- Weaving length
- Construction footprint

### 2.1.3 Circulatory Roadway Width ($W_c$)
- Must accommodate the **design vehicle** (truck/bus) without the rear wheels tracking over the central island.
- **Range:** 5.0 m (mini) to 11.0 m (rural double-lane).
