topics.title: Causes of Road Accidents - Influence of Road
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Causes of Road Accidents - Influence of Road

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Causes of Road Accidents — Influence of Road

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In traffic safety engineering, **Road Accidents** are defined as unplanned, unintended, and unexpected events occurring on a roadway involving one or more moving vehicles, pedestrians, or other road users, resulting in property damage, injury, or fatality. The **"Influence of Road"** refers to the aggregate contribution of roadway engineering characteristics—geometric design, pavement surface, cross-section, roadside features, and traffic control devices—toward the causation or severity of road accidents.

According to the standard three-factor accident causation model (popularized as the **Haddon Matrix**, 1968), the contributing factors are classified under three pre-crash, crash, and post-crash phases: **Human, Vehicle, and Road/Environment**. For this module, we isolate the **Road factor** and study its independent and interactive influence on accident frequency, type, and severity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET637 — Module 4):**
> *"Causes of Road Accidents: Influence of Road, Vehicle, and Human. The role of road design, geometry, surface conditions, and roadside features in accident causation."*
> The current sub-topic addresses exclusively the **Road** dimension, with Human and Vehicle handled in separate sub-modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **marble rolling down a hallway**. The marble (vehicle) and the person pushing it (driver) are the obvious actors. But what if the hallway floor is wet and slippery? What if it has sharp, sudden bends? What if there are obstacles sticking out from the walls? Even a perfectly behaved marble on a perfectly handled push will crash if the **environment is hostile**.

A road is that hallway. The marble cannot see, judge, or brake—it is entirely at the mercy of the path. This is exactly how road geometry, surface friction, and roadside design either *prevent* or *invite* accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway (Intuitive Level):**
> Roads do not "cause" accidents in the moral sense, but they are *latent failure surfaces*. A poorly designed road multiplies the probability of human error and vehicle failure. Good road design is therefore the **passive safety net** of any transportation system.

### 1.3 Standard Road Safety Metrics Used in This Module

The following bolded metrics appear repeatedly in KTU questions and international road safety literature:

- **AADT (Annual Average Daily Traffic)** — vehicles/day
- **Accident Rate (R)** — accidents per million vehicle-kilometres
- **EPDO (Equivalent Property Damage Only)** — severity-weighted accident index
- **AMF (Accident Modification Factor / Crash Modification Factor)** — multiplicative factor expressing change in accident frequency after a road modification
- **Skid Resistance (SFC or SN)** — Sideways Force Coefficient / Skid Number
- **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** — metres
- **IRC** — Indian Roads Congress (the national standard-setting body)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Road Influences on Accidents

Road-influenced accident causation can be broken down into **five engineering domains**. Each domain is examined for its mechanism, observable symptom, and corrective countermeasure.

### 2.1.1 Road Geometry (Alignment)

| Sub-Factor | Mechanism of Accident Causation | Common Observable Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal alignment** (sharp curves) | Centrifugal force exceeds tyre-pavement friction; vehicle skids or rolls | Run-off-road, head-on collisions on reverse curves |
| **Vertical alignment** (steep grades) | Brake fade on long descents; loss of traction on upgrades | Rear-end collisions, runaway vehicles |
| **Sag curves** | Hidden dip; underpass sight obstruction | Sudden braking, nose-to-tail crashes |
| **Crest curves** | Reduced SSD; driver cannot stop within visible length | High-speed rear-end and head-on crashes |
| **Inadequate super-elevation** | Side friction demand exceeds design limit | Skidding on curves, especially for two-wheelers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Empirical, IRC SP:73-2018):** Accident rate on curves with radius < 200 m is approximately **2.5×** higher than tangent sections of the same AADT.

### 2.1.2 Road Surface (Pavement) Condition

The pavement acts as the **single contact point** transmitting all driver inputs (steering, braking, accelerating). Its deficiencies are therefore disproportionately dangerous.

- **Skid Resistance Loss:** Polished aggregates (especially under heavy truck traffic), bleeding of bitumen, and surface contamination (oil, water, mud) reduce the available friction coefficient *f*.
- **Rutting and Shoving:** Wheel-path depressions trap water → hydroplaning risk at speed.
- **Potholes and Raveling:** Cause sudden swerving, loss of control, two-wheeler instability.
- **Roughness (IRI > 4 m/km):** Increases driver fatigue and reduces steering precision.
- **Edge Drop-off:** Unpaved shoulder edge ≥ 50 mm causes loss of control for errant vehicles.

### 2.1.3 Cross-Sectional Deficiencies

- **Inadequate carriageway width** — vehicles brush against each other or the edge.
- **Absence of paved shoulder** — no recovery zone for errant vehicles.
- **Improper camber / cross-slope** — poor drainage → water pooling → aquaplaning.
- **Median deficiencies** — absence of raised median on divided highways → head-on collisions.
- **Narrow lane width (< 3.0 m)** — lateral clearance violations, especially for heavy vehicles.

### 2.1.4 Roadside Environment

The "clear zone" concept (AASHTO) specifies the lateral distance from the travelled way that must be free of fixed obstacles.

- Unprotected trees, poles, and culvert headwalls.
- Absence of crash barriers / guardrails at embankments > 3 m.
- Ditches with steep sides.
- Bridge parapets of inadequate height.

### 2.1.5 Traffic Control and Visibility Deficiencies

- Missing / faded pavement markings (lane lines, stop lines, edge lines).
- Absence or poor placement of warning signs (curve warning, speed limit, school zone).
- Inadequate **road lighting** — night-time accident rates are 1.5–2× daytime rates on unlit roads.
- Missing cat's eyes / reflective markers.
- Obscured sightlines due to vegetation or advertising hoardings.

## 2.2 The Accident Rate Formula (Most-Tested KTU Equation)

The **most frequently asked quantitative concept** in this sub-module is the road accident rate per unit exposure:

$$R \;=\; \dfrac{A \times 10^{6}}{V \times L \times 365 \times N}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Accident rate (accidents per million vehicle-kilometres)
- $A$ = Number of recorded accidents during the study period
- $V$ = AADT (vehicles/day) on the road stretch
- $L$ = Length of the road stretch (km)
- $365$ = days in a year
- $N$ = Number of years in the study period
- $10^{6}$ = Conversion factor (to express rate per million vehicle-km)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** When the period is exactly 1 year, $N=1$ and the $365N$ reduces to $365$. Examiners often simplify to per-million-vehicle-km by using the above formula directly.

## 2.3 Accident Modification Factor (AMF) — Concept

The **AMF (or CMF)** quantifies the *change* in expected accident frequency when a specific road feature is altered:

$$\Delta A \;=\; A_{\text{before}} \times (\text{AMF} - 1)$$

- $\text{AMF} > 1.0$ → the modification is expected to **increase** accidents.
- $\text{AMF} < 1.0$ → the modification is expected to **decrease** accidents.
- $\text{AMF} = 1.0$ → the modification has **no expected effect**.

**Example (commonly tested):** Widening lane from 2.7 m to 3.3 m typically has an AMF of 0.78 for run-off-road accidents (22% expected reduction).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accident Rate | $R = \dfrac{A \times 10^{6}}{V \times L \times 365 \times N}$ | accidents / $10^{6}$ veh-km | Compare two road stretches |
| 2 | EPDO | $\text{EPDO} = 9.5\,F + 3.5\,I + P$ (where $F$=fatal, $I$=injury, $P$=PDO) | dimensionless index | Severity-weighted comparison |
| 3 | Expected Accidents after Modification | $A_{\text{after}} = A_{\text{before}} \times \text{AMF}$ | accidents/yr | Countermeasure evaluation |
| 4 | Stopping Sight Distance | $\text{SSD} = 0.278\,V\,t + \dfrac{V^{2}}{254\,(f \pm n)}$ | metres | Design check against vertical/horizontal obstructions |
| 5 | Intermediate Sight Distance | $\text{ISD} = 2 \times \text{SSD}$ | metres | Two-way movement on single lane |
| 6 | Overtaking Sight Distance | $\text{OSD} = d_{1} + d_{2} + d_{3} + d_{4}$ | metres | Crest curve design |
| 7 | Skid Resistance (frictional) | $f = \dfrac{V^{2}}{254\,R} - e$ | dimensionless | Curve design check |
| 8 | Reduction in accidents | $\%\,\text{Reduction} = (1 - \text{AMF}) \times 100$ | % | Numerical problems |
| 9 | Reaction time (assumed) | $t = 2.5$ s (IRC standard) | seconds | SSD numerical |
| 10 | Superelevation design | $e + f = \dfrac{V^{2}}{127\,R}$ | dimensionless | Horizontal curve |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In the SSD formula, the sign inside the parenthesis is **'+' on a downgrade** and **'−' on an upgrade**. Students frequently miss the sign and lose 1 mark in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is used by:

- **NHAI (National Highways Authority of India)** for Blackspot identification under the iRAD/IRAD portal.
- **State PWDs** to rank high-accident stretches for treatment.
- **Urban local bodies** in Road Safety Audit (RSA) as mandated by the **Motor Vehicle (Amendment) Act 2019**.
- **International consultants** applying the **iRAP** (International Road Assessment Programme) star-rating protocol, which scores roads 1–5 stars based on inherent safety design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) and its Link to Accidents

The SSD is the *minimum* sight distance required for a driver travelling at design speed $V$ (km/h) to perceive an obstruction, react, and brake the vehicle to a stop before reaching it. SSD is the single most-cited design parameter that, when violated, leads to rear-end and collision-with-fixed-object accidents.

### 3.1.1 Logical Breakdown of SSD

We split the stopping process into two physically distinct phases:

**Phase 1 — Perception–Reaction Time (PRT), denoted $t$ seconds.**
During this interval, the vehicle continues at the design speed $V$ (assumed unchanged). The distance covered is called the *lag distance*.

$$d_{\text{lag}} = V_{\text{(m/s)}} \times t = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{3600} \times t = 0.278\,V\,t$$

where $V$ is in km/h and $t$ in seconds.

**Phase 2 — Braking Distance, during which the vehicle decelerates uniformly from $V$ to zero.**
The work done by braking force equals the loss of kinetic energy:

$$F_{\text{brake}} \times d_{\text{brake}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,V^{2}$$

Since the braking force on a level road is $F_{\text{brake}} = f \cdot m \cdot g$, where $f$ is the longitudinal friction coefficient:

$$d_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{(m/s)}}^{2}}{2\,f\,g}$$

Substituting $V_{\text{m/s}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{km/h}}}{3.6}$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$d_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{(V/3.6)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times f} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{254.05\,f}$$

For **upgrades** (the vehicle brakes while moving uphill), the grade assists braking, so the effective deceleration becomes $(f + n)$ where $n$ is the grade (decimal). For **downgrades**, it is $(f - n)$.

**Summing the two phases** (braking on level, $n=0$):

$$\boxed{\;\text{SSD} \;=\; 0.278\,V\,t \;+\; \dfrac{V^{2}}{254\,f}\;}$$

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Question:** A rural highway has design speed $V = 80$ km/h. The pavement is in worn condition with $f = 0.35$. IRC specifies a perception–reaction time of $t = 2.5$ s. A crest curve on this road currently provides a sight distance of only 95 m.
(a) Compute the required SSD.
(b) Determine whether the curve is geometrically safe.
(c) If the design is unsafe, suggest a quantitative engineering remedy.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SSD} &= 0.278\,V\,t + \dfrac{V^{2}}{254\,f} \\
&= 0.278 \times 80 \times 2.5 + \dfrac{(80)^{2}}{254 \times 0.35} \\
&= 0.278 \times 200 + \dfrac{6400}{88.9} \\
&= 55.6 + 72.0 \\
&= 127.6 \;\text{metres (approx. 128 m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The available sight distance is 95 m, which is **less than** the required 128 m. Therefore, the curve is **unsafe** for the design speed.

**Remedy options (with quantitative logic):**

- *Reduce design speed* to whatever $V^*$ satisfies: $0.278 V^* (2.5) + (V^*)^{2}/(254 \times 0.35) = 95$. Solving: $V^* \approx 64$ km/h. Impose a speed limit of 60 km/h.
- *Improve friction* (resurface with high-SRI aggregates) so that $f$ increases. To make SSD = 95 m at 80 km/h: $0.278 \times 80 \times 2.5 + (80)^{2}/(254\,f) = 95 \Rightarrow (80)^{2}/(254\,f) = 39.4 \Rightarrow f = 0.638$ — unrealistic.
- *Flatten the crest* by lengthening the curve to provide ≥ 128 m sight distance (most desirable long-term solution).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Road Factor vs. Accident Type vs. Countermeasure

This matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** on "Discuss the influence of road on accidents" or "How do road factors contribute to accidents?".

| Road Factor | Sub-Factor | Typical Accident Type Triggered | Severity Bias | Standard Countermeasure (IRC / AASHTO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Sharp horizontal curve ($R < 200$ m) | Run-off-road, rollover, head-on | High (fatal) | Increase radius, super-elevate, install chevron signs + guardrails |
| **Geometry** | Crest vertical curve (insufficient SSD) | Rear-end (vehicles) | High | Lengthen curve, lower design speed, advance warning signs |
| **Geometry** | Long downgrade (> 5%, > 500 m) | Runaway trucks, jackknife | Very high | Climbing lane, escape ramp, brake-check area |
| **Surface** | Low skid resistance ($SFC < 0.35$) | Skidding, loss of control in wet | Medium-High | Resurface with calcined bauxite / grooving |
| **Surface** | Potholes / raveling | Two-wheeler fall, swerving | Medium | Pothole repair programme (annually) |
| **Surface** | Rut depth > 10 mm | Hydroplane, loss of steering | High | Mill and resurface; improve mix design |
| **Cross-section** | Lane width < 3.0 m | Sideswipe, mirror-clip | Medium | Lane widening programme |
| **Cross-section** | No paved shoulder | Run-off-road, rollover into drain | High | Provide 1.5–2.5 m paved shoulder |
| **Cross-section** | No median on divided highway | Head-on (crossover) | Very high (fatal) | Install raised median, anti-glare screens |
| **Roadside** | Tree/pole within clear zone | Hit fixed object | High (fatal for two-wheelers) | Remove/relocate obstacle; install barriers |
| **Roadside** | Embankment > 3 m without barrier | Roll-down crash | Very high | W-beam or thrie-beam guardrail |
| **Visibility** | Missing curve warning sign | Curve approach at excessive speed | High | Install delineators, advance warning |
| **Visibility** | Poor night lighting | Pedestrian hit, run-off-road | High (pedestrians) | LED street lighting @ 30–40 lux |
| **Visibility** | Faded lane markings | Lane departure, head-on at night | Medium | Thermoplastic re-marking (every 1–2 years) |
| **Junction** | Uncontrolled T-junction | Angle collision | High | Provide STOP sign, channelisation, RRF (rumble strips) |
| **Junction** | Acute-angle merging | Side-swipe, rear-end | Medium | Realign to near-perpendicular (≥ 70°) |

## 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example on Accident Rate

**Question (KTU Pattern):** A 12 km stretch of NH-66 recorded 84 accidents over 3 years. The AADT measured at the mid-section is 18,000 veh/day. Compute the accident rate per million vehicle-kilometres.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 84 \;\text{accidents} \\
V &= 18{,}000 \;\text{veh/day} \\
L &= 12 \;\text{km} \\
N &= 3 \;\text{years} \\
R &= \dfrac{A \times 10^{6}}{V \times L \times 365 \times N} \\
&= \dfrac{84 \times 10^{6}}{18{,}000 \times 12 \times 365 \times 3} \\
&= \dfrac{84 \times 10^{6}}{2.3652 \times 10^{8}} \\
&\approx 0.355 \;\text{accidents per million vehicle-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (important for full marks):** The result of **0.355 accidents / $10^{6}$ veh-km** falls within the typical range for Indian NHs (0.3–0.8) but is on the lower side, indicating the stretch performs close to expected safety levels. KTU examiners award full marks only when the numerical result is *interpreted*, not merely computed.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Road-Influenced Accident Causation Tree

The following diagram maps the **influence hierarchy** of road factors leading to accident events, suitable for reproducing in KTU 14-mark essay-type questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Road Accident Event] --> B[Pre-Crash Latent Conditions]
    B --> B1[Road Geometry Deficiency]
    B --> B2[Pavement Surface Failure]
    B --> B3[Cross-Section Inadequacy]
    B --> B4[Roadside Hazard]
    B --> B5[Visibility and Traffic Control Gap]
    A --> C[Trigger Event]
    C --> C1[Driver Error]
    C --> C2[Vehicle Failure]
    C --> C3[Environmental Change Rain Fog]
    A --> D[Crash Phase]
    D --> D1[Run-Off-Road]
    D --> D2[Rear-End]
    D --> D3[Head-On]
    D --> D4[Sideswipe]
    D --> D5[Hit Fixed Object]
    A --> E[Post-Crash Severity]
    E --> E1[Barrier Absence]
    E --> E2[No Median]
    E --> E3[Unforgiving Roadside]
    E --> E4[No Lighting]
    B1 --> F1[Sharp Curve Steep Grade Poor SSD]
    B2 --> F2[Low Skid Potholes Rutting Bleeding]
    B3 --> F3[Narrow Lane No Shoulder Bad Camber]
    B4 --> F4[Trees Poles Culvert Headwalls]
    B5 --> F5[Missing Signs Faded Markings Dark Zone]
    F1 --> A
    F2 --> A
    F3 --> A
    F4 --> A
    F5 --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Engineering Decision Process for Road-Safety Treatment

This flowchart is the **methodology expected in KTU 14-mark answers** on "Explain how road safety engineering identifies and treats accident-prone road features".

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Blackspot via Accident Data] --> S2[Step 2 Conduct Road Safety Audit RSA]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Diagnose Road Factor Influence]
    S3 --> S4{Is the Defect Geometric Surface Cross-Section Roadside or Visibility}
    S4 --> G1[Geometry Treatment: Realign Curve Lengthen Vertical Curve]
    S4 --> G2[Surface Treatment: Resurface Groove Improve Drainage]
    S4 --> G3[Cross-Section Treatment: Widen Lane Add Shoulder Raise Median]
    S4 --> G4[Roadside Treatment: Install Barrier Remove Obstacle]
    S4 --> G5[Visibility Treatment: Add Signs Markings Lighting]
    G1 --> S5[Step 4 Apply AMF based Countermeasure]
    G2 --> S5
    G3 --> S5
    G4 --> S5
    G5 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Step 5 Estimate Expected Accident Reduction]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6 Implement and Monitor using Before After Study]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7 Feedback to RSA Database for Continuous Improvement]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Layered Relationship Between Driver, Vehicle, and Road

This block diagram clarifies the **interactive nature** of accident causation — emphasising that road is a *latent* factor and not a sole cause.

```mermaid
graph LR
    D[Driver Behaviour] -->|Error Proneness| X[Accident Event]
    V[Vehicle Condition] -->|Mechanical Failure| X
    R[Road Environment] -->|Latent Failure Surface| X
    D -.->|Compensates For| R
    V -.->|Compensates For| R
    R -.->|Forgiving Design Absorbs Error From| D
    R -.->|Forgiving Design Absorbs Failure From| V
    X --> Y[Severity Outcome: Fatal Injury PDO]
    Y --> Z[Post-Crash Response EMS Hospital Trauma Care]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in an exam:** If a student writes *"Road caused the accident"*, the examiner deducts marks for the deterministic phrase. The KTU-accepted phrasing is: *"Road acted as a latent failure surface that amplified driver error / vehicle failure into an accident event."* This wording is what the marking scheme rewards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]
**"List any six road-related factors that influence accident occurrence."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer (6 points, 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Horizontal alignment** — presence of sharp curves with radius below the design minimum.
2. **Vertical alignment** — steep grades and inadequate crest/sag curve lengths violating SSD.
3. **Pavement surface condition** — loss of skid resistance, potholes, rutting, and bleeding.
4. **Cross-section deficiencies** — narrow lane width, missing paved shoulder, poor camber.
5. **Roadside hazards** — unprotected trees, poles, embankments, and culvert headwalls within the clear zone.
6. **Visibility and traffic control** — missing/faded signs, markings, and absence of road lighting.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**"Define the term 'Stopping Sight Distance'. State its importance in road accident prevention."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Stopping Sight Distance is the minimum sight distance available on a road at any point that enables a driver travelling at the design speed to perceive an obstruction, apply brakes, and bring the vehicle to a complete stop without collision.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**

- Violation of SSD at **crest curves** causes rear-end collisions when drivers cannot see a slowed or stopped vehicle ahead.
- Violation of SSD at **horizontal curves** causes head-on or run-off-road collisions with oncoming traffic or roadside objects.
- SSD is a **fundamental design input** in IRC:73-2018 for both vertical and horizontal alignment; any geometric element not meeting SSD is a blackspot by definition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail how **road geometry** influences road accidents. Discuss the role of horizontal alignment, vertical alignment, and cross-section in accident causation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 15 km section of an arterial road registered 126 accidents over 2 years. The AADT is 22,000 veh/day. Compute the accident rate per million vehicle-kilometres. If resurfacing the road with high-friction aggregates reduces accidents by an AMF of 0.65, estimate the expected annual reduction in accidents. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The geometric design of a road sets the **kinematic envelope** within which vehicles must operate safely. Any deviation from design norms translates directly into a measurable accident risk.

**1. Horizontal Alignment (≈ 2 marks)**
- **Sharp curves** of radius less than the minimum specified by IRC:73 induce centrifugal demand that exceeds the available side-friction. Vehicles, especially two-wheelers, may skid or overturn.
- **Reverse curves** without sufficient tangent in between produce sudden steering reversals → loss of control.
- **Inadequate super-elevation** on curves leads to tyre-pavement slip; over-supplied super-elevation causes slow-moving vehicles to slide inward.
- *Causative accident types:* run-off-road, head-on, rollover.

**2. Vertical Alignment (≈ 2 marks)**
- **Steep downgrades** (> 5%) cause heavy-vehicle brake-fade and runaway crashes.
- **Crest curves with SSD violation** create "hidden valleys" where drivers cannot perceive a stopped vehicle until too late.
- **Sag curves** hide the road beyond the dip, encouraging late braking.
- *Causative accident types:* rear-end (crest), runaway (grade), head-on (sag).

**3. Cross-Section (≈ 2 marks)**
- **Narrow lanes** (< 3.0 m) produce lateral clearance violations; heavy-vehicle mirrors clip adjacent vehicles.
- **Absence of paved shoulder** removes the recovery zone, causing errant vehicles to overturn in the drain.
- **Missing median on high-speed divided roads** permits cross-median head-on crashes.
- *Causative accident types:* sideswipe, run-off-road, head-on.

**4. Synthesis (≈ 1 mark)**
Thus, road geometry is the *passive engineering envelope* of safety. The **Haddon Matrix** identifies geometric non-conformance as a pre-crash latent condition; correcting it is the most cost-effective accident prevention intervention because it operates continuously without requiring behavioural change from drivers.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the accident rate R:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{A \times 10^{6}}{V \times L \times 365 \times N} \\
&= \dfrac{126 \times 10^{6}}{22{,}000 \times 15 \times 365 \times 2} \\
&= \dfrac{126 \times 10^{6}}{2.409 \times 10^{8}} \\
&\approx 0.523 \;\text{accidents per } 10^{6}\text{ veh-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average annual accident count before treatment:**

$$
A_{\text{annual, before}} = \dfrac{126}{2} = 63 \;\text{accidents/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply AMF for resurfacing:**

$$
A_{\text{annual, after}} = A_{\text{annual, before}} \times \text{AMF} = 63 \times 0.65 = 40.95 \;\text{accidents/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected annual reduction:**

$$
\Delta A = 63 - 40.95 = 22.05 \;\text{accidents/year}
$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = (1 - 0.65) \times 100 = 35\%
$$

**Result (with interpretation):** The current accident rate of **0.523 per $10^{6}$ veh-km** is moderate by Indian standards. Resurfacing with high-friction aggregate is expected to reduce accidents by **~22 per year (35%)** — making it a highly cost-effective intervention with a typical benefit-cost ratio of 5:1 to 10:1 for high-friction surfacing on curved NH sections.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With neat sketches (or block diagrams), discuss the **influence of pavement surface condition on road accidents**. Describe the engineering measures used to mitigate surface-related accidents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal curve on a two-lane rural highway has radius $R = 150$ m and design speed $V = 65$ km/h. The pavement has a worn surface with coefficient of side friction $f = 0.15$. Compute:
(i) the super-elevation required,
(ii) whether the design is adequate by IRC standards, and
(iii) the maximum safe speed at the available $e = 0.07$ super-elevation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**1. Mechanisms by which pavement surface influences accidents (≈ 4 marks):**

- **Inadequate skid resistance (low SFC/SN):** Polished aggregates (especially under wet conditions) reduce available friction. A vehicle entering a curve at design speed cannot generate the lateral force demanded, leading to skidding. Skid-related accidents account for ~30–40% of wet-weather crashes.
- **Rutting (> 10 mm):** Wheel-path depressions trap water → hydroplaning at speed > 80 km/h.
- **Potholes, raveling, patching irregularities:** Force sudden evasive steering; two-wheelers particularly vulnerable.
- **Bleeding of bitumen:** Creates a glassy surface with extremely low friction in dry and wet conditions alike.
- **Edge drop-off:** Shoulders settling below carriageway level cause loss of steering control when drivers drift off.
- **Surface contamination:** Oil spillage, mud, and loose gravel from the unpaved shoulder reduce friction.

**2. Mitigation measures (≈ 3 marks):**

- **Resurfacing with high-friction aggregate** (calcined bauxite, basalt) — typical SFC rise from 0.35 to 0.65.
- **Surface grooving / texturing** to improve macrotexture and water dispersal.
- **Pothole patching protocol** with performance-based contracts.
- **Anti-skid treatments** at approaches to junctions, sharp curves, and pedestrian crossings.
- **Edge-line rumble strips** to alert drifting drivers.
- **Routine pavement condition surveys** using British Pendulum Tester or GripTester.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the super-elevation required to balance the centrifugal effect at $V = 65$ km/h:**

The IRC design equation is:

$$
e + f = \dfrac{V^{2}}{127\,R}
$$

where $V$ is in km/h, $R$ in metres, and $e, f$ are decimals.

$$
\dfrac{V^{2}}{127\,R} = \dfrac{(65)^{2}}{127 \times 150} = \dfrac{4225}{19050} = 0.2218
$$

Now, IRC caps the maximum super-elevation at $e_{\max} = 0.07$ for plain/rolling terrain. We first try $e = 0.07$ and compute the residual friction demand:

$$
f_{\text{required}} = 0.2218 - 0.07 = 0.1518
$$

The available $f$ is 0.15. The required $f$ (0.1518) is **slightly greater** than available (0.15) by a margin of 0.0018 — within practical engineering tolerance.

**(i) Required super-elevation:** $e = 0.07$ (the IRC maximum). If terrain permits a higher $e$ (up to 0.10 in hilly terrain per IRC), then $e$ could be increased to relieve friction demand; however, the safer approach is to constrain $e$ to 0.07 and ensure friction is upgraded.

**(ii) Adequacy check:** Since $f_{\text{required}} = 0.152 \leq f_{\text{available}} = 0.15$? **No**, the required (0.152) is marginally higher than available (0.15). Therefore, the design is **just inadequate** — speed should be reduced, or surface upgraded.

**(iii) Maximum safe speed at $e = 0.07$ and $f = 0.15$:**

$$
V_{\text{max}}^{2} = 127\,R\,(e + f) = 127 \times 150 \times (0.07 + 0.15) = 127 \times 150 \times 0.22
$$

$$
V_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{4188.6} = 64.72 \;\text{km/h}
$$

**Result with interpretation:** The existing 65 km/h design is **borderline-unsafe**. To maintain design speed, the pavement must be resurfaced to raise $f$ to at least 0.155. Alternatively, impose a regulatory speed limit of 60 km/h.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap (SSD problems):** Marks lost by writing "$f - n$" on an upgrade. The correct form is "braking deceleration = $g(f + n)$ on upgrade".
> 2. **Unit conversion trap (accident rate problems):** Marks lost by forgetting to multiply $V \times L \times 365 \times N$ — examiners check the denominator order of magnitude.
> 3. **AMF interpretation trap:** Some students write "AMF = 0.65 means accidents increase by 65%". Correct: AMF = 0.65 means accidents **reduce by 35%** (1 − 0.65).
> 4. **Phrase "Road causes accident":** Always use "Road acts as a latent failure surface" or "Road is a contributory factor". Examiners deduct 1 mark for deterministic phrasing.
> 5. **Sketch omission:** A 7-mark question with a "with neat sketch" directive receives a 1–2 mark penalty if the diagram is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Road accident definition** — unplanned event on a roadway producing damage, injury, or fatality.
- **Haddon's three factors** — Human, Vehicle, Road/Environment; this module isolates **Road**.
- **Five engineering domains of road influence:** geometry, surface, cross-section, roadside, and visibility/control.
- **Horizontal alignment** — radius < 200 m is high-risk; super-elevation + side friction must satisfy $e + f = V^{2}/(127R)$.
- **Vertical alignment** — crest/sag curves must provide SSD; long downgrades need climbing lanes and escape ramps.
- **Surface condition** — skid resistance (SFC ≥ 0.35 for NH), rutting, potholes, bleeding are the major surface culprits.
- **Cross-section** — minimum lane width 3.0 m; paved shoulder ≥ 1.5 m; raised median on divided roads.
- **Roadside** — clear zone 9 m for 80 km/h; barriers for embankments > 3 m; remove obstacles.
- **Visibility** — signs every 500 m in hilly terrain, retroreflective markings, LED lighting at 30–40 lux.
- **Accident Rate formula:** $R = A \times 10^{6} / (V \times L \times 365 \times N)$ — must memorise and interpret.
- **AMF interpretation:** AMF < 1 means reduction; % reduction = $(1 - \text{AMF}) \times 100$.
- **SSD formula:** $\text{SSD} = 0.278 V t + V^{2}/(254 f)$ — sign convention $f \pm n$ for grade.
- **EPDO** weightage: 9.5 fatal, 3.5 injury, 1 PDO (Kadiyali convention).
- **IRC documents to remember:** IRC:73-2018 (geometric design), IRC:SP:55 (accident investigation), MoRTH specifications for pavement friction.
- **iRAP star rating** — 1-star roads have 10× the fatality risk of 5-star roads.
- **Examination phrasing tip:** "Latent failure surface" and "forgiving roadside" are examiner-favoured terms worth recalling.
- **Numerical safety check:** Always check $f_{\text{required}} \leq f_{\text{available}}$ for horizontal curves — and present the result with a *practical interpretation* (e.g., "impose speed limit" or "upgrade surface"), not just the number.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle

# 🚗 VEHICLE — Fundamentals in Traffic Engineering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> In the context of Traffic Engineering and Management, a **Vehicle** is any device used for transporting people or goods on a roadway, treated as the fundamental **unit of traffic flow**. The study of vehicle characteristics — including **static dimensions**, **dynamic performance**, **weight**, **power**, and **kinematic behaviour** — is essential for the geometric design of roads, capacity analysis, and traffic management.

### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 💡

Think of vehicles as the **"blood cells"** of a transportation system. Just as blood cells have specific sizes and behaviours that determine how blood flows through arteries, vehicles have specific dimensions and dynamic capabilities that dictate how they move through roadways. If the cells are too large, they clog the system; if their acceleration is too slow, the system becomes inefficient. The **road is designed around the vehicle** — never the other way around.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vehicle Study is Critical:**
> - Roadway width, lane width, and clearance are all determined by **vehicle width** and **swept path**.
> - Turning radii, intersection design, and parking bays depend on **vehicle length and turning radius**.
> - Capacity and Level of Service depend on how vehicles perform under different **speed-acceleration regimes**.

### 1.2 Classification of Vehicles (KTU Standard)

Vehicles are broadly classified based on the mode of propulsion and use:

| Category | Examples | Power Source | Typical Role in Traffic |
|----------|----------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Motorized vehicles** | Cars, buses, trucks, two-wheelers | IC engine / Electric | Primary traffic stream |
| **Non-motorized vehicles** | Bicycles, rickshaws, animal carts | Human / Animal | Mixed traffic streams |
| **Public transport vehicles** | Buses, mini-buses, metro coaches | IC engine / Electric | Mass transit |
| **Freight vehicles** | Trucks, trailers, multi-axle vehicles | Diesel / Electric | Goods movement |
| **Emergency vehicles** | Ambulances, fire tenders | IC engine | Priority vehicles |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Always mention that Indian roads follow **IRC (Indian Roads Congress) standards**, particularly **IRC:64 – 1990** and **IRC:SP:73 – 2015** for design vehicle classifications, while **MoRTH** notifications govern legal vehicle dimensions.

### 1.3 Static Vehicle Characteristics

Static characteristics are measured when the vehicle is **stationary** and form the basis of **geometric design**:

- **Vehicle Length (L):** Tip-to-tip dimension. Standard car ≈ **4.5–5.0 m**, bus ≈ **10–12 m**, truck ≈ **10–12 m**, semi-trailer ≈ **18 m**.
- **Vehicle Width (W):** Side-to-side dimension. Standard car ≈ **1.7–1.8 m**, bus ≈ **2.5 m**, truck ≈ **2.5 m** (max legal in India = **2.5 m** for most categories).
- **Vehicle Height (H):** Ground-to-top dimension. Standard car ≈ **1.5 m**, bus ≈ **3.2 m**, truck ≈ **3.5–4.2 m**.
- **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW):** Total weight including payload. Ranges from **1.5 tonnes** (small car) to **49 tonnes** (multi-axle truck in India).
- **Wheelbase:** Distance between front and rear axles, influences turning radius.

### 1.4 Dynamic Vehicle Characteristics

Dynamic characteristics govern the **running behaviour** of the vehicle:

- **Maximum speed (V_max):** The top speed attainable on a level road.
- **Acceleration capability (a):** Rate of change of velocity, typically **0.8–1.5 m/s²** for cars, **0.4–0.8 m/s²** for trucks/buses.
- **Deceleration capability (d):** Braking efficiency, typically **3.0–4.5 m/s²** for cars, **2.0–3.0 m/s²** for heavy vehicles.
- **Power-to-weight ratio (P/W):** Determines hill-climbing ability and speed maintenance on gradients.
- **Brake reaction time (t_r):** The driver perception-reaction time, typically **2.5 seconds** (per IRC standards).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Design Philosophy:** The **design vehicle** is the largest vehicle expected to use the facility frequently. For highway design in India, the design vehicle is typically a **truck/bus combination**, while for urban streets, a **single unit truck (SUT)** is often used.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vehicle Dimensions and Design Standards

### 2.1.1 IRC Standard Vehicle Categories (India)

The **Indian Roads Congress (IRC)** classifies vehicles into several categories for design purposes. The most commonly referenced standard is **IRC:64 – 1990**, which specifies the following design vehicles:

| Design Vehicle | Length (m) | Width (m) | Height (m) | Min. Turning Radius (m) |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Car (P)** | 5.0 | 1.8 | 1.5 | 6.0 |
| **Two-wheeler (TW)** | 1.8 | 0.6 | 1.0 | 1.5 |
| **Three-wheeler (Auto)** | 3.2 | 1.4 | 1.7 | 3.5 |
| **LCV (Light Commercial)** | 6.0 | 2.1 | 2.6 | 7.0 |
| **Bus / Truck (SUT)** | 11.0 | 2.5 | 4.0 | 12.0 |
| **Semi-trailer (MUT)** | 16.0 | 2.5 | 4.0 | 12.0 |
| **Truck-trailer combination** | 18.0 | 2.5 | 4.0 | 13.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Always quote values from **IRC:64 – 1990** or the **latest MoRTH notification**. Maximum legal dimensions in India (as per MoRTH, updated 2023) are:
> - Length: **12 m (2-axle truck)** to **18.5 m (multi-axle)**
> - Width: **2.5 m** (all categories)
> - Height: **4.2 m** (general), **4.75 m** (containers)
> - GVW: **49 tonnes** (multi-axle)

### 2.2 Vehicle Performance Equations

The motion of a vehicle on a road is governed by fundamental kinematic and dynamic equations. The **resistance to motion** includes rolling resistance, gradient resistance, and air resistance.

#### 2.2.1 Forces Acting on a Moving Vehicle

1. **Tractive Force (Ft):** Generated by the engine at the wheels, propelling the vehicle forward.
2. **Rolling Resistance (Fr):** Frictional resistance between tyres and pavement.
3. **Gradient Resistance (Fg):** Component of weight acting along the slope.
4. **Air Resistance (Fa):** Drag force due to vehicle moving through air.

The **equation of motion** is:

$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a + M \cdot a$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mass of the vehicle (kg)
- $a$ = Acceleration (m/s²)
- $F_r$ = Rolling resistance
- $F_g$ = Gradient resistance
- $F_a$ = Air resistance

#### 2.2.2 Specific Resistance Formulas

**Rolling Resistance (Fr):**
$$F_r = f_r \cdot W = f_r \cdot M \cdot g$$

Where $f_r$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance, typically **0.015** for cars on good pavement, **0.020–0.030** for trucks.

**Gradient Resistance (Fg):**
$$F_g = W \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot i$$

Where $i$ is the gradient expressed as a decimal (e.g., 2% = 0.02).

**Air Resistance (Fa):**
$$F_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A \cdot V^2$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Air density (typically **1.2 kg/m³**)
- $C_D$ = Drag coefficient (cars: **0.3–0.4**, trucks: **0.6–0.8**)
- $A$ = Frontal area (m²)
- $V$ = Velocity (m/s)

### 2.3 Braking Distance and Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Formula Zone:** Braking distance and SSD are **guaranteed 14-mark questions** in the KTU ESE.

**Braking Distance (L_braking):** The distance travelled from the instant brakes are applied until the vehicle comes to a complete stop.

$$L_{braking} = \frac{V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot (f \pm i)}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Initial speed (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $f$ = Coefficient of longitudinal friction (**0.35–0.40** for design)
- $i$ = Grade (+ for upgrade, - for downgrade)

**Lag Distance (L_lag):** Distance travelled during perception-reaction time.

$$L_{lag} = V \cdot t_r$$

Where $t_r$ = reaction time (typically **2.5 s** for design per IRC).

**Stopping Sight Distance (SSD):**

$$\text{SSD} = L_{lag} + L_{braking} = V \cdot t_r + \frac{V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot (f \pm i)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** SSD is the **minimum sight distance** required on any road segment for safe stopping. It directly governs vertical curve design (summit curve length) and horizontal sightline offsets on curves.

### 2.4 Passenger Car Equivalent (PCE) — Critical KTU Topic

**Definition:** The **Passenger Car Equivalent (PCE)** of a vehicle is the number of passenger cars that would use the same roadway capacity as one such vehicle under prevailing traffic, roadway, and control conditions.

| Vehicle Type | PCE on Urban Roads | PCE on Rural Highways |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Car** | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| **Two-wheeler** | 0.5 | 0.5 |
| **Auto-rickshaw** | 1.0 | 1.5 |
| **LCV** | 1.5 | 2.0 |
| **Bus / Truck** | 2.5–3.0 | 3.0–4.0 |
| **Cycle** | 0.2 | 0.5 |
| **Animal cart** | 0.4 | 1.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** For Indian conditions, the **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM) – Indo-HCM (IRC:SP:73 – 2015)** provides PCE values. The PCE depends on the **mix of traffic**, **roadway width**, and **gradient**.

### 2.5 Hill-Climbing Ability

For a vehicle climbing a gradient $i$ at constant speed, the required power is:

$$P = \frac{M \cdot g \cdot (f + i) \cdot V}{\eta}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mass (kg)
- $V$ = Velocity (m/s)
- $\eta$ = Drivetrain efficiency (**0.85–0.90** for modern vehicles)
- $f + i$ = Total resistance coefficient

The **maximum gradient climbable at constant speed** is determined by equating the engine's maximum tractive power to the resistance forces.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Constants / Units |
|---------|---------|------------------------|
| **Lag distance** | $L_{lag} = V \cdot t_r$ | $t_r = 2.5$ s (design) |
| **Braking distance** | $L_{braking} = \dfrac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}$ | $g = 9.81$ m/s², $f = 0.35$ |
| **SSD** | $\text{SSD} = V \cdot t_r + \dfrac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}$ | $f = 0.35$ to $0.40$ |
| **Rolling resistance** | $F_r = f_r \cdot M \cdot g$ | $f_r = 0.015$ to $0.030$ |
| **Gradient resistance** | $F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \approx M \cdot g \cdot i$ | $i$ in decimal |
| **Air resistance** | $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V^2$ | $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ |
| **Engine power required** | $P = \dfrac{M \cdot g \cdot (f+i) \cdot V}{\eta}$ | $\eta = 0.85$ |
| **Passenger Car Unit** | $\text{PCU} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PCE_i \cdot n_i$ | n_i = number of vehicle type i |
| **Dynamic capacity** | $C = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{S}$ | $V$ in km/h, $S$ = spacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** When calculating SSD with **speed in km/h**, convert to m/s by dividing by **3.6**. Many KTU students lose marks by forgetting this conversion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Braking Distance (Detailed)

We start with the **fundamental kinematic equation of motion** under constant deceleration.

**Step 1: Initial Setup**

A vehicle is moving with initial velocity $u$ (m/s). Brakes are applied, producing a constant deceleration $a$ (m/s²). Let $v$ be the final velocity and $s$ the distance covered.

We know that the frictional force at the tyre-pavement interface provides the deceleration:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot M \cdot g$$

Where $\mu$ is the coefficient of longitudinal friction, $N$ is the normal reaction (= $M \cdot g$ on level road).

**Step 2: Applying Newton's Second Law**

$$M \cdot a = \mu \cdot M \cdot g$$

$$\therefore a = \mu \cdot g$$

**Step 3: Work-Energy Approach**

The kinetic energy of the vehicle must be dissipated by friction work over the braking distance $L_b$:

$$\frac{1}{2} M u^2 = F_{friction} \cdot L_b$$

$$\frac{1}{2} M u^2 = \mu \cdot M \cdot g \cdot L_b$$

**Step 4: Solving for Braking Distance**

$$L_b = \frac{u^2}{2 \mu g}$$

This is the basic **braking distance on a level road**.

**Step 5: Including Gradient Effect**

On a gradient $i$ (positive = upgrade, negative = downgrade), the gravitational force component also acts on the vehicle:

- For **downgrade** (descending): gravity **assists** the motion, increasing effective deceleration, so:
$$L_b = \frac{u^2}{2 g (\mu + i)}$$

- For **upgrade** (ascending): gravity **opposes** the motion, decreasing effective deceleration, so:
$$L_b = \frac{u^2}{2 g (\mu - i)}$$

**Step 6: Generic Form**

$$\boxed{L_b = \frac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}}$$

Where the sign convention is: **+ for downgrade** (longer braking distance, safer); **- for upgrade**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This formula is used to design **summit (crest) vertical curves** on highways. If a driver cannot see an obstruction at distance SSD, the crest curve length must be increased.

## 3.2 Derivation of Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)

**Step 1: Components of SSD**

SSD has two components:
1. **Lag distance** ($L_{lag}$): Distance travelled during driver perception-reaction time.
2. **Braking distance** ($L_b$): Distance travelled after brakes are applied until stop.

$$\text{SSD} = L_{lag} + L_b$$

**Step 2: Lag Distance**

Assuming constant speed $V$ during the reaction time $t_r$:

$$L_{lag} = V \cdot t_r$$

**Step 3: Substituting Braking Distance**

Using the previously derived braking distance formula:

$$L_b = \frac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}$$

**Step 4: Final SSD Expression**

$$\boxed{\text{SSD} = V \cdot t_r + \frac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

**Problem:** Calculate the SSD for a design speed of **80 km/h** on a road with gradient **+3%** (upgrade). Take $t_r = 2.5$ s, $f = 0.35$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert speed**

$$V = \frac{80}{3.6} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute lag distance**

$$L_{lag} = V \cdot t_r = 22.22 \times 2.5 = 55.56 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute braking distance**

For **upgrade**, effective friction is $(f - i) = (0.35 - 0.03) = 0.32$.

$$L_b = \frac{V^2}{2g(f - i)} = \frac{(22.22)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.32}$$

$$L_b = \frac{493.83}{6.2784} = 78.66 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Total SSD**

$$\text{SSD} = 55.56 + 78.66 = \boxed{134.22 \text{ m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** The same problem solved for **downgrade** ($-3\%$) would give effective friction $(f + i) = 0.38$, and a smaller braking distance. The **critical case for design is always the downgrade** because it produces a longer stopping distance. KTU students must show the design-worst-case.

## 3.3 Derivation of PCU (Passenger Car Unit) Calculation

**Step 1: Definition Recap**

$$\text{PCU} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PCE_i \times n_i$$

**Step 2: Worked Example**

A traffic stream has the following composition observed over 1 hour:

| Vehicle Type | Count (n_i) | PCE (IRC:SP:73) |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Cars | 600 | 1.0 |
| Two-wheelers | 800 | 0.5 |
| Buses | 50 | 3.0 |
| Trucks | 100 | 3.0 |
| Cycles | 200 | 0.2 |
| Auto-rickshaws | 150 | 1.0 |

**Step 3: Calculate Total PCU**

$$\text{PCU}_{cars} = 600 \times 1.0 = 600$$

$$\text{PCU}_{2W} = 800 \times 0.5 = 400$$

$$\text{PCU}_{bus} = 50 \times 3.0 = 150$$

$$\text{PCU}_{truck} = 100 \times 3.0 = 300$$

$$\text{PCU}_{cycle} = 200 \times 0.2 = 40$$

$$\text{PCU}_{auto} = 150 \times 1.0 = 150$$

$$\text{Total PCU} = 600 + 400 + 150 + 300 + 40 + 150 = \boxed{1640 \text{ PCU/hr}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to PCU per minute (for capacity comparisons)**

$$\text{Flow} = \frac{1640}{60} = 27.33 \text{ PCU/min}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Hill Climbing Gradient (Maximum Sustainable Grade)

**Step 1: Power Balance**

For a vehicle to maintain **constant speed** $V$ on a gradient $i$, the engine tractive power must equal the sum of resistance powers:

$$P_{engine} = P_{rolling} + P_{gradient} + P_{air}$$

**Step 2: Force-Speed Form**

At constant speed, all resistance forces must be balanced by tractive force:

$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a$$

**Step 3: Substituting Individual Forces**

$$F_t = f_r \cdot M \cdot g + M \cdot g \cdot i + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V^2$$

**Step 4: Solving for Maximum Gradient**

Ignoring air resistance for simplicity (or absorbing it into $f_r$):

$$i_{max} = \frac{F_t}{M \cdot g} - f_r$$

**Step 5: With Engine Power Limit**

If the engine's maximum power is $P_{max}$ (Watts), the maximum sustainable speed on a given grade $i$ is:

$$V = \frac{P_{max} \cdot \eta}{M \cdot g \cdot (f + i)}$$

Solving for $i$:

$$\boxed{i = \frac{P_{max} \cdot \eta}{M \cdot g \cdot V} - f}$$

**Step 6: Numerical Example**

A truck of mass **15,000 kg** with maximum power **180 kW** and efficiency **0.85** travels at **constant 50 km/h (13.89 m/s)**. Take $f_r = 0.02$. Find the maximum sustainable gradient.

**Solution:**

$$i = \frac{P_{max} \cdot \eta}{M \cdot g \cdot V} - f_r$$

$$i = \frac{180{,}000 \times 0.85}{15{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 13.89} - 0.02$$

$$i = \frac{153{,}000}{2{,}043{,}500} - 0.02 = 0.0749 - 0.02 = 0.0549$$

$$\boxed{i \approx 5.5\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The IRC recommends maximum gradient of **3%–4%** in plains, **5%–6%** in rolling terrain, and up to **7%** in mountainous terrain (per IRC:73).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the term "Passenger Car Equivalent (PCE)". Why is the PCE concept important in traffic engineering on Indian roads?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Passenger Car Equivalent (PCE)** of a vehicle is defined as the number of passenger cars displaced from a roadway under the prevailing traffic, roadway, and control conditions by the addition of a single subject vehicle of a different type, while maintaining the same level of service.

**Importance on Indian roads:**

1. **Mixed traffic composition:** Indian roads have a heterogeneous mix — two-wheelers, auto-rickshaws, cars, buses, trucks, cycles, and animal carts — that must be converted to a common unit for meaningful analysis.

2. **Capacity estimation:** Highway capacity is always expressed in **PCU/hour**, allowing direct comparison with international standards (HCM).

3. **Design uniformity:** Lane capacity, intersection design, and signal timing all require flow values in PCU to be consistent and design-compatible.

4. **Performance evaluation:** Helps quantify the relative impact of heavy/slow vehicles on traffic flow.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** PCE = **P**assenger **C**ar **E**quivalent — used to bring **equivalence** in a mixed traffic stream.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List any four static and four dynamic characteristics of a vehicle that are relevant to traffic engineering design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Static Characteristics (measured when stationary):**
1. **Vehicle length** — affects parking bay, turning radius, queue storage.
2. **Vehicle width** — determines lane width and clearances.
3. **Vehicle height** — governs vertical clearance under bridges/overpasses.
4. **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** — influences pavement structural design and bridge load rating.

**Dynamic Characteristics (measured during motion):**
1. **Maximum speed** — influences design speed and SSD.
2. **Acceleration capability** — determines gap acceptance and merging behaviour.
3. **Deceleration capability** — governs braking distance and SSD.
4. **Power-to-weight ratio** — determines hill-climbing ability and speed maintenance on grades.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker:** Students often miss listing all four categories. A clear 4-static + 4-dynamic table always scores full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various **vehicle characteristics** (both static and dynamic) that are considered in traffic engineering design. Mention the relevant **IRC standards** for vehicle dimensions in India. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A vehicle is travelling at a design speed of **60 km/h** on a road with a downgrade of **2%**. Calculate the **stopping sight distance (SSD)**. Take perception-reaction time $t_r = 2.5$ s and coefficient of longitudinal friction $f = 0.35$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**
Vehicle characteristics form the fundamental input for geometric design of roads, capacity analysis, and safety evaluation. They are broadly classified into **static** (at rest) and **dynamic** (in motion) characteristics.

**Step 2: Static Characteristics (3 marks)**

| Characteristic | Typical Values | Engineering Significance |
|----------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Length (L) | Car: 5 m, Bus: 12 m, Truck: 10–12 m | Parking bay, queue storage, intersection design |
| Width (W) | Car: 1.8 m, Bus/Truck: 2.5 m (max legal) | Lane width, shoulder design |
| Height (H) | Car: 1.5 m, Truck: 4.2 m | Vertical clearance under structures |
| Wheelbase | Car: 2.5–3.0 m, Bus: 6.0 m | Turning radius, swept path |
| GVW | 1.5 t (car) to 49 t (multi-axle) | Pavement and bridge design |

**Step 3: Dynamic Characteristics (2 marks)**

| Characteristic | Typical Values | Significance |
|----------------|----------------|--------------|
| Maximum speed | 60–120 km/h | Design speed, SSD |
| Acceleration | Car: 0.8–1.5 m/s², Bus: 0.4–0.8 m/s² | Gap acceptance, merging |
| Deceleration | Car: 3.0–4.5 m/s², Truck: 2.0–3.0 m/s² | Braking distance, SSD |
| Power-to-weight | 50–100 W/kg (cars) | Hill climbing, gradient |
| Reaction time | 2.5 s (design) | Lag distance, SSD |

**Step 4: IRC Standards (1 mark)**
- **IRC:64 – 1990:** Geometric design standards, design vehicle definitions.
- **IRC:SP:73 – 2015:** Indo-HCM, PCE values.
- **MoRTH Notifications:** Legal vehicle dimensions, weight limits.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Design speed $V = 60$ km/h
- Gradient $i = -2\%$ (downgrade, so use $f + i$ in denominator)
- Reaction time $t_r = 2.5$ s
- Coefficient of friction $f = 0.35$

**Step 1: Convert speed to m/s** (1 mark)

$$V = \frac{60}{3.6} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Lag Distance** (2 marks)

$$L_{lag} = V \cdot t_r = 16.67 \times 2.5 = 41.67 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Braking Distance (downgrade case)** (2 marks)

For downgrade, effective deceleration coefficient is $(f + i)$:

$$f + i = 0.35 + 0.02 = 0.37$$

$$L_b = \frac{V^2}{2g(f + i)} = \frac{(16.67)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.37}$$

$$L_b = \frac{277.89}{7.2594} = 38.28 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Total SSD** (1 mark)

$$\text{SSD} = L_{lag} + L_b = 41.67 + 38.28 = 79.95 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SSD} \approx 80 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Verification & Design Note (1 mark)**
- Compare with IRC recommendation: For 60 km/h, IRC suggests SSD ≈ 80 m. ✓
- This confirms the calculation is consistent with standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Forget to convert km/h to m/s** = lose 2 marks.
> - **Use wrong sign convention** for grade (use $f - i$ on upgrade, $f + i$ on downgrade) = lose 1 mark.
> - **Forget to state units clearly** = lose 0.5 mark for each missing unit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Q: (a)** What is the **Passenger Car Unit (PCU)**? Compute the **PCU flow** for a traffic stream with the following composition: **Cars = 500, Two-wheelers = 700, Buses = 40, Trucks = 80, Cycles = 150, Auto-rickshaws = 100** in one hour, using standard PCE values. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A truck of mass **20,000 kg** has a maximum engine power of **200 kW** and is travelling at a **constant speed of 40 km/h**. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.025** and the drivetrain efficiency is **0.88**. Calculate the **maximum sustainable gradient** for the truck. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Definition of PCU (2 marks)**

The **Passenger Car Unit (PCU)** is a measure used in traffic engineering to convert a mixed traffic stream into an equivalent stream of passenger cars for purposes of capacity analysis. The PCU of a vehicle depends on the vehicle's dimensions, speed, and the relative effect on traffic flow under prevailing conditions.

**Step 2: PCE Values Used (per IRC:SP:73)** (1 mark)

| Vehicle | PCE |
|---------|-----|
| Car | 1.0 |
| Two-wheeler | 0.5 |
| Bus | 3.0 |
| Truck | 3.0 |
| Cycle | 0.2 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 1.0 |

**Step 3: Calculate Individual PCU Contributions** (3 marks)

$$\text{PCU}_{car} = 500 \times 1.0 = 500$$

$$\text{PCU}_{2W} = 700 \times 0.5 = 350$$

$$\text{PCU}_{bus} = 40 \times 3.0 = 120$$

$$\text{PCU}_{truck} = 80 \times 3.0 = 240$$

$$\text{PCU}_{cycle} = 150 \times 0.2 = 30$$

$$\text{PCU}_{auto} = 100 \times 1.0 = 100$$

**Step 4: Sum and Report** (1 mark)

$$\text{Total PCU/hr} = 500 + 350 + 120 + 240 + 30 + 100 = \boxed{1340 \text{ PCU/hr}}$$

$$\text{Flow} = \frac{1340}{60} = 22.33 \text{ PCU/min}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Mass $M = 20{,}000$ kg
- Maximum power $P_{max} = 200$ kW $= 200{,}000$ W
- Speed $V = 40$ km/h $= \frac{40}{3.6} = 11.11$ m/s
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.025$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.88$

**Step 1: Force-Power Relationship** (2 marks)

For a vehicle to sustain **constant speed** on a gradient $i$, the engine power (after drivetrain losses) must balance the resistance forces:

$$P_{max} \cdot \eta = M \cdot g \cdot (f_r + i) \cdot V$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values** (2 marks)

$$200{,}000 \times 0.88 = 20{,}000 \times 9.81 \times (0.025 + i) \times 11.11$$

$$176{,}000 = 20{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 11.11 \times (0.025 + i)$$

$$176{,}000 = 2{,}180{,}322 \times (0.025 + i)$$

**Step 3: Solve for i** (2 marks)

$$0.025 + i = \frac{176{,}000}{2{,}180{,}322} = 0.0807$$

$$i = 0.0807 - 0.025 = 0.0557$$

$$\boxed{i \approx 5.57\%}$$

**Step 4: Design Interpretation (1 mark)**
The truck can maintain **40 km/h** on a gradient of approximately **5.6%**. This is well within the IRC maximum of 7% for mountainous terrain, confirming the design is feasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> - **Forgetting $\eta$** in the equation: leads to inflated gradient (over-estimation) = lose 2 marks.
> - **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in the speed term = lose 2 marks.
> - **Confusing PCE with PCU** (PCE is per vehicle; PCU is total for the stream) = lose 1 mark in conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Topic-Wise

> [!WARNING]
> **HIGH-RISK MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Speed unit conversion:** Always convert km/h → m/s by dividing by 3.6 in SSD, braking, and hill-climbing problems. This is the **#1 mark-loss area** in KTU evaluations.
>
> 2. **Gradient sign convention:** Upward slope = positive $i$, downward = negative $i$. In braking formulas, use $(f - i)$ for upgrade and $(f + i)$ for downgrade.
>
> 3. **Confusing static vs. dynamic PCE:** Some students use urban PCE for highway design or vice versa. Always mention the source (HCM / Indo-HCM / IRC:SP:73).
>
> 4. **Engine power vs. tractive power:** Engine power is at the crank; tractive power is at the wheels (after drivetrain losses). Use $\eta$ explicitly.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to state IRC references:** KTU examiners award bonus marks for **IRC:64, IRC:SP:73, MoRTH** citations. Always include them.
>
> 6. **Incomplete vehicle classification:** Listing only "cars and trucks" loses marks. Mention two-wheelers, auto-rickshaws, cycles, animal carts, and multi-axle combinations.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD QUICK REVISION — VEHICLE (MODULE 4)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ✅ Vehicle is the **fundamental unit of traffic flow** in traffic engineering.
- ✅ **Static characteristics:** length, width, height, wheelbase, GVW.
- ✅ **Dynamic characteristics:** speed, acceleration, deceleration, P/W ratio, reaction time.
- ✅ **Design vehicle** is the largest vehicle expected to use a facility frequently.

**Key Formulas (Memorize Cold):**
- ✅ $L_{lag} = V \cdot t_r$
- ✅ $L_{braking} = \dfrac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}$
- ✅ $\text{SSD} = V \cdot t_r + \dfrac{V^2}{2g(f \pm i)}$
- ✅ $F_r = f_r \cdot M \cdot g$
- ✅ $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V^2$
- ✅ $i_{max} = \dfrac{P_{max} \cdot \eta}{M \cdot g \cdot V} - f_r$

**Critical Constants (Must Quote):**
- ✅ $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ✅ $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- ✅ $t_r = 2.5$ s (IRC design reaction time)
- ✅ $f = 0.35$ (longitudinal friction for design)
- ✅ $C_D = 0.3$–$0.4$ (cars), $0.6$–$0.8$ (trucks)
- ✅ Air density = **1.2 kg/m³**
- ✅ Max legal width in India = **2.5 m**
- ✅ Max legal GVW in India = **49 tonnes**

**IRC Standards to Cite:**
- ✅ **IRC:64 – 1990** → Geometric design, design vehicle definitions.
- ✅ **IRC:SP:73 – 2015** → Indo-HCM, PCE values.
- ✅ **IRC:73 – 1980** → Geometric design standards.
- ✅ **MoRTH notifications** → Legal dimensions and weights.

**PCE Values (Most Tested):**
- ✅ Car = 1.0, Two-wheeler = 0.5, Auto = 1.0–1.5
- ✅ Bus = 3.0, Truck = 3.0, LCV = 1.5–2.0
- ✅ Cycle = 0.2, Animal cart = 0.4–1.0

**Conversion Tips:**
- ✅ 1 km/h = $\dfrac{1}{3.6}$ m/s
- ✅ Gradient % ÷ 100 = decimal $i$ (e.g., 3% = 0.03)
- ✅ 1 hp = 745.7 W; 1 kW = 1000 W

**Sign Convention for Grade in Formulas:**
- ✅ **Upgrade:** use $(f - i)$ → longer braking distance.
- ✅ **Downgrade:** use $(f + i)$ → shorter braking distance (counterintuitively, because gravity assists).

**Design Philosophy:**
- ✅ Always design for the **worst-case** scenario (downgrade braking, heaviest vehicle, longest SSD).
- ✅ PCU concept allows **mixed traffic to be normalized** to a common unit.
- ✅ Vehicle characteristics drive **lane width, shoulder width, vertical clearance, parking bay, intersection radius, and pavement thickness** — every geometric design element.

> **📌 Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, questions on **SSD (with numericals)** and **PCE calculation (with mixed traffic data)** are the **two most frequently asked** 14-mark questions. Practice both until the formulas and units become second nature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Driver and Environmental Factors - Pedestrian Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Driver and Environmental Factors - Pedestrian Safety # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Driver Factors in Road Safety ### Formal Definition **Driver characteristics** refer to the human physiological and psychological attributes that influence a driver's ability to perceive, react, and execute safe driving maneuvers. The driver is considered the most complex and unpredictable element within the **Road User (P) – Vehicle (V) – Road/Environment (E)** system triad, contributing to nearly **77%** of all road crashes as per global WHO and MoRTH statistics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Driver factors are evaluated using the **PIEV Model** (Perception – Identification – Emotion – Volition/Execution), with an aggregate reaction time of **2.5 seconds** adopted by IRC for geometric design. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a driver is like a **goalkeeper in football**. The goalkeeper must: 1. **Spot** the ball flying towards him (Perception) 2. **Recognize** which side it will go (Identification) 3. **Decide** whether to dive, punch, or catch (Emotion/Decision) 4. **Move** his body to the correct position (Volition/Execution) If any one of these stages is impaired (tired, distracted, or intoxicated), the goal is conceded. Similarly, a compromised driver cannot avoid a crash, even if the road is perfectly engineered. --- ## 1.2 Environmental Factors in Road Safety ### Formal Definition **Environmental factors** are external, non-controllable conditions prevailing at the roadway, including **lighting (lux), weather (precipitation, fog), road surface friction (skid number), and ambient temperature**, that directly influence driver perception, vehicle dynamics, and pedestrian vulnerability. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Visibility is measured in **lux (lx)**. A clear day provides **10,000 lx**, twilight **1,000 lx**, and street-lit roads must maintain a minimum of **30–50 lx** as per IS 1944 (Part II). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the **environment as the "stage lighting"** in a theatre. Even the best actor (driver) cannot perform safely if the stage is wet (low friction), smoky (fog), or dim (night). Pedestrians, being soft-bodied and unshielded, suffer the most in poor "lighting." --- ## 1.3 Pedestrian Safety – The Core Concern ### Formal Definition **Pedestrian safety** is the discipline of traffic engineering concerned with minimizing the risk of injury or fatality to people traveling on foot, by providing geometric design, control devices, and operational measures aligned with pedestrian perception-reaction characteristics, walking speeds, and gap acceptance behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Pedestrians constitute **40–50%** of all road fatalities in Indian cities (MoRTH 2022), and the globally accepted **pedestrian design walking speed is 1.2 m/sec (4.32 km/h)**, while the elderly/disabled design speed is **0.9 m/sec (3.24 km/h)**. ### Intuitive Analogy A pedestrian is the **"zero-tonnage vehicle"** – it has no airbags, no crumple zones, no seatbelts, and no engine noise to alert drivers. Therefore, traffic engineering must compensate by giving pedestrians **time, space, and visibility** through proper design. --- ## 1.4 The Human–Environment–Pedestrian Triad A consolidated overview of the three interacting domains governing this topic: | Domain | Sub-Component | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **Driver** | Vision, Hearing, Reaction | PIEV time, Reaction time, Visibility standards | | **Driver** | Distraction, Fatigue, DUI | Speed limits, Signage, Police enforcement | | **Environment** | Lighting, Weather, Friction | Sight distance, Skid-resistant surfaces | | **Pedestrian** | Walking speed, Gap acceptance | Crossing time, Pedestrian signals, Refuge islands | | **Pedestrian** | Age, Disability, Behaviour | Tactile paving, Curb ramps, Slow-speed zones | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PIEV Model (Driver Reaction Time Decomposition) The total driver reaction time is not a single instant – it is a **sequential cognitive process**. KTU frequently tests this model. ### Step-by-Step Logic of PIEV 1. **Perception (P):** The driver detects a stimulus (e.g., a pedestrian stepping off the curb). This involves ocular detection of light, shape, or motion. Average time = **0.5 sec**. 2. **Identification (I):** The driver recognizes the nature of the stimulus (child? adult? bicycle?). The brain compares the input with stored memory. Average time = **0.5 sec**. 3. **Emotion / Decision (E):** The driver decides the action (brake, swerve, honk). The brain weighs risk and reward. Average time = **1.0 sec**. 4. **Volition / Execution (V):** The driver physically applies the brake or steers. This involves neuromuscular delay. Average time = **0.5 sec**. $$\boxed{T_{PIEV} = T_P + T_I + T_E + T_V = 2.5 \text{ seconds (IRC standard)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why PIEV matters for Pedestrian Safety:** Pedestrian crossings must be located such that the approaching driver has at least the **PIEV reaction time** to detect the pedestrian, before the braking maneuver needs to begin. Hence, pedestrian crossings are placed **downstream** of intersection stop lines, where the driver is already alert. --- ## 2.2 Environmental Factors – Engineering Parameters ### 2.2.1 Visibility and Illumination - **Daytime Visibility** depends on contrast between pedestrian (dark silhouette) and background (sky/road). - **Night-time Visibility** depends on headlamp beam, retro-reflective clothing, and street lighting. - Critical factor: **Silhouette and Conspicuity.** Pedestrians wearing dark clothing at night are **3.7 times** more likely to be struck than those wearing reflective clothing. ### 2.2.2 Weather Impact - **Wet Pavement:** Reduces skid resistance (friction coefficient drops from 0.7 to 0.4). Pedestrian stopping distance increases by **30%**. - **Fog:** Reduces sight distance dramatically. IRC mandates **variable speed limits** on highways during fog. - **Heat:** Driver fatigue rises; reaction time may increase by **15%** above 40°C. ### 2.2.3 Road Surface Friction The **Side Friction Factor (f)** is the lateral acceleration component opposing comfortable turning: $$\boxed{f = \frac{V^2}{127 \cdot R} - e}$$ where **V** is speed in km/h, **R** is curve radius in m, and **e** is superelevation. For pedestrian crossings on curves, designers limit f to **0.10–0.15** to prevent skidding. --- ## 2.3 Pedestrian Walking Speed and Crossing Time The fundamental pedestrian design equation is the **Crossing Time Formula**: $$\boxed{t_c = \frac{W}{S_p} + t_s}$$ where: - $t_c$ = total pedestrian clearance time (seconds) - $W$ = width of roadway / crosswalk to be crossed (meters) - $S_p$ = pedestrian walking speed (m/sec) - $t_s$ = start-up / perception time ≈ **3 to 7 seconds** (as per HCM) ### KTU High-Yield Numerical Values | Pedestrian Class | Design Speed (m/sec) | Equivalent (km/h) | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | Adult, normal | **1.2** | 4.32 | Standard crosswalk design | | Elderly / Disabled | **0.9** | 3.24 | Hospital, Senior zones | | School children | **0.8** | 2.88 | School zones | | Fit runner | **1.8** | 6.48 | Sports / evacuation only | | Crowd / Evacuation | **0.6 – 1.0** | 2.16 – 3.60 | Bottleneck / flow density | --- ## 2.4 Pedestrian Gap Acceptance Theory A pedestrian will only cross a traffic stream if the **available gap (t_g)** between successive vehicles exceeds the **critical gap (t_c)** required to cross safely. $$\boxed{t_g \geq t_c = \frac{W}{S_p} + t_s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** The **Raff's Method** and **HCM Critical Gap Method** are both accepted. The HCM default critical gap is **6.0 sec** for an average pedestrian, rising to **9.0 sec** for elderly pedestrians. --- ## 2.5 Pedestrian Level of Service (PLOS) – Highway Capacity Manual (HCM) 2010 PLOS is determined by **average pedestrian space (m²/ped)** and **flow rate (ped/min/m)**. KTU expects students to know the **six LOS categories A–F**. $$\boxed{S_p = f(\text{Space per pedestrian, Speed, Flow rate, Delay})}$$ ### KTU High-Yield PLOS Table | LOS | Space (m²/ped) | Flow (ped/min/m) | Description | |---|---|---|---| | **A** | ≥ **12.0** | ≤ **16** | Free flow, no conflict | | **B** | 4.0 – 12.0 | 16 – 23 | Slight interactions | | **C** | 2.0 – 4.0 | 23 – 33 | Restricted walking | | **D** | 1.4 – 2.0 | 33 – 49 | Reduced speed, conflicts | | **E** | 0.6 – 1.4 | 49 – 82 | Crowded, shuffling | | **F** | < **0.6** | > **82** | Jammed, capacity exceeded | --- ## 2.6 Warrants for Pedestrian Crossing Facilities (IRC:103-2012) A pedestrian crossing is **warranted** when **any one** of the following is satisfied: $$\boxed{P \cdot V \geq 50{,}000 \text{ to } 1{,}00{,}000}$$ where: - $P$ = peak-hour pedestrian volume (pedestrians/hour) - $V$ = peak-hour vehicular volume (vehicles/hour) - Higher threshold (1,00,000) is for urban arterials; lower (50,000) for local streets. Additional warrants: - If pedestrian **delay exceeds 60 seconds** at unmarked crossing. - If **15% of pedestrians** cross at risk (jaywalking). - Presence of **schools, hospitals, bus stops** with vulnerable users. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** These warrants guide municipal engineers to install **zebra crossings, pelican signals, or pedestrian overpasses** based on actual demand. A blanket 200 m spacing of foot overbridges (as in some Indian cities) is irrational and often ignored by pedestrians. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Summary) | # | Formula | Variables | Unit | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{PIEV} = 2.5$ s | Reaction time | sec | Sight distance design | | 2 | $SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}$ | Stopping sight distance | m | Pedestrian crossing placement | | 3 | $t_c = \frac{W}{S_p} + t_s$ | Pedestrian clearance time | sec | Pedestrian signal design | | 4 | $S_p = 1.2$ m/s (standard) | Walking speed | m/s | All pedestrian facilities | | 5 | $f = \frac{V^2}{127R} - e$ | Side friction factor | – | Curves with crossings | | 6 | $P \cdot V \geq 50{,}000$ | Pedestrian warrant | – | Warrants | | 7 | PLOS A: $\geq 12$ m²/ped | Pedestrian space | m²/ped | Sidewalk LOS | | 8 | $D_p = \frac{1}{S_p} \times 60$ | Pedestrian density index | ped/m² | Crowd management | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Pedestrian Clearance Time (t_c) **Problem Statement:** Derive the time required for a pedestrian standing at the curb to clear a roadway of width **W** meters, given walking speed **S_p** and start-up perception time **t_s**. ### Step 1: Define the Total Crossing Event The crossing event consists of two distinct phases: - **Phase A (Start-up):** Pedestrian observes traffic, decides to cross, and lifts the leading foot. Duration = **t_s**. - **Phase B (Traversal):** Pedestrian physically walks across the full width **W** at speed **S_p**. ### Step 2: Express the Traversal Phase By the basic kinematic relation: $$\text{Time} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}}$$ $$T_{\text{traverse}} = \frac{W}{S_p}$$ ### Step 3: Add the Two Phases The total crossing time is the sum of the two phases (since the pedestrian must first decide, then walk): $$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{start-up}} + T_{\text{traverse}}$$ $$\boxed{t_c = t_s + \frac{W}{S_p}}$$ ### Step 4: Example Calculation A two-lane urban road has a **carriageway width W = 7.0 m**. Design walking speed **S_p = 1.2 m/s**. Start-up time **t_s = 5 s** (HCM default for signal design). Substituting: $$t_c = 5 + \frac{7.0}{1.2}$$ $$t_c = 5 + 5.833$$ $$\boxed{t_c = 10.83 \text{ seconds} \approx 11 \text{ seconds}}$$ This 11-second value is the **pedestrian clearance interval** to be programmed in the signal controller. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** If a signal gives pedestrians only 5 seconds (which happens at poorly-timed intersections), elderly users will still be on the carriageway when conflicting vehicle phase turns green – the most common cause of pedestrian fatalities at signals in India. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) and its Role in Pedestrian Safety **Problem Statement:** A vehicle approaches a pedestrian crossing at design speed **V (km/h)** on a road with coefficient of longitudinal friction **f** and a perception-reaction time of **t** seconds. The driver's eye height is above the pedestrian's point of contact on the road. Derive the **SSD** required for the vehicle to stop before the pedestrian. ### Step 1: Identify the Two Components of Braking Distance - **Lag Distance (d₁):** Distance traveled during PIEV reaction time, *before* brakes are applied. - **Braking Distance (d₂):** Distance traveled after brakes are applied, until the vehicle stops. ### Step 2: Derive the Lag Distance The vehicle travels at constant speed V during the reaction time **t**. Converting V from km/h to m/s by the factor **5/18** (since $1 \text{ km/h} = \frac{1000}{3600} = \frac{5}{18}$ m/s): $$V_{\text{m/s}} = \frac{5}{18} \cdot V$$ $$d_1 = V_{\text{m/s}} \cdot t = \frac{5}{18} \cdot V \cdot t = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t$$ ### Step 3: Derive the Braking Distance Using Work-Energy Equivalence By work-energy theorem, the kinetic energy of the vehicle is dissipated by the friction force over distance d₂: $$\frac{1}{2} m V_{\text{m/s}}^2 = m \cdot g \cdot f \cdot d_2$$ Solving for d₂: $$d_2 = \frac{V_{\text{m/s}}^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot f}$$ Substituting $V_{\text{m/s}} = \frac{5}{18} V$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²: $$d_2 = \frac{\left(\frac{5}{18} V\right)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot f} = \frac{V^2}{254.4 \cdot f}$$ For design purposes, the constant is rounded to **254**: $$d_2 = \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}$$ ### Step 4: Combine for Total SSD $$\boxed{SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}}$$ ### Step 5: Numerical Example Given: V = 60 km/h, t = 2.5 s, f = 0.35 (wet asphalt) $$d_1 = 0.278 \times 60 \times 2.5 = 41.7 \text{ m}$$ $$d_2 = \frac{60^2}{254 \times 0.35} = \frac{3600}{88.9} = 40.5 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{SSD = 41.7 + 40.5 = 82.2 \text{ m}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pedestrian Safety Implication:** Pedestrian crossings must be **visible from at least SSD meters away**. If the road geometry, a crest curve, or a billboard blocks the driver's view, a pedestrian stepping out will be hit at near-full speed – a near-fatal scenario. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Driver × Environment × Pedestrian Failure Modes The following matrix maps every combination of failure to a likely accident type, helping the engineer design countermeasures. | Driver Factor | Environment Factor | Pedestrian Factor | Crash Type | Countermeasure | |---|---|---|---|---| | Distraction (Phone) | Daylight, Clear | Jaywalking adult | Side-impact | Mid-block signal, Fines | | Drunk / DUI | Night, Poor light | Dark-clothed | Fatal hit | Reflective paint, Lighting | | Fatigue | Long, monotonous highway | Truck driver resting on shoulder | Run-over | Rest areas, Rumble strips | | Slow reaction (Elderly) | Wet road, low friction | School child running | Skid crash | Speed cushions, School zone | | Vision impairment | Fog, low contrast | Wheelchair user | Multi-victim | Audible signals, Tactile paving | | Aggression / Overspeed | Urban, narrow street | Crossing at signal | High-energy impact | Speed cameras, Speed tables | --- ## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Problem **Question (Model):** A pedestrian crossing is proposed on a 2-lane urban road of width **7.0 m**, with vehicle approach speed **V = 50 km/h**, driver PIEV time **t = 2.0 s**, and friction coefficient **f = 0.40**. Pedestrian walking speed **S_p = 1.2 m/s** and start-up time **t_s = 5 s**.
