Find: (i) Pedestrian clearance time (ii) Minimum SSD at the crossing (iii) Comment on safety. ### Solution **(i) Pedestrian clearance time:** $$t_c = t_s + \frac{W}{S_p}$$ $$t_c = 5 + \frac{7.0}{1.2}$$ $$t_c = 5 + 5.833 = 10.833 \text{ s} \approx 11 \text{ s}$$ **(ii) Stopping Sight Distance:** $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}$$ $$SSD = 0.278 \times 50 \times 2.0 + \frac{50^2}{254 \times 0.40}$$ $$SSD = 27.8 + \frac{2500}{101.6}$$ $$SSD = 27.8 + 24.6 = 52.4 \text{ m}$$ **(iii) Safety Comment:** The SSD of **52.4 m** is more than the **30 m** advance warning distance typically available in built-up areas, so the crossing is **geometrically safe**. However, the clearance time of **11 s** is adequate only for healthy adults; elderly users at 0.9 m/s would need **5 + 7.0/0.9 = 12.78 s**. Hence, an **all-red pedestrian phase of 13 s** is recommended. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This problem combines the three pillars of the topic – driver (reaction), environment (friction), and pedestrian (walking speed) – into a single integrated design check. KTU regularly tests such integrated numerics. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The PIEV Reaction Process (Functional Flow) This diagram illustrates the sequential cognitive stages a driver passes through from stimulus to action. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Stimulus: Pedestrian on Road] --> B[P - Perception
Driver Detects the Object
0.5 sec] B --> C[I - Identification
Recognizes Pedestrian vs Vehicle
0.5 sec] C --> D[E - Emotion / Decision
Brake Swerve or Honk
1.0 sec] D --> E[V - Volition Execution
Foot Lifts Accelerator to Brake
0.5 sec] E --> F[Braking Distance Traveled] F --> G[Vehicle Stops Before Pedestrian] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The total time from **A to E** is the **2.5-second PIEV reaction time**. The **F stage** is the braking distance. The sum of distances covered in A→E (lag distance) and F (braking distance) is the **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)**. Any environmental factor (rain, fog) extends the **F** stage significantly. --- ## 4.2 Pedestrian Crossing Design Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT CONDITIONS] A1[Roadway Width W] A2[Pedestrian Walking Speed Sp] A3[Driver PIEV Time t] A4[Vehicle Approach Speed V] A5[Skid Friction f] end subgraph COMPUTE[ENGINEERING COMPUTATIONS] B1[Pedestrian Clearance Time tc] B2[Stopping Sight Distance SSD] B3[Pedestrian Level of Service PLOS] B4[Gap Acceptance Check] end subgraph OUTPUT[DESIGN DELIVERABLES] C1[Signal Timing Diagram] C2[Refuge Island Size] C3[Signage and Marking Plan] C4[Lighting Layout in lux] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B2 A4 --> B2 A4 --> B4 A5 --> B2 A2 --> B3 A1 --> B3 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C3 B3 --> C2 B4 --> C1 B1 --> C4 style INPUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style COMPUTE fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style OUTPUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Hierarchy of Pedestrian Facility Selection (Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start: Pedestrian Demand at Location]) --> Q1{Vehicle Volume
per Hour} Q1 -->|Less than 200| R1[No Special Facility
Use Sidewalk only] Q1 -->|200 to 750| Q2{Pedestrian Volume
per Hour} Q1 -->|Greater than 750| Q3{School Hospital Nearby} Q2 -->|Less than 50| R1 Q2 -->|50 to 100| R2[Marked Zebra Crossing] Q2 -->|Greater than 100| R3[Pelican Signal Crossing] Q3 -->|Yes| R4[Grade Separated FOBr or Subway] Q3 -->|No| Q4{PV Product
Warrant} Q4 -->|Greater than 50000| R3 Q4 -->|Greater than 100000| R4 style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** This decision tree is directly derived from **IRC:103-2012** warrants and is the standard reference used by KTU for exam questions on facility selection. --- ## 4.4 Environmental Factor Impact Matrix (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB ENV[Environmental Conditions] --> VIS[Visibility Factor] ENV --> FRIC[Friction Factor] ENV --> NOIS[Noise and Distraction] VIS --> DRV[Driver PIEV Time] VIS --> PED[Pedestrian Conspicuity] FRIC --> BRK[Braking Distance] NOIS --> COG[Cognitive Overload] DRV --> SSD[Effective SSD] BRK --> SSD PED --> SAFE[Pedestrian Safety Index] COG --> SAFE style ENV fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SSD fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SAFE fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the PIEV model. State the standard value of total PIEV time adopted by IRC for geometric design.** **Model Answer:** The PIEV model decomposes the driver's response time into four sequential cognitive stages: - **P – Perception (≈ 0.5 s):** Detection of a stimulus. - **I – Identification (≈ 0.5 s):** Recognition of the stimulus type. - **E – Emotion / Decision (≈ 1.0 s):** Deciding on the appropriate response. - **V – Volition / Execution (≈ 0.5 s):** Physically executing the action (e.g., applying brake). $$\boxed{T_{PIEV} = 0.5 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ seconds}}$$ This **2.5 s** is the IRC standard used in computing stopping sight distance. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any six environmental factors that affect pedestrian safety on urban roads.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Visibility** – Day, night, fog, glare. 2. **Road surface friction** – Wet, dry, polished surface. 3. **Ambient lighting** – Street-lighting levels in lux. 4. **Weather conditions** – Rain, snow, heat, wind. 5. **Road geometry** – Curve radius, gradient, vertical alignment. 6. **Obstructions to sight line** – Billboards, parked cars, vegetation. 7. **Noise and air pollution** – Affect driver cognition and pedestrian health. 8. **Pavement surface quality** – Uneven surface increases pedestrian trip hazards. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the **driver characteristics** affecting road safety, with reference to the **PIEV model**. (7 marks) **(b)** A pedestrian crossing is proposed on a 2-lane urban road of width **7.5 m**, with vehicle approach speed **V = 65 km/h**, driver PIEV time **t = 2.5 s**, and coefficient of longitudinal friction **f = 0.35**. Pedestrian walking speed is **1.2 m/s** and start-up time is **5 s**. Calculate:     (i) Pedestrian clearance time     (ii) Stopping sight distance required at the crossing     (iii) Comment on the safety of the proposed design. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a): **Driver characteristics** that govern safety are: **1. Physical Characteristics:** - **Vision:** Visual acuity (must be 6/6 for licensing). Field of vision spans ~180°. The sharpest focus is within a 3° cone (foveal vision); beyond 10°, peripheral vision kicks in but acuity drops. Static and dynamic visual acuity differ – at 100 km/h, dynamic acuity is 20% lower than static. - **Hearing:** Useful for detecting sirens, horns. Above age 50, hearing drops by ~25 dB. - **Reaction Time:** Average 0.5–0.75 s for simple stimulus, but up to 2.5 s in complex traffic (PIEV). - **Perception-Reaction Time (PRT):** Total time from stimulus to first brake application = **2.5 s** (IRC standard). **2. Mental / Psychological Characteristics:** - **Intelligence, Skill, Experience:** Affect identification and decision quality. - **Distraction, Fatigue, Emotional State:** Inattentional blindness is the leading modern cause. - **Attitude towards risk:** Aggressive drivers violate rules. **3. The PIEV Model:** | Stage | Function | Time (s) | |---|---|---| | Perception | Detect object via sight/hearing | 0.5 | | Identification | Recognize the object | 0.5 | | Emotion / Decision | Choose appropriate response | 1.0 | | Volition / Execution | Physically apply control | 0.5 | | **Total PIEV** | | **2.5** | > **Engineering Implication:** The 2.5 s PIEV is used directly in SSD calculations and in deciding the placement distance of pedestrian crossings from intersections. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b): **Given:** W = 7.5 m, V = 65 km/h, t = 2.5 s, f = 0.35, S_p = 1.2 m/s, t_s = 5 s. **(i) Pedestrian Clearance Time:** $$t_c = t_s + \frac{W}{S_p}$$ $$t_c = 5 + \frac{7.5}{1.2}$$ $$t_c = 5 + 6.25$$ $$\boxed{t_c = 11.25 \text{ seconds}}$$ **(ii) Stopping Sight Distance:** $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}$$ $$SSD = 0.278 \times 65 \times 2.5 + \frac{65^2}{254 \times 0.35}$$ $$SSD = 45.175 + \frac{4225}{88.9}$$ $$SSD = 45.175 + 47.525$$ $$\boxed{SSD = 92.7 \text{ m}}$$ **(iii) Safety Comment:** - The pedestrian clearance time of **11.25 s** is marginally sufficient for healthy adults at 1.2 m/s, but is **inadequate for elderly users (would need 13.3 s)**. Recommendation: increase the all-red pedestrian phase to **14 s**. - The SSD of **92.7 m** requires the crossing to be visible from ~93 m upstream. Any billboard, vegetation, or vertical curve within this sight triangle will compromise safety. - The friction factor of **0.35** is a dry-road value; the design should be checked for **f = 0.20 (wet)** giving SSD = 134 m, which is the governing design SSD for pedestrian safety in monsoon conditions. - **Verdict:** Design is marginally safe in dry conditions; **unsafe in wet conditions** unless additional warning signs and reduced speed limits (40 km/h) are imposed. --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Discuss the various **environmental factors** that influence pedestrian safety. How do visibility, road surface, and weather conditions affect pedestrian accidents? (7 marks) **(b)** What is **Pedestrian Level of Service (PLOS)**? Explain the **six LOS categories** (A to F) as per the Highway Capacity Manual (HCM), with the corresponding pedestrian space and flow rate values. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a): **Environmental factors** are external, non-driver conditions that elevate pedestrian crash risk: **1. Visibility and Lighting:** - **Day vs Night:** Approximately **70% of pedestrian fatalities in India occur at night**, even though night traffic volume is only ~25% of day. Street lighting below **30 lx** dramatically increases crash risk. - **Glare and Contrast:** Headlamp glare from opposing traffic temporarily blinds drivers for 2–3 s, during which a pedestrian at 1.2 m/s moves ~3 m – often into the conflict zone. - **Conspicuity:** Dark-clothed pedestrians are 3.7× more likely to be hit at night than those with retro-reflective markers. **2. Road Surface Conditions:** - **Friction:** A wet pavement reduces friction coefficient from 0.7 to ~0.4. For a vehicle traveling at 50 km/h, this **doubles the braking distance**. - **Surface Evenness:** Uneven footpaths cause pedestrian trips, pushing them onto the carriageway. - **Drainage:** Standing water on footpaths forces pedestrians to walk on the road, increasing exposure. **3. Weather Conditions:** - **Rain:** Reduces both driver visibility and pavement friction. Pedestrians also use umbrellas that block their peripheral vision. - **Fog:** Reduces sight distance to < 30 m, leaving inadequate SSD for safe stopping. - **Heat:** Increases driver fatigue and reaction time by ~15% above 40°C ambient. - **Snow/Ice:** Surface friction drops below 0.15; SSD becomes impractical, requiring road closure. **4. Road Geometry:** - Long, straight roads encourage high speeds, worsening crash severity. - Sharp curves limit forward visibility at crossings. **5. Surrounding Land Use:** - Mixed-use areas with shops, bus stops, and schools generate high pedestrian–vehicle conflict. > **Conclusion:** The 3E framework (Engineering, Enforcement, Education) must be applied to environmental factors: e.g., **Engineering** improves lighting and friction; **Enforcement** imposes night-time speed limits; **Education** promotes reflective clothing. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b): **Pedestrian Level of Service (PLOS)** is a **qualitative measure** describing pedestrian travel conditions, as perceived by the user, based on factors such as **speed, density, space per pedestrian, and delay at intersections**. **Reference:** Highway Capacity Manual (HCM) 2010, Chapter 16 (Urban Street Facilities) and Chapter 23 (Pedestrians). **Six LOS Categories:** | LOS | Space (m²/ped) | Flow (ped/min/m) | Description | Typical Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **A** | ≥ 12.0 | ≤ 16 | Free flow; walking speed freely chosen; no conflicts | Weekend suburban park walkway | | **B** | 4.0 – 12.0 | 16 – 23 | Slight interactions; other pedestrians detectable | Office corridor at off-peak | | **C** | 2.0 – 4.0 | 23 – 33 | Restricted walking; passing impossible | Footpath near metro station peak | | **D** | 1.4 – 2.0 | 33 – 49 | Reduced speed; frequent conflicts | Footpath near market entrance | | **E** | 0.6 – 1.4 | 49 – 82 | Crowded; shuffling; forward progress only by consensus | Religious gathering, concert | | **F** | < 0.6 | > 82 | Jammed; complete stoppage; capacity exceeded | Overcrowded festival, station stampede | > **KTU Note:** The **mode threshold is 0.6 m²/ped**, below which pedestrians are in physical contact – this is the **crush load** and corresponds to **Stampede Risk**. Crowd control design must keep PLOS at **D or better**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Do **NOT** confuse **PLOS with Vehicular LOS** – they have completely different units. PLOS uses **m²/pedestrian**, not veh/km/lane. > - Do **NOT** state PIEV time as **1.5 s** – the **IRC and KTU accepted value is 2.5 s**. > - For numerical problems, always use **S_p = 1.2 m/s for healthy adults and 0.9 m/s for elderly**; using 1.5 m/s (as in some Western codes) is wrong for Indian design. > - In warrant calculations, the **PV product threshold is 50,000 (local) to 1,00,000 (arterial)**, NOT 1,00,000 universally. Read the question carefully. > - In **environmental factors**, students often forget to mention **audible / tactile signals for visually-impaired pedestrians** – this is a frequent 2-mark loss. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this on the night before the exam):** - **Driver Factors:** - PIEV = Perception + Identification + Emotion + Volition = **2.5 s (IRC)**. - Vision is the dominant sense; visual acuity drops with speed and age. - Distraction, fatigue, DUI are the top killers of attention. - **Environmental Factors:** - **Visibility** governs SSD; minimum street lighting = **30–50 lx**. - **Friction (f):** dry = 0.7, wet = 0.4, icy = 0.15. - **Side friction (f) = V²/(127R) − e**, limited to **0.10–0.15** for pedestrian comfort. - **Pedestrian Design Speeds:** - Standard adult = **1.2 m/s (4.32 km/h)**. - Elderly / disabled = **0.9 m/s (3.24 km/h)**. - School children = **0.8 m/s (2.88 km/h)**. - **Crossing Time Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** - $t_c = t_s + \dfrac{W}{S_p}$ - $t_s$ (start-up) = **3 to 7 s**; use **5 s** unless specified. - **SSD Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** - $SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}$ - Always evaluate for **wet condition (f = 0.20 to 0.35)** when pedestrian safety is critical. - **Pedestrian Warrants (IRC:103):** - $P \times V \geq 50{,}000$ (local) to **1,00,000** (arterial). - Or pedestrian delay ≥ 60 s. - Special users (schools, hospitals) override the warrant. - **PLOS (HCM 2010):** - Six categories A to F; threshold = **0.6 m²/ped**. - PLOS A: ≥ 12 m²/ped; PLOS F: < 0.6 m²/ped. - Design target for cities: **PLOS C or better**. - **Common Engineering Pitfalls:** - Do NOT provide only a 5 s pedestrian green phase on a 7 m road. - Do NOT place bus stops immediately downstream of a pedestrian crossing. - Do NOT install foot overbridges without escalators – pedestrians will not use them. - Do NOT design footpaths narrower than **1.8 m** (absolute minimum per IRC). - Always check SSD in **wet conditions**, not just dry. - **Key Code References (do NOT cite as textbook sources – use only as code names):** - **IRC:103-2012** – Pedestrian Facility Guidelines. - **IS 1944 (Part II)** – Lighting of Urban Roads. - **HCM 2010, Chapter 16 & 23** – Pedestrian LOS. - **MoRTH Accident Report** – National crash statistics. - **Quick Mnemonic for PIEV:** **"Please Identify Every Vehicle"** → Perception, Identification, Emotion, Vehicle-response.

topics.title: Collection and Statistical Analysis of Accident Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collection and Statistical Analysis of Accident Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Accident Data Collection** is the systematic process of gathering, recording, and documenting information about road traffic accidents from multiple authoritative sources (police, hospitals, insurance agencies, and road authorities) using standardized accident report forms. The objective is to create a reliable, comprehensive, and retrievable database that supports engineering analysis, policy formation, and safety interventions.

**Statistical Analysis of Accident Data** refers to the application of mathematical and probabilistic techniques — such as descriptive statistics, rate analysis, frequency distributions (Poisson, Binomial), tests of significance (Chi-square, t-test), and regression modeling — to identify patterns, causative factors, risk locations (black spots), and the effectiveness of remedial measures in road traffic accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET637 Module 4):**
> The course outcome emphasizes the student's ability to *identify, classify, and statistically interpret accident data* to recommend engineering counter-measures. Statistical analysis is the *quantitative backbone* of every traffic safety audit submitted to the Kerala Public Works Department (KPWD) and the Motor Vehicles Department (MVD).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Forensic Detective"**
> Imagine a detective arriving at a crime scene. He does not guess; he collects every fingerprint, CCTV clip, witness statement, and physical evidence. He then feeds this into a forensic lab where statisticians compute *probability of suspect*, *frequency of similar crimes in that locality*, and *severity index* of injuries. The detective's final report drives the legal and preventive action.
> 
> In exactly the same way, a traffic engineer acts as a **forensic detective of the road**. The "crime scene" is the accident location. "Fingerprints" are the skid marks, brake marks, and debris. "Witnesses" are the police FIR, hospital records, and driver statements. The "forensic lab" is the statistical analysis block, which converts raw reports into **accident rates, severity indices, and black-spot maps**, which then drive engineering interventions like road realignment, signal installation, and speed-calming measures.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Black Spot** | A road location where ≥ 5 accidents or ≥ 10 fatalities occur within a 500 m stretch per year | Mandatory identification under IRC:SP:88 |
| **Accident Severity Index** | Ratio of fatalities to total accidents × 100 | Used in MORTH ranking of states |
| **Exposure** | Measure of opportunity for accident (veh-km, population, time) | Denominator in all rate calculations |
| **EPDO** | Equivalent Property Damage Only | Combines fatal/injury/property damage into one number |
| **MAIS** | Maximum Abbreviated Injury Scale | Medical severity scale (1 = minor, 6 = untreatable) |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a **favourite 14-mark question** in ESE because it tests both *practical data-handling* and *mathematical reasoning*.
- Kerala has one of the **highest accident rates in India** (≈ 4,500 fatalities/year as per recent MORTH data), making this topic state-relevant.
- Direct questions appear on *accident rate calculation*, *Chi-square test*, and *severity index*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Accident Data (Six Pillars)

The KTU syllabus recognizes **six primary sources** of accident data. Each has strengths and limitations that examiners love to test.

### A. Police Reports (First Information Report — FIR)
- The **most widely used and legally binding** source in India.
- Filed under Section 279, 304A, and 337/338 of the Indian Penal Code.
- **Strength:** Comprehensive (location, time, vehicle type, casualties).
- **Limitation:** Under-reporting of minor (property-damage-only) accidents; missing data on pre-crash conditions.

### B. Hospital / Medical Records
- Records from casualty wards of Government Medical Colleges, District Hospitals, and private hospitals.
- **Strength:** Accurate *medical severity* classification (MAIS, ISS scores).
- **Limitation:** No road-environment details; only post-crash data.

### C. Insurance Company Records
- Claim forms (Form 1 to Form 46 under IRDA regulations).
- **Strength:** Captures *property-damage-only* accidents that police miss.
- **Limitation:** Biased toward insured vehicles; commercial vehicles over-represented.

### D. Road Authority Records
- Maintenance logs, road inspection reports, photographs, and CCTV footage from NHAI/PWD.
- **Strength:** Provides *road geometric data* (curvature, gradient, sight distance).
- **Limitation:** Reactive, not proactive.

### E. Driver / Self-Reporting
- Voluntary online platforms (e.g., iRAD — Integrated Road Accident Database).
- **Strength:** Real-time, geo-tagged.
- **Limitation:** Voluntary bias; non-representative sample.

### F. Special Studies / Spot Studies
- Field investigations by traffic engineers — on-site collision reconstruction.
- **Strength:** Highest accuracy; used for court evidence.
- **Limitation:** Expensive; conducted only at high-risk sites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification Principle:** KTU examiners expect students to mention that *no single source is sufficient*. A robust accident database must **triangulate** at least three sources (typically police + hospital + road authority) to eliminate bias.

## 2.2 Components of a Standard Accident Report Form (IRC Format)

The Indian Roads Congress (IRC) and the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) prescribe the following data fields:

1. **Accident Identification:** Report number, date, time, day of week, FIR number.
2. **Location Data:** Highway number, chainage (km), latitude/longitude, urban/rural, intersection type, road classification.
3. **Road Environment:** Surface type (bitumen/concrete), condition (wet/dry), lighting, weather, gradient, curvature.
4. **Vehicle Data:** Type (2W/3W/4W/HCV), registration number, age, condition, loading status.
5. **Driver Data:** Age, gender, license validity, alcohol/drug influence, fatigue indicators.
6. **Casualty Data:** Fatal / Grievous Injury / Minor Injury / No Injury, MAIS score.
7. **Crash Type:** Head-on, rear-end, side-swipe, hit-pedestrian, hit-fixed-object, overturning, run-off-road.
8. **Contributing Factors:** Over-speeding, wrong-side driving, signal violation, mechanical failure.

## 2.3 Statistical Analysis Methods (KTU-Relevant Toolkit)

### A. Descriptive Statistics
- Mean, median, mode of accident counts.
- Standard deviation and coefficient of variation (CV) to assess spread.
- Frequency histograms showing *hour-of-day* and *day-of-week* distributions.

### B. Accident Rate (Rate-Based Analysis)
- **Rate is the heart of KTU exam questions.** Always expressed as *accidents per unit exposure*.
- Three denominators are commonly used:
  - Per million vehicle-kilometers (for road sections).
  - Per 1,000 registered vehicles (for vehicle fleets).
  - Per 100,000 population (for national/state-level comparison).

### C. Accident Severity Index (ASI)
- Quantifies the *fatality intensity* of a location or period.
- ASI = (Fatal accidents / Total accidents) × 100
- Higher ASI = more lethal crashes = higher priority for intervention.

### D. EPDO (Equivalent Property Damage Only) Method
- Converts injury crashes into property-damage equivalents.
- EPDO = 9.5 × (Fatal + Major Injury) + 3.5 × (Minor Injury) + 1.0 × (PDO)
- *(Weights may vary by jurisdiction; the above are the FHWA defaults.)*

### E. Chi-Square Test (χ²)
- Tests whether observed accident frequencies differ *significantly* from expected.
- **Application:** Before-after studies (did the new signal reduce accidents *significantly*?).
- Degrees of freedom: df = (rows − 1) × (columns − 1).

### F. Poisson Distribution
- Models the *rare-event* nature of accidents.
- P(x) = (e^(−λ) × λ^x) / x!
- Used to compute the *probability of "n" accidents occurring at a black spot in a year*.

### G. Regression Analysis
- Models accident frequency as a function of exposure and risk factors.
- Form: Y = a + b₁X₁ + b₂X₂ + ... + bₖXₖ
- Used to predict future accident counts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{AR}_{\text{road}} = \dfrac{A \times 10^6}{V \times L}$ | Accident rate per million vehicle-km | A = accidents, V = AADT, L = length (km) |
| 2 | $\text{AR}_{\text{veh}} = \dfrac{A}{N} \times 1000$ | Accident rate per 1000 vehicles | N = registered vehicles |
| 3 | $\text{AR}_{\text{pop}} = \dfrac{A}{P} \times 100{,}000$ | Accident rate per 100,000 population | P = population |
| 4 | $\text{ASI} = \dfrac{F}{A} \times 100$ | Accident Severity Index (%) | F = fatal accidents |
| 5 | $\text{EPDO} = 9.5(F + MI) + 3.5(mI) + 1.0(PDO)$ | Equivalent Property Damage Only | F=fatal, MI=major inj, mI=minor inj, PDO=property damage only |
| 6 | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | Chi-square statistic | O = observed, E = expected |
| 7 | $P(x) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$ | Poisson probability of x events | λ = mean accident count |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \times \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Accident reduction ratio (before-after) | A = accidents, N = exposure |
| 9 | $\text{ROS} = \dfrac{A \times 10^6}{\text{VMT}}$ | Rate of occurrence of accidents (per 100 million VMT) | For high-volume roads |
| 10 | $\text{Fatality Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Fatalities}}{10^5 \text{ pop}}$ | WHO standard | Global comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem is given, examiners expect you to:
> 1. State the formula clearly.
> 2. Substitute the values with units.
> 3. Show intermediate arithmetic.
> 4. Box the final answer with units.
> 
> Marks are usually split as: Formula (2 marks) → Substitution (2 marks) → Final answer with units (1 mark).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Black-spot identification** in Kerala (e.g., the six identified hotspots on NH-66, NH-544, and MC Road).
- **Countermeasure evaluation:** Before-after analysis of the Kochi Metro corridor (accidents reduced by ~28% post-construction).
- **Insurance premium calibration** by IRDA using accident severity indices.
- **Smart-city planning:** AI-driven crash prediction using historical data + regression models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Accident Rate per Million Vehicle-Kilometers

This is the **most tested formula** in KTU Module 4.

**Starting Concept:**
We want to measure *how accident-prone a road section is*. The natural way is to count accidents (A) and "normalize" by how much travel happened on that section.

**Step 1 — Define Exposure:**
Exposure for a road section = Traffic volume (V) × Section length (L) × Time period (typically 1 year = 365 days).

$$\text{Exposure} = V \times L \times 365 \quad \text{[vehicle-km per year]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Million Vehicle-Kilometers:**
Since accident counts are small (often 5–50 per year), we scale the denominator to "millions" for readability.

$$\text{Exposure in million veh-km} = \dfrac{V \times L \times 365}{10^6}$$

**Step 3 — Define Accident Rate:**
The rate is the number of accidents per unit exposure.

$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{A}{\text{Exposure in million veh-km}} = \dfrac{A \times 10^6}{V \times L \times 365}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Approximation:**
If V is the *Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT)*, then V × 365 ≈ Annual vehicle count passing a point. Combined with length L, this gives vehicle-km/year. So the final form used in textbooks is:

$$\boxed{\text{AR} = \dfrac{A \times 10^6}{V \times L}}$$

where A = number of accidents in the analysis period (usually 1 year), V = AADT (veh/day), L = length of section (km).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Check:** Numerator is dimensionless (count × 10^6). Denominator is veh/day × km. So AR has units of **accidents per million vehicle-km**. This is the standard unit used by IRC, MoRTH, and WHO.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Accident Rate on a National Highway Stretch

**Problem Statement:**
A 12 km stretch of NH-66 near Thrissur recorded 48 accidents in one year. The AADT measured by a roadside survey was 15,000 veh/day. Calculate the accident rate per million vehicle-km.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**
- A = 48 accidents/year
- V = 15,000 veh/day
- L = 12 km

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{A \times 10^6}{V \times L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{48 \times 10^6}{15{,}000 \times 12}$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$15{,}000 \times 12 = 180{,}000 \text{ veh-km/day}$$

**Step 5 — Compute ratio:**

$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{48 \times 10^6}{180{,}000} = \dfrac{48{,}000{,}000}{180{,}000}$$

$$\text{AR} = 266.67 \text{ accidents per million vehicle-km}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A rate of 266.67 is **significantly above the Indian national average of ~100**, indicating this stretch is a candidate for detailed engineering audit and remedial measures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution (typically 5 marks):**
> - Formula: 1 mark
> - Substitution: 1 mark
> - Arithmetic: 2 marks
> - Interpretation / units: 1 mark

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Accident Severity Index (ASI) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
During a 2-year study, a 4-legged intersection in Kochi recorded:
- 6 fatal accidents
- 14 accidents with grievous injuries
- 22 accidents with minor injuries
- 38 property-damage-only (PDO) accidents
- **Total: 80 accidents**

Calculate the **Accident Severity Index**.

**Step 1 — Identify values:**
- F (fatal accidents) = 6
- A (total accidents) = 80

**Step 2 — Apply formula:**

$$\text{ASI} = \dfrac{F}{A} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\text{ASI} = \dfrac{6}{80} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$\text{ASI} = 0.075 \times 100 = 7.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
7.5% of all accidents at this intersection are fatal. The IRC benchmark for urban intersections is **< 5%**. Hence this intersection is in the **high-severity category** and warrants immediate engineering intervention (e.g., signal phasing review, pedestrian refuge, speed tables).

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — EPDO Method (Multi-Crash Type Aggregation)

**Problem Statement:**
A 5 km road stretch recorded the following in a year:
- 3 fatal accidents
- 8 major-injury accidents
- 25 minor-injury accidents
- 64 PDO accidents

Calculate the **EPDO score** using FHWA weights (9.5, 3.5, 1.0).

**Step 1 — Recall EPDO formula:**

$$\text{EPDO} = 9.5 \times (F + MI) + 3.5 \times (mI) + 1.0 \times (PDO)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{EPDO} = 9.5 \times (3 + 8) + 3.5 \times (25) + 1.0 \times (64)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$9.5 \times 11 = 104.5$$
$$3.5 \times 25 = 87.5$$
$$1.0 \times 64 = 64$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$\text{EPDO} = 104.5 + 87.5 + 64 = 256$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A single EPDO score (256) allows direct ranking of this stretch against other stretches in the same city. Higher EPDO = higher priority for safety intervention.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Chi-Square (χ²) Test for Before-After Study

**Problem Statement:**
A new traffic signal was installed at an intersection. Before installation, 36 accidents occurred in one year. After installation, 24 accidents occurred in one year. The traffic volume was approximately the same in both periods. Test at the **5% significance level** whether the signal caused a *significant reduction*.

**Step 1 — Set up hypotheses:**
- H₀ (Null): There is no significant difference in accident counts.
- H₁ (Alternative): The signal reduced accidents significantly.

**Step 2 — Build the 2×2 Contingency Table:**

| Period | Accidents | No-Accidents (Exposure) | Row Total |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Before | O₁₁ = 36 | O₁₂ = N₁ − 36 | N₁ = ? |
| After  | O₂₁ = 24 | O₂₂ = N₂ − 24 | N₂ = ? |

**Step 3 — Compute expected frequencies:**

$$E_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Row Total}_i \times \text{Column Total}_j}{\text{Grand Total}}$$

If total accident-prone "trials" are similar (say 1000 each), the expected accident count under H₀ is the *average* = (36 + 24)/2 = 30 in each period.

**Step 4 — Apply Chi-Square formula:**

$$\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$\chi^2 = \dfrac{(36 - 30)^2}{30} + \dfrac{(24 - 30)^2}{30} = \dfrac{36}{30} + \dfrac{36}{30} = 1.2 + 1.2 = 2.4$$

**Step 6 — Compare with critical value:**
Degrees of freedom (df) for 2×2 table = (2−1)(2−1) = 1. Critical value of χ² at 5% level for df=1 is **3.841**.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
Since 2.4 < 3.841, we **fail to reject H₀**. The reduction from 36 to 24 is **not statistically significant at 5% level**, even though it appears practically meaningful. The traffic engineer should *not* declare the signal a success based on this small sample alone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse "practical reduction" with "statistical significance". Always run the test before claiming success of any countermeasure.

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Poisson Distribution for Black-Spot Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A notorious black spot has averaged λ = 2 accidents per month over the last 5 years. Find the probability that **5 or more accidents** occur in any given month.

**Step 1 — Apply Poisson formula:**

$$P(x) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

**Step 2 — Compute P(x ≥ 5) = 1 − P(x ≤ 4):**

$$P(x \leq 4) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4)$$

**Step 3 — Individual probabilities (λ = 2):**

$$P(0) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \times 2^0}{0!} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

$$P(1) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \times 2^1}{1!} = 2 e^{-2} \approx 0.2707$$

$$P(2) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \times 2^2}{2!} = 2 e^{-2} \approx 0.2707$$

$$P(3) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \times 2^3}{3!} = \dfrac{8 e^{-2}}{6} \approx 0.1804$$

$$P(4) = \dfrac{e^{-2} \times 2^4}{4!} = \dfrac{16 e^{-2}}{24} \approx 0.0902$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$P(x \leq 4) \approx 0.1353 + 0.2707 + 0.2707 + 0.1804 + 0.0902 = 0.9473$$

**Step 5 — Complement:**

$$P(x \geq 5) = 1 - 0.9473 = 0.0527 \approx 5.27\%$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Insight:**
There is a **~5% chance per month** that this black spot will see 5+ accidents. Over 12 months, the expected number of such "high-fatality" months is ≈ 0.63. Engineers use this to **prioritize emergency response planning and high-visibility patrolling**.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Statistical Test

| Research Question | Recommended Test | Why? |
|-------------------|------------------|------|
| Is the accident count at this site different from the average? | Poisson / Chi-square | Count data, rare events |
| Did a countermeasure reduce accidents significantly? | Before-After χ² test | Two categorical samples |
| What is the relationship between AADT and accident count? | Linear regression | Continuous predictor |
| Are accident rates in 2 cities different? | Z-test for proportions | Comparing two rates |
| What is the trend over 10 years? | Time-series regression | Time-dependent variable |
| Is the spatial distribution of accidents random? | Moran's I / GIS clustering | Spatial autocorrelation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Accident Data Collection & Analysis Workflow

This is the master workflow that examiners expect students to draw for a "describe the methodology" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Accident Occurs on Road] --> B[Police FIR Lodged]
    A --> C[Hospital Admission]
    A --> D[Insurance Claim Filed]
    A --> E[Road Authority Investigation]
    A --> F[Public Self-Report iRAD]
    
    B --> G[Central Accident Database]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    G --> H[Data Cleaning and Validation]
    H --> I[Statistical Analysis Engine]
    
    I --> J[Descriptive Statistics]
    I --> K[Accident Rate Calculation]
    I --> L[Chi Square Test]
    I --> M[Poisson Modeling]
    I --> N[Regression Analysis]
    
    J --> O[Pattern Identification]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    
    O --> P[Black Spot Identification]
    O --> Q[Countermeasure Design]
    O --> R[Before After Evaluation]
    
    P --> S[Engineering Intervention]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    
    S --> T[Reduced Accidents and Improved Safety]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Accident Database System

For topics requiring complex physical layouts, a functional block diagram is more suitable.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Police FIR Module]
        I2[Hospital EMR Module]
        I3[Insurance Module]
        I4[CCTV and Road Sensors]
    end
    
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
        P1[Data Normalization Engine]
        P2[Geotagging Service]
        P3[Validation Rules Engine]
        P4[Statistical Computing Core]
    end
    
    subgraph STORAGE[Storage Layer]
        S1[Accident Record Warehouse]
        S2[Vehicle Registry]
        S3[Driver History Database]
        S4[Road Inventory Database]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Accident Rate Reports]
        O2[Black Spot Heat Maps]
        O3[Chi Square and Test Outputs]
        O4[Dashboard for Decision Makers]
    end
    
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P2
    
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> S1
    
    S1 --> P4
    S2 --> P4
    S3 --> P4
    S4 --> P4
    
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P4 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Choosing Accident Data Analysis Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: What is Your Research Goal?] --> B{Is the Data Count or Continuous?}
    B -->|Count| C{Is the Event Rare?}
    B -->|Continuous| D[Use Regression Analysis]
    
    C -->|Yes, Less than 10 Percent| E[Use Poisson Distribution]
    C -->|No| F[Use Normal Approximation]
    
    A --> G{Comparing Two Groups?}
    G -->|Yes, Counts| H[Use Chi Square Test]
    G -->|Yes, Means| I[Use t Test]
    G -->|No, Multiple Variables| J[Use ANOVA or Multiple Regression]
    
    A --> K{Spatial Pattern Question?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Use GIS and Morans I]
    K -->|No| M[Proceed with Standard Methods]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Severity Index Hierarchy for Accident Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Road Traffic Accident] --> B{Fatality Involved?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Fatal Accident Weight 9.5]
    B -->|No| D{Grievous Injury Hospitalized?}
    
    D -->|Yes| E[Major Injury Weight 9.5]
    D -->|No| F{Minor Injury First Aid Only?}
    
    F -->|Yes| G[Minor Injury Weight 3.5]
    F -->|No| H[Property Damage Only Weight 1.0]
    
    C --> I[Aggregate EPDO Score]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    
    I --> J[Rank Sites for Remediation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU):** In the ESE, when asked to "draw the accident data collection flowchart" (often a 7-mark sub-part), always label each block with the *type of data* flowing in/out and the *responsible agency*. This fetches 1–2 extra marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any six sources of accident data collection.**

**Model Answer:**

The six primary sources of accident data in India are:

1. **Police Reports (FIR):** The most comprehensive and legally binding source. Filed under IPC sections 279, 304A, 337, 338.
2. **Hospital / Medical Records:** Provide accurate *severity classification* (MAIS scores, ISS) from casualty wards of government and private hospitals.
3. **Insurance Company Records:** Capture *property-damage-only* accidents that are often missed by the police.
4. **Road Authority Records:** NHAI/PWD maintenance logs, CCTV footage, and road inspection reports containing *geometric data*.
5. **Driver / Self-Reporting:** Voluntary reporting through platforms like *iRAD* (Integrated Road Accident Database) by MoRTH.
6. **Special Spot Studies:** Field investigations by traffic engineers for on-site collision reconstruction.

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** Write *any 6* with a *one-line description*. Do not exceed 1 page. A neat numbered list scores full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Accident Severity Index (ASI) and state its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Accident Severity Index (ASI) is the percentage of fatal accidents relative to the total number of accidents at a given location, road stretch, or during a study period.

$$\text{ASI} = \dfrac{\text{Number of fatal accidents}}{\text{Total number of accidents}} \times 100$$

**Significance:**

1. It quantifies the *lethality* of accidents, distinguishing a low-frequency high-severity site from a high-frequency low-severity site.
2. It helps **prioritize** engineering interventions — a stretch with ASI > 5% in urban areas is considered critical.
3. It is used by **MORTH** to rank states and by **IRC** to flag black spots.
4. It is a key input to the *EPDO* (Equivalent Property Damage Only) method for multi-crash-type ranking.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Question A:**
**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of collection of accident data. Discuss the strengths and limitations of each source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** On a 10 km stretch of a State Highway, 36 accidents were recorded in one year. The AADT was 8,000 veh/day. Calculate:
- (i) Accident rate per million vehicle-km. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) If 9 of these accidents were fatal, compute the Accident Severity Index. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If the road authority installed rumble strips and the accidents dropped to 22 in the next year (AADT unchanged at 8,500), test at 5% significance whether the reduction is significant. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Methods of Collection of Accident Data (7 marks)**

Accident data is collected through **six major sources**:

**1. Police Reports (FIR):**
- **Strength:** Comprehensive, legally valid, mandatory, covers location, time, vehicle, and casualty data.
- **Limitation:** Under-reports minor/property-damage accidents; missing pre-crash conditions.

**2. Hospital Records:**
- **Strength:** Accurate medical severity data (MAIS, ISS).
- **Limitation:** No road/environment data; only post-crash information.

**3. Insurance Records:**
- **Strength:** Captures property-damage accidents ignored by police.
- **Limitation:** Biased toward insured and commercial vehicles.

**4. Road Authority Records:**
- **Strength:** Road geometric data (curvature, gradient, sight distance), CCTV footage.
- **Limitation:** Reactive, not proactive.

**5. Driver/Self-Reporting (iRAD):**
- **Strength:** Real-time, geo-tagged, growing coverage.
- **Limitation:** Voluntary participation bias.

**6. Special Spot Studies:**
- **Strength:** Highest accuracy; used in court evidence.
- **Limitation:** Expensive and time-consuming; only at high-risk sites.

> **Conclusion:** A robust database must *triangulate* at least three sources to eliminate bias. The Indian **iRAD** (Integrated Road Accident Database) project by MoRTH is the modern unified platform.

---

#### **Part (b) (i) — Accident Rate (2 marks)**

Given: A = 36, V = 8,000 veh/day, L = 10 km.

$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{A \times 10^6}{V \times L} = \dfrac{36 \times 10^6}{8{,}000 \times 10} = \dfrac{36 \times 10^6}{80{,}000}$$

$$\text{AR} = 450 \text{ accidents per million vehicle-km}$$

---

#### **Part (b) (ii) — Accident Severity Index (2 marks)**

Given: Fatal accidents F = 9, Total accidents A = 36.

$$\text{ASI} = \dfrac{9}{36} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Interpretation:** This stretch is in the *critical* category (IRC threshold for rural highways is ~10–15%). Immediate intervention required.

---

#### **Part (b) (iii) — Chi-Square Test for Significance (3 marks)**

**Hypotheses:**
- H₀: No significant change in accident frequency.
- H₁: Accident frequency has reduced significantly.

**Step 1 — Build observed data:**

| Period | Accidents | No-Accidents (assumed) | Total |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|-------|
| Before | 36 | 964 | 1000 |
| After  | 22 | 978 | 1000 |
| Total  | 58 | 1942 | 2000 |

*(Exposure in terms of accident-prone trips assumed constant at 1000/year each period for illustration.)*

**Step 2 — Compute expected frequencies:**

$$E_{11} = \dfrac{1000 \times 58}{2000} = 29$$
$$E_{12} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1942}{2000} = 971$$
$$E_{21} = \dfrac{1000 \times 58}{2000} = 29$$
$$E_{22} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1942}{2000} = 971$$

**Step 3 — Compute χ²:**

$$\chi^2 = \dfrac{(36-29)^2}{29} + \dfrac{(964-971)^2}{971} + \dfrac{(22-29)^2}{29} + \dfrac{(978-971)^2}{971}$$

$$\chi^2 = \dfrac{49}{29} + \dfrac{49}{971} + \dfrac{49}{29} + \dfrac{49}{971}$$

$$\chi^2 = 1.690 + 0.050 + 1.690 + 0.050 = 3.48$$

**Step 4 — Compare with critical value:**
df = (2−1)(2−1) = 1. Critical χ² at 5% = 3.841.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Since 3.48 < 3.841, we **fail to reject H₀**. The reduction (36 → 22) is *not statistically significant* at the 5% level. The traffic engineer must continue monitoring and consider other factors (regression-to-the-mean, exposure change).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert AADT × length to vehicle-km — losing 1 mark.
> 2. Using "fatalities" instead of "fatal accidents" in ASI — losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. Not stating the critical value or degrees of freedom in χ² — losing 1 mark.
> 4. Confusing *practical* reduction (numerically) with *statistical* significance — losing 1 mark.
> 5. Not writing units in the final answer — losing 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Question B:**
**(a)** Describe the statistical methods used in the analysis of accident data. Explain Poisson distribution and Chi-square test with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data refers to accidents at an urban intersection over 3 years:

| Year | Fatal | Grievous | Minor | PDO | Total |
|------|-------|----------|-------|-----|-------|
| 2022 | 4     | 12       | 25    | 49  | 90    |
| 2023 | 3     | 10       | 22    | 45  | 80    |
| 2024 | 2     | 9        | 19    | 40  | 70    |

Calculate:
- (i) Year-wise Accident Severity Index. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Year-wise EPDO score. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on the trend. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Statistical Methods in Accident Analysis (7 marks)**

The following statistical methods are routinely used:

**1. Descriptive Statistics:** Mean, median, mode, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation of accident counts. Frequency histograms of hour-of-day, day-of-week, and month-of-year.

**2. Accident Rate Analysis:** Normalizes accident count by exposure (veh-km, vehicles, or population). The three standard forms are:
- Per million vehicle-km (road sections).
- Per 1,000 vehicles (vehicle fleets).
- Per 100,000 population (state/national).

**3. Accident Severity Index (ASI):** Quantifies fatality intensity.

**4. EPDO (Equivalent Property Damage Only):** Single composite score combining all crash types using FHWA weights (9.5, 3.5, 1.0).

**5. Chi-Square (χ²) Test:** Tests whether observed frequencies differ significantly from expected. Used in *before-after studies* of safety interventions.

**6. Poisson Distribution:** Models *rare events* like accidents. Probability mass function:

$$P(x) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

Used to compute the probability of "n" accidents occurring at a black spot given the historical mean λ.

**Example:** A black spot has averaged λ = 3 accidents/month. Probability of 0 accidents next month:

$$P(0) = e^{-3} \times 3^0 / 0! = 0.0498 \approx 5\%$$

**7. Regression Analysis:** Models accident frequency as a function of exposure (AADT) and risk factors. Used to develop *Safety Performance Functions (SPFs)*.

**8. Time-Series Analysis:** Detects trends, seasonality, and the effect of policy changes (e.g., seat-belt law impact).

**Example of Chi-Square:**
A 4-legged intersection recorded 50 accidents in 2022 and 38 in 2023 (same traffic). Build 2×2 table, compute χ². If χ² > 3.841 (df=1, 5%), the reduction is statistically significant.

---

#### **Part (b) (i) — Year-wise ASI (2 marks)**

$$\text{ASI}_{2022} = \dfrac{4}{90} \times 100 = 4.44\%$$
$$\text{ASI}_{2023} = \dfrac{3}{80} \times 100 = 3.75\%$$
$$\text{ASI}_{2024} = \dfrac{2}{70} \times 100 = 2.86\%$$

---

#### **Part (b) (ii) — Year-wise EPDO (3 marks)**

Using weights: 9.5 for fatal/major, 3.5 for minor, 1.0 for PDO.

$$\text{EPDO}_{2022} = 9.5 \times (4 + 12) + 3.5 \times 25 + 1.0 \times 49 = 9.5 \times 16 + 87.5 + 49$$
$$= 152 + 87.5 + 49 = 288.5$$

$$\text{EPDO}_{2023} = 9.5 \times (3 + 10) + 3.5 \times 22 + 1.0 \times 45 = 9.5 \times 13 + 77 + 45$$
$$= 123.5 + 77 + 45 = 245.5$$

$$\text{EPDO}_{2024} = 9.5 \times (2 + 9) + 3.5 \times 19 + 1.0 \times 40 = 9.5 \times 11 + 66.5 + 40$$
$$= 104.5 + 66.5 + 40 = 211$$

---

#### **Part (b) (iii) — Trend Comment (2 marks)**

1. **All three metrics (ASI, EPDO, total accidents) show a clear declining trend** from 2022 to 2024.
2. **Total accidents:** 90 → 80 → 70 (≈ 11% reduction per year).
3. **EPDO:** 288.5 → 245.5 → 211 (≈ 14% reduction per year).
4. **ASI:** 4.44% → 3.75% → 2.86% — fatality intensity falling faster than total accidents, indicating *successful safety interventions* (e.g., signal improvement, pedestrian refuge, speed cameras).
5. The intersection can be **re-classified from high-priority to medium-priority** for further safety investment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes on EPDO:**
> 1. Using **fatalities** (count of dead persons) instead of **fatal accidents** — both can be asked; read carefully.
> 2. Using wrong weights (e.g., 10 and 5 instead of 9.5 and 3.5). Always *state the weights you are using*.
> 3. Forgetting that *major injury* and *fatal* sometimes share the same weight in FHWA — students often use different weights.
> 4. Not interpreting the trend qualitatively — examiners expect a *concluding remark*.

---

## 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Mark Losers

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfalls Across All Sub-Questions:**
> 1. **No Units in Final Answer:** A rate of "266.67" without "accidents per million vehicle-km" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Formula Without Derivation / Justification:** Always state *why* the formula is used (e.g., "to normalize by exposure").
> 3. **Confusing "fatal accidents" vs "fatalities":** A single accident can have multiple fatalities. ASI uses *fatal accidents* (per IRC), not *deaths*.
> 4. **Skipping the "Engineering Interpretation":** A numerical answer without interpretation is incomplete. Always end with one sentence of *engineering meaning*.
> 5. **Not Mentioning Triangulation / Multiple Sources:** A 7-mark question on "data collection" that lists only 2–3 sources will be marked down. Aim for at least 4–5 sources with strengths and limitations.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** Accident data = systematic collection + statistical interpretation of crash information.
> 
> ✅ **Six Sources:** Police FIR, Hospital, Insurance, Road Authority, Self-Report (iRAD), Spot Studies — *triangulate at least three*.
> 
> ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
> - AR = (A × 10⁶) / (V × L) — road-section rate
> - AR = (A / N) × 1000 — per-vehicle rate
> - AR = (A / P) × 100,000 — per-capita rate
> - ASI = (F / A) × 100
> - EPDO = 9.5(F + MI) + 3.5(mI) + 1.0(PDO)
> - χ² = Σ[(O − E)² / E]
> - Poisson: P(x) = e^(−λ) λˣ / x!
> 
> ✅ **Statistical Tests:**
> - χ² for *categorical* comparisons (before-after).
> - Poisson for *rare events* (accident counts).
> - Regression for *continuous predictors* (AADT vs accidents).
> 
> ✅ **Engineering Tools:**
> - Black-spot identification (≥ 5 accidents / 500 m / year).
> - EPDO ranking for prioritization.
> - Before-after χ² test for countermeasure evaluation.
> 
> ✅ **Indian Context:**
> - IRC:SP:88 (Manual on Road Safety Audit).
> - MoRTH's iRAD database (since 2021).
> - Kerala ranks among top 5 states in accident fatality rate.
> 
> ✅ **Common Units to Remember:**
> - veh/day (AADT)
> - veh-km (exposure)
> - accidents per million veh-km (rate)
> - accidents per 1,000 vehicles (fleet rate)
> - accidents per 100,000 population (state rate)
> 
> ✅ **Examiner's Golden Rule:** *Every numerical answer MUST have:*
> 1. Formula stated (1 mark).
> 2. Values substituted with units (1 mark).
> 3. Arithmetic shown (1–2 marks).
> 4. Final boxed answer with units (0.5 mark).
> 5. One-line engineering interpretation (0.5–1 mark).
> 
> ✅ **Mnemonic for Six Sources:** **"PHIRDS"** — **P**olice, **H**ospital, **I**nsurance, **R**oad authority, **D**river (self), **S**pot studies.
> 
> ✅ **Mnemonic for Statistical Tools:** **"PCRT"** — **P**oisson (rare events), **C**hi-square (comparison), **R**egression (continuous), **T**rend (time-series).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision and Condition Diagram
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET637 - TRAFFIC ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collision and Condition Diagram

# Module 4 — Collision and Condition Diagram

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Collision Diagram — Formal Definition

A **Collision Diagram** (also referred to as an *Accident Diagram* or *Crash Diagram*) is a schematic, scaled plan view of an intersection or roadway segment on which all reported traffic collisions occurring over a defined study period (typically **1 to 3 years**) are plotted using standardized symbols. Each symbol encodes the **collision type**, **direction of movement**, **severity**, and **environmental conditions** at the moment of the crash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A collision diagram is a one-page graphical representation of the road/intersection where every police-reported accident is plotted using standardized symbols to identify patterns in type, location, and timing of crashes. It is the **first diagnostic tool** in the accident analysis process.

### 1.2 Condition Diagram — Formal Definition

A **Condition Diagram** is a detailed, scaled plan showing all *physical and environmental characteristics* of a roadway or intersection **independent of any accident data**. It documents geometric, operational, and roadside features that may contribute to crashes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *condition diagram* shows **what the road looks like** (geometry, signs, markings, terrain, land use). A *collision diagram* shows **what went wrong** (accidents, types, severities). Both are used **together** during accident analysis.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Before diagnosing an illness, the doctor:
- First draws a **map of the patient's body** (skeleton, organs) — this is the **Condition Diagram**.
- Then marks every spot where the patient felt pain, when it happened, and how severe it was — this is the **Collision Diagram**.

In traffic engineering:
- The **Condition Diagram** is the "map" of the road (lanes, signs, signals, sight lines, trees, poles, driveways, gradients).
- The **Collision Diagram** is the "pain chart" (every crash plotted with type, direction, severity).

When overlaid mentally, the engineer can see **why** crashes cluster at specific spots — perhaps poor sight distance, confusing signage, or inadequate lighting.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics in Collision Analysis

The following standardized metrics are universally used (and required for KTU answers):

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Accident Rate | $R$ | accidents per **million vehicle-kilometers** |
| Accident Frequency | $N$ | accidents per year |
| Severity Index | $SI$ | dimensionless (weighted) |
| Study Period | $T$ | years |
| Traffic Volume | $AADT$ | vehicles per day |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners expect you to use **AADT (Annual Average Daily Traffic)** and **MVM (Million Vehicle Miles) or MVK (Million Vehicle Kilometers)** as standard units when quoting accident rates.

### 1.5 Purpose of These Diagrams (KTU Bloom Level: *Understand / Apply*)

1. **Identify collision patterns** — clustering by location, type, or time.
2. **Establish causation hypotheses** — correlate with physical conditions.
3. **Rank sites** for safety improvements.
4. **Justify remedial measures** (signals, signs, geometric changes).
5. **Monitor effectiveness** of countermeasures over time.
6. **Support legal and administrative** documentation.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Condition Diagram

A properly drawn **Condition Diagram** must include the following elements (these are KTU-favorite listing questions):

**A. Roadway Geometry**
- Number of lanes and lane widths (typically **3.5 m** for urban, **3.7 m** for rural)
- Median type and width (raised, painted, flush)
- Pavement surface type and condition
- Horizontal curvature and super-elevation
- Vertical gradient (%)
- Cross-slope / camber
- Shoulder type and width

**B. Traffic Control Devices**
- Signal heads, location, phasing
- Stop signs, yield signs
- Pavement markings (lane lines, stop bars, crosswalks)
- Pedestrian signals and push buttons

**C. Roadside Features**
- Curbs, sidewalks, footpaths
- Street lighting poles
- Trees, utility poles, drainage structures
- Adjacent land use (residential, commercial, school, hospital)
- Driveways and access points

**D. Visibility and Sight Distance**
- Available stopping sight distance (SSD)
- Intersection sight distance (ISD)
- Vertical and horizontal sight obstructions

**E. Environmental Conditions**
- Drainage patterns
- Terrain classification (level, rolling, mountainous)

### 2.2 Standardized Symbols for Collision Diagrams

Each accident type has a unique, internationally accepted symbol. KTU expects students to **draw and label** at least 6–8 of these:

| Symbol | Accident Type |
|---|---|
| →→ (arrows aligned) | **Rear-end collision** |
| ←→ (arrows opposed) | **Head-on collision** |
| →↘ (perpendicular) | **Angle / Side-impact collision** |
| →‖→ (parallel) | **Sideswipe (same direction)** |
| →‖← (parallel) | **Sideswipe (opposite)** |
| →↻ | **Right-turn collision** |
| ↺← | **Left-turn collision** |
| ✕ (crossed) | **Pedestrian collision** |
| ◯ (circle at arrow tip) | **Collision with parked vehicle** |
| ⊥ (arrow into wall) | **Collision with fixed object** |
| ↗ off-road | **Run-off-road** |

### 2.3 Severity Coding

Severity is indicated by symbol **fill** or **number suffix**:

- **Solid/filled symbol** → Fatal accident
- **Half-filled symbol** → Serious / Grievous injury
- **Empty/hollow symbol** → Minor injury
- **Dotted outline** → Property Damage Only (PDO)

### 2.4 Time and Condition Annotations

A well-prepared collision diagram also annotates:
- **Time of day** (peak / off-peak / night) — often shown with subscript number next to symbol
- **Day of week** or **month** (to detect seasonal patterns)
- **Weather condition** (rain / fog / clear) — by symbol color or letter
- **Road surface condition** (wet / dry / icy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Tip:** In 14-mark questions, examiners often supply a *tabulated accident data* and ask you to draw the collision diagram. Always show **at least 10–15 plotted accidents**, label axes (N-S / E-W), and include a **legend**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Accident Analysis Metrics

| Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accident Rate | $R = \dfrac{N \times 10^6}{365 \times AADT \times L \times T}$ | accidents per MVM (or MVK) | Comparing segments of different lengths and volumes |
| Accident Frequency | $N$ | accidents / year | Absolute count |
| Severity Index | $SI = \dfrac{9.5 F + 3.5 I + P}{N}$ | weighted score | Overall severity profile |
| Critical Accident Rate | $R_c = R_a + K \sqrt{\dfrac{R_a}{M}} + \dfrac{1}{2M}$ | accidents/MVM | Poisson-based statistical test |
| EPDO (Equivalent PDO) | $EPDO = 9.5 F + 3.5 I + 1.0 P$ | PDO equivalents | Economic ranking of sites |
| Conflict Rate | $CR = \dfrac{C}{V}$ | conflicts/1000 veh | Surrogate safety measure |

Where:
- $F$ = number of fatal accidents
- $I$ = number of injury accidents  
- $P$ = number of PDO accidents
- $N$ = total number of accidents
- $L$ = length of segment (km or miles)
- $T$ = study period (years)
- $K$ = probability factor (1.645 for 95% confidence)
- $M$ = million vehicle-kilometers of exposure
- $C$ = number of traffic conflicts
- $V$ = traffic volume in thousands

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **never use the vertical bar `|` inside math tables**. The vertical pipe breaks markdown table rendering. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in formal LaTeX notation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Safety Manual (HSM)** uses collision diagrams as Step 1 of network screening.
- **Blackspot identification** programs in India (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways) mandate collision diagrams.
- **Road Safety Audits (RSA)** require condition + collision diagrams for Stage 5 audits.
- **Insurance and legal investigations** rely on these diagrams for forensic reconstruction.
- **Smart city traffic management** integrates digitized collision diagrams with GIS layers for real-time hotspot detection.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Accident Rate Formula

The accident rate normalizes raw accident counts by exposure — defined as the product of vehicle-kilometers traveled during the study period.

**Step 1 — Express the total vehicle-kilometers of travel on the segment.**

Daily vehicle-kilometers = $AADT \times L$  
(where $AADT$ is Annual Average Daily Traffic in veh/day, $L$ is segment length in km)

**Step 2 — Convert to annual vehicle-kilometers.**

$$V_{annual} = 365 \times AADT \times L$$

**Step 3 — Convert to million vehicle-kilometers (MVK) for the study period $T$ years.**

$$V_{MVK} = \frac{365 \times AADT \times L \times T}{10^6}$$

**Step 4 — Define accident rate $R$ as accidents per MVK.**

$$R = \frac{N}{V_{MVK}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4.**

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{N \times 10^6}{365 \times AADT \times L \times T}\,}$$

This is the canonical form expected in KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> If the question uses **MVM** (Million Vehicle Miles), replace $L$ in km with $L$ in miles. 1 mile = 1.609 km.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Accident Rate Computation

**Problem (Model KTU 3-mark):**
A 2.0 km urban arterial has experienced 12 reported accidents over 2 years. The AADT is 8,500 veh/day. Calculate the accident rate in accidents per million vehicle-kilometers.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $N = 12$ accidents
- $L = 2.0$ km  
- $AADT = 8{,}500$ veh/day  
- $T = 2$ years

Apply the formula:

$$R = \frac{N \times 10^6}{365 \times AADT \times L \times T}$$

$$R = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{365 \times 8500 \times 2.0 \times 2}$$

Calculate denominator step-by-step:
- $365 \times 8500 = 3{,}102{,}500$
- $3{,}102{,}500 \times 2.0 = 6{,}205{,}000$
- $6{,}205{,}000 \times 2 = 12{,}410{,}000$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12.41 \times 10^6} = 0.967 \text{ accidents/MVK}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.97 \text{ accidents per million vehicle-kilometers}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Critical Accident Rate (Poisson Test)

The Critical Accident Rate is used to determine whether a site's observed accident rate is **statistically significantly higher** than the system-wide average — a key step before declaring a location a "blackspot."

**Step 1 — Assume accidents follow a Poisson distribution** with mean $\lambda = R_a \times M$, where $R_a$ is the average system accident rate and $M$ is the exposure (in MVK) at the test site.

**Step 2 — Standardize the Poisson distribution** using the normal approximation for large $M$:

$$Z = \frac{X - \lambda}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

**Step 3 — For 95% confidence**, set $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$. Solving for $X$ (the critical accident count):

$$X_c = \lambda + 1.645 \sqrt{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by exposure $M$** to convert count to rate:

$$R_c = \frac{\lambda}{M} + \frac{1.645}{M}\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{M}} = R_a + 1.645 \sqrt{\frac{R_a}{M}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply a continuity correction** (subtract 0.5 from numerator) and divide by $2M$:

$$\boxed{\,R_c = R_a + 1.645\sqrt{\frac{R_a}{M}} + \frac{1}{2M}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $K$ replaces 1.645 in the general formula. For 95% confidence, $K = 1.645$; for 99% confidence, $K = 2.33$; for 90% confidence, $K = 1.28$.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure to Prepare a Collision Diagram

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** (often asked for 7 marks):

**Step 1 — Site Selection**
Choose the intersection or segment based on complaint frequency, police reports, or preliminary accident data.

**Step 2 — Data Collection**
- Obtain police accident reports (FIRs) for **at least 1 year** (ideally 3 years).
- Extract: date, time, type, severity, vehicles involved, weather, road conditions, driver actions.

**Step 3 — Prepare the Base Map**
- Draw roadway geometry **to scale** (use 1:200 for intersections, 1:500 for segments).
- Show lane widths, medians, curbs, sidewalks, signal poles, signs, and pavement markings.
- This is essentially the **Condition Diagram** overlay.

**Step 4 — Define Symbol Legend**
- Pre-decide symbols for each accident type.
- Pre-decide severity coding (fill, color, number).
- Pre-decide annotations (time, weather).

**Step 5 — Plot Each Accident**
- Locate the approximate point of impact on the plan.
- Draw movement arrows showing pre-crash vehicle paths.
- Use the appropriate symbol and severity coding.
- Annotate time/date/condition as a subscript.

**Step 6 — Analyze Patterns**
- Cluster analysis: which locations have repeated crashes?
- Type analysis: which maneuver is most frequent?
- Time analysis: is there a peak-hour pattern?
- Severity analysis: are clusters of fatal crashes present?

**Step 7 — Correlate with Condition Diagram**
- Overlay mentally (or physically) with condition features.
- Hypothesize causes: poor sight distance, confusing geometry, slippery surface, inadequate lighting.

**Step 8 — Recommend Countermeasures**
- Engineering: realignment, signals, signs, markings, lighting.
- Enforcement: speed limits, red-light cameras.
- Education: driver awareness programs.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Collision Diagram vs. Condition Diagram

| Parameter | Collision Diagram | Condition Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Shows *where* and *how* crashes occur | Shows *what* the site looks like |
| **Data Source** | Police accident reports | Field survey, design drawings, Google Earth |
| **Time Dimension** | Includes date, time, day of week | Time-invariant (snapshot) |
| **Accidents Plotted** | Yes — all reported crashes | No accidents shown |
| **Scale** | Schematic (not necessarily to scale) | Must be to scale |
| **Update Frequency** | Updated annually as new data arrives | Updated only when physical features change |
| **Primary User** | Safety analyst, traffic engineer | Design engineer, road safety auditor |
| **KTU Question Type** | "Draw and explain…" / "Identify patterns from given data" | "List the features shown in a condition diagram" |
| **Output** | Pattern identification, hot-spot map | Physical deficiency identification |
| **Limitation** | Underreporting bias (only police-reported) | Does not show accident history |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workflow — From Data to Countermeasure

The complete engineering workflow using both diagrams is shown below:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Problem Site] --> B[Collect Police Accident Reports]
    B --> C[Tabulate Accident Data 1 to 3 years]
    C --> D[Conduct Field Survey]
    D --> E[Prepare Condition Diagram]
    E --> F[Prepare Collision Diagram]
    F --> G[Pattern Analysis]
    G --> H{Cluster Detected?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Cross Reference with Condition Diagram]
    H -->|No| J[Continue Monitoring]
    I --> K[Hypothesize Causation]
    K --> L[Recommend Countermeasures]
    L --> M[Engineering Fix]
    L --> N[Enforcement Fix]
    L --> O[Education Fix]
    M --> P[Re-evaluate After 1 Year]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{Accidents Reduced?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Document Best Practice]
    Q -->|No| S[Iterate - New Countermeasures]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Accident Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A1[Police FIR Data]
        A2[Field Survey Data]
        A3[Traffic Volume Counts]
    end
    
    subgraph Processing
        B1[Condition Diagram Builder]
        B2[Collision Diagram Builder]
        B3[Statistical Engine]
    end
    
    subgraph Analysis
        C1[Pattern Recognition]
        C2[Hotspot Identification]
        C3[Causation Hypotheses]
    end
    
    subgraph Output
        D1[Countermeasure List]
        D2[Priority Ranking]
        D3[Cost-Benefit Report]
    end
    
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> B3
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Standardized Collision Symbols (Visual Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Rear End] -->|"→ →"| A1
    A2[Head On] -->|"← →"| A2
    A3[Angle] -->|"→ ↓"| A3
    A4[Sideswipe Same] -->|"= ="| A4
    A5[Sideswipe Opposite] -->|"= ="| A5
    A6[Left Turn] -->|"↰→"| A6
    A7[Right Turn] -->|"→↱"| A7
    A8[Pedestrian] -->|"→ ✕"| A8
    A9[Fixed Object] -->|"→ |"| A9
    A10[Run Off Road] -->|"→/"| A10
```

### 4.4 Severity Coding Convention

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Fatal Accident] -->|Solid Black Fill| S1
    S2[Grievous Injury] -->|Half Filled| S2
    S3[Minor Injury] -->|Hollow Outline| S3
    S4[Property Damage Only] -->|Dotted Outline| S4
```

### 4.5 Schematic Layout of a Typical 4-Leg Intersection (Base Map)

```
                    N
                    │
        ┌───────────┼───────────┐
        │           │           │
        │   Lane ←──┼──→ Lane   │
   W ───┤           │           ├─── E
        │   Lane ←──┼──→ Lane   │
        │           │           │
        └───────────┼───────────┘
                    │
                    S
        [Medians, crosswalks, stop bars, signal heads
         shown on Condition Diagram overlay]
```

> [!TIP]
> When drawing for KTU answers, **always show North arrow, scale, legend, study period, and data source** at the bottom of your collision diagram. Examiners specifically check for these.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define a collision diagram. List any six standardized symbols used to represent different types of accidents on a collision diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A collision diagram is a schematic plan view of an intersection or road segment on which all reported traffic accidents over a study period (usually 1–3 years) are plotted using standardized symbols to identify patterns in type, location, and timing of crashes.

Six standardized symbols:

1. **→ →** — Rear-end collision
2. **← →** — Head-on collision  
3. **→ ↓** — Angle / Side-impact collision
4. **→ ‖ →** — Sideswipe (same direction)
5. **→ ‖ ←** — Sideswipe (opposite direction)
6. **→ ✕** — Pedestrian collision

*(Any six valid symbols — full 3 marks. Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for symbols list.)*

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between a collision diagram and a condition diagram. Mention any four features shown in a condition diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Collision Diagram | Condition Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Shows | Accident history and patterns | Physical features of the site |
| Time | Dynamic (varies with crashes) | Static (snapshot) |
| Accident data | Yes — all reported crashes plotted | No accidents shown |

Four features shown in a condition diagram:
1. Roadway geometry (lane widths, medians, curvature, gradient)
2. Traffic control devices (signals, signs, pavement markings)
3. Roadside features (curbs, sidewalks, lighting, trees, utility poles)
4. Adjacent land use and sight distance conditions

*(1 mark difference + 2 marks for features. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure to prepare a collision diagram for a four-legged intersection. List the data required and the symbols used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data refers to accidents reported on a 1.5 km stretch of an urban road over a period of 2 years. The AADT is 6,000 veh/day. Determine:
- (i) The accident rate in accidents per million vehicle-kilometers
- (ii) The EPDO (Equivalent Property Damage Only) value, given 2 fatal, 5 injury, and 8 PDO accidents. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

The procedure to prepare a collision diagram consists of the following steps:

**Step 1 — Site identification and data collection (2 marks)**

The first step is to select the site based on prior complaint frequency or preliminary accident data. Police accident reports (FIRs) covering a minimum period of 1 year (ideally 3 years) are collected. From each report, the following data are extracted:

- Date and time of accident
- Type of collision (rear-end, angle, head-on, etc.)
- Severity (fatal, grievous injury, minor injury, PDO)
- Vehicles involved and their direction of travel
- Weather and road surface conditions
- Driver actions (over-speeding, overtaking, jumping signal)

**Step 2 — Preparation of base map (2 marks)**

A scaled plan of the intersection is drawn. This base map includes all physical features — roadway geometry, lane widths, medians, signals, signs, crosswalks, and curbs. The base map is essentially the condition diagram overlay, drawn to a scale of typically 1:200 for intersections.

**Step 3 — Symbol definition and legend (1 mark)**

Standardized symbols are chosen for each accident type. Severity is indicated by symbol fill (solid = fatal, half-filled = grievous, hollow = minor, dotted = PDO). Time and weather are annotated as subscripts or in distinct colors.

**Step 4 — Plotting and analysis (2 marks)**

Each accident is plotted at its approximate point of impact with directional arrows showing pre-crash vehicle paths. After all accidents are plotted, pattern analysis is performed: clustering by location, type, and time. The collision diagram is then correlated with the condition diagram to identify causation factors, leading to countermeasure recommendations.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Length of road, $L = 1.5$ km  
- Study period, $T = 2$ years  
- $AADT = 6{,}000$ veh/day  
- Number of fatal accidents, $F = 2$  
- Number of injury accidents, $I = 5$  
- Number of PDO accidents, $P = 8$  
- Total accidents, $N = 2 + 5 + 8 = 15$

**Part (i): Accident rate in accidents/MVK** (3 marks)

$$R = \frac{N \times 10^6}{365 \times AADT \times L \times T}$$

Compute denominator:
- $365 \times 6{,}000 = 2{,}190{,}000$
- $2{,}190{,}000 \times 1.5 = 3{,}285{,}000$
- $3{,}285{,}000 \times 2 = 6{,}570{,}000$

Substitute:

$$R = \frac{15 \times 10^6}{6.57 \times 10^6} = 2.283 \text{ accidents/MVK}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 2.28 \text{ accidents per million vehicle-kilometers}}$$

**Part (ii): EPDO value** (4 marks)

The Equivalent Property Damage Only formula uses the FHWA standard weights:

$$EPDO = 9.5 F + 3.5 I + 1.0 P$$

Substitute:

$$EPDO = 9.5 \times 2 + 3.5 \times 5 + 1.0 \times 8$$

$$EPDO = 19.0 + 17.5 + 8.0$$

$$\boxed{EPDO = 44.5 \text{ PDO equivalents}}$$

This value can be used to compare this site with other sites for prioritization of safety improvements.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is a condition diagram? List all the major features that should be shown on a condition diagram of an urban intersection. Explain how the condition diagram is used along with the collision diagram for accident analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At a signalized intersection, the average system accident rate is 1.8 accidents per MVK. The exposure at the test site is 12 MVK. Using the Poisson-based critical accident rate method, determine the critical accident rate at 95% confidence level. State whether a site with 25 accidents is to be flagged as a blackspot. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

A condition diagram is a detailed scaled plan of an intersection or roadway segment that shows all physical and environmental features of the site, prepared independently of any accident data.

**Major features of a condition diagram (4 marks):**

**A. Roadway Geometry:**
- Number of lanes and lane widths
- Median type (raised / painted / flush) and width
- Horizontal curvature, super-elevation
- Vertical gradient and cross-slope
- Shoulder type and width
- Pavement type and condition

**B. Traffic Control Devices:**
- Signal heads, signal phasing, controller location
- Stop signs, yield signs, regulatory signs
- Pavement markings (lane lines, stop bars, pedestrian crossings)
- Pedestrian signals and push buttons

**C. Roadside Features:**
- Curbs, sidewalks, footpaths
- Street lighting poles and luminaires
- Trees, utility poles, drainage inlets
- Adjacent land use (residential, commercial, institutional)
- Driveways, access points, parking areas

**D. Visibility and Environmental Features:**
- Available stopping sight distance
